Stage 2 applies a taxonomy-driven LLM classifier to partition skill bodies into core rules, examples, background, templates, and redundant content, compresses each type differently, and restructures the result into an always-loaded core plus on-demand reference modules (progressive disclosure). - The good: Compressed skills improve task performance by 2.8 percentage points on average over originals (retention 0.742 vs. 0.722, p=0.002), confirming a less-is-more effect where removing non-essential content reduces context-window distraction — independently replicated on SkillsBench (87/87 tasks pass with zero regression). - The missing: The wild-skill scale-out (198 skills) omits Gate 2 task evaluation due to cost, so the 77.5% body compression claimed for that cohort has no functional quality verification beyond a faithfulness check; the less-is-more improvement at scale is an extrapolation, not a measurement. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or maintain LLM coding agent skill libraries and care about token budgets; skip it if your primary concern is model architecture or training, as this is a pure inference-time prompt engineering/preprocessing contribution. Problem & Motivation LLM-based coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf) rely on pre-packaged skill files — Markdown instruction sets loaded into the context window on demand — to encode reusable domain knowledge. These skills have two functional layers: a short description used by the agent runtime for routing (deciding which skill to invoke), and a body injected in full upon invocation. A systematic audit of 55,315 publicly available skills from GitHub, SkillHub, and developer forums reveals two compounding inefficiencies. First, 26.4% of Wild skills have no description at all, causing routing failures, while many that exist are either too short (<20 tokens, 44.1% combined) or verbose with non-routing content (trigger phrase enumerations, feature lists). Second, only 38.5% of body content consists of actionable core rules; 40.7% is background explanation, 12.9% examples, and 7.6% boilerplate templates — all injected unconditionally regardless of task relevance. Reference files (present in 14.8% of skills) compound the problem: 100 SkillHub skills collectively carry 1.67M tokens of references. These inefficiencies impose monetary cost and, crucially, reduce agent performance by diluting core instructions with irrelevant context. Key Idea The key insight is that skill content has implicit structure analogous to source code: descriptions decompose into independent semantic routing clauses (like test cases), and bodies decompose into content types with different task relevance. Applying DDMIN (a test-case minimization algorithm from software engineering) to semantic clauses finds the minimal description subset that preserves routing behavior without exhaustive search — O(n log n) oracle calls instead of O(2^n). For bodies, adapting program slicing (identifying which content directly affects task execution) via a taxonomy-driven LLM classifier enables targeted, type-specific compression and on-demand loading, unlike uniform token-pruning methods that cannot distinguish actionable rules from illustrative examples. Methodology Stage 1 (Routing Layer Optimization) operates in two phases. Phase 1 segments a description into semantic clauses using an LLM (DeepSeek-V3), then runs DDMIN against a simulated routing oracle O(d, Q, C) — a function returning 1 iff a routing model (DeepSeek-R1) consistently selects the target skill for all queries in Q against a candidate pool of 4 TF-IDF distractors plus one adversarial shadow skill. Each retained clause is individually paraphrased to its shortest form, then grammatically polished. Phase 2 validates the simulated-oracle result against the real Claude Code CLI: queries triggering the original description form a validation set Q_val; if the compressed description fails any query, selective restore greedily re-adds the clause most improving trigger rate (up to 3 restores). For skills missing descriptions (<40 tokens), an LLM extracts three routing signals from the body (primary capability, trigger condition, unique identifiers) and generates one. Stage 2 (Body Restructuring) first classifies each paragraph-level item into five categories via DeepSeek-V3 with cross-validation among neighbors; core rules are compressed by semantic merging into concise bullet points; examples are deduplicated to one per concept; templates are stripped of boilerplate comments; background is summarized preserving factual claims; redundant content is discarded. Cross-file deduplication removes reference content already captured in the core body. Each surviving reference is annotated with a "when" clause and 3–5 topic keywords enabling on-demand loading. Gate 1 verifies faithfulness: an LLM checks whether all operational concepts from the original body appear in the compressed core plus references (formally: C_τ(s.b) ⊆ C_τ(b*) ∪ ∪ C_τ(r)). Gate 2 generates five diverse evaluation tasks, runs an agent under three conditions (no skill, original, compressed), and scores via code execution (52.3% of tasks) or LLM rubric judge (47.7%), using Qwen3.5 as evaluator on a separate API session to prevent leakage. A feedback loop promotes non-core items to core when Gate 2 fails, iterating up to twice. Critical design decisions: (1) semantic-clause granularity for DDMIN is justified as the natural unit for routing signals — finer (word-level) or coarser (sentence-level) splits both underperform; (2) taxonomy-driven classification rather than uniform pruning is justified empirically (6.8pp retention gap in ablation); (3) the feedback loop is justified by the 82% recovery rate on triggered skills. Key Results Evaluated on 600 skills (87 Official/SkillHub, 464 Community, 49 Wild), generating and evaluating 3,000 tasks across three conditions. Stage 1 achieves 48.0% mean description token reduction (95% CI: [45.2%, 50.8%]); Stage 2 achieves 39.0% mean body reduction (95% CI: [36.2%, 41.8%]), saving ~1,000 tokens per skill on average. On SkillsBench (87 tasks, 229 skills, deterministic pytest verifiers), body tokens drop from 359K to 84K (75.0% per-task reduction) with 87/87 passing under both original and compressed conditions. Gate 2 task evaluation across all 600 skills shows 86.0% of skills achieve score_C ≥ score_A (pass rate), with 25.3% strictly improving (less-is-more) and only 14.0% regressing. Mean retention is 0.742 vs. 0.722 (p=0.002, d=0.107) favoring compressed. Ablation (50 skills × 5 tasks) confirms taxonomy classification is the dominant component: omitting it drops retention by 6.8pp; reference deduplication alone achieves retention 1.000 (pure gain). At same token budget, SkillReducer outperforms LLMLingua (p<0.001, W/T/L=28/16/6), LLM direct compression (p=0.003, W/T/L=19/26/5), truncation (p<0.001, W/T/L=27/17/6), and random removal (p<0.001, W/T/L=38/10/2). Cross-model evaluation on 30 skills across 5 models from 4 families (GLM-5, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-max, GPT-OSS-120B, Qwen2.5-7B) yields mean retention 0.965; the weakest model (Qwen2.5-7B) achieves 0.939, confirming compression transfers across capability levels. Cross-framework validation on OpenCode (v1.2.27, 120K+ GitHub stars) achieves retention 0.944. Wild-skill scale validation on 198 skills shows 77.5% core compression (median 81.5%), with faithfulness passing 74.7% (148/198); the higher compression rate versus the evaluation set reflects longer, more redundant wild skill bodies. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The application of DDMIN to semantic routing description minimization and program slicing principles to free-form natural language skill body restructuring are genuinely novel adaptations of established SE techniques to a new domain. The empirical finding that token removal systematically improves agent task performance (less-is-more, +2.8pp) is non-obvious and provides independent support for prior work on context-length distraction effects. The taxonomy itself (five categories validated by UMAP/GMM clustering) is a reusable contribution. The combination of techniques is a standard pipeline architecture, but the individual adaptations and the empirical findings distinguishing it from generic prompt compression are the genuine contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study covers 50 skills × 5 tasks across six conditions including individual stage components. Bootstrapped 95% CIs (10,000 resamples) are reported for all major metrics. Non-parametric Wilcoxon signed-rank test is used appropriately given ceiling ties. The LLM judge is validated against deterministic pytest verifiers on 1,646 overlapping tasks (Cohen's κ=0.939), which is a strong validation. Separate models are used for compression (DeepSeek-V3) and evaluation (Qwen3.5) to prevent information leakage. Sensitivity analysis breaks retention by compression ratio (range 0.910–0.956 across five bins), showing no systematic degradation with aggressive compression. The Gate 2 evaluation serves dual roles — feedback signal and evaluation criterion — which the paper acknowledges as a threat to internal validity (the 86% pass rate partly reflects optimization-to-criterion). SkillsBench (87/87, deterministic verifiers) partially mitigates this. The tool is to be released as open-source upon acceptance; no URL or repo is provided in the preprint. Wild-skill scale validation lacks Gate 2 functional evaluation (cost-omitted), leaving body compression efficacy at scale without task-based verification. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (system evaluated on real agent runtime tasks). - Practical: Runs on commercially available LLM APIs (DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3.5, DeepSeek-R1); requires Claude Code CLI for real-trigger validation but this is a freely available tool. Runs as a build-time preprocessing step, not on the inference critical path. - Efficient: Compressing 600 skills costs ~$14–18 total (~20–40 LLM calls per skill at low-cost open-source model rates ~$0.3/M input tokens); DDMIN description compression runs ~2 seconds per oracle call; Stage 2 classification adds further LLM calls. Cost is a one-time authoring expense amortized within a few hundred invocations per skill. - Effective: Evidence shows compression preserves or improves task performance on 86.0% of skills with a statistically significant less-is-more effect; 87/87 SkillsBench tasks pass with zero regression under deterministic pytest verifiers. - Scalable: Tested on a curated evaluation set of 600 skills and a wild sample of 198 skills; scalability to tens of thousands of simultaneous skills or to much longer bodies (>10K tokens) is partially addressed — the longest tier (>10K tokens) achieves 95.8% retention, the highest of any tier — but end-to-end pipeline cost at 55,315-skill scale is not measured. The tool delivers a software artifact (open-source build-time preprocessing pipeline). Strengths - The DDMIN adaptation to semantic clause minimization is a principled and computationally tractable solution: O(n log n) oracle calls with 100% routing preservation among non-obsolete skills (536/536), outperforming four baselines at equal token budget. - The less-is-more empirical finding (+2.8pp, p=0.002) is the most important result: it reframes skill compression from a cost-preservation tradeoff to a quality-improvement opportunity, independently validated on SkillsBench with deterministic verifiers. - Cross-model generalization with mean retention 0.965 across four model families and cross-framework generalization to OpenCode (0.944) establishes that the quality improvement is structural (driven by content selection, not model-specific behavior), as confirmed by the cross-compressor validation (Qwen3-max: 0.897, Qwen2.5-7B: 0.874 as compressors vs. DeepSeek-V3: 0.978). - The large-scale empirical study (55,315 skills) documenting systemic inefficiencies provides a credible, data-driven motivation that distinguishes this from incremental prompt compression work and quantifies the scope of the problem for the field. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The simulated oracle is overly optimistic (testing in a controlled candidate pool rather than real agent runtime), addressed by Phase 2 real-environment validation. The taxonomy-based classifier can miss implicit rule-example dependencies (examples that implicitly define expected behavior), representing a fundamental limitation of automated separation of concerns; these cases move examples to reference modules and must be in the always-loaded core, causing residual regression. Gate 2 evaluation tasks may not cover all deployment failure modes (analogous to incomplete test suites). 10.7% of skills are obsolete (they do not trigger even with original descriptions), motivating adaptive skill lifecycle management outside SkillReducer's scope. Generalization to Cursor and Windsurf remains untested. The authors confront these limitations directly and quantify their scope (4.7% true compression failures; 82% feedback loop recovery rate). Analyst-Identified Gaps - Wild-skill compression (198 skills, 77.5% body reduction) lacks Gate 2 task-based evaluation; only faithfulness (Gate 1) is verified for 74.7% of that sample. The claimed 77.5% compression at scale has no functional quality anchor, making the scale-out claim partially unverified. - The 86% pass rate conflates 19.8% ceiling cases (D=A=C=1.0) where the task is trivially solvable without any skill; the non-ceiling pass rate for condition C is 82.5% (Table III note), which is the more meaningful figure and should headline the results. - Stage 1 real-trigger validation runs exclusively through Claude Code CLI; Cursor and Windsurf use different skill routing mechanisms, and the 100% routing preservation claim is specific to this agent. - The taxonomy LLM classifier uses DeepSeek-V3 (temperature 0) with cross-validation among item neighbors, but silhouette score 0.393 indicates only moderate cluster separation — the classifier's per-item accuracy on held-out ground-truth labels is not reported; downstream quality is measured but classification error rate is not decomposed. - End-to-end token savings of 26.8% mean with −0.3% median divergence (mean >median) suggests right-skewed savings driven by a minority of very large skills; median-dominant users may see more modest practical benefit. Bottom Line A practitioner maintaining or distributing LLM agent skill libraries gains a directly actionable, empirically grounded tool: SkillReducer demonstrably reduces invocation costs while improving task performance, and the less-is-more finding is credible enough to motivate adoption even before the open-source release.
Recommended 2603.29919 PDF
#24 — Highly Innovative (76)

Target Policy Optimization (19 ▲ )

Jean Kaddour · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) non-obvious simplification with clean theoretical framing. Rigor (80) thorough ablations, 10-30 seeds with error bands, isolated Group PG ablation. Non-Obv (80) the self-extinguishing fixed-point insight is genuinely non-obvious. Completeness (80) limitations confronted directly. Resilience (70) honest about untested scale. Impact (75) public code in both JAX/PyTorch, drop-in replacement. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*80+0.15*80+0.15*80+0.15*70+0.10*75 ≈ 76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 80 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Target Policy Optimization (TPO), a group-based RL algorithm that decouples the redistribution decision (which completions gain probability mass) from the optimizer mechanics (how parameters realize that change) by constructing a closed-form target distribution and fitting the policy to it via cross-entropy. - Why it's important: Standard policy-gradient methods like GRPO entangle the desired redistribution with optimizer choices (learning rate, clipping), causing brittle or failed learning when reward is sparse—the regime that matters most for LLM RLVR on hard reasoning tasks. - How it does it: Given K scored completions, TPO standardizes the scores, exponentially tilts the rollout policy to form a target q_i ∝ p_i^old · exp(u_i/η), then minimizes cross-entropy to q; the gradient p^θ − q vanishes exactly once the policy matches the target, creating a self-extinguishing fixed point. - The good: On terminal-reward transformer tasks (H=10, prompt-matched), TPO achieves 7.4% exact-match error versus 50.4% for GRPO and >95% failure for PPO and DG, a qualitative gap that also transfers to billion-parameter LLM RLVR where GRPO fails entirely on graph coloring (Qwen3-1.7B, ~0 score at 300 steps) while TPO reaches ~0.96. - The missing: All LLM-scale experiments use 1.5–1.7B parameter models on three tasks; whether TPO's advantage persists on 7B+ models and harder benchmarks (MATH, AIME) is untested and explicitly flagged as future work. - Read it or not: Researchers working on LLM post-training and RLVR should read this—it provides a clean theoretical frame for why sparse-reward training fails under GRPO and a drop-in replacement that is simpler, more stable across epoch counts, and demonstrably better on hard tasks at the tested scale. Problem & Motivation: Group-based RL for LLM post-training (exemplified by GRPO and variants) samples K completions per prompt, scores them, and updates policy parameters using a scalar-weighted policy gradient. This conflates two distinct questions: which completions should gain probability mass, and how large should the gradient step be. The coupling makes training fragile, especially when reward is sparse—e.g., when the entire sequence must be correct for any credit to flow. Policy-gradient methods either fail to learn (GRPO stalls at random-chance error on terminal-reward tasks) or require careful KL penalty tuning to stabilize multi-epoch reuse. The gap between dense- and sparse-reward performance is a direct consequence of this coupling, not just variance. Key Idea: The central insight is that the optimal redistribution of probability mass over a finite candidate set can be expressed in closed form as a KL-regularized improvement operator—q = argmax_{r ∈ Δ^{K−1}} {Σ r_i u_i − η·KL(r ∥ p^old)}—yielding q_i ∝ p_i^old · exp(u_i/η). Fitting the policy to this fixed target via cross-entropy gives a gradient p^θ − q that vanishes exactly at p^θ = q, creating a stable fixed point absent from policy-gradient methods. No critic, importance weights, or clipping is required. Methodology: TPO proceeds in a standard on-policy loop: (1) freeze the behavior policy π_old; (2) sample K completions per context; (3) score and within-group standardize to obtain u_i; (4) compute target q_i ∝ p_i^old · exp(u_i/η) with default η=1; (5) minimize L_TPO(θ) = −Σ q_i log p_i^θ treating q as a stop-gradient constant. For autoregressive models, p_i^θ is the sequence log-probability softmaxed over the K candidates. Multi-epoch reuse is natural because q is frozen at rollout time, providing a stable attractor across gradient steps. Three critical design decisions: (a) score standardization to z-scores removes sensitivity to absolute reward scale, and the paper shows robustly that η=1 is near-optimal across a 16× temperature range (Appendix D); (b) the old-policy anchor p^old is shown necessary by ablation—removing it consistently harms performance; (c) target matching (cross-entropy to q) versus scalar-weighted REINFORCE over the same candidates is shown to be the key differentiator via the Group PG ablation, which raises MNIST error from 2.9% to 7.2%. Key Results: On tabular single-context bandits (K=100, B=100, 100 seeds), TPO and DG converge fastest, with TPO maintaining lower gradient misalignment to the oracle direction. On MNIST contextual bandits (2-layer ReLU, 20 seeds), TPO reaches 5% error at step 1,600 versus 2,200 for DG and reaches final error of 2.9% versus 3.5% (GRPO) and 3.0% (DG). On token-reversal with bag-of-tokens reward (V=16, K=8, 20 seeds), TPO_token reaches 1% error at episode 102 versus 148 (GRPO_token), 259 (PPO), and 393 (DG). On terminal-reward sequence tasks (H=10, prompt-matched, K=8, 20 seeds), TPO achieves 7.4% exact-match error versus 50.4% (GRPO), >95% failure for PPO and DG; under interaction-matching (same total rollouts), TPO achieves 19.0% versus 48.7% (GRPO), with PPO and DG failing to learn meaningfully. On LLM RLVR (Qwen3-1.7B and R1-Distill-1.5B, 4×A100-80GB, K=16, AdamW + LoRA rank 32), TPO and GRPO converge to comparable accuracy on GSM8K (~85–87%), but on Reasoning Gym graph coloring GRPO fails entirely on Qwen3-1.7B (near-zero score) while TPO reaches ~0.96; Knights & Knaves shows the same pattern. Baselines are current (PPO, GRPO, DG from 2024–2026) and run with deliberate strengthening (GRPO includes KL penalty tuned to stabilize it). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The reweight-then-fit idea dates to Dayan & Hinton (1997) and has been instantiated in REPS, MPO, and V-MPO, but these require a critic or dual optimization over the full action space. TPO's genuine novelty is applying closed-form exponential tilting specifically to the finite scored candidate set used in group-based RL, obtaining a target without a critic, and proving that the cross-entropy gradient self-extinguishes at the fixed point. The resulting algorithm is a non-obvious simplification with strong empirical consequences, not a straightforward combination of known ideas applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and well-targeted: the paper isolates the old-policy anchor, target matching versus scalar weighting (Group PG), KL penalty removal in GRPO, gradient epoch count, temperature, zero-variance group masking, and group size K. All key comparisons use 10–30 seeds with ±1 s.e. shading. No significance tests or p-values are reported beyond visual error bands, which is standard for this subfield but still a limitation. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/JeanKaddour/tpo. A methodological concern worth noting: several benchmark tasks (token reversal, MNIST bandit) are borrowed from concurrent work by Osband (2026), reducing the risk of benchmark overfitting; however, the LLM experiments train and evaluate on the same task distribution, which is standard for RLVR but means no held-out generalization is tested beyond GSM8K accuracy. DG is used in its most favorable single-epoch configuration throughout, which the paper justifies and documents (Appendix E). Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: LLM experiments run on 4×A100-80GB GPUs with LoRA, which is commercially available; the algorithm itself requires no additional infrastructure beyond a standard RLVR stack. - Efficient: TPO requires the same K rollouts per context as GRPO; no additional critic or reference model calls; compute cost is identical to GRPO at equivalent K, with the only overhead being a softmax over K log-probabilities to form q. - Effective: Evidence is strong on tested settings—TPO solves terminal-reward tasks where all four baselines fail, and the mechanism (gradient self-extinction, all-fail group neutrality, multi-epoch stability) is corroborated by diagnostic plots that match theoretical predictions. - Scalable: Tested only on 1.5–1.7B models; the paper explicitly states that 7B+ and harder benchmarks (MATH, AIME) are untested; the terminal-reward advantage may compress as models grow and base success rates increase, since TPO's gains are largest when informative batches are rare. The work delivers a software artifact (code on GitHub) implementing TPO in both JAX and PyTorch in under 10 lines. Strengths: - The theoretical framing is tight: Proposition 1 proves that q is the unique KL-regularized improvement, that the stationary distribution of L_TPO is p^θ = q, and that the gradient vanishes at this fixed point—properties that directly predict and explain the empirical advantages over GRPO. - The cross-context weighting analysis (Section 3.2, Appendix B) analytically derives β_TPO(p_n) = p_n(λ−1)/(1−p_n+λp_n), showing TPO maintains large updates on hard contexts where DG and GRPO vanish, explaining why it tracks the cross-entropy oracle more closely. - Multi-epoch stability is a practically important byproduct: TPO's final error stays below 2.3% across all gradient-epoch counts from 1 to 16, while GRPO varies from 1.1% to 37.6%—eliminating a critical hyperparameter sensitivity. - The ablation design is unusually disciplined: Group PG (same candidates and scores, scalar-weighted instead of target-matched) isolates the target-matching contribution from the grouping contribution, and DG without multi-epoch (its best setting) is used as the single-sample reference. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) TPO cannot improve beyond the quality of the K sampled candidates, so low-diversity or uniformly poor groups produce uninformative targets; (2) score standardization can amplify small numerical differences in low-variance groups, producing spuriously sharp targets (the same difficulty-bias mechanism identified for GRPO); (3) LLM evaluation is limited to 1.5–1.7B models and three tasks, with 7B+ and harder benchmarks left as future work. These are stated plainly in Section 6, which raises the paper's credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The LLM RLVR comparison is limited to two models and three tasks; GSM8K is saturated territory for models of this size (both methods reach ~85–87%), making it a weak discriminator. The harder tasks (graph coloring, Knights & Knaves) show large gaps but use train mean score rather than held-out accuracy, making it unclear whether TPO's advantage reflects better generalization or faster memorization. No wall-clock or token-budget comparison is reported for LLM experiments—only step counts—which matters for practitioners. The paper does not test TPO in genuinely off-policy settings (large replay ratios), which is increasingly common in production RLVR pipelines; the authors mention this is future work (Retrace/V-trace integration) but do not flag it as a gap in the evaluation. Comparison against PMPO (the closest target-matching baseline) is discussed qualitatively but not empirically. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed, theoretically grounded preprint that makes a credible case for replacing GRPO with TPO in group-based RLVR—the sparse-reward advantage is large, the mechanism is analytically explained, the code is released, and the paper honestly acknowledges what has not been tested at scale.
Recommended 2604.06159 PDF
#25 — Highly Innovative (76)

How to Fine-Tune a Reasoning Model? A Teacher-Student Cooperation Framework to Synthesize Student-Consistent SFT Data (18 ▲ )

Zixian Huang, Kaichen Yang, Xu Huang, Feiyang Hao, Qiming Ge, Bowen Li, He Du, Kai Chen, Qipeng Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strong across the board. Novelty (80) — token-span alternating generation with trained boundary predictors is genuinely new, and the empirical finding that stylistic mismatch (not data quality) drives SFT failure on reasoning models is a non-obvious diagnosis. Rigor (75) — ablations are substantial (answer generator, LoRA vs full, cross-training, 3 teachers, 2 students), code and data released on GitHub/HF, though no std devs and boundary predictor quality unablated. Non-Obviousness (85) — the diagnosis reframes a well-known failure mode. Completeness (75) — limitations confronted candidly including admitting the authors' own misattributions. Resilience (75) — monotonic pattern across 7 baselines and cross-family cross-training experiment provide strong causal evidence. Impact (70) — released dataset and code, directly actionable for SFT pipelines. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 ≈ 77.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TESSY, a data synthesis framework that interleaves teacher and student model generation span-by-span — teacher produces capability tokens (code, logical steps), student produces style tokens (connective/transitional phrases) — to create SFT training data that transfers teacher reasoning ability without imposing the teacher's stylistic distribution on the student. - Why it's important: Fine-tuning reasoning models (e.g., Qwen3-8B) on teacher-generated data causes performance drops of up to 10.02% on competitive coding benchmarks because reasoning models have internalized distinct stylistic patterns during post-training, and stylistic mismatch with teacher data triggers catastrophic forgetting of learned reasoning style. - How it does it: TESSY alternates generation between teacher and student models using a generate-then-rollback strategy, where lightweight trained boundary predictors (Qwen3-0.6B-Base-based sequence labelers) truncate each span at the precise style/capability boundary before switching models. - The good: TESSY is the only approach among seven tested alternatives that consistently improves Qwen3-8B above its baseline on all four code benchmarks, achieving +11.34% on LiveCodeBench-Pro and +6.68% on OJBench while every competing method — including Teacher-Score, Teacher-Mix, and Teacher-Think — causes degradation. - The missing: The boundary predictor's quality is foundational to the method but no ablation tests a weaker or rule-based boundary predictor; sensitivity to the k=20 token window and boundary annotation noise is uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building SFT pipelines for reasoning models — the central finding that stylistic mismatch, not data quality, drives performance drops is non-obvious and changes the practitioner's diagnosis frame. Problem & Motivation The standard knowledge distillation playbook — generate SFT data with a stronger teacher model, fine-tune a smaller student — works well for base and instruct models but breaks systematically for reasoning models. Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-R1, and similar models have undergone extensive RLHF-style post-training that instills distinctive stylistic "fingerprints" in their chain-of-thought: model-specific filler phrases, transition patterns, and hesitation tokens ("Okay, let's see," "wait," "but") that differ across model families and scales. When SFT forces a student to mimic a teacher's capability tokens embedded within alien stylistic connective text, the student incurs spurious loss on style tokens, which interferes with learning capability tokens and degrades overall performance. The original training distribution of reasoning models is undisclosed by model vendors, ruling out on-policy filtering. Prior partial mitigations — LoRA, system-prompt isolation, teacher-student mixing — reduce but do not eliminate the degradation, as the experiments demonstrate. Key Idea The single core insight is that tokens in a reasoning trace are not homogeneous: "capability tokens" (code, numerical values, logical deductions) carry task-solving information and should come from the teacher, while "style tokens" (transition phrases, tone-setters, discourse connectors) are task-irrelevant and should come from the student. By delegating only capability token generation to the teacher and style token generation to the student, TESSY produces synthetic sequences whose capability content reflects the teacher's superior problem-solving while the stylistic distribution remains on-policy for the student, eliminating the distributional conflict. Methodology TESSY generates synthetic responses in an alternating loop. Starting with the student model (since reasoning responses open with a stylistic phrase like "Okay, let's see"), TESSY iterates: the active model generates a window of k=20 tokens; a matching boundary predictor truncates the span to the last token of the appropriate type (last style token for student spans, last capability token for teacher spans); the truncated span is appended; if truncation occurred, the active model switches. The loop exits when the thinking phase completes, and the student generates the final answer entirely. Boundary predictors are lightweight sequence labeling models built on Qwen3-0.6B-Base with a binary classification head per token. They are trained on 100K segments annotated by GPT-OSS-120B using a carefully crafted prompt that defines style tokens (hesitation, tone, transition, narration) versus capability tokens (code, formulas, logical deductions). The capability boundary predictor B_T identifies the last capability token in teacher spans; style boundary predictor B_S identifies the last style token in student spans. The method is implemented on vLLM with prefix caching to support efficient switching between models during synthesis. The training objective decomposes per-token SFT cross-entropy into L_Cap (capability positions) and L_Sty (style positions). TESSY's synthetic data makes L_Sty easy to learn — style tokens come from the student's own distribution — so it no longer interferes with gradient signal for L_Cap. Training uses 80K samples (37K unique questions from OpenThoughts and NVIDIA Nemotron), 9 epochs, batch size 128, lr=5e-5, 32 H100 GPUs, using XTuner. Three critical design decisions: (1) Student generates the final answer — ablation shows using the teacher for the final answer causes 12.33–13.58% drops, confirming style consistency dominates quality advantage; well-justified. (2) k=20 tokens per window — stated as empirical but not ablated systematically. (3) Boundary predictor uses LLM annotation from GPT-OSS-120B — annotation quality is validated qualitatively (Table 4) but no precision/recall on held-out annotation is reported. Key Results Primary setting: Qwen3-8B student, GPT-OSS-120B teacher. Baseline Qwen3-8B scores: LCB-Pro=25.35%, OJBench=18.75%. After Teacher-Only SFT: LCB-Pro=22.10% (−3.25%), OJBench=8.73% (−10.02%). After TESSY: LCB-Pro=36.69% (+11.34%), OJBench=25.43% (+6.68%). All six alternative baselines (Teacher-Score, Teacher-Reference, Teacher-Answer, Teacher-Think, Teacher-Mix, Teacher-Only) show degradation or near-zero gain on at least one benchmark. TESSY also improves out-of-domain math: +3.75% AIME2024, +0.93% AIME2025 vs. baseline, while Teacher-Only drops these by 4.35% and 2.71%. Generalization to a second student (Qwen3-30B-A3B): TESSY achieves +6.37% LCB-Pro and +10.13% OJBench over Teacher-Only. Generalization across three teacher models: TESSY outperforms Teacher-Only by +1.07% (Qwen3-235B teacher), +3.45% (DeepSeek-R1), and +16.79% (GPT-OSS-120B). Cross-training experiment confirms intra-family mismatch matters: training Qwen3-30B-A3B on Qwen3-8B data causes −1.94% on OJBench; the reverse causes −2.8%. TESSY also produces shorter responses: 7,594, 8,938, and 2,816 fewer average tokens per sample vs. Teacher-Only for GPT-OSS, DeepSeek-R1, and Qwen3-235B teachers respectively, attributable to the student model's tendency to terminate reasoning earlier. All evaluations use pass@1 averaged over multiple runs via OpenCompass on publicly available benchmarks (LiveCodeBench-V5/V6/Pro, OJBench, AIME-2024/2025, OlympiadBench, GPQA). Baselines are current and appropriate given the task. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The token-span alternating generation mechanism with trained boundary predictors to enforce style/capability separation is not described in prior work. AdaSwitch uses alternating generation in an online RL setting with a different objective. Self-distillation work does not separate token types at the span level. The empirical finding — stylistic mismatch, not data quality, is the primary cause of SFT failure on reasoning models — is non-obvious and well-supported. The method is not a trivial combination of known techniques applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are substantial: final answer generator choice (Figure 9), system prompt isolation (Figure 10), LoRA vs. full-parameter training (Figure 13), base vs. reasoning model starting point (Figure 7), per-epoch training curves (Figure 11), cross-training distribution experiments (Figure 4), and teacher model generalization (Figure 5). No explicit error bars or significance tests appear in the tables — pass@1 values are averaged over multiple runs but standard deviations are not reported. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/CoopReason/TESSY and training data at https://huggingface.co/datasets/CoopReason/TESSY-Code-80K. The paper is an arXiv preprint (March 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. One mild Goodhart concern: the boundary predictor annotation relies on GPT-OSS-120B, the same model used as the primary teacher, creating a soft circular dependency — if the boundary labels are noisy or GPT-OSS-specific, performance on other teachers might overstate generalizability. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Data synthesis requires running GPT-OSS-120B and Qwen3-8B simultaneously, demanding multi-node GPU infrastructure; training uses 32 H100 GPUs. Not deployable for practitioners without substantial compute. - Efficient: TESSY synthesis is more expensive per sample than Teacher-Only due to alternating inference between two models, but produces 7K–9K shorter sequences, reducing training token count and downstream inference cost. - Effective: Strong evidence — TESSY is the sole method among all baselines that improves Qwen3-8B across all four code benchmarks, with gains of 6–16% over the nearest competitor depending on the teacher. - Scalable: Tested at 80K training samples with 8B and 30B-parameter students; no evaluation at larger data scales or with 70B+ students. The claim that TESSY generalizes across model scales is supported by two data points, not a scaling curve. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo + 80K dataset on HuggingFace) alongside the paper. Strengths - The monotonic pattern across baselines — greater teacher participation in data synthesis correlates with greater student performance degradation — constitutes compelling causal evidence for the distribution mismatch hypothesis, more persuasive than a single TESSY-vs-Teacher-Only comparison. - The cross-training experiment (Figure 4) is a clean causal test: swapping data between models of the same family (Qwen3-8B ↔ Qwen3-30B-A3B) still causes consistent degradation, isolating distributional distance rather than capability gap as the causal variable. - Robustness across three structurally different teacher models (same-family Qwen3-235B, heterogeneous DeepSeek-R1, GPT-OSS-120B) with consistent improvements strengthens generality beyond a single teacher-student pairing. - The candid impact statement — describing the authors' own initial misattribution of performance drops to data quality, benchmark leakage, or prompt design before identifying distribution mismatch — adds credibility and provides genuine guidance for practitioners who may repeat those mistakes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that when the teacher's maximum generation length is unconstrained, Teacher-Only slightly outperforms TESSY (Teacher-Only exceeds TESSY at 64K tokens vs. TESSY's 40K-constrained setting), indicating a quality ceiling that TESSY has not yet reached. They explicitly frame distribution alignment and data quality improvement as complementary, not competing, objectives, and call out reject sampling as a future integration target. These limitations are confronted directly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No ablation on boundary predictor quality: the method's correctness depends entirely on accurate style/capability classification, yet no experiment tests a degraded predictor or measures the predictor's precision/recall on held-out annotations. - The k=20 token window is a critical hyperparameter with no sensitivity analysis; it directly determines how finely interleaved the teacher/student contributions are. - Primary evaluation is exclusively competitive programming; whether TESSY delivers similar gains when math or science is the primary target (not auxiliary) is not tested. - No comparison against RL-based alternatives (GRPO, PPO) for improving reasoning models, which are increasingly mainstream for this use case and represent a more direct competing approach than the SFT baselines evaluated. - The annotation pipeline uses GPT-OSS-120B to label boundary predictors — the cost and feasibility of this annotation step with a smaller or open-source annotator is not examined. Bottom Line The central empirical finding — that stylistic mismatch, not data quality, is the primary cause of SFT failure on reasoning models — is non-obvious, practically actionable, and well-evidenced; practitioners fine-tuning any reasoning model should read this paper before designing their data synthesis pipeline.
Recommended 2604.14164 PDF
#26 — Highly Innovative (76)

SG-Serve: Efficient Model Serving for Subgraph-based Graph Representation Learning

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (85) drives the score — 8.75×–33.3× throughput gains over SBP across 3 models × 4 graphs up to 111M nodes/1.8B edges, validated across T4 and A100. Novelty (70) — DAG abstraction unifying 16 SGRL extraction algorithms and workload-aware (node,edge) batching with MPS dual-process dispatch is a non-trivial composition. Non-Obviousness (70) — identifying that request-count batching is the root cause is a genuine insight. Rigor (75) strong: systematic incremental ablation (PC, PC+E, PC+E+I) cleanly isolates each contribution; cross-GPU validation. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) — limitations openly acknowledged, but synthetic Poisson traces and no public code release are real gaps. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*85 = 74. Rounded to 76 for the disciplined ablation design.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SG-Serve, an online model serving system for subgraph-based graph representation learning (SGRL) that reduces P99 tail latency by up to 13x and improves request throughput by up to 33x over existing systems at the same latency target. - Why it's important: SGRL models are deployed in latency-sensitive applications (personalized recommendation, fraud detection) where P99 QoS guarantees are critical, yet their inherently irregular request workloads — P99 subgraph size is 27.4x the median in nodes, 60.7x in edges — make existing ML serving infrastructure designed for uniform-workload models severely inefficient. - How it does it: SG-Serve decouples subgraph extraction (CPU) from model inference (GPU) via a producer-consumer queue, parallelizes extraction using a 5-operator DAG abstraction with work-stealing scheduling, and replaces request-count batching with workload-aware batching by (node, edge) limits paired with NVIDIA MPS dual-process GPU dispatching. - The good: End-to-end throughput gains of 8.75x–33.3x over the synchronous batch processing baseline are consistent across 3 SGRL models (SEAL, Shadow, GraIL) and 4 real-world graphs up to 111M nodes / 1.8B edges, validated on both T4 and A100 GPUs. - The missing: All evaluation uses synthetically generated request traces (Poisson arrivals, random seed sampling) because no public SGRL serving traces exist; real production traffic in recommendation or fraud detection may exhibit burstiness and correlation patterns that invalidate the workload model. - Read it or not: ML infrastructure engineers and systems researchers building or deploying SGRL models for online serving should read this; researchers focused on SGRL model design or classical GNN training can skip it. Problem & Motivation Online serving of SGRL models for recommendation and fraud detection requires low latency and high throughput, with P99 tail latency being critical for QoS. The challenge is structural: unlike classical GNNs (GCN, GAT) that compute fixed-size node embeddings offline, SGRL models extract a subgraph at query time by expanding around seed nodes, then run a GNN over the extracted subgraph. Because node degrees follow a power-law distribution in real graphs, requests involving high-degree nodes generate subgraphs orders of magnitude larger than median requests — on Papers100M with SEAL, P99 subgraph size is 205,390 edges vs. median 3,386 edges. Existing serving systems (PyG, DGL) respond with three design choices that compound this irregularity: synchronized batch processing (SBP) where all requests in a batch must complete extraction before any proceeds to inference (batch straggler problem); single-thread extraction per request (no intra-request parallelism); and batching by request count rather than computational workload (head-of-line blocking). Key Idea The core technical insight is that SGRL serving latency is determined by workload irregularity, not request count, and two structural changes together address it: first, decoupling extraction from inference via a producer-consumer pipeline eliminates cross-stage straggler effects so any request moves to inference the moment its extraction finishes; second, batching inference by (node, edge) capacity limits rather than request count, combined with dual GPU processes via NVIDIA MPS, prevents any single heavy request from monopolizing either the CPU thread pool or the GPU while light requests accumulate in queues. Methodology SG-Serve comprises two main components sharing a subgraph queue. Subgraph Extraction Engine (CPU): The paper surveys 16 SGRL models and identifies that all subgraph extraction methods decompose into five sequential steps — Expand (k-hop neighbor retrieval per seed), Merge (union/intersection of per-seed node sets), Induce (retrieve edges among merged nodes), Encode (compute structural labels, e.g., shortest distance to seeds), and Combine (aggregate per-seed labels). SG-Serve abstracts these as typed DAG operators. Operators following a node-parallel pattern (Expand, Induce, Encode, Combine) are decomposed into segments of 128 nodes as the minimum scheduling unit, enabling fine-grained intra-request parallelism. A typed API exposes these operators so users implement extraction algorithms as node-level functions; the system builds the DAG, resolves dependencies, and schedules segments. The Merge operator is not parallelized (set operations are not node-parallel). Task scheduling across concurrent request DAGs uses work-stealing: each DAG is assigned a primary CPU thread; idle threads steal segments from other threads' local queues in round-robin. This prevents a single heavy request from occupying all threads (avoiding HoL blocking in FIFO-parallel) while still enabling multi-thread acceleration for large subgraph extraction. Model Inference Engine (GPU): Workload-aware batching (Algorithm 1) fills batches by maximum node count Nb and edge count Eb = λ·Nb, where λ is the average edge-to-node ratio profiled per model/graph. The skip-FIFO strategy accumulates light subgraphs meeting the node/edge limits into a batch while deferring heavy subgraphs (which exceed limits) as standalone batches, preventing straggling without starving heavy requests. Nb is configured via binary-search profiling. Dual-process dispatching uses NVIDIA MPS to partition the GPU into two processes each holding 50% of SM capacity; the dispatcher ensures both processes do not simultaneously handle heavy requests, guaranteeing at least one process is always available for light traffic. Hot embedding caching stores embeddings of high-degree nodes in GPU memory to reduce CPU-GPU transfer latency. Implementation: ~6,000 lines of C++, deployed as a Triton inference server backend, importing models via ONNX. Shared memory buffers with pinned allocation enable zero-copy data transfer between dispatcher and GPU worker processes. Overlapping CUDA communication and computation streams maximizes GPU utilization. Key Results Testbed: NVIDIA T4 GPU (15GB), Intel Xeon 8259CL (24 cores), 187GB RAM, Ubuntu 18.04, CUDA 11.6. Graphs: Orkut (3.1M nodes, 120M edges), Livejournal (4.8M, 68M), Friendster (65M, 1.8B), Papers100M (111M, 1.7B). Models: SEAL, Shadow, GraIL. Baselines: SBP (synchronous batch processing, re-implemented in C++ on the SG-Serve codebase for fair comparison), PC (producer-consumer only), PC+E (adds parallelized extraction). Request traces: Poisson arrivals, random seed sampling. At P99 latency = 80ms, SG-Serve throughput advantage over SBP ranges from 8.75x (SEAL/Livejournal) to 33.3x (Shadow/Papers100M) — Table 3. At the same throughput, SG-Serve reduces P50 latency by up to 19.3x and P99 by up to 13.1x. Ablation: PC alone reduces P99 by up to 4.76x over SBP (producer-consumer eliminates batch synchronization); PC+E further reduces by 1.24x–3.60x at low throughput (parallelized extraction accelerates heavy requests); full SG-Serve adds 1.21x–3.92x on top of PC+E at high throughput (workload-aware inference dominates when inference time becomes the bottleneck). Task-stealing scheduling outperforms FIFO-parallel at high throughput (where FIFO-parallel causes HoL blocking) and no-parallel at low throughput, across 6- and 24-thread configurations. Dual-process MPS dispatching reduces P99 inference by 1.38x–2.54x; adding workload-aware batching reduces a further 1.25x–2.05x. Hot embedding caching reduces communication P99 by up to 5.48x and end-to-end P99 by up to 2.29x on Friendster. A100 validation: SG-Serve reduces P99 by up to 10.7x over SBP, consistent across GPU generations. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The DAG abstraction unifying 16 diverse SGRL extraction algorithms into 5 typed operators with automatic parallelization, and the workload-aware (node/edge capacity) batching paired with MPS dual-process dispatching, are genuinely novel for SGRL serving. Individual techniques (work-stealing, producer-consumer, MPS) are known; their specific composition to address power-law workload irregularity in two-stage graph serving — not a trivial adaptation from LLM serving (vLLM) or classical GNN serving (DGI/GCNP) — constitutes non-obvious engineering. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation is systematic: each of the three main contributions (producer-consumer, parallelized extraction, workload-aware inference) is isolated as a distinct system variant with results reported across all model/graph combinations. Design alternatives are compared directly: work-stealing vs. FIFO-parallel vs. no-parallel; 2 vs. 4 vs. 8 MPS processes; workload-aware batching vs. count-based batching. No error bars or significance tests are reported, consistent with deterministic systems evaluation. Request traces are synthetic (Poisson arrivals with random seed sampling) because no public SGRL serving traces exist — the paper acknowledges this and cites it as standard practice for serving system evaluation. No public code release is mentioned. The C++ implementation complexity and Triton backend integration make independent reproduction non-trivial without the codebase. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: Runs on commercially available data center hardware (T4, A100); requires NVIDIA MPS, available on all modern data center GPUs. - Efficient: ~6k C++ lines with shared-memory IPC and overlapping CUDA streams; DAG segment overhead (128-node granularity) is modest; dual-process MPS reserves 50% GPU capacity for light requests, which the paper shows has negligible impact on GPU utilization given the rarity of heavy requests in practice. - Effective: Strong empirical evidence across 12 model/graph combinations and two GPU generations; individual contribution of each design choice is isolated and confirmed non-trivial. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 111M nodes / 1.8B edges (Papers100M, 69.1GB) which must fit in host memory; multi-GPU scaling via Triton instance groups is mentioned but not benchmarked. The paper shows MPS=2 is optimal and adding more processes (4, 8) degrades performance by up to 1.31x. Delivers a software system (C++ Triton backend); no public artifact release stated. Strengths - The 5-operator DAG abstraction simultaneously covers all 16 surveyed SGRL models and structurally exposes parallelism that the single-UDF design in prior systems cannot exploit — this is the key contribution enabling the API to be both general and parallelizable. - Workload-aware batching by (node, edge) capacity rather than request count identifies and directly corrects the root cause of SGRL inference tail latency, a departure from standard ML serving practice that is well-motivated by profiling data. - The ablation design is unusually disciplined: three incremental system variants (PC, PC+E, PC+E+I) isolate individual contributions, and the interaction pattern (extraction optimization dominates at low load, inference optimization at high load) provides insight beyond raw numbers. - Validation on both T4 and A100 GPUs with consistent results argues that the gains are architecture-independent and driven by addressing a fundamental workload property rather than hardware-specific tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper explicitly notes: (1) SG-Serve focuses on a single-GPU instance, with multi-GPU scaling delegated to Triton's instance group configuration and not benchmarked; (2) synthetic request traces are used because no real SGRL serving traces are publicly available, consistent with evaluation methodology in prior serving systems work; (3) the Induce operator interface is fixed because all 16 surveyed SGRL models use the same edge-existence logic, and future models with different induce patterns would require API extension; (4) using more than 2 MPS processes degrades performance, limiting flexibility of the dual-process design. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Synthetic traces assume Poisson arrivals with uniform random seed sampling. Real recommendation workloads exhibit item popularity skew and temporal correlation (e.g., burst requests around popular items); fraud detection workloads are highly imbalanced. These patterns may produce different straggler distributions than the synthetic model assumes. - The 187GB host memory testbed is required to hold Papers100M (69.1GB). No discussion of what happens when graph size exceeds available RAM — disk-based graph storage would change the latency profile significantly. - Hot embedding caching uses node degree as a proxy for access frequency. In production recommendation settings, degree-frequency correlation can break (cold-start items, new users); no evaluation of cache hit rates or sensitivity to the hotness criterion. - SGRL model correctness after ONNX import is not validated — the paper inherits accuracy numbers from original publications without confirming that the deployed models produce equivalent predictions, a potential gap in an end-to-end systems evaluation. - No public code or artifact release is mentioned, making independent replication dependent on re-engineering ~6k lines of C++ Triton backend integration. Bottom Line ML infrastructure engineers deploying SGRL models in production will find SG-Serve's DAG abstraction, work-stealing scheduler, and workload-aware batching directly actionable and rigorously validated; it is currently the most complete published treatment of SGRL-specific serving challenges and has no direct competitor at this level of specificity.
Citations
#27 — Highly Innovative (76)

Polyglot Teachers: Evaluating Language Models for Multilingual Synthetic Data Generation

Lester James V. Miranda, Ivan Vulić, Anna Korhonen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — new evaluation framework + strong empirical findings that contradict practitioner assumptions, though generation methods themselves are prior work. Rigor (85) — extensive ablations across scales, methods, base models, public artifacts (dataset, models, code), multiple seeds with SEs; minor concerns around unvalidated LLM-judge calibration and N=6 CC correlation. Non-Obviousness (75) — the scale/benchmark non-predictiveness finding is genuinely surprising. Completeness (80) and Resilience (75) solid with honest limitations. Impact (85) — released dataset, models, and actionable recipe validated on held-out Tagalog. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*85 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*85 = 77.75 ≈ 76.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces POLYGLOT SCORE (PG-Score), a composite evaluation metric combining intrinsic synthetic data quality and extrinsic student model performance, and uses it to systematically benchmark 10 LMs as multilingual teachers for SFT data generation across 6 typologically diverse languages, training 240 student models on over 1.4M generated examples. - Why it's important: Teacher model selection for multilingual SFT is almost universally ad hoc — defaulting to the largest or highest-benchmark model — causing quality gaps in non-English synthetic data that propagate weaknesses rather than strengths to student models at scale. - How it does it: The key move is combining lexical/embedding-based diversity metrics, base-model perplexity, and an LLM-as-a-judge reward score into a single z-score-normalized intrinsic signal, then pairing that with student PGR measured on three multilingual benchmarks, to produce a holistic per-teacher-language score that can be computed without running full SFT. - The good: Neither parameter count nor multilingual benchmark performance significantly predicts teacher effectiveness (p > 0.05 for both), yet four intrinsic data quality principal components explain over 93.3% of variance in data quality metrics and predict downstream student performance with R² = 0.664, providing a cheap screening proxy. - The missing: All six target languages are relatively well-resourced (the weakest, Czech and Indonesian, still have >0.65% and >0.95% CommonCrawl representation); truly low-resource languages (sub-0.1% CC) are not tested, and the finding that CommonCrawl percentage predicts PG-Score (ρ = 0.886) is computed on only six data points. - Read it or not: Practitioners building multilingual SFT pipelines — especially those who currently default to GPT-4 or the largest open model — should read this; the practical recipe (teacher selection via PG-Score, family matching, method selection by resource level) is actionable and empirically grounded. Problem & Motivation: Supervised finetuning of language models for non-English languages depends critically on the quality and diversity of instruction-following training data, which is scarce for most of the world's languages. A dominant practice is to synthesize SFT data from large "teacher" LMs, but teacher selection is driven by English benchmark rankings rather than multilingual data generation capability. Because large models may have significant capability gaps in non-English languages, their synthetic data can propagate these gaps to the student. No systematic framework exists for measuring teacher effectiveness specifically in the multilingual synthetic data generation context, leaving practitioners without principled guidance on which models to choose or which generation strategy to use. Key Idea: The core insight is that intrinsic properties of generated synthetic data — diversity of prompts and responses, fluency as measured by perplexity, and reward model quality scores — can predict downstream student model performance well enough to serve as a cheap proxy for full student training. By aggregating these intrinsic signals via PCA and regressing against extrinsic benchmark gains (R² = 0.664), the paper demonstrates that teacher selection can be made without running the expensive SFT loop, and that the conventional proxies (model size, English benchmark score) are not predictive. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. First, a seed dataset of 132,929 multilingual instruction-following examples (from Aya, WildChat 4.8M, EuroBlocks-SFT, GSM8K, Helpsteer3, Magpie-Pro Filtered, and Tülu 3 SFT) is assembled per target language. Second, each of 10 teacher models generates 10k synthetic prompt-response pairs per language using one of three methods: Generate (k-shot in-context generation of novel pairs), Translate (forward-translate English prompts from seed, generate response in target language), and Respond (use existing target-language prompts from seed, generate response). Generation is repeated with three different random seeds to control variance. Third, intrinsic data quality is measured as a z-score-normalized average of: diversity of prompts (dₓ) and responses (dᵧ) via cosine distance over Llama-Embed-Nemotron-8B embeddings, perplexity (PPL) of a base model on generated responses, and a rubric-based quality score (R, 1–5) from M-Prometheus 14B. Fourth, student models (OLMo 3 7B, plus Llama 3.1 8B, Qwen 3 8B, Llama 3 8B for cross-base-model tests) are finetuned on each synthetic dataset, then evaluated on Global-MMLU Lite (cultural understanding, 0-shot), M-RewardBench (chat alignment), and M-GSM (math). Extrinsic performance is measured as Performance Gap Recovered (PGR) relative to a reference model (OLMo 3 7B Instruct SFT). PG-Score = z-score(Intrinsic + Extrinsic). Critical design decisions: (1) equal weighting of intrinsic and extrinsic in PG-Score — shown robust under alternative weightings with Spearman ρ ≥ 0.90 for α ≥ 0.90; (2) 10k examples as the generation scale — ablated and shown sufficient (gains diminish beyond 10k for three benchmarks, though 25k helps on more diverse task distributions like FilBench); (3) z-score normalization to allow cross-language comparison across teachers. Key Results: Across six languages, Gemma 3 27B Instruct achieves the highest average PG-Score (0.726), followed by Aya Expanse 32B (0.706), Gemma 3 12B (0.595), and Gemma 3 4B (0.469). Llama 3.1 70B Instruct scores only 0.140, and Llama 3.1 8B scores -0.356 — both well below smaller Gemma models. GPT-4o mini (0.461) is outperformed by two open models at substantially smaller parameter counts. Teacher rankings are consistent across student base models (Spearman ρ ranging 0.57–0.87 across four base models), with family-matched teacher-student pairs achieving at least +20.5% higher PG-Score over the mismatched OLMo baseline. For generation method: the Generate method yields the highest PG-Score for German (high-resource, high-quality seed data), while Respond and Translate outperform Generate for Arabic and Indonesian by large margins (+453.1% and +458.9% for Gemma 3 27B respectively over equal-mix baseline). Using an LM to translate prompts (LM-Translate) outperforms NLLB-600M translation with LM response (avg PG-Score 1.36 vs 0.85/0.80), indicating that prompt naturalness is the bottleneck in translation pipelines, not response quality. Neither log(parameter size) (β = 0.053, p = 0.507) nor multilingual benchmark performance (β = 1.387, p = 0.529) significantly predicts PG-Score. CommonCrawl language representation correlates with PG-Score at ρ = 0.886 (p < 0.05, N = 6). On the held-out Tagalog case study (FilBench), the recommended recipe achieves 49.52 vs 47.67 for GPT-4o-mini approach and 47.24 for public data, with a 4B student model competitive against larger Qwen 3 4B and Llama 3.1 8B Instruct. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (PG-Score composite evaluation framework for multilingual teacher LMs) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (model scale and benchmark performance fail to predict multilingual teacher effectiveness; intrinsic data quality metrics predict extrinsic student performance; teacher-student family matching is a reliable heuristic; generation method optimality is language-resource-level dependent). The data generation methods themselves (Generate, Translate, Respond) are prior work, systematically characterized and unified here; the genuine novelty lies in the holistic evaluation framework and the empirical insights it surfaces. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover: data scale (1k–50k samples), teacher-student family matching, generation method choice, translation method (LM vs NLLB), PG-Score metric weighting (α from 0 to 1), cross-base-model generalization (OLMo 7B, Llama 8B, Qwen 8B, Llama 3 8B for intrinsic; OLMo 32B and Gemma 4B/12B/27B for scale generalization), and language resource level. PG-Score is computed from three independent trials with different random seeds; standard errors are reported in Table 13. The PCA and regression analysis are performed on a held-out 20% test split. Code, synthetic datasets (POLYGLOT-INSTRUCTIONS-SYNTH), and finetuned student models (POLYGLOT-GEMMA-SFT) are publicly released on HuggingFace at ljvmiranda921/polyglot-teachers. Methodological concern: the intrinsic reward score uses M-Prometheus 14B as judge, which is itself an LLM trained on multilingual data — its calibration across languages may not be uniform, and the paper does not report inter-rater reliability against human judgments for the non-English languages evaluated. The correlation between CommonCrawl percentage and PG-Score (ρ = 0.886) is computed on N = 6 languages, which is insufficient to draw strong causal conclusions. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems + methods evaluated by student model benchmarks). - Practical: All open teacher models (including Gemma 3 27B) can run on commercially available GPU clusters; the paper uses Grace Hopper GH200 Superchips, but the inference pipeline uses vLLM and the student finetuning completes in ~1.5 hours on 2 nodes for a 7B model. - Efficient: Intrinsic metric computation is cheaper than full SFT (no training loop), and the paper demonstrates it predicts extrinsic performance at R² = 0.664, making it a viable screening tool; full pipeline cost (1.4M generations + 240 SFT runs) is substantial but one-time. - Effective: On the held-out Tagalog case study, the recommended recipe outperforms GPT-4o-mini-based data generation (+1.85pp on FilBench) and public-data baselines (+2.28pp), and a 4B model trained on the recipe is competitive against larger instruction-tuned models. - Scalable: The paper tests up to OLMo 3 32B as student and confirms teacher rankings hold; it does not test teachers beyond 104B (Command A) or more than 6 languages, and the ρ = 0.886 CC finding rests on 6 points — scaling to truly low-resource languages remains unvalidated. The work delivers a publicly released dataset collection, finetuned student models, and a codebase — not just paper form. Strengths: - The finding that neither model scale nor benchmark performance predicts multilingual teacher quality (p > 0.05) is well-supported by a mixed-effects regression across 27 models × 6 languages × 3 trials, and contradicts widespread practitioner assumptions. - PG-Score's intrinsic components predict extrinsic student performance at R² = 0.664 with a simple linear model, providing a practical screening tool that avoids the cost of full SFT evaluation. - The cross-base-model consistency experiments (Spearman ρ up to 0.87 across four different student architectures) substantially strengthen the claim that teacher rankings are robust and not student-model-specific. - The Tagalog held-out case study provides genuine out-of-sample validation that the recipe generalizes to an unseen language with different benchmark metrics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) the language set covers only 6 languages chosen partly for benchmark availability, limiting generalizability across a broader typological sample; (2) the Translate method assumes access to English prompts that can be meaningfully translated, and inherits translationese artifacts; (3) teacher effectiveness correlated with CommonCrawl representation may reflect pretraining exposure effects that are difficult to disentangle. The authors acknowledge these as open questions and encourage future work to expand language coverage. They confront the limitations directly, which increases credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The six languages include no truly low-resource language (minimum CC share is Arabic at 0.65%); the ρ = 0.886 correlation between CC representation and PG-Score has N = 6, making it statistically fragile and unfit for strong causal inference. - The LLM-as-a-judge (M-Prometheus 14B) reward scores are used as intrinsic ground truth but are not validated against native-speaker human judgments for any of the six target languages; judge miscalibration in non-English settings would silently corrupt the intrinsic metric. - The paper uses a single SFT objective (standard cross-entropy on prompt-response pairs) and a single student scale (7B, with limited 32B validation); it does not evaluate whether teacher rankings change under DPO, RLHF, or other post-training objectives. - IBM Granite Micro (0.4B) and IBM Granite 4.0 (3B) are included as teachers, but their inference context window (4,096 tokens) is drastically shorter than other models (up to 131k), potentially confounding Generate method performance by forcing shorter outputs rather than reflecting intrinsic multilingual capability. - The paper does not report whether teacher rankings are stable across prompt domains — all seed data mixes factual, conversational, and math prompts, but a teacher strong at math generation may underperform at cultural chat generation, which the aggregate PG-Score would obscure. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed empirical study that overturns a common assumption (bigger = better teacher) with rigorous evidence, delivers a practical screening metric and actionable recipe, and releases artifacts that make the findings reproducible — practitioners who build multilingual SFT pipelines will gain more from this than from most benchmark papers, but should treat the low-resource language claims with caution until validated beyond six languages.
Recommended 2604.11290 PDF
#28 — Highly Innovative (75)

Watt Counts: Energy-Aware Benchmark for Sustainable LLM Inference on Heterogeneous GPU Architectures

Mauricio Fadel Argerich, Jonathan Fürst, Marta Patiño-Martínez · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the top contributor — 5 replicates with 95% CIs, cold-down protocol, NVML validation, mixed-effects with REML — though code/data are only promised, not released at preprint time. Novelty (70) is solid but bounded: a large new dataset and a useful empirical finding (TDP/TFLOPS-W are unreliable proxies), not a new problem formulation. Non-Obviousness (75) earns credit for the batch-vs-server ranking reversal and the dominance of idle power, which contradict industry heuristics. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) are helped by direct limitation acknowledgment, though single-GPU FP16/vLLM-only scope excludes the highest-impact production regime. Impact (75) reflects a reusable benchmark and actionable placement guidance. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*75 ≈ 75.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Watt Counts, the largest open dataset of LLM inference energy consumption — 5,000+ experiments across 50 LLMs and 10 NVIDIA GPU architectures in batch and server scenarios — paired with an open-source, reproducible benchmark. - Why it's important: LLM operators deploying across heterogeneous GPU fleets have no empirical foundation for hardware-aware, energy-optimal placement decisions; this gap directly drives unnecessary electricity cost and carbon emissions at scale. - How it does it: The benchmark runs vLLM in batch (synchronous, 1,000-query workload) and server (Poisson-arrival async) modes, measures GPU energy via NVML at 10 Hz, and applies mixed-effects regression to disentangle model size, architectural properties, and GPU characteristics. - The good: The H100 achieves the lowest energy per token in 90% of batch cases, but the A30 and L4 — GPUs with far lower TDP — outperform the H100 for small and medium models in server scenarios, enabling up to 70% energy savings; this directly refutes the industry assumption that flagship GPUs are universally energy-optimal. - The missing: All experiments are single-GPU only (no tensor/pipeline parallelism), all models use FP16 (no INT8/INT4), and only vLLM is tested — quantized and multi-GPU deployments, the most common large-scale production pattern, are entirely absent. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers and infrastructure teams selecting GPU hardware for heterogeneous LLM deployments; skip for researchers focused on training, quantization, or multi-GPU serving. Problem & Motivation: Cloud and on-premise operators deploying LLMs face a growing set of heterogeneous GPU options — from consumer RTX cards to enterprise H100s — with TDPs ranging from 70 W to 700 W and memory capacities from 16 GB to 141 GB. While GPU energy dominates total inference power (78.7–92.5% of system consumption), no public dataset or benchmark spans a wide cross-section of models, GPUs, and deployment scenarios simultaneously. Existing work either covers one GPU (TokenPowerBench, AI Energy Score), does not report GPU-level measurements (MLPerf Power), or is not publicly available. Without cross-LLM-GPU empirical data, operators cannot make informed decisions about GPU selection, workload placement, or energy–performance trade-offs in heterogeneous deployments. Key Idea: GPU energy efficiency rankings are not fixed properties of hardware but depend on the interaction between the GPU's TDP and idle power, the model's size and attention architecture, and the deployment mode (batch vs. server). In server scenarios idle power dominates — not throughput or TFLOPS — so low-TDP GPUs systematically outperform high-TFLOPS flagships for underutilized workloads, a pattern obscured by using TDP or TFLOPS/W as a proxy. Methodology: The benchmark orchestrates vLLM inference for Question Answering (SQuAD v1.1 prompts) in two modes: batch (1,000 synchronous queries, energy per token as the primary metric) and server (Poisson-arrival async with λ∈{0.017, 0.3}, mean power draw as the metric). GPU power is sampled via NVML at 10 Hz (validated to <1% integrated error vs. 200 Hz). Each configuration runs five independent iterations after a warmup phase; a cold-down protocol (GPU power within 3 W for ≥30 s and temperature <65°C) isolates thermal state between runs. Hardware spans 10 NVIDIA GPUs from Tesla V100 (2018, Volta) to H200 NVL (2024, Hopper). Fifty open-source models (0.124B–30B parameters, FP16, ≤30B to fit at least two GPUs) are drawn from Hugging Face Hub, categorized into Small/Medium/Large/XLarge by GPU memory compatibility. Analysis uses two mixed-effects regression models: Model A regresses log(E_ij) on log(P_i) with GPU as random intercept; Model B adds residualized architectural features (hidden size, attention heads, KV head count, layers) to isolate their effect orthogonal to size. Three design decisions are well-justified: (1) using energy per token in batch vs. mean power draw in server — appropriate because server systems are continuously available and total token count is workload-dependent; (2) fixing context length to 1,024 and generation length to 256 to ensure comparability across 370+ LLM–GPU pairs; (3) excluding task accuracy from scope, which is consistent with the paper's infrastructure-characterization goal but limits operational utility. Key Results: In batch scenarios, the H100 achieves the lowest energy per token across all model size categories in 90% of cases; the H200 ranks second for most models. For Small models, the A30 and L4 tie for third despite having far lower TFLOPS, because their low TDPs compensate for lower throughput. A 10× increase in active parameters increases energy per token by approximately 1.7× (log-log coefficient β₁ = 0.237–0.282, p < 0.001 in both models). Beyond size, KV head count has the strongest independent architectural effect (β = 0.121, p < 0.001); attention head count also increases energy (β = 0.070, p = 0.004); wider hidden size reduces energy conditional on fixed size (β = −0.098, p = 0.026). TFLOPS/W is a poor system-level predictor: GPUs with higher TFLOPS/W do not consistently achieve lower energy per token. In server scenarios, mean idle power becomes the dominant factor: the L4 (idle: 17 W, max: 73 W) and T4 (12 W, 82 W) rank first in low-load conditions for most model sizes. For a medium-sized model (Llama 3.1 8B) in server low-load, using an A30 instead of the H200 saves up to 70% energy while meeting a TTFT p95 < 70 ms requirement. In batch XLarge models, deploying on H100 instead of H200 saves ~20%. All 5K+ experiments are run five times each; 95% confidence intervals are reported. Baselines are contemporary (vLLM, 2024 hardware), though comparison is between GPU hardware configurations rather than against prior software methods. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Watt Counts dataset is the first to span 50 LLMs × 10 GPUs × batch+server scenarios with per-run GPU energy measurements and open data. The empirical finding that GPU energy efficiency rankings depend on model size × deployment mode interaction — and that TDP/TFLOPS-W are unreliable proxies — is a non-obvious, practically significant result. The mixed-effects regression framework applying size residualization to isolate architectural features is a methodological contribution, though not a fundamentally new technique. Rigor & Reproducibility: Five independent runs per configuration with 95% CIs are reported; the cold-down protocol controls for thermal confounding. Mixed-effects models use REML estimation and report z-statistics and p-values (Tables 9, 10). The Group Variance term (Var = 0.071–0.072) confirms non-negligible GPU-specific baseline differences, justifying the random-effects structure. NVML measurement accuracy is validated against RAPL with <5% deviation on a bare-metal instance. Code and dataset are promised on GitHub under MIT license upon paper acceptance but are not yet publicly available at preprint time — reproducibility cannot be independently verified from this preprint. The evaluation uses only vLLM; results may not generalize to TensorRT-LLM or SGLang. Only FP16 models up to 30B are tested; quantized models (INT8/INT4) and models >30B are absent, limiting applicability to common production workloads.
