Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurement study) + SYSTEMS (open benchmark tool). - Practical: The benchmark runs on bare metal and cloud instances without external power meters, using standard NVIDIA GPU hardware spanning consumer (RTX 3090, 4090) to enterprise (A100, H100, H200). - Efficient: Total dataset collection consumed ~180 kWh (~17 kgCO2); the benchmark is designed for community extension and individual GPU characterization without full replication of all 5K+ experiments. - Effective: The dataset directly produces actionable hardware selection guidance — e.g., L4 or A30 for small/medium models in server-constrained deployments — supported by specific measured energy savings, not just rankings. - Scalable: The single-GPU scope is a hard boundary; the paper explicitly notes that multi-GPU tensor and pipeline parallel deployments introduce communication overheads that would alter energy rankings, and this is deferred to future work with no experiment or bound provided. The software artifact (benchmark) is designed to scale to new GPUs and models via community contribution. The work delivers both a dataset artifact (promised open release) and a reusable benchmark tool. Strengths: - The dataset fills a genuine and consequential gap: 5K+ experiments across 370+ distinct LLM–GPU pairs is a 10× increase over the nearest comparable open dataset (MLPerf Power: 33 combinations), with full per-GPU energy metering. - The finding that TDP and TFLOPS/W are poor energy proxies — backed by 50-model empirical evidence and a mixed-effects model — overturns a common industry heuristic with statistical rigor. - The deployment-scenario analysis (RQ3) is practically important: showing that GPU rankings reverse between batch and server scenarios, and that idle power (rarely disclosed by manufacturers) is the dominant server-side factor, provides direct operational guidance. - Measurement methodology is careful: cold-down protocol, thermal throttling guard (65°C threshold), NVML accuracy validation, and five independent iterations per configuration give confidence intervals that are actually meaningful. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) single-GPU scope excludes multi-GPU tensor/pipeline parallelism; (2) only FP16 weights, no INT8/INT4 quantization; (3) only vLLM tested, so results may differ for TensorRT-LLM or SGLang; (4) task accuracy not evaluated; (5) CPU, DRAM, and cooling overhead not captured (GPU validated as 78.7–92.5% of system total); (6) dataset construction itself emitted ~17 kgCO2. These are confronted directly and constitute a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The SQuAD v1.1 QA task is a narrow workload proxy — LLM inference energy is known to depend on prompt length, output length, and token distribution, none of which are varied systematically here (all prompts use fixed max context 1,024, generation 256). A single inference task cannot characterize energy behavior across summarization, code generation, or retrieval-augmented generation workloads where context lengths may be 4–128× longer. The study also restricts models to ≤30B parameters in FP16, which excludes the most widely deployed production models (Llama 3 70B, Llama 3.1 405B, Mixtral 8×22B) — precisely the cases where energy optimization has the highest absolute impact. The claim that findings generalize to quantized workloads is stated as an expectation without any supporting experiment. Finally, the benchmark uses a fixed Poisson arrival rate range (λ = 0.017–0.3) that may not represent real traffic burstiness or the long-tail of user request distributions. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating GPU procurement or placement for heterogeneous LLM inference deployments will find directly actionable, empirically grounded guidance here that does not currently exist elsewhere in the literature; the single-GPU FP16 scope is a real constraint but the dataset and benchmark establish a reusable foundation the community can extend.
Search Results 2604.09048 PDF
#29 — Highly Innovative (75)

FORGE:Fine-grained Multimodal Evaluation for Manufacturing Scenarios (82 ▲ )

Xiangru Jian, Hao Xu, Wei Pang, Xinjian Zhao, Chengyu Tao, Qixin Zhang, Xikun Zhang, Chao Zhang, Guanzhi Deng, Alex Xue, Juan Du, Tianshu Yu, Garth Tarr, Linqi Song, Qiuzhuang Sun, Dacheng Tao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is strong: real-world 2D+3D data at 0.02mm precision, scenario-based OOD train/test splits, near-ceiling grounding probes (Gemini-3-Flash 98.9%) that cleanly isolate domain knowledge as the binding constraint, with code and data released on GitHub/HuggingFace. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (75) reflect the genuine finding that visual grounding is NOT the bottleneck for manufacturing MLLMs — a reversal of field assumptions — plus the counter-intuitive result that Ref-Cond/ICD harm 3V performance due to multi-view spatial confusion. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are solid, though SurfInsp's fully synthetic defects and coverage limited to 14 fastener-class categories cap the ceiling. Impact (75) reflects directly actionable findings redirecting future domain-knowledge injection work. Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*75+0.15*78+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*75 ≈ 75.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FORGE, a fine-grained multimodal benchmark of ~12,000 real-world samples (2D images + 3D point clouds) covering three manufacturing tasks — workpiece verification, structural surface inspection, and assembly verification — and evaluates 18 MLLMs against it. - Why it's important: Existing manufacturing MLLM benchmarks lack model-number-level granularity and multimodal (image + point cloud) coverage, meaning current evaluations systematically underestimate the difficulty of real factory tasks that require distinguishing subtle part variants. - How it does it: The benchmark encodes fine-grained domain semantics (e.g., exact model numbers like M10 vs. M20) and uses three progressively informative evaluation protocols (Zero-Shot, Reference-Conditioned, In-Context Demonstration) alongside three bottleneck analyses that decouple visual grounding from domain-knowledge failures. - The good: The bottleneck analysis is the paper's strongest intellectual contribution — it demonstrates with near-ceiling grounding scores (Gemini-3-Flash: 98.9% single-image grounding) that MLLM failures on FORGE are caused by absent manufacturing domain knowledge, not poor visual localization, directly redirecting future research effort. - The missing: Surface defect generation (SurfInsp) is entirely synthetic (simulated cracks, dents, deformations via morphology algorithms) while the workpiece images are real; this modality mismatch and the synthetic nature of the hardest perceptual task are not adequately interrogated. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on MLLM evaluation or industrial AI, as the dataset, bottleneck methodology, and finding that ICD helps where Ref-Cond does not are directly actionable; skip it if your interest is in the underlying MLLM architectures rather than their evaluation. Problem & Motivation: Modern manufacturing requires distinguishing specific part variants — a nut of model M16 versus M18, or a screw of a particular thread pitch — for material sorting, quality inspection, and assembly verification. Traditional computer vision systems classify workpieces into coarse categories but cannot reason about fine-grained model-number-level attributes, assembly compatibility constraints, or multi-part spatial relationships. Existing MLLM benchmarks for manufacturing (MMAD, MME-Industry, DesignQA, FailureSensorIQ) either lack 3D data, lack real-world collection, or operate at coarse workpiece-category granularity without model-number distinctions. Meanwhile, MLLMs are increasingly proposed as autonomous manufacturing agents, yet their actual capability at these fine-grained tasks has not been measured rigorously or with real factory data. Key Idea: The central technical insight is a two-axis decomposition of MLLM failure: visual grounding (can the model locate and label a part in an image?) versus domain-knowledge reasoning (does the model know what distinguishes M16 from M18, or which washer variant belongs to a given assembly?). By constructing dedicated bottleneck probes — isolated grounding tasks with near-ceiling ground truth, missing-part detection with explicit assembly specs, and raw point cloud text serialization — the paper shows that grounding is largely solved by frontier models while domain knowledge is the binding constraint. This reframing is the contribution that transcends benchmark construction. Methodology: FORGE consists of two data subsets. The Point Cloud Subset contains 585 raw samples across 14 workpiece categories and 90 model numbers, scanned at 0.02 mm precision; batch samples for WorkVeri and AssyVeri are synthesized by stitching 4–5 individual clouds via CloudCompare. The Image Subset contains ~3,000 images from four scenarios captured with a 50-megapixel Leica lens. Surface defects (Crack, Deformation, Dent, Cut) for SurfInsp are simulated via morphology-based algorithms with non-rigid deformation, augmented with 20 random rotations yielding ~30,000 samples. All 3D point clouds are rendered as 3-view orthographic projections (front, side, top) to bridge the modality gap for general-purpose MLLMs that lack native 3D encoders. Tasks are formulated as multiple-choice questions (MCQs). Evaluation runs under three settings: Zero-Shot (test query only), Ref-Cond (additional correct-case reference images), and ICD (Ref-Cond plus solved demonstration examples). Error scenarios are stratified into two difficulty levels: Different Workpiece (coarse) and Different Model Number (fine-grained). Exact-match accuracy against a weighted random-chance baseline is the metric. Three bottleneck analyses isolate: (A) visual grounding via Set-of-Mark coordinate/letter matching on 500 single-image and 513 cross-image pairs; (B) missing-part detection via assembly spec completion on 240 image and 137 3V cases; (C) raw point cloud text input on 420–460 cases per task. Fine-tuning uses Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct with full-parameter SFT (bfloat16, 3 epochs, 4×8 GPUs, lr=1e-5), trained on one scenario and evaluated on a held-out scenario to test transferable reasoning. Key Results: Across 18 models (open: Gemma-3-27B, InternVL3-78B, Llama-4-Maverick, Mistral-3-8B/14B/Large, Qwen3-VL-8B/235B, Kimi-K2.5, GLM-4.6V; closed: GPT-5/5.2, O3, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Claude-4.5-Opus, Seed-1.6), top performance on WorkVeri (3V, ICD) reaches 78.26% for Kimi-K2.5 (open) and 79.26% for Gemini-2.5-Flash (closed), versus a 25% random baseline. On AssyVeri (3V, ICD), Kimi-K2.5 reaches 80.85% and Gemini-2.5-Flash 100.00% on specific workpiece sub-tasks, but overall scores remain well below ceiling. SurfInsp is the hardest task: even Gemini-2.5-Flash achieves only ~47% on Crack under Zero-Shot (random = 20%), and most open-source models cluster near random on SurfInsp across all settings. Key counter-intuitive finding: for 3V modality, Ref-Cond and ICD hurt performance on WorkVeri and AssyVeri compared to Zero-Shot (workpiece-level tasks drop more than model-number tasks under Ref-Cond), because multi-view spatial variability across reference and test images introduces spatial confusion rather than helpful comparison. Raw point cloud text input degrades all models to near 20% random on SurfInsp and AssyVeri. Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL-3B achieves 53.8% on WorkVeri 3V NUTS scenario (held-out) from a 28.2% base — a 90.8% relative improvement — approaching Qwen3-VL-235B (54.4%), a 78× larger model. On AssyVeri image CNC scenario, SFT improves from 24.0% to 30.5% (27.1% relative gain), surpassing several frontier models. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The dataset is the first real-world manufacturing benchmark combining aligned 2D images and 3D point clouds at model-number granularity. The empirical finding that visual grounding is not the bottleneck for manufacturing MLLM failures (versus domain knowledge) is genuinely novel and challenges the prevailing assumption in the field. The three-task design and bottleneck decomposition methodology are non-trivial extensions beyond prior work. The individual components — MCQ evaluation, Set-of-Mark prompting, SFT fine-tuning — are standard; what is new is their combination in this diagnostic framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: The bottleneck analyses are well-designed and internally consistent: near-ceiling grounding results (98.9% for Gemini-3-Flash) convincingly eliminate visual localization as a confound, and the missing-part detection probe (74.9–90.7% accuracy on images) provides a clean intermediate test. No error bars or significance tests are reported for main benchmark results; given the MCQ format with deterministic decoding (temperature=0.0 for bottleneck analyses), single-run results are likely stable, but this is not verified. The scenario-based train/test split (train on CHS, test on NUTS for WorkVeri; train on MES+PES, test on CNC for AssyVeri) is a credible out-of-distribution protocol that avoids memorizing specific layouts. Code is released at github.com/AI4Manufacturing/FORGE and data at huggingface.co/datasets/AI4Manufacturing/forge. A limitation: SurfInsp defects are entirely synthetic (simulated via algorithms), which may not reflect the statistical distribution of real factory defects — this is acknowledged implicitly but not tested with real defect samples. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark + systems evaluation). - Practical: The benchmark runs on commercially available hardware via API access to closed-source models and consumer GPUs for open-source; fine-tuning used 4×8 GPUs (32 total), which is accessible. - Efficient: Evaluation cost scales with 18 models × ~12,000 samples; the 3V rendering pipeline adds preprocessing overhead but is a one-time cost; SFT for the 3B model required modest compute (8,337 training examples, 3 epochs). - Effective: The benchmark exposes real capability gaps — best models achieve ~54–79% on WorkVeri/AssyVeri versus 25% random, confirming the tasks are non-trivial and that SFT meaningfully closes the gap on held-out scenarios. - Scalable: The benchmark covers 14 workpiece categories and 90 model numbers; it does not test whether findings generalize to the hundreds of part families and thousands of model variants in actual factories, and this is not claimed. The work delivers a dataset and benchmark (HuggingFace + GitHub), not a production software tool. Strengths: - The bottleneck decomposition is intellectually rigorous: near-ceiling visual grounding scores for top models provide strong evidence that domain knowledge, not perception, is the binding constraint, reversing a common assumption and giving the community a clear target. - The counter-intuitive finding that Ref-Cond and ICD harm 3V performance (due to spatial confusion across multi-view reference images) is a concrete, reproducible result with a mechanistic explanation, not just a data point. - Scenario-based train/test splitting for SFT evaluation genuinely tests transferable manufacturing reasoning rather than layout memorization. - The dataset is real-world collected (2D images with 50MP sensor, point clouds at 0.02mm precision), addressing a fundamental weakness of prior synthetic manufacturing benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that SurfInsp relies on simulated defects (Crack, Deformation, Dent, Cut via morphology algorithms), and acknowledge that 3D point cloud text serialization is ineffective for general MLLMs. They also note the flat washer detection failure (23.3–60.0% across models) reflects a domain-knowledge gap rather than a perception failure, per their bottleneck analysis. These are confronted directly and increase the work's credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: First, all SurfInsp defects are algorithmically synthesized; no validation against real factory defect distributions is provided, making it unclear whether MLLM failure on SurfInsp reflects inability to detect real manufacturing defects or artifacts of the simulation. Second, the benchmark covers only 14 workpiece categories (screws, nuts, washers, brackets) — all fastener/fixture hardware — which is a narrow slice of manufacturing; the degree to which findings generalize to machined parts, PCBs, or complex assemblies is unstated. Third, the fine-tuning experiment uses 8,337 training examples for WorkVeri and only 627 for AssyVeri, yet both are presented as evidence of transferability; the low-data regime for AssyVeri makes the 27.1% relative gain hard to interpret without a learning curve. Fourth, Seed-1.6 achieves only 43.3% on normal cases in AssyVeri while performing strongly on missing-part cases — a systematic bias toward predicting anomalies — which suggests the MCQ answer distribution may be imbalanced in ways that inflate some scores; this is noted briefly but not fully characterized. Bottom Line: Manufacturing MLLM researchers and practitioners will find FORGE the most rigorous real-world benchmark currently available for fine-grained part recognition and assembly reasoning, with the bottleneck decomposition delivering a finding concrete enough to directly motivate future domain-knowledge injection work.
Recommended 2604.07413 PDF
#30 — Innovative (74)

STTID: High-Performance Sparse Tensor-Train Interpolative Decomposition

arcb.csc.ncsu.edu · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (85) drives the score — public C++/CUDA code on GitHub, and STTID is the sole functional method on several tensors where all baselines fail (OOM or divergence). Novelty (75) — adapting PRRLU/ID to produce architecturally sparse TT-cores is a principled advance. Rigor (65) — clean layered ablation and FROSTT/KG benchmarks, but single-run timings, no cross-run stats, no comparison to concurrent Zhang et al. preprint, and preprint status. Non-Obviousness (70) — skeleton-based sparsity preservation is non-obvious. Completeness (65) — fill-in and ≤5D ceiling are honestly confronted. Resilience (65) — unresolved fill-in and downstream-task-proxy concerns bound claims. Weighted (systems): 0.15*75+0.15*65+0.10*70+0.15*65+0.15*65+0.30*85 ≈ 72.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STTID, a sparse Tensor-Train Interpolative Decomposition algorithm that decomposes large-scale sparse tensors directly in COO format while preserving sparsity in the output TT-cores, with a hybrid CPU-GPU implementation achieving 77.9×–550.3× GPU speedups over CPU. - Why it's important: Existing TT decomposition methods either destroy sparsity in TT-cores (TT-SVD) or fail to converge on sparse tensors (TT-cross), making large sparse tensors in quantum simulation, knowledge graphs, and PDEs intractable — STTID is the first method that handles them at all. - How it does it: STTID replaces the SVD/cross-decomposition backbone with a sparse partial rank-revealing LU (PRRLU) that selects actual rows and columns (skeleton indices) from the data, so TT-cores are built from entries of the original sparse tensor and structurally inherit its sparsity, augmented by selective data separation and hash-table-based Gaussian elimination to manage fill-ins. - The good: STTID is the only method among five evaluated that successfully decomposes 4D/5D tensors with up to 27,843 nonzeros (Rnd5/Rnd6) and achieves exact decomposition on knowledge-graph tensors, while all four baselines fail via OOM errors or divergence. - The missing: Fill-in during PRRLU is a structural problem the authors acknowledge but do not solve — for JF17K3, fill-in remains substantial, and no block-based or fill-reducing pivoting strategy is implemented, leaving scalability at higher tensor orders or denser structures uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Researchers working on high-dimensional sparse tensor decomposition for quantum simulation, knowledge graphs, or scientific computing should read this; those needing TT decomposition of dense or only moderately sparse tensors can skip it. Problem & Motivation: High-dimensional sparse tensors arise in quantum chemistry and simulation, recommendation systems, knowledge graphs, and solution of high-dimensional PDEs. Tensor-Train (TT) decomposition offers a compact representation that tames the curse of dimensionality, but two classes of existing TT algorithms fail on sparse inputs. TT-SVD computes TT-cores via sequential SVD on matricized tensor slices, producing dense orthogonal cores that discard sparsity and require memory proportional to the full tensor size — infeasible for large sparse inputs. TT-cross algorithms, designed for partially observable tensor functions, perform back-and-forth sweeps updating skeleton indices from sparse tensor slices; since slices of a sparse tensor contain mostly zeros, the skeleton updates receive negligible signal, preventing convergence or causing singular matrix inversions. This leaves no functioning TT algorithm for the large sparse tensors that routinely arise in real applications. Beyond decomposition, downstream computations (tensor reconstruction, contraction, completion) benefit substantially from sparse TT-cores, which existing methods cannot produce reliably. Key Idea: The central insight is to use interpolative decomposition (ID) rather than SVD or cross decomposition as the TT backbone: PRRLU selects actual rows and columns of each matricized tensor slice as skeleton indices, and the resulting TT-core is literally a subset of the columns of the sparse input matrix. Because the TT-core entries are chosen from nonzero entries of the original tensor (not computed as linear combinations that fill in zeros), sparsity is architecturally preserved without regularization tuning. COO format enables O(nnz) reshaping via coordinate recomputation, making this practical. Methodology: TT-ID sweeps from mode 1 to d-1. At iteration k: (1) reshape the residual tensor T into matrix M (in COO format) with shape (n_k r_{k-1}, N/(n_k r_{k-1})); (2) apply PRRLU to M to find rank-r skeleton row/column indices (I_k, J_k) and permutation arrays perm_r, perm_c; (3) extract column skeleton C = M(:, J_k) and cross matrix X_k = M(I_k, J_k); (4) reshape C into 3D TT-core G_k; (5) set residual R = M(I_k, :) for the next iteration. The TCI form of the TT (Equation 2) stores the decomposition using only skeleton indices and input tensor entries — no explicit matrix inverses are stored, since G_k X_k^{-1} can be evaluated efficiently via LU factors. SPRRLU (Algorithm 2) proceeds in three phases per iteration s: Phase 1 finds the pivot entry (maximum absolute value in submatrix Ms) in O(M.nnz) via COO traversal; Phase 2 performs row/column pivoting by swapping index entries in COO arrays; Phase 3 applies outer-product Gaussian elimination, updating nonzero entries of the separated submatrix Mt using hash table Mt_H for O(1) lookup and insertion. Three algorithmic optimizations address the O(u.nnz × v.nnz × M.nnz) complexity of naive sparse PRRLU: (1) Selective data separation partitions M into Ms (needing update) and already-processed entries concurrent with Phase 2, reducing Phase 3 complexity to O(u.nnz × v.nnz × Mt.nnz). (2) Hash table-based Gaussian elimination replaces the dense perm_c array (up to 465 GB for mode-500 5th-order tensors) with a hash map, reducing storage and lookup to O(1) expected cost. (3) Hybrid CPU-GPU implementation offloads PRRLU to NVIDIA GPUs: Phase 1 uses cuBLAS cublasIdamax_64; Phase 2 parallelizes permutation across CUDA threads; Phase 3 uses cuco::static_map on-device hash tables with kernel fusion combining Phases 2-3 and shared memory to localize atomic operations. Critical design decisions: (i) COO over CSR/CSC for reshaping — well-justified: dimension-agnostic pivoting requires O(nnz) traversal, impossible with pointer-based formats. (ii) PRRLU over RRQR for skeleton selection — justified on two grounds: PRRLU provides both row and column pivots in one pass; RRQR introduces dense orthogonal factors incompatible with sparse storage. (iii) cuco::static_map for on-device hash table (linear probing, fixed capacity) over multi_map/dynamic_map — justified by throughput benchmarks; dynamic_map currently lacks GPU-bulk APIs. Key Results: Quality comparison (Fig. 3) on 6 random tensors (4D/5D, power-law nonzero distribution, 91–27,843 NNZs) and 8 real-world tensors (knowledge graphs + FROSTT: up to 6.3M NNZs). Baselines: TT-SVD, TT-SVD-lasso (l1-regularized TT-SVD from TensorLy), TT-cross-qr (baseline TT-cross), TT-cross-lu (improved TT-cross variant). STTID consistently produces the sparsest TT-cores (core density orders of magnitude below TT-SVD) at comparable reconstruction error. TT-SVD-lasso achieves up to 2 orders of magnitude higher sparsity than TT-SVD on Rnd3/Rnd4 but is input-dependent and fails on Rnd1/Rnd2. Both TT-cross methods produce sparse TT-cores when they converge but fail on Rnd3–Rnd6 via singular matrix inversion (TT-cross-qr) or non-convergence (TT-cross-lu). STTID is the sole functional method for Rnd5 (100,100,100,100; 12,729 NNZs) and Rnd6 (50,50,50,50,50; 27,843 NNZs). Exact decomposition (E_r ~ 10^{-14}–10^{-16}) is achieved on all four knowledge-graph tensors. Performance (Fig. 4) on 2 large random + 8 real-world tensors: Dense TT-ID on CPU requires 9298.2s (Rnd5) and 11725.4s (Rnd6); STTID CPU: 15.3s and 18.6s (607.7× and 630.4× faster). GPU vs. CPU STTID: 77.9×–169.9× on A5000; 194.3×–550.3× on H100. PRRLU accounts for >95% of total runtime. Ablation on Wiki3/JF17K3 (Fig. 5): data separation reduces runtime by >50% on CPU; hash tables add 9.4× and 50.0× over data separation; GPU H100 adds 29.4×–70.1× over optimized CPU; kernel fusion + shared memory adds ~10%. Downstream: Sparse TT reconstruction achieves 12.1× (Rnd5) and 29.8× (Rnd6) speedup over dense reconstruction using STTID's sparse TCI-form TT-cores. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The adaptation of interpolative decomposition (PRRLU) to the TT setting for preserving sparsity in output TT-cores is genuinely novel — prior TT-ID work (e.g., TT-cross) exists but was not designed for sparse tensors and does not produce sparse output. The COO-native sparse PRRLU with selective data separation and hash-table Gaussian elimination, combined with the hybrid CPU-GPU implementation, constitutes a new algorithmic system. The sparsity preservation via skeleton selection (architectural, not regularization-based) is a substantive methodological advance over TT-SVD-lasso. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation study is thorough: each of four optimization stages is timed independently on two representative tensors, allowing unambiguous attribution of speedup. Reconstruction error metric (relative Frobenius norm) is standard. No runtime variability, error bars, or multiple-run statistics are reported — GPU timings appear to be single-run. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/tensorworld/STTID.git, enabling reproduction. Evaluation uses FROSTT (community standard) and knowledge-graph tensors from prior published work. Missing data points in Fig. 3 are explicitly flagged and explained (Section VII-B). The paper does not compare against a recent closely related preprint (Zhang et al., arXiv:2503.18921, cited as [105]) on interpolative decompositions for sparse tensors — this is an important omission that reviewers at IPDPS will likely flag. The paper is submitted to IPDPS 2026 (peer-review pending); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability: SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on commodity hardware (AMD Threadripper with 503 GB RAM for CPU; NVIDIA A5000 and H100 for GPU). The hash-table approach specifically solves the 465 GB dense permutation-vector problem that would otherwise make large-mode tensors infeasible on any hardware. - Efficient: Memory scales with nnz rather than full tensor size; STTID CPU is 607–630× faster than dense TT-ID at the tested scale. The primary cost is PRRLU (>95% of runtime), and fill-ins during Gaussian elimination can increase nnz substantially, as observed for JF17K3. - Effective: STTID produces sparse TT-cores with reconstruction errors within a small factor of TT-SVD (the theoretically optimal approximation) on random tensors, and achieves exact decomposition on knowledge graphs. It handles all tested tensors where baselines fail entirely. - Scalable: Tested on tensors up to 5D with up to 6.3M nonzeros. Fill-in growth is data-dependent and not fully controlled (JF17K3 shows sustained high nnz counts during decomposition, Fig. 6). Behavior at 10D or with 60M+ nonzeros is untested and unknown; the claim of scalability to larger tensors is not empirically supported beyond the tested range. The paper delivers a software artifact (C++/CUDA codebase at GitHub) alongside the algorithmic contribution. Strengths: - Skeleton-based sparsity preservation is principled: TT-cores built from selected tensor entries are architecturally sparse without regularization parameters to tune, making robustness independent of data statistics. - COO-native PRRLU with dimension-agnostic pivoting is a clean algorithmic insight that enables O(nnz) reshaping — the asymptotic advantage over CSR/CSC (O(nnz²)) is substantial and experimentally confirmed. - The ablation study quantifies each optimization layer precisely, providing a reproducible engineering blueprint: data separation (>50% reduction), hash tables (9.4–50×), GPU parallelism (29–70×), kernel fusion (~10%). - STTID is the only demonstrated method for decomposing the tested large sparse tensors — the experimental proof of exclusive feasibility is the strongest possible argument for the contribution's practical necessity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that fill-in during PRRLU is a persistent structural problem — JF17K3 shows sustained high nonzero counts throughout decomposition (Fig. 6) that data separation does not fully mitigate. They acknowledge CC-common as a boundary case where high nonzero density causes STTID CPU to underperform dense TT-ID. They note that cuco::static_map's concurrency limitation (no simultaneous find-and-update for existing key-value pairs) required a workaround using fetch_add, and that dynamic_map (which would handle capacity growth) currently lacks GPU-bulk API support. Future work on block-based strategies is proposed but not implemented. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single-run timing: all reported runtimes appear to be single measurements with no standard deviation. GPU performance is sensitive to memory layout and thermal state; multi-run statistics are standard and their absence is a reproducibility concern. - Dimensionality ceiling: all tested tensors are at most 5D. TT decomposition is primarily motivated for 6D–20D settings (e.g., high-dimensional PDEs, quantum circuits); STTID's fill-in behavior and convergence properties at d ≥ 6 are entirely uncharacterized. - Proxy metric only: the evaluation uses reconstruction error as quality metric. For the stated applications (quantum simulation, knowledge-graph completion, tensor completion), downstream task performance on sparse STTID TT-cores vs. baselines is never measured — reconstruction error is a proxy. - No comparison with Zhang et al. [105] (arXiv:2503.18921, March 2025): this concurrent preprint on interpolative decompositions for sparse tensors is cited in related work but not experimentally compared. Given its direct relevance, the omission is significant. - Fill-in characterization is missing: the paper observes that fill-in is data-dependent but provides no analytical or empirical characterization of which tensor structures are fill-in-friendly, making it impossible to predict when STTID will be practical without running it. Bottom Line: Sparse TT decomposition has been an open problem, and STTID delivers the first practical solution with a working GPU implementation and public code — practitioners dealing with large sparse multidimensional data should evaluate it, though the unresolved fill-in problem and lack of testing beyond 5D leave open questions about its limits.
Search Results
#31 — Innovative (74)

MoBiE: Efficient Inference of Mixture of Binary Experts under Post-Training Quantization

Zhixiong Zhao, Zukang Xu, Zhixuan Chen, Dawei Yang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — components (joint SVD, gradient-weighted Hessian, null-space projection) each adapted from prior work, but the MoE-specific co-design and identification of three distinct failure modes is a genuine contribution. Rigor (75) — thorough ablations (per-component, calibration robustness with seed sweep, λ sensitivity across 3 archs/3 corpora, plug-and-play into two backbones), 6 diverse MoE architectures 1B–80B, code release announced; error bars on main results absent. Non-Obviousness (75) — the zero-overhead NGES fusion into mandatory scales is clever. Completeness (70) — limitations confronted, but missing 4-bit GPTQ/AWQ baseline is a significant gap. Resilience (70) — throughput measured only on one small model; accuracy-on-Mixtral gap vs MxMoE unaddressed. Impact (80) — deployable PTQ with 2.33× throughput and >90% expert memory reduction on real hardware. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MoBiE, the first post-training binarization framework tailored specifically for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs, addressing three MoE-specific failure modes of existing binary quantization methods. - Why it's important: MoE LLMs require all expert weights resident in memory even though only a sparse subset activate per token, making compression critical for deployment on memory-constrained hardware; naive application of dense-LLM binarization causes catastrophic accuracy collapse. - How it does it: MoBiE combines joint SVD across experts to extract a shared high-precision backbone (CEJD), gradient-reweighted Hessian saliency aligned with downstream task loss (GLAS), and null-space projection to confine binarization perturbations to routing-insensitive directions (NGES), all fused into the mandatory 1-bit row/column scaling factors at zero extra storage cost. - The good: On Qwen3-30B-A3B, MoBiE at ~1.34 effective bits reduces WikiText2 perplexity by 52.2% and improves average zero-shot accuracy by 43.4% over the best prior binary baseline, while delivering 2.33× inference throughput and >90% reduction in activated expert memory versus FP16. - The missing: MoBiE is never compared against practical INT4 quantization baselines (e.g., GPTQ-4bit or AWQ-4bit) that practitioners actually deploy, leaving the real deployment tradeoff versus the dominant production standard unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers working on deploying large MoE LLMs on memory-constrained hardware should read this; the problem framing, three identified failure modes, and the zero-overhead routing-stability fix (NGES) are genuine contributions that practitioners will want to adopt. Problem & Motivation: MoE-based LLMs achieve strong performance by replacing dense feed-forward layers with pools of specialized experts and a learned sparse router. This architecture requires all expert weights to reside in memory simultaneously at inference time, even though only top-k experts activate per token—GPT-OSS-120B, for example, holds 240+ GB of weights in FP16 while activating only ~5B parameters at decode time. Weight binarization to ±1 is the most aggressive compression approach and maps directly to fast bitwise operations on commodity hardware. Existing binary methods (BiLLM, ARB-LLM) were designed for dense transformers and fail when applied to MoE models because they treat experts independently (ignoring structural redundancy across the 10–512 experts in a layer), use local per-layer Hessian saliency that misaligns with downstream task loss, and ignore the MoE router, allowing binarization perturbations to distort the discrete top-k routing distribution—causing tokens to be dispatched to wrong experts, which shatters expert specialization and exponentially amplifies quantization error. Prior MoE quantization work (MoEQuant) addresses some of these issues at 2-bit but binarization of MoE models remained unexplored. Key Idea: The core insight is that expert weight matrices within a single MoE layer share a low-dimensional semantic subspace (CKA similarity 0.3–0.6 across GPT-OSS, DeepSeek, Qwen architectures), and that binarization errors confined within the null space of the token activation covariance are invisible to the router because they do not affect the routers' softmax logits. Exploiting both facts simultaneously—factoring out the shared backbone at high precision and projecting residual errors into routing-insensitive directions—allows near-full-precision routing fidelity at 1-bit storage cost for the expert-specific parts. Methodology: MoBiE is a three-stage post-training quantization (PTQ) pipeline that requires no fine-tuning and uses 128 WikiText2 calibration samples. Stage 1 – CEJD: Expert weight matrices W^(1)…W^(N) within one MoE layer are horizontally concatenated and jointly SVD-decomposed: [W^(1),…,W^(N)] = UΣ[V^(1),…,V^(N)]^T. The shared backbone UΣ ∈ ℝ^(O×r) is retained at 8-bit; only the lightweight expert-specific projections V^(i) ∈ ℝ^(r×I) are binarized. Because the majority of variance is absorbed by UΣ, discretization error in V^(i) is confined to a stable orthogonal coordinate system. The shared component adds an O² parameter overhead, which is negligible vs. the N×O×I expert body in typical MoE configurations. Stage 2 – GLAS: Standard GPTQ-style saliency uses local Hessian H=2XX^T, weighting reconstruction error uniformly across outputs. GLAS replaces this with a global Hessian H_global,j = (D_j X)^T(D_j X) where D_j = diag(∂ℓ/∂Z_j) injects downstream loss gradients, causing the saliency metric s_ij = w²_ij / [H^{-1}_{global,j}]²_{ii} to reflect task-level sensitivity. The mixed-order binarization then gives high-saliency columns a second-order residual correction and applies first-order binarization to low-saliency columns, with the threshold set at the 95th percentile of column-aggregated saliency. Stage 3 – NGES: The null space of the token activation covariance X_gate X_gate^T is computed via SVD, yielding null-space projector P = U_1 U_1^T. NGES constrains the binarization error ΔW to satisfy X(W−Ŵ)P ≈ 0 by introducing row/column scaling modulation vectors β^r ∈ ℝ^m and β^c ∈ ℝ^n fused into the mandatory ARB-LLM row/column scales via Hadamard product: Ŵ = (β^r ⊙ α^r)·Sign(W)·(α^c ⊙ β^c). The bivariate objective (Eq. 12) minimizes deviation from the null-space-projected target plus Tikhonov regularization (λ=0.2) solved via 15-iteration ALS with closed-form per-step updates. Critical design decisions: (1) retaining UΣ at 8-bit rather than binarizing it is well-justified by compression analysis showing the O² overhead is negligible vs. NOI; (2) λ=0.2 is validated across three architectures and three corpora with an ablation showing clear optimality; (3) fusing β^r, β^c into existing scales incurs zero storage overhead and is well-justified by the NGES routing-similarity ablation. Key Results: Evaluated on six MoE architectures spanning 1B–80B active parameters (OLMoE-1B-7B, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, Qwen3-30B-A3B, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct, GPT-OSS-20B) against BiLLM, ARB-LLM (1-bit), AWQ, GPTQ (2–3 bit), MoEQuant, NoWag. On WikiText2 perplexity (Table 2), MoBiE consistently achieves the lowest perplexity at ~1.3–1.6 effective bits across all six models; on OLMoE, MoBiE at 8-bit shared UΣ (1.46 eff. bits) reaches 14.37 vs. FP16 baseline 6.65, while BiLLM gives 20.32 and ARB-LLM gives 15.49. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, MoBiE perplexity 12.80 vs. BiLLM 52.78 and ARB-LLM 26.76. On seven zero-shot benchmarks (Table 2), MoBiE averages 57.58 on Qwen3-30B vs. 33.01 (BiLLM) and 40.14 (ARB-LLM). MoBiE at 1.35 bits matches or beats GPTQ at 3 bits on several models while using less than half the memory. Ablation (Table 4) shows CEJD contributes the largest individual gain, and all three components together boost Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct zero-shot accuracy by 15.47 points over the no-module baseline. Routing fidelity (layer-wise cosine similarity S_ℓ): BiLLM/ARB-LLM degrade to S_ℓ < 0.8 in deeper layers; MoBiE+NGES maintains S_ℓ ≥ 0.9 across all layers. Efficiency: on Qwen1.5-MoE with batch=4, prefill=1024, decode=256, throughput improves from 22.05 to 51.38 tokens/s (2.33×); activated memory falls from 9.64 GB to 1.88 GB. Instruction-tuned models (Table 9): GPTQ and MoEQuant at 2-bit cause HumanEval accuracy to collapse to 0 on Qwen-MoE-14B-Chat; MoBiE preserves ~80% of FP16 performance and shows <28% drop. Against MxMoE (mixed-precision, exhaustive search): MoBiE retains >91% of MxMoE performance at a tiny fraction of its search cost, though on Mixtral MxMoE's 67.89 avg still exceeds MoBiE's 64.48 avg (Table 7). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The combination of joint cross-expert SVD with null-space-guided routing stabilization is novel within the quantization literature. CEJD extends constrained low-rank approximation to the multi-expert setting. GLAS adapts the gradient-guided Hessian idea of Kim et al. (2025) specifically to MoE's cross-expert context. NGES applies null-space projection from Zhao et al. (2025a) specifically to suppress the discrete routing distortion unique to MoE architectures. No single component is entirely new in isolation, but their MoE-specific co-design and the empirical demonstration that prior binary methods fail catastrophically on MoE models constitutes a genuine contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 4 decompose all three components, demonstrating individual and joint contributions with monotone improvement trends. The calibration robustness ablation (Table 8/Fig. 8) varies both sample count (32–512) and seed (2–100 random seeds) and finds <1% relative perplexity variation, credibly ruling out calibration sensitivity. The regularization ablation (Table 10/Fig. 9) tests λ ∈ {0.1, 0.2, …, 0.9} on three architectures and three corpora, finding λ=0.2 consistently optimal. The plug-and-play ablation (Figs. 10–11) verifies GLAS and NGES integrate into BiLLM and ARB-LLM backbones without altering other settings, demonstrating backbone-agnostic gains. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; multiple random seeds are used only for the calibration ablation, not the main results. Code is stated to be available at a URL marked "MoBiE" (the actual URL is not legible in the manuscript as rendered). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.06798v3, April 14 2026); no peer review record is stated. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: MoBiE runs on NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs (24 GB VRAM) without fine-tuning, requires only 128 calibration samples, and adds ~6 minutes to BiLLM's quantization time—deployable on accessible hardware. - Efficient: Activated parameter memory drops from 9.64 GB to 1.88 GB for Qwen1.5-MoE (>80% reduction); expert-layer memory reduced >90%; throughput 2.33×; quantization time competitive with BiLLM. Shared UΣ at 8-bit adds modest storage vs. pure 1-bit but is justified by accuracy gains. - Effective: Perplexity and zero-shot accuracy evidence on six diverse MoE architectures spanning two orders of magnitude in size (1B–80B active params) credibly demonstrates the method solves the stated problem; instruction-tuned model results (Table 9) show practical task viability. Qualitative generation examples (Table 11) corroborate the quantitative results. - Scalable: Tested up to Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct (80B total, 32 active parameters); throughput and memory numbers are reported only for Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B; no throughput measurements are provided for the larger models. Scalability to 100B+ total parameter models is claimed but not experimentally demonstrated. The paper delivers a software artifact (code release announced). Strengths: - The identification and individual empirical characterization of three distinct MoE-specific failure modes (redundancy, saliency misalignment, expert-shift) provides a principled and well-motivated decomposition of the problem that prior work missed. - NGES's routing-stability fix is technically elegant: achieving null-space-aware routing preservation by fusing two compact vectors into the already-required row/column scales imposes literally zero storage overhead—a non-obvious design achievement at the 1-bit constraint. - Calibration robustness (Table 8) and λ sensitivity (Table 10) ablations are unusually thorough by PTQ standards, substantially reducing concerns about brittleness to calibration choices. - The 2.33× throughput and >90% expert-memory reduction are hardware-verified (not just theoretical), and the comparison spans six architecturally diverse models including very recent Qwen3-generation MoE LLMs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) CEJD and GLAS add implementation complexity over uniform quantization; (2) calibration-deployment distribution shift can degrade saliency quality; (3) null-space correction mitigates but does not fully eliminate routing instability, and rare expert misallocation cases remain. These are confronted directly in the Limitations section—a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: First, the paper never compares against 4-bit PTQ baselines (GPTQ-4bit, AWQ-4bit), which are the dominant production standard for LLM deployment and would anchor the practical cost-benefit tradeoff for practitioners weighing 1-bit vs. 4-bit. Second, throughput and memory benchmarks are reported only on Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B; the claim of practical efficiency for the 30B–80B models tested in accuracy experiments is unsupported by any runtime measurement. Third, while Table 7 includes MxMoE (mixed-precision exhaustive search), on Mixtral MxMoE's average zero-shot accuracy (67.89) exceeds MoBiE's (64.48)—the paper acknowledges MxMoE's higher cost but does not comment on this accuracy gap directly. Fourth, the global average bitwidth of ~1.11 bits is computed over the entire model including non-expert components (attention, embeddings) which are not binarized; the effective bitwidth of the MLP/expert layers alone is higher, and this averaging somewhat understates the compression ratio relative to a full 1-bit model. Fifth, activation quantization is entirely absent; inference still requires FP16 or BF16 activations, leaving a potential efficiency bottleneck unaddressed. Bottom Line: MoBiE makes a substantive and reproducible advance in MoE LLM compression that practitioners targeting memory-constrained inference should adopt over prior binary methods, but the absence of a 4-bit baseline comparison and single-model throughput benchmarks leave the full deployment case incompletely argued.
Search Results 2604.06798 PDF
#32 — Innovative (74)

Q-Zoom: Query-Aware Adaptive Perception for Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models

Yuheng Shi, Xiaohuan Pei, Linfeng Wen, Minjing Dong, Chang Xu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile — plug-and-play module with throughput measurements and Pareto analysis. Impact (82) is the dominant dimension — 4.39× throughput, 73.2% token reduction, orthogonal integration with RL-trained models demonstrated, project page with code. Rigor (75) — comprehensive ablations (Tables IV-VIII), ground-truth-supervised baseline validates self-distillation, single-GPU reproducible setup, but no error bars and LLM-as-judge same-family bias. Novelty (65) — consistency-aware routing labels and tri-state self-distillation are genuinely new; cross-modal attention for RoI is established. Non-obviousness (65). Completeness (65) — no formal limitations, no RefCOCO/grounding eval. Resilience (65). Weighted (systems, 15/15/10/15/15/30): 0.15*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*82 ≈ 71.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 82
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Q-Zoom, a plug-and-play two-stage adaptive perception framework for MLLMs that attaches a lightweight Dynamic Gating Network and a Self-Distilled Region Proposal Network (SD-RPN) to a frozen backbone's intermediate feature space, routing simple queries to coarse-feature generation and complex queries to localized high-resolution RoI re-encoding—all within a single prefilling pass. - Why it's important: Naively scaling visual tokens to handle fine-grained tasks (document OCR, high-resolution scene perception) floods self-attention quadratically with redundant tokens, crippling inference throughput; existing alternatives either waste compute on all queries or require expensive multi-pass prefilling and auto-regressive decoding before any RoI is extracted. - How it does it: A consistency-aware training sample generation strategy derives binary routing labels without human annotation by evaluating accuracy across a monotonically increasing resolution trajectory, and a self-supervised tri-state distillation paradigm mines pseudo-labels from the model's own internal cross-modal attention maps to train the SD-RPN without any external grounding annotation. - The good: On Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Q-Zoom surpasses the 4,096-token brute-force baseline by 2.5% on High-Resolution benchmarks while constraining max tokens to 576, delivering a 4.39× throughput acceleration and 73.2% token reduction, establishing a dominant Pareto frontier over all evaluated competitors. - The missing: No evaluation on open-ended generation tasks or grounding benchmarks (e.g., RefCOCO); the incremental gain over the authors' own prior SD-RPN baseline is marginal on several LLaVA suites, and the Post-SFT stage is architecturally restricted to MRoPE-equipped Qwen models. - Read it or not: Worth reading for MLLM efficiency researchers and practitioners running Qwen-family models on document/OCR or high-resolution visual pipelines; those focused on open-ended generation, video, or non-Qwen architectures will find applicability limited. Problem & Motivation MLLMs require high-resolution visual inputs for fine-grained tasks—document understanding, OCR, dense scene perception—but current architectures respond by uniformly scaling all queries to maximum resolution, producing a quadratic blow-up in self-attention token count. This brute-force paradigm ignores two fundamental inefficiencies: (1) query-level redundancy—many user queries can be answered from coarse global features, so forcing full high-resolution processing wastes compute; (2) spatial sparsity—even for detail-demanding queries, only a small, task-relevant region matters, yet the entire image is encoded. Existing workarounds fall into three unsatisfying camps: training-free heuristics require multiple redundant prefilling passes to extract attention crops; SFT-based tools demand massive paired annotation datasets; and RL-based think-with-image paradigms shift burden to lengthy auto-regressive Chain-of-Thought decoding, introducing severe latency before any RoI is identified. Key Idea The core insight is that MLLM intermediate transformer layers already encode robust cross-modal visual grounding signals—specifically, the cross-attention between user query tokens and visual tokens at middle layers reliably distinguishes task-relevant foreground from background. Rather than consulting any external detector, one can attach lightweight R-layer branches directly to the frozen backbone at split depth B, use these branches to predict a query-conditioned routing decision (gate) and a dense spatial RoI heatmap (SD-RPN), and train both via self-supervision derived entirely from the model's own behavior, eliminating human annotation, external grounding pipelines, and additional decoding passes. Methodology Q-Zoom operates as three coordinated modules attached to a frozen base MLLM: 1. Dynamic Gating Network: Shares weights with the SD-RPN branch (R=3 tunable transformer layers appended at layer B). During the single prefilling pass, intermediate hidden states H^B_context are processed to produce a routing probability Y^pred via a linear projection head and sigmoid. The gate is trained by Consistency-Aware Sample Generation: images are run at multiple resolutions r_1 < r_2 < ... < r_k, and only valid transition samples are retained (model fails at lower resolutions, succeeds at higher)—noisy cases where behavior is non-monotonic are discarded. Binary Cross-Entropy loss trains the gate to predict whether high-resolution refinement will change the answer. At inference, a threshold τ_gate routes each (image, query) pair to direct generation or the RoI branch. 2. Self-Distilled Region Proposal Network (SD-RPN): When the gate triggers RoI extraction, the same R-layer branch repurposed as a spatial prediction head generates a dense heatmap M̂_RoI by computing cross-modal attention between the final user query token embedding Q_RoI and the dense visual feature sequence K_v (both projected from the B+R-th layer). Pseudo-labels are constructed via: (a) sink-token filtering—tokens with anomalously large L2-norm features are zeroed out; (b) tri-state label assignment—high-confidence foreground tokens (attention score ≥ τ_fg × a_max) labeled 1, low-confidence background tokens (score ≤ τ_bg × a_max) labeled 0, and ambiguous middle-range tokens labeled −1 (ignored in loss). Optimal thresholds empirically: τ_fg = 0.20, τ_bg = 0.05. The bounding box enclosing the foreground region crops the high-resolution source image; the crop is re-encoded by the vision encoder and inserted just before user query tokens, with prefix KV-cache reuse to avoid recomputing the coarse context through layers 1–B. 3. Continuous Spatio-Temporal Alignment + Post-SFT: Because the cropped RoI loses its global spatial context under MRoPE, a dual-axis positional adjustment is applied: (a) a temporal shift assigns the RoI tokens a time offset δ = min(H,W) to logically distinguish them from source tokens sharing the same spatial footprint; (b) spatial interpolation maps the RoI token grid IDs back to the original bounding box coordinates in the source image's spatial frame. Post-SFT then fine-tunes only the LLM backbone (vision encoder and projector remain frozen) on ~7K hard samples mined via LLM-as-a-Judge: cases where the base model answers correctly but the unaligned RoI integration causes failure, isolating spatial distraction errors. This targeted fine-tune teaches the model to balance dense local RoI features with global context. Critical design decisions: (1) Operating in intermediate feature space avoids a separate prefilling pass—well-justified by the 1.4s vs. 15.9s latency comparison against Thyme. (2) Tri-state label assignment is the correct response to the empirically confirmed bimodal distribution of attention scores with an ambiguous middle range (Figure 5)—ablation Table VIII validates both thresholds independently. (3) Prefix KV-cache reuse prevents the secondary prefill from reprocessing system prompt and coarse visual tokens—a sound engineering optimization confirmed by throughput measurements. Key Results All experiments use the LMMS-Eval framework on a single NVIDIA A6000 GPU. Reported metrics are constrained to 576 max visual tokens for Document & OCR benchmarks and 4,096 for High-Resolution benchmarks. Document & OCR (Table II): On Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline (DocVQA 92.0, ChartQA 83.0, OCRBench 82.8, InfoVQA 70.1, TextVQA 81.1, average 81.8), Q-Zoom achieves 94.3 / 85.6 / 85.4 / 79.4 / 83.5 = 85.6 average (+3.8), at 0.81× baseline throughput. By comparison, AdaptVision (RL-based) achieves 0.06× throughput—Q-Zoom is >10× faster while outperforming it by +1.0% average. Against SD-RPN (authors' prior work), Q-Zoom adds +1.1% average. High-Resolution & Vision-Centric (Table III): On Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Q-Zoom achieves 72.3% average (+8.1% over baseline), surpassing Thyme by 1.1% and DeepEyes by 1.2%, at 0.86× baseline throughput versus Thyme's 0.21×. Pareto curves (Figure 8b) show Q-Zoom at 576 max tokens achieves 66.7% accuracy versus the baseline's peak of 64.2% at 4,096 tokens, delivering 4.39× throughput and 73.2% token reduction simultaneously. Orthogonality to RL models: Integrating Q-Zoom with ZwZ-Qwen2.5-VL-7B yields an additional +6.6% on High-Resolution tasks; ZwZ-Qwen3-VL-4B gains +5.2%, confirming the framework is complementary to thinking-based paradigms. Ablation (Table IV): Each of the three components (SD-RPN, Post-SFT, Dynamic Gate) contributes positively on Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Post-SFT specifically recovers general QA performance (MME, MMS) that degrades slightly with SD-RPN alone, confirming its role in restoring global spatial reasoning. Baselines are current and competitive: ViCrop (ICLR 2025), AdaptVision (CVPR 2026), Thyme (arXiv 2025), DeepEyes (arXiv 2025/ICLR 2026). Scale tested: 3B–7B parameter models; no evaluation beyond 7B. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. Genuinely novel: (1) consistency-aware resolution-trajectory labeling for routing supervision without human annotation; (2) tri-state self-distillation pseudo-label pipeline for RPN training that eliminates external grounding supervision; (3) continuous spatio-temporal positional encoding for reintegrating cropped RoI tokens into global spatial context. Standard combination applied to new setting: using cross-modal attention maps for RoI localization is established (ViCrop, Focus); the innovation is the distillation training paradigm and gating integration rather than the attention-map signal itself. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are comprehensive and well-structured: Table IV isolates each component's contribution, Table V compares against training-free RoI alternatives, Table VI sweeps backbone split depth B and RPN capacity R, Table VII evaluates pseudo-label data scaling vs. a ground-truth supervised baseline, Table VIII ablates threshold sensitivity. The ground-truth-supervised baseline (Table VII) achieving 78.0% versus the self-distilled model at 78.1% (50K samples) is a particularly strong validation of the self-distillation paradigm. No standard deviation or multiple-seed results are reported—single-run numbers throughout. Project page listed at https://yuhengssi.github.io/Q-Zoom/ but code availability is not explicitly confirmed in the paper text. Methodological concern: the LLM-as-a-Judge for Post-SFT uses same-model-family judges (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct judging Qwen2.5-VL-3B and 7B), which may introduce in-family bias in hard-sample selection. The SD-RPN trained on self-distilled pseudo-labels from the backbone it modifies creates a circular dependency that the ground-truth ablation mitigates but does not fully rule out. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (plug-and-play module deployed on real hardware with throughput measurements). - Practical: Yes—all training and evaluation run on a single NVIDIA A6000 (48 GB), and the module freezes the base MLLM, requiring only the lightweight branch weights to be trained. - Efficient: Document & OCR tasks run at 0.81× baseline throughput (slight overhead from gate + RoI branch when triggered), but this is >10× faster than AdaptVision and achieves 4.39× throughput over brute-force 4,096-token scaling on High-Resolution tasks; token cost reduction is 53–73% depending on benchmark type. - Effective: Yes for VQA-style document, OCR, and high-resolution visual tasks across five model families; evidence is on held-out benchmark splits with competitive baselines. No evidence on open-ended generation, grounding (RefCOCO), or video tasks. - Scalable: Tested at 3B–7B; the quadratic token savings argument implies larger gains at higher sequence lengths, but no experiment validates performance at 30B+ scale or under significantly different query distributions. Post-SFT is Qwen-only (MRoPE requirement). The work delivers a software module (trained branch weights + inference integration) that attaches to existing MLLM checkpoints; training recipes and hyperparameters are fully specified in the appendix. Strengths - The consistency-aware training sample generation is technically clever: it derives deterministic routing labels without human annotation or expensive RL by exploiting the monotonic accuracy-resolution relationship, and the instability filter (discarding non-monotonic cases) is a principled solution to label noise that the training curve in Figure 9a validates quantitatively. - The tri-state pseudo-label assignment directly addresses a real empirical problem (Figure 5 shows the bimodal attention score distribution with a genuinely ambiguous middle range) rather than assuming clean foreground/background separation; the threshold ablation confirms both boundaries contribute independently. - The Pareto frontier framing is honest—throughput is measured alongside accuracy on the same GPU throughout, and the paper directly compares against RL-based models at both metrics rather than hiding the efficiency cost of competitors. - Demonstrated integration with RL-trained thinking models (ZwZ-Qwen variants) and orthogonal accuracy gains confirm Q-Zoom is truly complementary to, not competing with, the RL reasoning paradigm. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper has no dedicated limitations section. Implicit acknowledgments: Post-SFT is restricted to Qwen models with MRoPE; Post-SFT hard-sample set is intentionally small (~7K); LLaVA gains from Q-Zoom are modest because low base resolution (336×336) forces the gate to trigger on nearly all high-resolution test images, limiting throughput gains. The authors confront these points briefly in the experimental analysis rather than isolating them as formal limitations, which reduces transparency. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No evaluation on spatial reasoning or grounding benchmarks (RefCOCO, GQA spatial) despite the mechanism being fundamentally a grounding one—the spatio-temporal alignment fix may not fully restore global spatial reasoning for tasks requiring relative object placement across large images. - The LLM-as-a-Judge using same-model-family at equal or larger scale introduces in-family bias; an independently annotated hard-sample validation set would strengthen the Post-SFT contribution's credibility. - No latency breakdown separating gate inference overhead from SD-RPN inference—the aggregate throughput numbers make it impossible to reason about where to tune the τ_gate threshold for deployment. - The incremental delta over the authors' prior SD-RPN (ICLR 2026) is thin on some suites: LLaVA-1.5-7B Document & OCR shows SD-RPN at 34.6 average vs. Q-Zoom at 34.7, raising the question of whether the Dynamic Gate and spatio-temporal alignment meaningfully contribute on weaker base models or primarily benefit stronger Qwen models. - All benchmarks are short-answer or multiple-choice VQA; performance on open-ended or multi-step reasoning tasks where routing errors compound is not assessed. Bottom Line Practitioners deploying Qwen-family MLLMs on document/OCR or high-resolution visual tasks gain a directly usable efficiency module with rigorously validated Pareto improvements; those outside the Qwen ecosystem or working on open-ended generation should read the SD-RPN predecessor paper first and treat Q-Zoom as an incremental extension.
