#33 — Innovative (74)

Representations Before Pixels: Semantics-Guided Hierarchical Video Prediction (6 ▲ )

Efstathios Karypidis, Spyros Gidaris, Nikos Komodakis · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) strong — thorough ablations, error bars over 3 seeds, code released, multi-dataset validation. Novelty (70) solid — hierarchical VFM-guided prediction with dynamic intermediate representation is a genuine compositional contribution. Non-Obviousness (75) the train-test mismatch solution (nested dropout + mixed supervision) is non-obvious and well-diagnosed. Completeness (75) good but evaluation confined to driving and DINOv2-probe circularity unacknowledged. Resilience (70) moderate — circularity concern and untested generality. Impact (70) code released, 7× convergence speedup is concretely useful. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*80+0.15*75+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*70 = ~74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Re2Pix, a hierarchical video prediction framework that autoregressively forecasts future DINOv2 VFM feature maps with a masked transformer, then conditions a latent diffusion model on those predicted features to synthesize photorealistic future frames. - Why it's important: End-to-end diffusion video predictors entangle semantic scene reasoning with appearance synthesis, causing object identity drift, structural degradation, and slow training convergence — compounding failures that are safety-critical in autonomous driving. - How it does it: Predicted VFM features serve as an explicit intermediate semantic scaffold between context and pixels; two robustness strategies — nested dropout over PCA-ordered feature channels, and 90/10 mixed supervision with ground-truth vs. predicted features — close the train-test distribution gap that would otherwise cause blurry inference outputs. - The good: Re2Pix achieves 7× faster convergence to FID=15 (20K vs. 140K iterations) and 14× faster segmentation mIoU convergence compared to a direct latent diffusion baseline, while simultaneously improving final FID (9.90 vs. 12.86), FVD (52.66 vs. 60.70), and segmentation mIoU (63.53 vs. 60.55) on Cityscapes. - The missing: Evaluation is confined to driving datasets (Cityscapes, nuScenes, CoVLA, KITTI); whether the hierarchical VFM-guided design helps on general video is untested, and the segmentation evaluation metric uses DINOv2-based probes that reward DINOv2-consistent outputs — creating a mild circularity the authors do not acknowledge. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building video prediction or world models for autonomous driving should read this; those working on general video generation can skim for the train-test mismatch solution but should not extrapolate the empirical claims. Problem & Motivation: Safe autonomous driving requires anticipating how scenes evolve — positions and identities of vehicles, pedestrians, and infrastructure — over multiple future frames. Current video prediction systems predict future frames end-to-end in a shared VAE latent space, where high-level scene semantics (what objects are present and where) and low-level appearance details (texture, lighting) are entangled. This entanglement causes temporal semantic inconsistencies (object identity drift, flickering, structural degradation), increases data requirements, slows convergence, and makes independent analysis or control of semantics vs. appearance impossible. Alignment approaches such as REPA and VideoREPA inject VFM guidance via auxiliary distillation losses during fine-tuning but still couple forecasting and rendering in one latent space and do not demonstrate convergence improvement when training from scratch. Pure semantic predictors (DINO-Foresight, DINO-WM) operate exclusively in feature space and cannot produce pixel-level output. Key Idea: Use predicted VFM features as a dynamic, evolving intermediate representation — not a static conditioning signal — that explicitly separates semantic forecasting from appearance synthesis. A masked transformer first predicts future DINOv2 feature maps; a diffusion model then generates future frames conditioned on those predictions. The non-obvious challenge is that this decomposition creates a train-test distribution mismatch: at training time, ground-truth DINOv2 features for future frames are available, but at inference the diffusion model receives noisier autoregressive predictions. Nested dropout (stochastic channel truncation respecting PCA ordering) and mixed supervision (90% ground-truth, 10% predicted features per batch) together regularize the diffusion model against over-reliance on clean, idealized conditioning signals. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. Stage 1 (Semantic Feature Prediction): DINOv2-Reg ViT-B/14 extracts per-frame feature maps h_t ∈ ℝ^{C_h × H_h × W_h} (C_h = 1152 channels via PCA of concatenated 3rd, 6th, 9th, 12th transformer block representations). A masked feature transformer G_h (12 layers, d=1152, built on DINO-Foresight architecture, N_c=4 context frames, N_p=1 prediction frame) autoregressively predicts h_{M+1:K} from h_{1:M} using Smooth L1 loss on masked future features. Stage 2 (Semantics-Guided Generation): Context frames are encoded to VAE latents z_{1:M} via a frozen causal 3D WAN2.1 VAE (8×8×4 spatiotemporal downsampling). A ~800M-parameter DiT (14 layers, 16 heads, 2048-dim, following Cosmos-Predict 1 architecture with 3D factorized RoPE, query/key normalization, RMSNorm, AdaLN-LoRA noise conditioning) denoises future VAE latents z_{M+1:K} conditioned on z_{1:M} and semantic features h_{1:K} (both context GT and future predicted). Critical design decisions: (1) Early Semantic Alignment fuses VFM features with VAE latents at the token input level via channel-wise summation after independent embedding, avoiding token count inflation — well-justified as a lightweight, non-intrusive conditioning mechanism. (2) Nested dropout stochastically retains only the first c ∈ {8,16,32,64,128,256,512,1152} channels of h_{1:K} with equal probability during training, exploiting PCA ordering where higher-variance components encode coarser semantics — the ablation confirms this is essential for generation quality. (3) Mixed supervision (90/10 GT/predicted) is validated in a three-way ablation showing each extreme degrades one metric family. Training uses EDM noise formulation with log-normal noise schedule, Adam (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.99), learning rate 0.6×2^{-10.5}, 40K/120K iterations on 8 H200 GPUs, effective batch size 8, 7–28 hours respectively. Key Results: Primary benchmark is Cityscapes (context frames 3–15 of a 25-frame sequence, predict frames 16–27, evaluate frame 19). Metrics: segmentation mIoU (all classes / moving objects), depth threshold accuracy δ₁ and AbsRel, FID, FVD. Against a standard diffusion baseline (782M params): Re2Pix (1.1B) achieves mIoU(A)=63.53 (+2.98), IoU(M)=62.29 (+4.65), δ₁=85.72 (+0.57), FID=9.90 (−2.96), FVD=52.66 (−8.04). A Baseline-Large (1.5B) model controls for parameter count and consistently underperforms Re2Pix, confirming gains are architectural rather than capacity-driven. Training convergence: FID=15 reached in 20K vs. 140K iterations (7× speedup); segmentation mIoU plateau reached 14× faster. Multi-dataset training (Cityscapes+nuScenes+CoVLA, 120K iterations): Re2Pix outperforms both Baseline and Baseline-Large on Cityscapes and nuScenes in-domain, and on KITTI zero-shot. Against large-scale pretrained-then-fine-tuned systems: Re2Pix surpasses Vista (NeurIPS 2024) and is competitive with fine-tuned Cosmos-Predict 2, despite training from scratch with far less data and compute. Error bars over 3 sampling runs (Table 7): Re2Pix FID=9.91±0.05, FVD=55.16±2.19 vs. Baseline FID=12.75±0.10, FVD=63.39±2.63 — differences are stable and significant. VFM sensitivity (Table 6): replacing DINOv2 with SigLIP-2 consistently outperforms the baseline, confirming robustness to encoder choice. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is: (1) explicit hierarchical decomposition where autoregressively predicted VFM features serve as a dynamic intermediate generative variable — distinct from static feature conditioning or alignment distillation — which the authors claim is the first use of VFM feature prediction to guide hierarchical video diffusion; (2) nested dropout + mixed supervision as a principled solution to train-test distribution shift in hierarchical generation. All components (DINOv2, masked transformers, DiT, WAN VAE, latent diffusion, REPA-style feature alignment) are existing; the novelty lies in their compositional design and the robustness engineering. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and well-controlled: separate ablations for nested dropout (Table 3), supervision strategy (Table 4), inference-time semantic component count (Table 5), VFM encoder choice (Table 6), and CFG-style representation guidance (Tables 8–9 in appendix). Error bars from 3 independent sampling runs are reported (appendix Table 7). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/Sta8is/Re2Pix. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.11707v1, 13 Apr 2026) and has not undergone peer review. One methodological concern: the segmentation and depth evaluation probes are trained on DINOv2 features, and Re2Pix explicitly conditions generation on DINOv2 features — this creates a circularity where the model is structurally incentivized to produce DINOv2-consistent outputs, which the evaluation metric rewards. The authors do not acknowledge this. The comparison to Cosmos-Predict 2 and Vista (pretrained on orders-of-magnitude more data) is labeled competitive but the gap in pretraining compute is not quantified. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a video prediction system. - Practical: Trains on 8 NVIDIA H200 GPUs in 7–28 hours; inference runs a standard DiT diffusion pipeline — commercially available hardware, compute-intensive but not exceptional. - Efficient: 7× convergence speedup over a same-scale diffusion baseline makes total training cost competitive with baselines at final performance; the ~800M parameter diffusion model is similar in scale to baselines (782M), so inference cost is not increased significantly. - Effective: Improvements are demonstrated simultaneously on semantic consistency and generation quality metrics across four driving datasets, including zero-shot transfer — not merely a proxy task. - Scalable: Tested on combined Cityscapes+nuScenes+CoVLA datasets (~80+ hours of driving video); performance improves with data scale. Long-horizon prediction (>12 frames), general video domains, and larger-scale stress testing are not evaluated. The paper delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) and a trained model architecture; model weights are not stated to be released. Strengths: - The train-test mismatch problem in hierarchical generation is real, non-obvious, and previously unaddressed in video prediction; nested dropout + mixed supervision is a concrete, clean solution with ablation evidence showing each component contributes independently. - The 7×/14× training convergence acceleration is the strongest practical finding — it substantially reduces the compute cost of training video diffusion from scratch, with a direct, controlled comparison. - Disentangling semantic forecasting from appearance synthesis without separate inference pipelines is an architectural contribution: Stage 1 is fast and interpretable (predicted DINOv2 features can be visualized); Stage 2 generates pixels conditioned on explicit structure. - The ablation showing robustness to VFM choice (DINOv2 vs. SigLIP-2) and to the number of inference-time PCA components (128 suffices, graceful degradation) indicates the design is not brittle. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that Re2Pix currently lacks explicit controllability (text prompts, trajectory constraints, scene graphs) and suggest this as future work. They acknowledge the potential for incorporating richer 3D cues (geometry, collaborative encoders). They note that predictions depend on VFM pretraining data quality and may inherit biases. They do not currently enable the CFG-style representation guidance in the main system, treating it as an exploratory finding. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) All four evaluation datasets are autonomous driving datasets — the paper makes no claim of generality, but the method's applicability to general video (e.g., activity, sports, indoor) is entirely uncharacterized. (2) The segmentation/depth evaluation uses DINOv2-based probes, introducing circularity: Re2Pix conditions generation on DINOv2 features, which structurally makes its outputs more DINOv2-interpretable; this is not acknowledged and could inflate apparent semantic consistency gains. (3) Only 12 future frames (≈0.75s at 16fps) are predicted; long-horizon autoregressive error accumulation from the feature transformer is untested. (4) The comparison to Cosmos-Predict 2 and Vista is described as competitive without quantifying the pretraining compute differential — readers may conflate method quality with pretraining advantage on the fine-tuned baselines. Bottom Line: A practitioner building video prediction or world models for autonomous driving gains concrete, well-supported techniques from this work — specifically the hierarchical VFM-guided design and its robustness strategies — and the 7×/14× convergence acceleration alone justifies adoption over direct latent diffusion baselines.
Recommended 2604.11707 PDF
#34 — Innovative (74)

Lyra 2.0: Explorable Generative 3D Worlds (17 ▲ )

Tianchang Shen, Sherwin Bahmani, Kai He, Sangeetha Grama Srinivasan, Tianshi Cao, Jiawei Ren, Ruilong Li, Zian Wang, Nicholas Sharp, Zan Gojcic, Sanja Fidler, Jiahui Huang, Huan Ling, Jun Gao, Xuanchi Ren · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (70) — decoupling geometric routing (Q/K-only canonical coordinate injection) from appearance synthesis, combined with one-step self-augmentation for drift correction, is a principled architectural contribution. Rigor (70) — thorough ablations isolating each component, in-domain + OOD evaluation, but single runs, CaM/SPMem reimplemented, and reconstruction model trained on own generator's outputs (circularity). Non-Obviousness (75) — Q/K-only injection and cheap one-step self-augmentation are non-obvious and load-bearing. Completeness (65) — static-only, no interactive user study, no memory/coverage stress tests. Resilience (70) — honest limitation acknowledgment. Impact (80) — strong Isaac Sim integration and DMD distillation make it interactively deployable, but GB200 hardware requirement and no code/weights release limit practical reproducibility. Weighted roughly 74.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Lyra 2.0, an interactive system that generates arbitrarily long, 3D-consistent video explorations from a single image and lifts them into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes and surface meshes deployable in simulation engines and interactive viewers. - Why it's important: Creating large-scale 3D environments for embodied AI, VR, and robotics simulation currently requires expensive real-world multi-view capture; a system that synthesizes explorable 3D worlds from a single photograph would remove that bottleneck at scale. - How it does it: A retrieve–generate–update autoregressive loop decouples geometric memory from appearance synthesis by using per-frame 3D geometry caches solely for visibility-based history frame retrieval and canonical coordinate correspondence injection into a DiT, while a self-augmentation training strategy—corrupting history latents and supervising the model to denoise toward clean targets—closes the train-test discrepancy that causes temporal drift. - The good: Lyra 2.0 achieves the best quantitative results across nearly all metrics on both in-domain DL3DV (FID 43.43, Style Consistency 87.46, Camera Controllability 64.67) and out-of-domain Tanks and Temples (FID 51.33, Style Consistency 85.07), with a clean ablation that isolates each component's contribution. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted entirely to static scenes; the paper never tests or claims correctness for dynamic content, and the visibility-based retrieval achieves only ~44–45% target-view coverage even at N_s=10 retrieved frames, meaning more than half of revisited pixels arrive with no historical grounding. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers working on 3D scene generation, video diffusion for simulation, or embodied AI world modeling should read this; it presents a well-engineered system backed by credible ablations and honest limitation acknowledgment, though practitioners targeting dynamic or outdoor-at-scale scenarios should note the static-world constraint. Problem & Motivation: Creating large, explorable 3D environments—needed for robot navigation training, VR, and interactive simulation—currently demands dense multi-view real-world capture, which is costly and unscalable. Recent video diffusion models can synthesize camera-controlled walkthroughs, which can then be lifted to 3D via feed-forward reconstruction (the "generative reconstruction" paradigm). However, extending this to long, complex trajectories with revisits exposes two fundamental failure modes in current autoregressive video models: spatial forgetting (previously viewed regions fall outside the temporal context window, causing hallucinated structures on revisit) and temporal drifting (small per-step synthesis errors compound across autoregressive steps, producing progressive color shifts and geometric distortions). Prior remedies address only one problem at a time: methods that accumulate a global 3D point cloud for conditioning suffer error amplification from imperfect depth estimates; methods that add longer context windows do not fix the train-test distribution gap that is the root cause of drift. Key Idea: The core insight is to decouple geometry from appearance: rather than conditioning the video diffusion model on rendered views of an accumulated 3D scene (which couples generation quality to 3D reconstruction quality), maintain per-frame point clouds exclusively as an index for retrieving which history frames are geometrically relevant and for establishing dense pixel-level correspondences, while leaving all appearance synthesis to the video model's pretrained generative prior. Self-augmentation training then closes the drift gap by replacing clean history latents (available during training but not at inference) with one-step-denoised approximations of the model's own degraded outputs, directly teaching drift correction. Methodology: Lyra 2.0 builds on Wan 2.1-14B DiT with a flow-matching objective. At each autoregressive step, the retrieve–generate–update loop proceeds as follows: (1) Retrieve: A 3D cache C stores per-frame depth maps and downsampled point clouds. For a target camera (T, K), visibility scores φ(i) are computed by projecting each cached point cloud onto the target image plane and counting visible, non-occluded points. Ns=5 frames are greedily selected to maximize collective pixel coverage. (2) Generate: Retrieved frames enter the DiT as spatial slots (encoded by the VAE without temporal compression). Dense 3D correspondences are established by forward-warping canonical coordinate maps C_j ∈ [-1,1]^{3×H×W} through the full-resolution depth (Eq. 3); the resulting 4-channel warped maps (coordinates + depth) are encoded via sinusoidal PE + MLP and injected into query/key projections of every transformer block—not into values, preserving the pretrained appearance prior. Temporal history is compressed via FramePack using variable-kernel patchification (recent frames fine-grained, distant frames coarse). Camera conditioning uses dual injection: depth-warped frame concatenated to the denoising latent, and 6D Plücker ray coordinates added to token features before attention Q/K projections. (3) Anti-Drift Self-Augmentation: With probability p_aug=0.7, history latents z_0^hist are corrupted by sampling t~U(0,0.5) and adding flow-matching noise; the model performs one forward pass to produce ẑ_0^hist, which replaces the clean latent as conditioning context while the target latent z_0^cur is always encoded from the ground-truth clean frame. This forces the model to denoise toward the clean target despite degraded conditioning, at the cost of one additional DiT forward pass during training. (4) 3D Reconstruction: A feed-forward 3DGS pipeline based on Depth Anything v3 (DAv3) is fine-tuned on 3,000 one-minute videos generated by the system; a modified Gaussian DPT head with k=2 downsampling reduces Gaussian count by 4×. Mesh extraction uses a hierarchical sparse OpenVDB grid with marching cubes. A DMD-distilled student model (4 denoising steps instead of 35, classifier-free guidance distilled in) achieves ~13× per-step speedup. Key Results: On DL3DV-Evaluation (in-domain): Ours achieves SSIM=0.388, LPIPS=0.498, FID=43.43, Subjective Quality=44.54, Style Consistency=87.46, Camera Controllability=64.67, Reprojection Error=0.076—best on nearly all metrics. On Tanks and Temples (out-of-domain): SSIM=0.384, LPIPS=0.552, FID=51.33, Style Consistency=85.07, Camera Controllability=63.87, Reproj. Error=0.069—again best or tied-best across metrics. For 3D scene generation (Ours Full): LPIPS-P=0.381, LPIPS-G=0.579, FID=65.94, Subjective Quality=20.52 on DL3DV; LPIPS-P=0.372, LPIPS-G=0.629, FID=72.47, Subj. Quality=18.80 on Tanks and Temples—outperforming all baselines including GEN3C, CaM, VMem, and SPMem paired with DAv3. The DMD distilled model achieves comparable per-frame quality (LPIPS, FID) to the 35-step model while reducing per-step generation time from ~194s to ~15s on a single NVIDIA GB200 GPU. Ablation on Tanks and Temples confirms each component: removing global point cloud (replacing per-frame cache) drops Camera Controllability from 63.87 to 49.86; removing self-augmentation drops Camera Controllability to 53.92 and Style Consistency to 77.98; removing FramePack drops Style Consistency to 80.61. Baselines include strong recent work (GEN3C, CaM, SPMem, VMem, Yume-1.5, HY-WorldPlay) rebuilt on Wan 2.1-14B where not open-sourced. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The system-level contribution (interactive long-horizon explorable 3D world generation from a single image) builds on prior video diffusion and 3DGS reconstruction components, but the combination of per-frame cache for pure information routing (without fused global representation), canonical coordinate warping injected into attention Q/K only, and the lightweight one-step self-augmentation training strategy are technically novel relative to the prior art. The key distinction from GEN3C and SPMem is architectural: decoupling geometric routing from appearance synthesis avoids the error-amplification feedback loop of conditioning on rendered views from an accumulated 3D scene. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and quantitative (Table 3), covering all four major design choices with consistent metrics on Tanks and Temples. The paper reports both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, which is credible. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; only single-run numbers are given. The WorldScore metrics (Subjective Quality, Style Consistency, Camera Controllability) are adopted from WorldScore [18] rather than invented by the authors, which is methodologically sound. However, CaM and SPMem are re-implemented by the authors rather than using author-provided code, introducing a potential reimplementation gap. Training was performed on 64 NVIDIA GB200 GPUs for 7,000 iterations—a compute budget inaccessible to most academic groups. No model weights, code, or dataset are released in this preprint (project page at https://research.nvidia.com/labs/sil/lyra2/ is listed but availability is not stated). LPIPS-P (consistency of the generated video) and LPIPS-G (quality vs. ground truth) are clearly distinguished, which is an analytical strength. This is a preprint; peer review has not been completed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (built tool evaluated by deployment). - Practical: The full model requires a single NVIDIA GB200 GPU for inference (194s per 80-frame step); the distilled model reduces this to 15s per step on the same GPU, making interactive use plausible but constrained to high-end hardware not commercially available to general users as of the paper's date. - Efficient: The distilled (4-step) variant is ~13× faster than the 35-step model with comparable per-frame quality but moderate camera controllability degradation; the 3D cache retrieval adds <1s per step overhead; the modified Gaussian DPT head reduces Gaussian count by 4× for real-time rendering. - Effective: Evidence for 3D scene generation quality is strong: best FID and Subjective Quality on both benchmarks, with LPIPS-P confirming underlying video 3D-consistency translates to better reconstruction; the Isaac Sim integration demonstration shows the output is directly usable in a physics engine. - Scalable: Long-horizon behavior is demonstrated qualitatively (~90m trajectory, 800+ frame revisit), but the paper does not report systematic stress tests (e.g., trajectories at 10× scene scale or 10,000+ frames); the 3D cache grows unboundedly and no memory cap or pruning strategy is described. The work delivers an interactive system with an associated inference pipeline; no weights or code are released in this preprint. Strengths: - The decoupling of geometric routing from appearance synthesis is architecturally principled: injecting canonical coordinate correspondences only into Q/K (not V) ensures the video model's appearance prior is not overridden by noisy depth-warped RGB content, which avoids a known failure mode of prior depth-warped conditioning. - The self-augmentation training strategy is computationally cheap (one additional DiT forward pass, p_aug=0.7) yet delivers substantial improvements: Camera Controllability jumps from 53.92 to 63.87 and Style Consistency from 77.98 to 85.07 on Tanks and Temples, with ablation clearly isolating the effect. - Out-of-domain evaluation on Tanks and Temples—a real-world multi-view benchmark not in the training distribution—demonstrates genuine generalization rather than benchmark overfitting, and the performance gap vs. baselines is maintained. - The DMD distillation achieving ~13× speedup with comparable visual quality (and distilling CFG into the student) is a practical engineering contribution that makes interactive use on single high-end GPUs plausible. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) the framework handles only static scenes and does not model dynamic objects, identified as important future work; (2) the model inherits photometric inconsistencies from the DL3DV training data (exposure variations across views), which can cause artifacts in 3DGS reconstruction; they suggest photometric compensation modules or synthetic photometrically-consistent datasets as remedies. These are stated plainly without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Target-view coverage plateaus at ~44–45% even with Ns=10 retrieved frames (Figure 9), meaning on average more than half of revisited pixels receive no geometric grounding from the spatial memory; the paper does not analyze how generation quality degrades as a function of coverage or trajectory complexity. - All quantitative results use camera poses estimated by ViPE from the generated videos, not ground-truth camera trajectories at inference; the true camera controllability under arbitrary user-specified trajectories (the primary use case) is not directly measured against a reference. - The fine-tuned DAv3 reconstruction model is trained on 3,000 videos generated by the same Lyra 2.0 system, creating a train-test circularity: quality of the reconstruction model is measured partly by how well it handles artifacts produced by the generator it was fine-tuned on, which may not reflect performance on videos from other generators or real captures. - No user study or human evaluation of the interactive exploration experience is reported despite the system being pitched as an interactive tool; Subjective Quality is a single scalar, not a structured usability assessment. - The paper does not report GPU memory consumption for the full model or the 3D cache size as a function of trajectory length, which are critical engineering constraints for practical deployment. Bottom Line: A practitioner building 3D simulation assets for embodied AI or a researcher studying long-horizon video generation will gain concrete, well-ablated technical insights from this paper—particularly the decoupled geometric routing design and the self-augmentation training trick—though the GB200-class hardware requirements and absence of released weights make immediate replication inaccessible to most.
Recommended 2604.13036 PDF
#35 — Innovative (74)

Strips as Tokens: Artist Mesh Generation with Native UV Segmentation (44 ▲ )

Rui Xu, Dafei Qin, Kaichun Qiao, Qiujie Dong, Huaijin Pi, Qixuan Zhang, Longwen Zhang, Lan Xu, Jingyi Yu, Wenping Wang, Taku Komura · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical/Systems profile (scored as empirical). Novelty (80) high — strip tokenization with in-vocabulary UV-boundary tokens is a genuinely new representational primitive, and unified tri/quad decoding via stride is non-obvious. Rigor (72) strong thanks to the controlled matched-budget tokenizer ablation isolating the representational gain (F1 0.455→0.560), though no error bars, no peer review, no confirmed code release. Non-Obviousness (80) — the strip-as-token insight is surprising. Completeness (70) — honest limitations, but quad evaluation lacks head-to-head against QuadGPT. Resilience (70). Impact (65) — no released weights/code yet. Composite ≈ 0.20·80+0.25·72+0.15·80+0.15·70+0.15·70+0.10·65 = 73.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SATO (Strips as Tokens), an autoregressive framework that tokenizes 3D meshes as connected triangle-strip sequences and natively encodes UV island boundaries as special tokens, producing artist-quality triangle and quadrilateral meshes with simultaneous UV segmentation from a single 0.5B-parameter model. - Why it's important: Artist-created meshes require clean edge flow, organized connectivity, and production-ready UV layouts that existing autoregressive methods cannot produce because their coordinate-sorted or patch-based tokenizations disrupt the continuous surface structure artists intentionally build into their work. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a greedy zipper-like strip extraction algorithm that serializes mesh faces into edge-sharing chains, embedding strip-transition tokens (C₁ᵗ) and UV-island-boundary tokens (C₁ᵘᵛ) directly into the first-level codebook vocabulary so that structural and semantic cues are inseparable from geometric content. - The good: In a strictly controlled large-scale ablation (64×A800 GPUs, 200K steps, identical architecture and training budget), replacing DeepMesh's tokenizer with SATO's raises F1 from 0.455 to 0.560 on the 150-shape test set, demonstrating that the tokenization alone — not model size or data — drives the improvement. - The missing: The strongest quad baseline (QuadGPT) withholds inference code and pretrained weights, precluding a quantitative head-to-head comparison on the task where SATO makes its boldest claim; the quad evaluation therefore rests on user studies and comparisons against classical remeshing methods rather than the closest learned competitor. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on autoregressive 3D generation, UV parameterization, or production mesh pipelines — SATO is the first unified method to jointly generate topology and UV charts, and its tokenization analysis is independently useful for anyone designing sequence representations of meshes. Problem & Motivation: Production-ready 3D assets for games, film, and simulation require meshes with organized edge flow, clean topology, and UV chart partitions compatible with texturing workflows. Generic geometry-processing pipelines (Delaunay remeshing, Marching Cubes) produce mathematically compact but artistically misaligned meshes; artists must manually retopologize and UV-unwrap such outputs, which is expensive and bottlenecks content creation. Existing autoregressive mesh generators either use coordinate-sorted vertex orderings that fragment the continuous edge-flow structure, or patch-based tokenizations that reset spatial context every 5–7 faces, preventing the model from learning long coherent surface runs. UV segmentation is handled post-hoc by separate tools (e.g., PartField + Blender unwrapping), which introduces multi-stage fragility and seams that ignore the mesh's topological structure. Key Idea: Triangle strips — sequences of triangles where each consecutive pair shares a full edge — already encode the edge-flow structure that artists impose on meshes. By serializing a mesh as a chain of such strips (using a greedy zipper traversal) and reserving special tokens for strip boundaries and UV island boundaries within the first level of a hierarchical coordinate codebook, the model receives an inductive bias that matches the macro-structural organization of artist-created assets. A secondary insight is that quadrilateral faces are geometrically identical to adjacent triangle pairs sharing an internal edge, so the same strip sequence can be decoded as either triangles (stride δ=1) or quads (stride δ=2) simply by changing the decoding rule at inference time — no separate model, vocabulary, or architecture is needed. Methodology: SATO represents a mesh M as a tuple (V, F) and proceeds through four components. (1) Hierarchical geometry quantization maps each vertex to a 512³ grid via a three-level hierarchy (4³, 8³, 16³ cells), yielding hierarchical tuples (c₁, c₂, c₃); a prefix-sharing strategy compresses consecutive vertices that share coarse coordinates, achieving an empirical token distribution of 20.7% / 35.0% / 44.3% across the three levels. (2) Strip-based serialization (Algorithm 1) builds an edge-to-face adjacency map, then grows strips greedily by traversing shared-edge boundaries; for triangles, each step adds one new vertex (stride δ=1); for quads, each step adds a swapped vertex pair (δ=2). The first vertex of each new strip uses the augmented vocabulary C₁* = C₁ᵍᵉᵒ ∪ C₁ᵗ ∪ C₁ᵘᵛ, where C₁ᵗ signals a strip transition and C₁ᵘᵛ signals a UV island boundary; UV islands are processed in bottom-to-top order, exhausting each island before transitioning. (3) Topology-specific decoding uses the structural tokens to reset the coordinate cache and topological frontier deterministically. (4) Three-stage training uses standard cross-entropy loss (Eq. 2): Stage I pretrained on ~1.47M triangle meshes (64×A800, ~200K steps, ~7 days); Stage II post-trains on 1.11M UV-annotated meshes (256×A800, ~80K steps, ~3 days) with the UV tokens introduced; Stage III fine-tunes on ~120K quad meshes (64×A800, ~25K steps, ~1 day). The backbone is a 0.5B-parameter Hourglass Transformer (21 layers, 8 heads, 1024-dim) with a 0.27B point-cloud-conditioned decoder; a per-run VAE encoder (trained from scratch, ~0.23B) cross-attends 3072 point-cloud tokens. The 9K-token truncated window (randomly sampled contiguous subsequences during training) addresses the mismatch between sequence length and attention window. Key design decisions: (a) the greedy lowest-coordinate-first strip seed strategy is deliberately sub-optimal for compression but produces a fixed, spatially coherent traversal the network can reliably learn — well-justified; (b) UV pretraining on triangle data first and injecting UV tokens in Stage II avoids degenerate solutions where the model neglects geometry to predict segment boundaries — well-justified by the ablation in Sec. 5.4.2; (c) the vocabulary size of 4800 (vs. DeepMesh's 4736) absorbs the extra transition tokens with minimal overhead — reasonable but not heavily ablated. Key Results: On a held-out 150-shape test set (50 shapes each from ShapeNet, Thingi10K, Objaverse, strictly excluded from training), SATO outperforms four publicly available baselines (MeshAnythingV2, TreeMeshGPT, BPT, DeepMesh) on all four metrics across all three datasets. Representative numbers: ShapeNet NC=0.975, CD=0.002, HD=0.032, F1=0.807 (vs. second-best DeepMesh: NC=0.967, CD=0.004, HD=0.037, F1=0.532); Objaverse F1=0.503 (vs. BPT's 0.265). A user study (25 professionals, 30 shape groups, ranking 1st–3rd) gives SATO a mean score of 2.61 vs. DeepMesh 1.17, BPT 1.40, TreeMeshGPT 0.57, MeshAnythingV2 0.18. For UV quality, SATO vs. PartField unwrapping: L2 Stretch 0.979 vs. 0.921 (lower is worse here — wait, all lower is better for distortion; PartField has lower L2 Stretch but SATO wins on Area Distortion 0.562 vs. 0.849, Angle Distortion 1.128 vs. 1.256, and Symmetric Dirichlet 5.156 vs. 6.283). The controlled tokenizer ablation (Table 9, 64×A800, 200K steps, same architecture) shows SATO tokenizer at NC=0.925, CD=0.014, HD=0.103, F1=0.560 vs. DeepMesh tokenizer at NC=0.908, CD=0.022, HD=0.108, F1=0.455. For quads, SATO scores 1.8 in user preference vs. 1.48 (NeurCross), 1.24 (CrossGen), 1.08 (QuadWild), 0.28 (QuadriFlow), 0.12 (IM); quantitative metrics on a single shape (Table 7) show SATO at NC=0.971, CD=0.001, HD=0.020, F1=0.857, competitive with remeshing methods that operate directly on ground-truth geometry. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The strip tokenization of 3D meshes for autoregressive generation is genuinely novel — triangle strips are a classical rendering primitive but had not been used to define token ordering for generative models. Encoding UV island boundaries as vocabulary tokens within the same sequence is first-in-class. The unified tri/quad decoding via stride parameter is a non-obvious consequence of the strip formulation. The hierarchical coordinate quantization and Hourglass Transformer backbone are carried over from prior work (DeepMesh, Meshtron); the novelty is entirely in the representation design and training curriculum. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and targeted: tokenizer comparison (Table 8/9) isolates the contribution of strip tokenization under matched conditions; UV training strategy ablation (Sec. 5.4.2 / Fig. 18) shows that training from scratch on UV data collapses, validating the two-stage approach; quad fine-tuning ablation (Fig. 19) shows qualitative improvement. Error bars are not reported for the main quantitative comparison (Table 2); no significance tests are reported. The user study uses 25 participants and ranking-based scores, which is reasonable but not large-scale. Code and pretrained weights are not mentioned as released in the paper; no repository URL is provided. The test set is explicitly stated to be strictly excluded from training data. The comparison omits QuadGPT (strong quad baseline) due to unavailable weights, and Hunyuan3D (commercial, closed-source) is compared only via its open-source 0.5B variant — these are acknowledged and defensible but weaken the quad evaluation. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — a trained generative model evaluated by quality metrics and user studies. - Practical: The model runs on A800 GPUs; inference uses KV-cache with temperature sampling at T=0.5, which is compatible with high-end workstation or cloud GPU hardware, though no inference latency numbers are reported. - Efficient: SATO's compression ratio of 0.283 tokens/face is between BPT (0.228) and DeepMesh (0.330), and its 9K-token window matches current generation methods; training requires 256×A800 GPU-days for the UV stage, which is substantial but in line with comparable systems. - Effective: The evidence — quantitative metrics on 150 held-out shapes, user study with domain professionals, controlled tokenizer ablation — consistently supports the claim that strip tokenization improves geometric and topological quality over patch-based alternatives; the UV quality improvement over PartField is supported by four standard parameterization distortion metrics. - Scalable: The method was trained at up to 256×A800 scale and tested on meshes up to 16,000 faces; no explicit stress-scale evaluation at 10×–100× face counts is presented, and the paper acknowledges that quad quality is currently bounded by the scarcity of high-quality quad training data. The paper delivers a trained model system; no software library or inference code release is mentioned. Strengths: - The controlled tokenizer ablation (Table 9) is methodologically clean: same architecture, same training budget, same data, tokenizer as the sole variable — this is the right experiment to run and its result (F1 0.455 → 0.560) credibly attributes the gain to the representation rather than scale. - Encoding UV island boundaries as vocabulary tokens — without adding sequence length overhead, since the transition tokens replace existing strip-start tokens — is an elegant design that avoids the multi-stage pipeline fragility of post-hoc UV methods. - The unified tri/quad decoding via a single stride parameter is practically valuable: it enables transfer of triangle-domain priors (which are data-rich) to the quad domain (data-scarce) without architectural changes, and the ablation confirms this transfer materially improves quad quality. - The three-stage curriculum (triangle pretraining → UV post-training → quad fine-tuning) is well-motivated and the ablations show each stage is load-bearing rather than incidental. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) odd-length quad strips can degenerate a final unpaired vertex to a triangle face; (2) near-spherical shapes receive less regular edge routing due to data bias toward non-spherical meshes in the training corpus; (3) quad quality is bounded by the scale and consistency of available quad-mesh datasets; (4) occasional less-regular edge routing on spherical shapes is a persistent corner case they plan to address with shape-adaptive routing priors. The authors confront these honestly and quantify the strip/triangle degeneration as rare in practice. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No inference latency is reported — for an artist-facing tool, generation speed per mesh matters and the 9K-token sequence with a 0.5B model on high-end hardware may be slow in practice. (2) The quad evaluation compares against classical remeshing methods (IM, QuadriFlow, QuadWild, NeurCross, CrossGen) that operate on ground-truth geometry, not point clouds — the comparison is structurally unfair in both directions (remeshers get clean input, SATO gets a noisier signal) and the paper does not disentangle these effects. (3) The UV evaluation measures UV segmentation quality (island boundaries) but the actual 2D parameterization is computed by Blender's unwrapping algorithm post-hoc — SATO does not learn UV coordinates, only chart partitions, and the benefit is measured through Blender's algorithm applied to those partitions. A reviewer would want to know whether the same Blender algorithm on PartField's charts would converge to similar quality with a better chart-boundary initializer. (4) Generalization to non-manifold or very high-face-count meshes (>16K) is not evaluated. (5) This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.09132v1); results have not undergone peer review. Bottom Line: SATO is the most complete autoregressive mesh generation system to date — it is the only method that jointly generates topology and UV segmentation at artist quality, and the controlled tokenizer ablation makes a credible case that strip serialization is the right representational primitive; practitioners building production 3D content pipelines should read this paper.
Recommended 2604.09132 PDF
#36 — Innovative (74)

Exploration and Exploitation Errors Are Measurable for Language Model Agents (21 ▲ )

Jaden Park, Jungtaek Kim, Jongwon Jeong, Robert D. Nowak, Kangwook Lee, Yong Jae Lee · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (85) drives the score — policy-agnostic trajectory-based exploration/exploitation metric is a genuinely new problem formulation grounded in classical graph theory. Non-Obviousness (82) — graph-theoretic stale score keyed to four-case taxonomy is non-trivial. Rigor (58) weak — only 3 seeds, no significance tests, R²=0.947 is across 13 aggregate points. Completeness (70) — honest limitations section. Resilience (60) — exploitation metric R²=0.006 is an unresolved central tension. Impact (75) — code released, actionable harness engineering result (+29pp). Weighted: ~72, bumped to 74 for strong novelty/NO combination.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a policy-agnostic metric grounded in classical graph theory that quantifies exploration and exploitation errors in LM agents from observed action trajectories alone, paired with procedurally generated partially observable 2D grid-map environments with unknown task DAGs. - Why it's important: Existing LM agent benchmarks report only task success rate, which cannot distinguish whether an agent failed because it explored poorly, exploited poorly, or both — a gap that blocks principled improvement of agents in open-ended decision-making tasks like AI coding and embodied AI. - How it does it: The metric tracks a per-timestep "stale score" (cyclomatic number of redundant loops + excess edge re-traversals + excess node re-visits within no-progress segments), attributes each increment to exploration, exploitation, or both based on whether the agent's required action is to explore or exploit, and flags an error whenever no progress is made and the stale score rises. - The good: Exploration error achieves R²=0.947 in predicting task success rate across 13 frontier LM agents, and a minimal rule-based harness engineering intervention lifts GPT-4.1 success from 63% to 92.6% and cuts its exploration error from 0.297 to 0.053. - The missing: The exploitation error metric is self-admittedly weak (R²=0.006 against success), and with only 3 random seeds per configuration and no significance tests, the fine-grained per-model behavioral differences claimed in Findings 2 and 5 lack statistical support. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or benchmarking LM agents who want diagnostic tools beyond pass rate, and for anyone designing agent harnesses — less urgent for practitioners focused only on task-level performance. Problem & Motivation: LM agents deployed on open-ended tasks (AI coding, workflow automation, embodied AI) must both explore unknown environments to discover task-relevant information and exploit acquired knowledge to complete sub-tasks in dependency order. In classical RL, exploration and exploitation are defined with respect to the agent's internal policy or value function, but for black-box LM agents, only the observed action sequence is available. Existing evaluation methods either rely on task success rate alone or compare trajectories against a fixed reference trajectory, which implicitly assumes a known optimal strategy and cannot isolate whether errors stem from poor exploration or poor exploitation. No framework previously existed that could quantitatively separate these two failure modes from trajectories alone, in a policy-agnostic way. Key Idea: The core insight is to use classical graph exploration theory (specifically, cyclomatic complexity and bounds on edge/node reuse from Tarjan/Edmonds/Panaite-Pelc results) to define a "stale score" that increases only when an agent makes structurally redundant moves — moves that no reasonable strategy would make — during a no-progress segment. By separately tracking which of four cases determines whether the agent should be exploring or exploiting at each timestep, the stale score increment can be attributed to exploration error, exploitation error, or both without knowing the agent's internal strategy. Methodology: The environment consists of a partially observable 2D grid map M (subset of ℕ²) and an unknown task DAG G=(N,E) with AND/OR prerequisite types. All task node labels are random four-character alphanumeric codes to strip semantic information. At each timestep, the agent receives admissible moves and any newly discovered task node information; it must traverse unobserved cells to discover nodes and achieve the goal node. The pending task set P(t) and unobserved cell set U(t) determine four cases dictating whether the required action is exploration (Case 1, 3), exploitation (Case 2), or either (Case 4). An action is flagged as an error if Gain(t→t+1)=0 and the stale score S_t = c_t + e_t + n_t increases, where c_t counts new independent cycles (cyclomatic number), e_t counts edge traversals beyond a budget of 2 in the no-progress segment, and n_t counts node visits beyond a budget of 2. Errors are attributed by case type. Error metrics are normalized by the number of timesteps in which each action type is required. Thirteen LMs across four families are evaluated (GPT-4.1/mini/nano, GPT-5.4/mini/nano, Gemini 3.1 Pro/Flash/Flash Lite, Claude Opus 4.6/Sonnet 4.6/Haiku 4.5, GPT-OSS-120B) at temperature 0. Four prompt variants (base, exploration-focused, exploitation-focused, balanced) × two reasoning modes = 8 prompt sets, 9 map configurations (3 exploitation demands × 3 DAG sizes), 3 seeds each, yielding 216 episodes per model. Critical design decisions: (1) the budget of 2 for edge/node reuse is motivated by classical graph traversal theory and is well-justified; (2) attributing Case-4 errors to both types simultaneously is a pragmatic choice since the agent could legitimately do either — this is acknowledged but not ablated; (3) symbolic node labels prevent semantic prior exploitation, which is well-justified for isolating raw exploration/exploitation capability but limits ecological validity. Key Results: Across 13 LMs, log exploration error and success rate show R²=0.947 (strong negative linear relationship), while log exploitation error and success rate show R²=0.006 (effectively no relationship). Top performers (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro) reach 100% success rates; small models (GPT-4.1 nano, GPT-5.4 nano) achieve near 0%. Exploration-focused prompts reduce exploration error (GPT-4.1 base: 0.123 → explore: 0.099) and yield the highest success rate (63% base → 80% explore for GPT-4.1). Harness engineering (injecting a structured memory summary: visited cells, frontier cells, discovered/activatable states) boosts Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite from 51.9% to 88.9% success (exploration error 0.172→0.030, exploitation error 0.135→0.071, steps 94.3→68.0) and GPT-4.1 from 63.0% to 92.6% (exploration error 0.297→0.053, exploitation error 0.160→0.044). Semantic information injection (4 hand-crafted pasta-cooking maps) increases GPT-4.1 success ~3× while Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite shows ~33% higher exploration error but 6× lower exploitation error with semantics — indicating models use semantic priors qualitatively differently. Notably, Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro both achieve 100% success but via different strategies: Opus exploits known information efficiently after ~50% episode progress while Gemini Pro continues exploring unobserved cells throughout. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW FORMULATION + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The paper introduces the problem of policy-agnostic quantitative separation of exploration vs. exploitation errors from LM agent trajectories — no prior work addresses this exact formulation. The metric itself is a genuine new construction, grounded in but extending classical graph exploration results to the task-DAG setting. The procedurally generated environment is a new benchmark. The core technique (combining cyclomatic number, edge reuse, and node reuse into a stale score keyed to a four-case taxonomy of agent state) is not a straightforward application of existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for prompt type (4 variants), harness engineering (2 models), and semantic information (2 models on hand-crafted maps). However, only 3 random seeds per configuration are used throughout, and no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for any finding. The R²=0.947 result is computed across 13 aggregate model-level points, which inflates apparent correlation strength and is not a per-episode statistical result. The exploitation error metric's weakness (R²=0.006) is acknowledged but not resolved. Code is publicly released (linked in the abstract). Model weights and datasets are not separately released (API-based evaluation). The harness engineering ablation covers only 2 of 13 models, and the semantic experiment uses only 4 hand-crafted maps with 5 seeds each — marginal sample sizes for the behavioral claims made. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile (evaluation framework + benchmark). - Practical: Runs entirely via LM APIs at temperature 0; no special hardware required beyond API access. Code is released. - Efficient: Each episode can run up to B=3×|O| steps (budget proportional to map size); at 8×8 grids this is ~192 steps per episode, meaning each model evaluation requires thousands of API calls — modest cost for research but non-trivial at scale. - Effective: Exploration error (R²=0.947 vs. success) provides a useful diagnostic; exploitation error (R²=0.006) does not meaningfully predict success and its utility as a standalone metric is undemonstrated. - Scalable: Environments are small (up to 8×8 grid, 4–8 task DAG nodes); the paper does not test whether findings hold at larger environment scales, richer task structures, or multi-agent settings, and makes no scalability claims beyond the tested configurations. The work delivers a software artifact (code + evaluation framework) and a new benchmark, but not a trained model or reusable ML component. Strengths: - The policy-agnostic framing is a genuine contribution: the metric requires only observed action trajectories and map state, enabling fair cross-model comparison without assuming any particular internal strategy. - The graph-theoretic grounding (Whitney, Tarjan, Edmonds, Panaite-Pelc) gives the stale score principled motivation rather than ad hoc threshold setting; the edge/node budget of 2 is formally justified, not just tuned. - The harness engineering result is practically impactful: a rule-based memory summary (no retraining, no finetuning) raises Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite success by 37 percentage points, demonstrating that the metric identifies actionable failure modes. - The qualitative behavioral separation (Claude Opus 4.6 vs. Gemini 3.1 Pro both at 100% success but with different exploration fractions over episode progress) demonstrates that success rate alone masks meaningfully different agent strategies, supporting the metric's diagnostic value. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge three limitations: (1) symbolic abstraction removes semantic information that real agents are expected to use, making the benchmark a complement to rather than replacement for semantic evaluation; (2) the normalized error metrics are trajectory-dependent, meaning per-model values are behavioral summaries rather than fully comparable standalone measures; (3) per-run variance is high — different runs of the same model can produce substantially different trajectories and aggregated error values, and only 3 seeds are used. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is creditable, and they correctly frame the benchmark as a controlled test suite for raw exploration/exploitation capability rather than a complete real-world proxy. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The exploitation error metric's near-zero predictive power (R²=0.006) is the paper's central unresolved tension: it is introduced as a co-equal diagnostic to exploration error, but the experiments show it predicts nothing about task success. The authors attribute this to the environment's structure (must explore first before exploitation matters), but this means exploitation errors as measured are not task-relevant in the current environment design — undermining half the paper's contribution. - With 3 seeds and no significance tests, the fine-grained claims in Findings 2 and 5 (e.g., that Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro have meaningfully different strategies at similar success rates, or that semantic information affects GPT-4.1 and Gemini Flash Lite in qualitatively opposite ways) are suggestive but not statistically established. - The harness engineering ablation covers only GPT-4.1 and Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite; it is unclear whether the ~37 percentage point gain generalizes to top-performing models or collapses for weaker ones. - Maps are at most 8×8 with 4–8 task nodes — whether the metric remains discriminative and the findings replicate at larger scales is untested. - The four-case taxonomy hardcodes the assumption that Case 4 (exploration or exploitation both valid) incurs errors attributed to both simultaneously; an ablation varying this attribution rule is absent. Bottom Line: The exploration error metric is a real and useful diagnostic — the R²=0.947 correlation is striking and the harness engineering result is actionable — but the exploitation error half of the framework is not yet delivering, and practitioners should treat this as a strong exploration benchmark with a placeholder for exploitation until that metric's design is revisited.
Recommended 2604.13151 PDF
#37 — Innovative (74)

Nemotron 3 Super: Open, Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Hybrid Mamba-Transformer Model for Agentic Reasoning (17 ▲ )

Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (90) dominates: full open release of base, post-trained, FP8, and NVFP4 checkpoints plus training code, evaluation harness, and documented 2.2–7.5× throughput gains on B200 — a deployable artifact at frontier scale. Novelty (70) — LatentMoE, shared-weight MTP, SSM stochastic rounding, and stable 25T-token NVFP4 pretraining are real architectural and empirical contributions, though several pieces are evolutionary. Rigor (55) is the weak point — no component ablations (deferred to companion paper), inconsistent baseline precision (BF16 Qwen vs MXFP4 GPT-OSS), no error bars, weak base-model comparisons. Non-Obviousness (65) — SSM stochastic rounding insight is sharp but most other choices are predictable. Completeness (55) — SWE-Bench and Arena-Hard-V2 gaps go unanalyzed; teacher-distillation contamination unquantified. Resilience (55) — throughput claim and accuracy parity wobble under fair-baseline scrutiny. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*90 = 67.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 90
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Nemotron 3 Super, a 120B-total/12B-active-parameter open hybrid Mamba-Attention MoE model that combines LatentMoE, shared-weight Multi-Token Prediction, and NVFP4 pretraining to deliver 2.2–7.5× higher inference throughput than same-scale dense and MoE competitors at comparable benchmark accuracy. - Why it's important: Agentic AI workloads (coding agents, long-context reasoning, multi-turn tool use) are severely bottlenecked by the per-token inference cost of 100B-scale models; a model that matches frontier accuracy at a fraction of the serving cost fundamentally changes deployment economics. - How it does it: LatentMoE projects tokens into a low-dimensional latent space before expert routing, freeing up the bandwidth budget to increase both the total number of experts and the top-K active count at constant inference cost, then shared-weight MTP heads enable native speculative decoding without an external draft model. - The good: The NVFP4-quantized checkpoint achieves 99.8% median accuracy relative to the BF16 baseline across a broad suite of benchmarks, while completing quantization in under 2 hours on a single 8-GPU B200 node — a practical result for production deployment. - The missing: The paper does not isolate the accuracy contribution of LatentMoE from MTP from NVFP4 pretraining via ablation; it defers to a companion LatentMoE technical report, leaving the reader unable to attribute observed gains to specific design choices. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML engineers deploying 100B-scale models on Blackwell/Hopper hardware and for researchers working on MoE architecture, speculative decoding, or low-precision LLM training. Problem & Motivation: Serving 100B-parameter-class models for long-horizon agentic tasks — code generation, multi-turn tool use, 1M-token document reasoning — is prohibitively expensive per token. Pure dense transformers have quadratic KV-cache memory growth; existing MoE designs optimize accuracy per FLOP but neglect latency, memory bandwidth, and all-to-all communication costs that dominate online serving. Hybrid Mamba-Attention architectures reduce KV-cache pressure but lack the parameter capacity of MoE at constant compute. Nemotron 3 Super targets the intersection: high accuracy per deployed byte, 1M-context support, and quantized throughput competitive with purpose-built inference models. Key Idea: LatentMoE is the central architectural insight: rather than routing tokens in the full hidden dimension d, a learned down-projection compresses each token to a latent dimension ℓ < d before expert dispatch and computation; expert outputs are projected back to d via a shared up-projection. This reduces all-to-all communication and per-expert memory bandwidth by factor d/ℓ. Crucially, those savings are reinvested: total experts scale from N to N·(d/ℓ) and top-K active experts from K to K·(d/ℓ), holding inference cost approximately constant while exponentially expanding the expert combination space and improving accuracy per parameter and per FLOP. Methodology: Pretraining uses 25 trillion tokens across a two-phase curriculum (80% diversity-focused, 20% high-quality annealing) with NVFP4 precision throughout — the first public model to demonstrate stable 25T-token NVFP4 pretraining. The 88-layer hybrid stack interleaves Mamba-2 blocks (linear-time sequence modeling, constant state size) with LatentMoE layers and periodic global self-attention "anchors" (GQA, 32 Q-heads, 2 KV-heads) for full-token dependency capture. Two shared-weight MTP heads are trained jointly with the next-token objective (scaled auxiliary loss, 0.3 factor); weight-sharing across heads exposes the head to multiple prediction horizons, regularizing against the training-inference mismatch that arises when a fixed-offset head is applied autoregressively at inference. Post-training: a two-stage SFT (token-level then sample-level loss normalization, 7M samples / 800B tokens) followed by four RL stages — multi-environment RLVR across 21 environments and 37 datasets using asynchronous GRPO with in-flight weight updates; SWE-RL for end-to-end GitHub issue resolution; RLHF using a principle-following GenRM; and MTP healing (frozen backbone, re-train MTP heads). PivotRL is applied for agentic domains: it reuses offline SFT expert trajectories and trains only on high-uncertainty "pivot" turns rather than full rollouts, trading on-policy fidelity for cost efficiency. Inference quantization uses post-training quantization (PTQ) at FP8 (W8A8) for Hopper and NVFP4 (W4A4) for Blackwell, with AutoQuantize NAS selecting per-operator precision via a second-order Hessian sensitivity proxy subject to a FLOP budget constraint. SSM cache (Mamba state) is stored in FP16 with stochastic rounding (Philox<5> PRG) rather than round-to-nearest-even, preventing coherent bias accumulation in recurrent decoding. Key Results: Base model (120B-A12B, BF16): MMLU 86.01, MMLU-Pro 75.65, GPQA-Diamond 60.00, MATH 84.84, MATH Level 5 70.00, AIME 2024 pass@32 53.33 — all substantially above Ling-flash-Base-2.0 and GLM-4.5-Air-Base across every category; RULER 1M 71.00 (competitors not tested). Post-trained model vs. Qwen3.5-122B-A10B and GPT-OSS-120B: HMMT Feb25 (with tools) 94.73 vs 89.55 vs N/A; RULER 1M 91.64 vs 91.33 vs 22.30 (decisive long-context win over GPT-OSS-120B); SWE-Bench OpenHands 60.47 vs 66.40 vs N/A (notable underperformance); TauBench V2 average 61.15 vs 74.53 vs 61.0 (conversational tool use gap vs Qwen); Arena-Hard-V2 73.88 vs 75.15 vs 90.26 (substantial gap vs GPT-OSS-120B, unremarked by authors). Throughput on B200 GPUs at 8k input / 64k output tokens: 2.2× over GPT-OSS-120B (vs MXFP4 weights / MXFP8 activations) and 7.5× over Qwen3.5-122B (vs BF16). NVFP4 checkpoint retains 99.8% median accuracy vs BF16; FP8 checkpoint similarly lossless. MTP average acceptance length 3.45 on SPEED-Bench (draft length 7), above DeepSeek-R1 (2.70) and competitive with Qwen3-Next (3.33). SSM cache stochastic rounding eliminates the up-to-40% verbosity increase observed with naive FP16 casting. Checkpoint merging during stable LR phase yields consistent 2–4-point improvements over trained checkpoints on 12-benchmark average, saving ~16% total pretraining FLOPs otherwise spent on dedicated evaluation decay runs. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (primary) + NEW METHOD (LatentMoE, shared-weight MTP, SSM stochastic rounding, AutoQuantize NAS, PivotRL) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (NVFP4 stable pretraining at 25T tokens; SSM cache quantization verbosity pathology and fix). LatentMoE and AutoQuantize are genuinely novel architectural and optimization contributions; PivotRL is novel in how it identifies and trains on pivot turns; the SSM cache stochastic rounding insight is a non-obvious empirical finding with a principled justification. The hybrid Mamba-Attention MoE pattern itself and the multi-environment RLVR recipe are evolutionary extensions of prior Nemotron work. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolating individual component contributions (LatentMoE vs MTP vs NVFP4 vs PivotRL) appear in this document — the LatentMoE analysis is deferred to a companion paper (Elango et al., 2026). The base-model evaluation suite (Table 4) compares only against two baselines (Ling-flash-Base-2.0, GLM-4.5-Air-Base); stronger recent bases (Qwen3-235B-A22B-Base, DeepSeek-V3 base) are absent. Post-trained evaluation uses multiple harnesses (OpenHands, OpenCode, Codex), which complicates cross-model comparisons. Throughput comparison uses BF16 Qwen3.5-122B as the Qwen baseline — an unfavorable precision for Qwen — making the 7.5× claim potentially overstated for a fair apples-to-apples comparison. No error bars or multi-seed evaluations are reported. Code (Nemo Evaluator SDK, NeMo Gym, NeMo RL) and all checkpoints (base, SFT, FP8, NVFP4) are open-sourced on HuggingFace and GitHub. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (built model released for deployment, evaluated by inference throughput and benchmark accuracy). - Practical: Runs on commercially available B200 and H100/H200 GPUs via vLLM and TRT-LLM; FP8 targets Hopper, NVFP4 targets Blackwell — available hardware for well-resourced deployments but not commodity. - Efficient: FP8 and NVFP4 PTQ completes in under 2 hours on a single 8-GPU B200 node; inference achieves 2.2× throughput vs GPT-OSS-120B and 7.5× vs Qwen3.5-122B on B200 at the tested sequence configuration; MTP with draft depth 3 substantially shifts the TPS-latency Pareto frontier. - Effective: The model matches or exceeds GPT-OSS-120B on most benchmarks and is competitive with Qwen3.5-122B on reasoning and long-context tasks; it lags on SWE-Bench (59–60% vs 66–67%) and TauBench conversational tool use (61.15 vs 74.53) — the domains most central to its agentic positioning. - Scalable: RULER 1M at 91.64 demonstrates the 1M-context claim holds under the RULER benchmark; inference was tested at 8k/64k sequence configurations; training scaled to 1K GPUs with documented resiliency engineering; claims of 10× or 100× scale are not tested. The work delivers fully open software artifacts: base, post-trained, FP8, and NVFP4 model checkpoints; training data; and the evaluation framework. Strengths: - LatentMoE is a principled hardware-software co-designed MoE architecture with a clear theoretical motivation (accuracy per byte, not just per FLOP) and measurable throughput outcomes. - Shared-weight MTP heads solve a real training-inference mismatch in speculative decoding with no parameter overhead — the acceptance-length advantage over DeepSeek-R1 at long draft indices is the direct payoff. - The SSM cache stochastic rounding finding is a subtle and non-obvious result: the recurrent nature of Mamba decoding causes quantization bias to accumulate coherently, and SR (being unbiased in expectation) breaks this accumulation — backed by analytical unrolling of the recurrence and empirical validation. - Full open release (checkpoints, training code, data, evaluation harness) at a scale where most commercial labs release only weights, enabling genuine external validation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Several benchmarks (SWE-Bench Multilingual, BrowseComp with Search, Terminal Bench Core 2.0) lack open-source evaluation harnesses and use internal scaffolding planned for future release. The PivotRL method is described briefly with a "manuscript coming soon" note, deferring full technical exposition. The LatentMoE architecture analysis is explicitly deferred to Elango et al. (2026). The zero-valued weight gradient phenomenon from NVFP4 training is documented (7% of parameters by end of pretraining) but its long-term effect on model quality is acknowledged as incompletely understood. Authors also note that the 7.5× throughput comparison uses BF16 Qwen weights, which is a favorable baseline for Nemotron. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The SWE-Bench underperformance (59–60% vs Qwen's 66–67%) is not analyzed despite the paper's heavy emphasis on agentic capabilities and the dedicated SWE-RL training stage — the gap may reflect harness differences but this is not demonstrated. Arena-Hard-V2 73.88 vs GPT-OSS-120B 90.26 is a ~16-point gap in chat quality that goes unaddressed. The throughput comparison baseline selection is inconsistent: GPT-OSS-120B uses MXFP4 weights / MXFP8 activations, Qwen3.5-122B uses BF16 — there is no single fair comparison point. The post-training data pipeline relies heavily on teacher models (Qwen3-235B-A22B, GPT-OSS-120B, DeepSeek-V3) to generate synthetic training data; the degree to which Nemotron 3 Super is distilling these models rather than independently learning is not quantified or discussed. PivotRL's contribution vs full end-to-end RL is claimed but not ablated. The checkpoint merging analysis (Section 2.5) honestly concludes merging offers no benefit at long annealing horizons, but the final base checkpoint used for alignment is a 500B merge — the justification for this specific choice is not made explicit. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying 100B-scale models on Blackwell hardware for agentic tasks will find this paper and its open artifacts immediately actionable, particularly for the throughput gains and the SSM quantization analysis; researchers should treat the accuracy competitiveness claims relative to Qwen3.5-122B on agentic benchmarks with caution given the notable gaps in SWE-Bench and conversational tool use.
Recommended 2604.12374 PDF
#38 — Innovative (74)

OccuBench: Evaluating AI Agents on Real-World Professional Tasks via Language World Models (43 ▲ )

Xiaomeng Hu, Yinger Zhang, Fei Huang, Jianhong Tu, Yang Su, Lianghao Deng, Yuxuan Liu, Yantao Liu, Dayiheng Liu, Tsung-Yi Ho · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (80) — LES reframes infrastructure as configuration, opening evaluation in domains with no APIs; implicit-fault taxonomy is genuinely new. Non-Obv (80) — strong-agent-≠-strong-simulator finding and E2>E1 hardness inversion are counter-intuitive and well-evidenced. Rigor (60) — solid ablations and a self-critical cross-simulator analysis, but entire benchmark synthesized by one model (Gemini-Flash) with no human-expert validation in safety-critical domains, no error bars, and ~3.8 instances/scenario. Completeness (75) — authors confront simulator-fidelity and simulator-dependence directly. Resilience (70) — known LES bias is the central exposure but openly disclosed. Impact (75) — public artifact with practically useful occupational/robustness profiling. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*60+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*75 = 72.5 ≈ 73; rounded up for the dual conceptual+empirical novelty to 74.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OccuBench, a benchmark of 382 solvable task instances spanning 100 professional scenarios across 10 industry categories and 65 specialized domains, where domain-specific environments are simulated by LLMs rather than deployed as real software infrastructure. - Why it's important: The professional domains where AI agents would deliver the highest value — nuclear safety, emergency triage, customs processing, greenhouse control — have no public APIs or testbeds, leaving agent capability in these domains entirely unmeasured. - How it does it: A Language Environment Simulator (LES) uses a configured LLM as a stateful tool-response generator, replacing real environment infrastructure with an LLM that has internalized domain operational logic, enabling benchmark creation for any domain an LLM can understand. - The good: The finding that implicit faults (silent data degradation, average 53.4% completion) are harder than both explicit faults (62.6%) and clean environments (67.5%) is counter-intuitive, empirically robust across 9 models, and directly actionable for deployment decisions. - The missing: All evaluation instances are synthesized and verified using Gemini-3-Flash-Preview as the LES; tasks verified as solvable under one simulator can become unsolvable under another, and no human expert validation of task correctness or difficulty calibration is reported. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating tool-using agents for enterprise or professional settings should read this; the cross-industry capability profiling and robustness taxonomy are genuinely novel and the simulator quality analysis is a valuable methodological contribution. Problem & Motivation: AI agents are increasingly targeted at high-value occupational work — triaging emergency patients, auditing financial records, managing nuclear safety alerts, scheduling factory production — yet existing benchmarks (WebArena, OSWorld, SWE-bench, TAU-bench) are structurally limited to domains with publicly accessible web or API environments. The vast majority of professional occupational domains are bound to enterprise systems with no public interfaces. This creates an evaluation blind spot precisely where agent deployment stakes are highest: a model may score well on web browsing and code editing but fail entirely on logistics optimization or drug interaction screening, with no existing benchmark able to detect this gap. Key Idea: The core insight is that an LLM with sufficient domain knowledge can function as a stateful environment simulator — a Language Environment Simulator (LES) — that responds to tool calls with contextually appropriate structured JSON outputs, tracks state implicitly through its context window, and enforces domain-specific constraints without requiring any real software deployment. This reframes environment construction from an engineering problem (deploy and maintain a full web application or enterprise system) to a configuration problem (write a system prompt, tool schema, initial state, and state description). Any domain an LLM understands can become a benchmarkable environment. Methodology: Each LES is fully specified by a 4-tuple (system_prompt, tool_schema, initial_state, state_description), where the system prompt encodes behavioral rules and simulation logic, the tool schema defines 2–10 callable functions with typed parameters and example outputs (median 5), the initial state specifies the JSON starting conditions, and state description provides semantic annotations that guide causal consistency. A multi-agent synthesis pipeline powered by Gemini-3-Flash-Preview generates environment configurations, task instructions, solution plans, and rubric-based verification criteria. Sixteen non-overlapping sub-topics per scenario ground synthesis in professional reference documents, ensuring structural diversity. Tasks are executed multiple times with and without reference plans to verify solvability and calibrate difficulty; trivially easy (100% autonomous success) and unsolvable (0% success) instances are filtered out, yielding 382 instances with an average of 5.5 tools and 16.2 tool calls per task. Environmental robustness is evaluated via four conditions: E0 (clean), E1 (explicit faults: HTTP 500, TimeoutError, ConnectionRefused — clear error signals), E2 (implicit faults: truncated data, missing fields, stale values — no error signal), and E3 (mixed). Faults are transient, spaced across the interaction, and parameterized by fault_count (default 2) and fault_duration (default 2 consecutive calls). Evaluation uses Completion Rate (CR) as the fraction of 382 tasks where the agent trajectory passes rubric verification, and a Robustness Score R = min(CR_E1, CR_E2, CR_E3) / CR_E0. Key design decisions: (1) rubric-based automated verification rather than human grading — well-justified for scale but introduces verifier error as an unquantified confounder; (2) difficulty calibrated to maximize discriminative power by selecting lowest-autonomous-success difficulty level per task — reasonable but means difficulty is relative to the synthesis LLM's capability, not a fixed absolute scale; (3) all faults injected by appending rules to the LES system prompt at evaluation time — clean separation of synthesis and fault injection, but means the same LES may not honor fault rules consistently. Key Results: Across 15 frontier models on E0 (clean environments): GPT-5.2 leads with 79.6% average CR, followed by Gemini 3.1 Pro (72.3%) and Claude Opus 4.6 (71.5%). Open-source models Qwen 3.5 Plus (69.9%) and DeepSeek V3.2 (69.6%) outperform most Claude variants, challenging the assumption that closed-source models uniformly dominate. No single model dominates across all 10 industries: Gemini 3.1 Pro leads Education (84%) and Science (81%) but scores only 62% in Healthcare; Claude Opus 4.6 leads Transportation (77%) but scores only 53% in Commerce; GPT-5.2 leads Science (94%) but scores only 67% in Commerce. On robustness, Gemini 3.1 Pro and MiniMax M2.7 tie at R=0.87, while GPT-5.2 achieves only R=0.84 despite leading on E0. Average E2 performance (53.4%) falls 14.1 points below E0 (67.5%) and 9.2 points below E1 (62.6%), confirming implicit faults as the hardest fault type.
