Reasoning effort ablation shows GPT-5.2 improves monotonically from 54.7% (none) to 82.2% (xhigh), a 27.5-point gain; Claude Opus 4.6 gains 3.6 points from low to max effort. Cross-simulator analysis: GPT-5.2 as simulator collapses average agent performance to 29.3% vs. 67.9% under Gemini Flash; pairwise ranking agreement between Gemini Flash and Qwen 3.5 Plus is 85.7% (24/28 pairs), confirming that capable simulators produce reliable rankings. Industry difficulty ranges from Business & Enterprise (avg 70.1%) to Transportation & Logistics (avg 56.2%), with Transportation and Education as hardest. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET + NEW METHOD (LES-based evaluation framework). The Language Environment Simulator approach is genuinely novel as a scalable mechanism for professional-domain agent evaluation — prior work either deploys real environments (expensive, narrow) or uses static QA (no interactive decision-making). The cross-industry robustness evaluation taxonomy (E0–E3 with implicit vs. explicit fault distinction) is a new contribution to agent evaluation methodology. The finding that strong agents are not necessarily strong simulators is a non-obvious empirical result with direct practical implications for LES-based evaluation design. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for fault parameters (fault_count and fault_duration), reasoning effort levels, and model size within families. Simulator quality is rigorously analyzed with three failure mode case studies (state fabrication, entity omission, rule invention) and quantified via pairwise ranking agreement across three simulators. No error bars or significance tests are reported for completion rates; with 382 total instances and scenarios averaging ~4 instances each, many industry-level percentages are based on fewer than 40 data points, making fine-grained comparisons (e.g., 62% vs. 67%) potentially within noise. Code and data are linked (project page, GitHub, data repository mentioned on the title page) but the specific repo URLs are not printed in the paper text. The entire benchmark is synthesized using Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, meaning task quality is bounded by that model's domain knowledge, and no independent human expert validation of scenario realism or solution correctness is reported — a significant methodological concern for claimed professional-domain coverage. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (new benchmark evaluated by running 15 frontier models across 382 task instances). - Practical: Evaluation requires running frontier LLMs (both agent and LES), which requires API access to closed-source models; the benchmark infrastructure itself is accessible to any researcher with such access. - Efficient: Each task averages 16.2 tool calls, and evaluation runs 15 models × 382 instances × 4 fault conditions = ~23,000 agent trajectories; compute cost is substantial but not prohibitive for well-resourced labs. - Effective: The benchmark discriminates meaningfully between models (range 53.4–79.6% on E0) and between industries (range 56.2–70.1%), and the robustness dimension reveals ordering differences invisible in clean-environment evaluation — it does solve the stated problem of enabling cross-industry agent evaluation. - Scalable: The LES approach scales to any domain an LLM can model, which is the paper's central claim; however, whether simulation fidelity degrades for highly specialized domains (e.g., nuclear reactor physics, complex financial regulations) beyond the 65 covered is not tested. The paper delivers a software artifact (benchmark dataset, evaluation framework, code) per the project page. Strengths: - The LES formalization genuinely solves a real infrastructure problem: it enables evaluation in domains that cannot be benchmarked any other way, and the four-tuple configuration abstraction is clean and reproducible. - The implicit fault taxonomy (E2) is a substantive intellectual contribution: the counter-intuitive finding that silent data degradation (E2: 53.4%) is harder than overt errors (E1: 62.6%) and mixed faults (E3: 54.4%) directly informs deployment readiness assessment in a way no prior benchmark captures. - The cross-simulator quality analysis is unusually self-critical: demonstrating that GPT-5.2 (the top-performing agent) is the worst simulator, with three concrete failure mode case studies (state fabrication, entity omission, rule invention), gives practitioners actionable guidance on simulator selection. - The occupational capability profiling (radar chart showing each model's distinct industry strengths) is a practically useful output that challenges the common practice of selecting models purely on aggregate rankings. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront two core limitations: (1) simulation fidelity — LESs model domain logic rather than domain data, so precise numerical correctness (e.g., financial calculations to the cent) cannot be verified and LES-based evaluation should be complemented with real-environment testing; (2) simulator dependence — results are tied to the specific simulator used during synthesis, tasks verified as solvable under Gemini Flash may be unsolvable under a different LES, and researchers should verify simulator quality before drawing conclusions. Both limitations are stated clearly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Beyond what the authors acknowledge: (1) No human expert validation of scenario realism or solution correctness is reported — for domains like nuclear safety or emergency triage, a domain expert reviewing even a sample of scenarios would substantially strengthen the claim that OccuBench measures real occupational competence rather than LLM-domain-knowledge proxies; (2) The evaluation set of 382 instances across 100 scenarios averages ~3.8 instances per scenario, making per-scenario and per-industry statistics based on very small samples — statistical noise in individual domain results is unquantified; (3) All models are evaluated with thinking/reasoning mode on, which is not a controlled baseline — a model that benefits differentially from reasoning may be advantaged in ways unrelated to tool-use competence; (4) The synthesis pipeline uses Gemini-3-Flash-Preview as both the LES and the synthesizer, creating a potential systematic bias in task design toward that model's strengths; (5) Rubric-based automated verification accuracy (false pass/fail rate) is not independently evaluated, leaving open how much measured CR reflects task completion vs. rubric compliance. Bottom Line: OccuBench makes a genuine and necessary contribution to agent evaluation infrastructure — the LES approach is practically important, the robustness taxonomy is novel, and any team deploying agents in professional enterprise settings should consult both the industry capability profiles and the implicit fault findings before selecting a model.
Recommended 2604.10866 PDF
#39 — Innovative (73)

CodeTracer: Towards Traceable Agent States (29 ▲ )

Han Li, Yifan Yao, Letian Zhu, Rili Feng, Hongyi Ye, Jiaming Wang, Yancheng He, Pengyu Zou, Lehan Zhang, Xinping Lei, Haoyang Huang, Ken Deng, Ming Sun, Zhaoxiang Zhang, He Ye, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) is the dominant weighted dimension — public framework + 3,326-trajectory step-annotated benchmark + reflective replay producing real Pass@1 gains across five backbones. Rigor (65) solid via clean component ablation and κ=0.73 IAA, but author-only annotation, missing significance tests, and an unexplained ~48% F1 ceiling temper it. Novelty (70) — hierarchical explore/state-change tree is a genuine architectural contribution. Non-Obv (65) and Completeness (70) good; Resilience (65) limited by absent comparisons to prior trajectory-analysis methods. Weighted (systems 15/15/10/15/15/30): 0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*65+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.30*80 = 70.75 ≈ 71… rounding with strong release/empirical findings to 73.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CodeTracer, a tracing framework that converts heterogeneous code agent run directories into structured hierarchical trace trees and performs automated failure onset localization, together with CodeTraceBench, a step-level annotated benchmark of 3,326 filtered trajectories spanning 4 agent frameworks, 5 LLM backbones, and 5 software engineering benchmarks. - Why it's important: As code agents execute increasingly long multi-step workflows with parallel tool calls, end-to-end success/failure labels hide when and why agents go off track, blocking systematic diagnosis and targeted recovery from early mistakes. - How it does it: CodeTracer separates exploration steps (same state) from state-changing steps (new child state) in a hierarchical trace tree built by evolving format-adaptive extraction, then a diagnosis agent traverses the tree issuing structured evidence queries to pinpoint the earliest failure-responsible stage and return a compact evidence set. - The good: Reflective replay — injecting CodeTracer's localized diagnostic signals as a prefix hint into originally failed runs under matched token budgets — consistently improves Pass@1 across all five backbones (e.g., Claude-sonnet-4: 41.6% → 48.3%, GPT-5: 32.6% → 38.2%), demonstrating that the localization output is actionable, not just descriptive. - The missing: F1 scores plateau at 46–48% even for the best configuration, meaning the majority of gold failure-relevant steps are still missed or mis-attributed; no analysis explains this ceiling, and no ablation tests whether the annotation protocol itself is the bottleneck. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating code agents who want process-level rather than outcome-level diagnostics; less relevant to those focused on model training or prompt engineering. Problem & Motivation: Code agents that autonomously fix bugs, refactor repositories, or interact with terminal environments execute dozens to hundreds of sequential tool calls. When these runs fail, practitioners have no principled way to determine which step first sent the agent off course — the error cascade can span many subsequent steps before manifesting in a test failure. Current benchmarks report only end-to-end pass rates, collapsing entire trajectories to a binary label. Manual trajectory inspection does not scale: a single run can span 40+ steps with interleaved exploration and patching. The paper targets failure onset localization — identifying the earliest stage and specific steps causally responsible for downstream failure — as a prerequisite for targeted recovery and agent improvement. Key Idea: The core insight is that agent steps fall into two distinct roles: exploration steps that read the environment without changing state (and thus remain children of the current state node), and state-changing steps that modify the codebase or execution environment (and thus create a new child state). Organizing the flat step sequence into this hierarchical trace tree compresses navigation, makes causal chains explicit, and allows a diagnosis agent to navigate by state-tree traversal rather than exhaustive step scanning. This tree indexing accounts for the single largest performance jump in ablation (18.3 F1 points). Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Evolving extraction scans a run directory, discovers its layout, looks up a registry of existing format-specific parsers, synthesizes a new parser if none matches, and normalizes steps into typed records (action, observation, diff, verification outcome). Parser accumulation amortizes layout discovery cost across runs sharing the same framework format. (2) Tree indexing classifies each normalized step as explore or state-change, building a hierarchical tree where each node is annotated with intent and outcome summaries, giving the diagnosis agent a compressed navigable index. (3) Diagnosis traverses the tree using structured evidence queries guided by four scoring signals — verification regression, diff magnitude, backtrack frequency, and exploration-to-action ratio — and outputs the failure-responsible stage, error-relevant step IDs, and a compact evidence set. Evaluation uses macro-averaged step-level Precision, Recall, and F1 against gold annotations on CodeTraceBench. The benchmark is constructed from 7,936 raw trajectories filtered to 3,326 (removing timeouts, truncated traces, misconfigured Docker environments, and trivially correct short runs), with stage labels and failure-critical chain labels assigned by the authors using chain-based backward tracing from the final failing test output. A random 15% subset was double-annotated; inter-annotator agreement reached Cohen's κ = 0.73. The reflective replay experiment feeds the localization output as a prefix hint into a fresh agent run under the same token and iteration budget as the baseline. Key Results: On CodeTraceBench's full split, CodeTracer achieves macro F1 of 46.57% (Claude-sonnet-4), 48.02% (GPT-5), and 46.14% (DeepSeek-V3.2) at step-level localization. Bare LLM baselines achieve 16.22%, 18.78%, and 16.33% F1 respectively — a ~3× improvement. Mini-CodeTracer (diagnosis loop alone, without evolving extraction or tree indexing) reaches ~19–24% F1, confirming that structural components, not just prompting, drive the gains. Component ablation isolates tree indexing as the dominant contributor (+18.3 F1 points over Mini-CodeTracer) and evolving extraction as secondary (+9.4 F1 points). Token costs for CodeTracer are lower than for bare LLM on the same task (56.8k vs 105.1k for Claude-sonnet-4) because hierarchical navigation narrows the evidence candidate set. On the reflective replay experiment, all five backbones improve Pass@1 on originally failed runs: Claude 41.6% → 48.3%, GPT-5 32.6% → 38.2%, DeepSeek 29.3% → 32.6%, Qwen 20.2% → 23.9%, Kimi-2.5 21.3% → 26.9%. Empirical analysis of 3,326 trajectories yields four quantified findings: (i) success plateaus after ~40 iterations across all backbones; (ii) framework orchestration complexity (SWE-Agent, OpenHands) consumes 2× the tokens of MiniSWE-Agent for only 2.4–5.5 pp additional success; (iii) ineffective steps nearly double from solved (22%) to unsolved (40%) trajectories; (iv) error types shift predictably across workflow stages (environment/dependency errors early; mislocalized edits and incorrect hypotheses later). Benchmarks used include SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Pro, MultiSWE-bench, SWE-PolyBench, and TerminalBench. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The hierarchical state-transition trace tree applied to code agent trajectory diagnosis is a genuine architectural contribution; the empirical findings on orchestration overengineering, iteration saturation, and the evidence-to-action gap are new observations at scale across heterogeneous frameworks and backbones. The individual components (LLM-based evidence retrieval, reflective replay) are combinations of known techniques applied in a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: The ablation table (Table 4) cleanly isolates evolving extraction and tree indexing contributions. Inter-annotator agreement (κ = 0.73) is reported for a 15% random subset. Error bars and significance tests are absent — F1 differences of a few points between configurations are not tested for statistical significance. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/NJU-LINK/CodeTracer. The benchmark annotation is performed entirely by the authors of the paper (not crowd workers or external annotators), which introduces potential bias. The reflective replay experiment is evaluated on originally failed runs only, not on a held-out split, so there is a risk that the diagnostic prompt contains information that leaks task structure. Evaluation is offline under matched token budgets, isolating diagnostic quality from interactive adaptation. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: CodeTracer runs as a post-hoc analysis layer on top of any LLM backbone via standard API calls; no specialized hardware is required. - Efficient: The diagnosis pass consumes 5.2k–8.4k tokens per run (backbone-dependent), amortized against the run's full trajectory cost; evolving extraction is invoked once per unique framework format. - Effective: Localization F1 ~48% confirms the system identifies failure-relevant steps substantially better than unstructured prompting, and reflective replay translates this into Pass@1 gains of 3.3–6.7 pp on failed runs — though ~52% of gold evidence steps remain unrecovered. - Scalable: Tested on trajectories of 10–300 steps; no evaluation at longer horizons or repositories larger than those in SWE-bench/TerminalBench is reported, and the claim of linear complexity in stage count is stated but not stress-tested at scale. The work delivers a public software artifact (framework + benchmark dataset) at the cited GitHub repository. Strengths: - The hierarchical explore/state-change tree is a principled, reusable abstraction that cleanly separates navigation structure from diagnostic reasoning, and its 18.3-pt F1 contribution in ablation is concrete evidence of value. - CodeTraceBench is a large, heterogeneous, step-level annotated benchmark (3,326 trajectories × 5 benchmarks × 4 frameworks × 5 backbones) that fills a genuine gap: existing SE agent benchmarks provide only outcome labels. - The reflective replay experiment closes the loop from diagnosis to recovery with a controlled matched-budget design, converting a tracing result into a downstream performance gain. - The empirical findings on orchestration overengineering and iteration budget saturation are actionable and counter-narrative for a field that often assumes bigger frameworks and more steps are better. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state four limitations: (1) CodeTraceBench does not cover the full design space of SE agents or real-world repositories, so generalization to other frameworks, domains, or deployment settings is uncertain. (2) Annotation labels such as "incorrect," "unuseful," and "error critical" involve annotator interpretation, especially for long trajectories with intertwined exploration and state changes. (3) Tracing and replay results are evaluated offline under matched budgets on previously collected runs, isolating diagnostic quality from interactive human oversight or online intervention. (4) Reflective replay experiments do not establish a general training signal or guarantee robust improvement across all task categories and model families. The authors engage with these limitations directly and with appropriate scope, which is credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The F1 ceiling of ~48% is left unexplained — the paper does not analyze whether this ceiling is caused by annotation ambiguity (κ = 0.73 suggests ~27% inter-rater disagreement), by fundamental limits of the tree-navigation approach on highly non-linear trajectories, or by backbone reasoning capacity. All annotation is performed by the paper's own authors (15 annotators across tasks they assigned themselves), which is a potential source of confirmation bias not addressed by the double-annotation protocol. The reflective replay improvement (3–7 pp) is reported only as Pass@1 on originally failed runs without controlling for which tasks; if CodeTracer's diagnostic signals are more effective on easy-to-recover tasks than hard ones, the overall gain may overstate practical utility. Baseline comparisons in the localization task are limited to bare LLM prompting and Mini-CodeTracer; no comparison to any prior trajectory analysis method (e.g., Bouzenia and Pradel 2025, Kuang et al. 2025) is included in Table 3, making it impossible to benchmark against the closest prior work. The industrial agent analysis (Claude Code) is described qualitatively in the appendix without quantitative localization results, leaving an open question about whether the method transfers to production-grade agents. Bottom Line: CodeTracer delivers a concrete, reproducible improvement in code agent trajectory diagnosis with a public benchmark and tool, and the reflective replay results make it practically relevant to anyone operating code agents at scale — but the ~48% F1 ceiling, author-only annotation, and absent comparisons to prior trajectory analysis work mean the paper's claims about the method's limits and relative standing need further external validation.
Recommended 2604.11641 PDF
#40 — Innovative (72)

Environmental Footprint of GenAI Research: Insights from the Moshi Foundation Model

Marta López-Rauhut, Loic Landrieu, Mathieu Aubry, Anne-Laure Ligozat · arxiv.org · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (75) drive the score — the 4% final-training / 89%-from-13%-of-runs findings are genuinely surprising and policy-relevant, enabled by unprecedented access to 3,534 run logs from a production lab. Rigor (70) is moderate: standard LCA methodology is rigorously applied with full parameter disclosure, but no formal uncertainty propagation, no sensitivity analysis on hardware lifetime, and preprint status reduce confidence. Completeness (75) is helped by very direct limitation acknowledgment. Resilience (65) is the main drag: single-lab, single-model, unusually novel task (speech-to-speech) and low-carbon French grid — the headline ratios almost certainly don't generalize. Impact (70) reflects a methodology and dataset, not a tool. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents the first full-lifecycle environmental assessment of a multimodal LLM research project (Moshi, a 7B-parameter speech-text model), quantifying compute and environmental impacts across all 3,534 training runs from early experiments through final training using actual internal logs. - Why it's important: Industry environmental reporting for AI almost exclusively covers the final training run, systematically underreporting true costs; this work demonstrates that final training represents less than 4% of total compute, making conventional reporting misleading for policy, benchmarking, and sustainability decisions. - How it does it: Kyutai provided raw training logs enabling compute attribution by module, training phase, and research goal, which the authors combine with a life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology covering four impact indicators (primary energy, GWP, water consumption, abiotic depletion) across both operational and embodied hardware impacts. - The good: The 90/10 finding — 13% of runs account for 89% of total compute — and the quantification that design/hyperparameter tuning alone consumes 75% of the research budget, supported by granular log data unavailable in any prior work. - The missing: The study covers a single model at a single lab on a single infrastructure (Scaleway, France-based, low-carbon grid); the unusually novel speech-to-speech task inflates the research-to-final ratio, and the authors acknowledge no comparable data from other labs exist to validate generalizability. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone working on AI sustainability policy, LCA methodology for ML, or responsible reporting standards; skip if seeking an efficiency technique or architecture contribution. Problem & Motivation: The AI field lacks accurate environmental accounting because existing reports — whether from academic groups or industry — almost universally report only the final training run and ignore the full research and development process: failed experiments, debugging, hyperparameter searches, ablation studies, and early exploratory runs. This omission is not trivial: MLLM research involves hundreds to thousands of training runs, and the true environmental cost is systematically undercounted. Regulators, sustainability researchers, and lab leaders cannot make informed decisions based on final-training-only carbon numbers. The specific gap this paper closes is empirical: nobody had quantified what fraction of total compute actually goes to non-final-training activities in a real, large-scale research project. Key Idea: The core insight is that the ratio of final-training compute to total research compute is a direct function of the novelty of the approach: the more exploratory the problem (e.g., first-ever end-to-end speech-to-speech model), the lower this ratio. By exploiting unprecedented access to Kyutai's internal training logs, the authors can decompose total compute across goal (training/validation/evaluation), module (main model, LLM backbone, tokenizer, data generator), and phase (experimentation, pre-training, post-training, fine-tuning, failed, debugging, ablation), and then map compute to environmental impact via LCA. Methodology: The pipeline has two major stages. First, compute analysis: Kyutai logs from 3,534 training runs on an NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD (127 DGX H100 nodes, Scaleway Paris datacenter) are categorized by module and research phase. Compute is measured in GPU-hours, with the unit being duration × number of GPUs per run. Second, environmental assessment: operational impacts (primary energy, GWP, water consumption, abiotic depletion) are computed by multiplying measured energy consumption by location-specific intensity factors (carbon intensity from EMBER, water intensity from EWIF, fossil depletion from ADEME). Embodied impacts are estimated using the Boavizta and ADEME per-component impact factors for GPUs, CPUs, RAM, SSDs, PSUs, motherboards, and cases, allocated proportionally to compute duration relative to hardware lifetime (assumed 4 years at 60% utilization). Critical design decisions: (1) allocating embodied impacts proportionally to GPU-hours rather than peak node ownership — reasonable but sensitive to the assumed hardware lifetime; (2) excluding data acquisition, inference/deployment, and end-of-life impacts — justified by data unavailability but creates a known undercount; (3) treating idle compute nodes as allocated to other workloads — reduces attribution but is an assumption that benefits Moshi's reported impact. Key Results: Total research compute: 3.26M GPU-hours (372 GPU-years). Final training runs: ~14 GPU-years, or 3.7% of total. Compute breakdown: 75% design and hyperparameter tuning, 11% failed runs, 8% ablation studies, 3.7% final training, 2.4% debugging. Compute concentration: 13% of runs account for 89% of total compute; 42% of runs are low-intensity (under one GPU-day) but contribute negligibly. Final-to-total ratios by module: main model pre-training 4%, tokenizer 1%, LLM backbone 15.5%, data generator 10.6%–8.2%. Environmental totals for the full project: 68 TJ primary energy, 319 tonnes CO2eq GWP, 19 megaliters water, 8 kg antimony-equivalent abiotic depletion. Comparison: 319 tonnes CO2eq equals 132 Paris–San Francisco round trips or the annual footprint of 39 people in France. Location sensitivity: GWP ranges from 1.83×10^5 kgCO2eq (Sweden) to 3.20×10^6 (Poland), a 17× span, while water consumption is inversely correlated (Sweden uses more water due to hydropower cooling). Hardware component breakdown: GPUs dominate primary energy (39.1 TJ) and GWP (164.6 tonnes), but power supplies dominate abiotic depletion; RAM modules (×32 per node) are a substantial aggregate contributor to GWP via fluorinated gas emissions during manufacturing. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The paper's genuine novelty is empirical: for the first time, complete internal training logs of an industry-scale MLLM project are analyzed with LCA-grade methodology. The LCA techniques themselves are standard adaptations of ISO 14040/44 applied to AI (following Boavizta, MLCA, and prior work). The novelty is the data access and the resulting breakdown, not a new method. Rigor & Reproducibility: Methodology is fully specified in Appendix B with all equations and parameter values (Table 6–8), making results reproducible given the same compute logs. Uncertainty is discussed qualitatively but no formal error bars are propagated; the sensitivity of embodied impact estimates to hardware lifetime and utilization assumptions is acknowledged but not quantified. No ablation is needed (this is a measurement study). The authors explicitly acknowledge scope exclusions (inference, data processing, end-of-life) as limitations. The training logs from Kyutai are shared at the data level, though access modalities are not specified; no code repository URL is mentioned. The study is a preprint (arXiv:2604.11154v1, April 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurement study using existing infrastructure logs). - Practical: The methodology requires access to internal training logs — currently only feasible for labs that retain and share granular run-level data; the LCA tooling (Boavizta, CodeCarbon) is publicly available. - Efficient: The assessment itself is computationally trivial; the bottleneck is data collection and organizational transparency, not compute. - Effective: The study directly measures what it claims to measure (compute distribution and environmental impact of Moshi's full R&D), provided Kyutai's logs are complete and correctly labeled — a reasonable assumption given the collaboration but unverifiable by external parties. - Scalable: The methodology scales trivially to other models if log access is granted; generalizing the quantitative findings (e.g., the 4% final-training ratio) to other labs or model types is not supported without additional data. The work delivers a methodology and dataset, not a software artifact or trained model. Strengths: - Unprecedented empirical access: 3,534 training run logs from a production AI lab, enabling the first compute breakdown that separates debugging, failed runs, hyperparameter search, ablations, and final training — no prior work has achieved this granularity. - Multi-indicator LCA: quantifying primary energy, GWP, water, and abiotic depletion simultaneously reveals trade-offs invisible in carbon-only analyses (e.g., Sweden's low carbon but high water cost, Poland's opposite profile), directly actionable for datacenter siting decisions. - Concrete policy-relevant finding: the ratio between final training and total R&D compute varies 4× to 15× by module novelty, providing an evidence-based argument that final-training-only reporting is structurally misleading and must be augmented with R&D phase reporting. - The 90/10 compute concentration finding (13% of runs = 89% of compute) identifies the highest-leverage intervention point: better early-stopping, diagnostics, and monitoring for runs above the one GPU-month threshold. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly list: exclusion of data acquisition, processing, and storage; exclusion of inference/deployment costs post-release; omission of end-of-life hardware impacts (due to lack of reliable data); restriction of water consumption to operational use phase only (not embodied water); uncertainty in some Boavizta/ADEME impact factors; and the fact that Kyutai rented additional nodes at an unspecified location for three months, omitted due to lack of data. The authors confront these limitations directly, which strengthens credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The study's biggest unacknowledged limitation is external validity: Moshi is an unusually novel model (first end-to-end speech-to-speech system) at a newly formed lab, which the authors themselves suggest inflates the research-to-final ratio. The 4% figure is almost certainly not representative of, say, a GPT-series fine-tune or a standard LLM pre-training run at an established lab with years of prior experimentation. The paper lacks a quantitative sensitivity analysis for the hardware lifetime and utilization assumptions, which can shift embodied GWP substantially. The operational GWP (319 tonnes) is computed using France's 2024 carbon intensity — one of the lowest in the world — meaning this is likely a best-case GWP among common datacenter locations. The paper does not evaluate the incremental environmental cost of alternative research practices (e.g., proxy model scaling laws) as concrete baselines against which the observed overhead could be judged. Bottom Line: This is a high-value empirical contribution that any researcher or policymaker working on AI sustainability or responsible reporting must read, because it provides the first credible evidence base showing that final-training-only carbon disclosures undercount true AI research footprints by at least 25× for novel model types — but the single-case-study design limits immediate generalization and practitioners should treat the specific ratios as illustrative rather than universal.
Search Results 2604.11154 PDF
#41 — Innovative (72)

ParSCo: Performance-Driven Par titioning and S cheduling Co-optimization Framework for Processor-based Emulation

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (80) is strong: peer-reviewed TODAES publication, full ablation across four components, hyperparameter sweep, proofs for the O(1) TOB gain theorem, strong modern baselines (hMetis, KaHyPar + scheduling), and public benchmark dataset release. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) driven by the genuinely non-obvious closed-form TOB gain update enabling scheduling-aware FM refinement at million-node scale, plus decomposed gain storage resolving fixed-node instability. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) solid — end-to-end time step measured via industrial Sphinx rather than a proxy, ablation shows joint necessity of all components. Impact (65) moderated by no public code/binary release and 125× gap between 9.14M-node evaluation and billion-gate deployment target. Weighted composite ~72.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ParSCo, a co-optimization framework that jointly minimizes hypergraph cut size and topological order balancing (TOB) across all stages of processor-based emulation (PBE) compilation — coarsening, initial partitioning, refinement, and scheduling — rather than treating partitioning and scheduling as independent sequential stages. - Why it's important: Processor-based emulators are the primary tool for functional verification of billion-gate VLSI designs; the suboptimal emulation time step (instruction cycles) produced by existing two-stage pipelines directly inflates verification wall-clock time, a critical bottleneck in the chip design flow. - How it does it: It embeds a TOB metric (normalized variance of node counts per topological level across partition blocks) into a combined objective Φ = Σcut(e) + βK(σ²_τ), derives closed-form O(1) TOB gain update theorems that make scheduling-aware moves tractable at million-node scale, and adds a mobility-guided partitioning-aware static scheduling phase after the final partition level. - The good: On the Titan23 public benchmark (22 designs, up to 1.09M nodes), ParSCo achieves 22% time step reduction and 70% TOB improvement over the industry-standard list scheduling baseline, with 55% CPU time reduction — all metrics moving in the same direction simultaneously. - The missing: The largest tested design (NVDLA, 9.14M gates) is two orders of magnitude below the billion-gate rack capacity that motivates PBE; scalability at true deployment scale is claimed but not empirically demonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for EDA researchers working on hardware emulation compilation or scheduling-aware hypergraph partitioning; the O(1) TOB gain theorem has broader applicability to any balanced-order partitioning problem. Problem & Motivation Functional verification of modern VLSI designs at tens-of-billions of gates requires processor-based emulation (PBE), where a circuit netlist is compiled into a partitioned set of logic gates distributed across a hierarchical array of interconnected Boolean processors. The compilation flow has two sequential stages: hypergraph partitioning (minimize inter-processor cut) then scheduling (assign topological execution levels to minimize time steps — the number of instruction cycles per emulation cycle). The core problem is that standard min-cut partitioning ignores topological order balance (TOB), which determines scheduling quality. The motivating example (Figure 1) shows that a min-cut partition can require more time steps than a slightly worse cut with better TOB. No prior work has addressed joint DAH partitioning and scheduling under the full industrial PBE constraint set: fixed nodes (IP modules preassigned to specific processors), grouping constraints (closely-related gates co-located), multi-dimensional balance, scheduling constraints (I/O and BRAM nodes must be co-located with specific neighbors), and hierarchical multi-level emulator architecture (rack→cluster→board→processor). Key Idea The single core insight is that topological order balance (TOB) — how evenly nodes are distributed across execution levels within each partition block — must be a first-class objective in all partitioning stages, not delegated to post-hoc scheduling. The key enabling mechanism is Theorem 2 (with Corollary 1): when a node's topological order changes from level m to level n, the induced change in the TOB metric K(σ²_τ) depends only on |P^m| and |P^n| (the counts of nodes at those two levels), reducing gain calculation from O(k) to O(1). This makes mobility-guided, TOB-aware node moves tractable in FM-style refinement and scheduling at million-node scale. Methodology ParSCo operates as a two-stage pipeline: preprocessing followed by a co-optimization algorithm. Preprocessing: Parse the synthesized LUT-based netlist to a directed acyclic hypergraph (DAH), merge duplicate hyperedges, run ASAP and ALAP scheduling to compute each node's mobility (scheduling freedom = τ_l(v) − τ_s(v)). Co-optimization uses multi-level hypergraph partitioning with four TOB-aware components: 1. TOB-aware coarsening with multiple constraints: Nodes are rated by a connectivity score incorporating both cut weight and TOB. Before rating, scheduling constraints are enforced by pre-contracting I/O/BRAM nodes with their successors/predecessors; grouping constraints co-locate group members; fixed nodes are assigned to their predetermined blocks. Parallel net removal is deliberately omitted — experiments show the overhead exceeds any quality benefit above 2M nodes. 2. Global Growing Initial Partitioning with Fixed Nodes: Extends the Super-Far Root Growing method from the conference predecessor [3] to handle fixed nodes. The key change: rather than storing combined gain in a single priority queue (which becomes unstable under fixed nodes due to hyperparameter sensitivity), cut gains are stored in k priority queues and TOB gains in k separate arrays of size |V|. The algorithm assigns each node by maximizing TOB gain among the k candidates from the top of each cutsize queue. 3. TopoRefine algorithms: FM-style refinement with combined gain gain_r(v) = ζΔ₂(v) + Σω(e), where Δ₂(v) is the TOB gain and the sum is the cutsize gain. Two greedy strategies: boundary nodes refinement (marks only cut-edge-adjacent nodes as free, moves for maximum cutsize reduction) and local refinement (all nodes eligible, TOB updates propagated using Theorem 2 in O(1)). Contracted nodes from scheduling/grouping constraints are never uncoarsened, enforcing constraint satisfaction. 4. Partitioning-aware static scheduling: After final-level partitioning, each node's topological order is adjusted within its mobility range [τ_s, τ_l] to maximize gain_r. Nodes are prioritized by ascending mobility. Forward and backward propagation updates neighbors' scheduling ranges and gains; if a propagation violates constraints (mobility exhausted, scheduling constraints broken), it is canceled and the algorithm rolls back to the best intermediate solution. Legalization ensures all final topological orders are scheduling-constraint-compliant. The combined objective is Φ(H, Π_H) = Σ_{e∈E} cut(e) + βK(σ²_τ), with β = 1/5 set empirically. The TOB metric K(σ²_τ) = Σ_{τ=0}^{τ_max} (σ²_τ / |P^τ|²) aggregates normalized variance of node counts per topological order across all blocks. The three critical design decisions — decomposed gain storage, O(1) TOB gain updates, and omitting parallel net removal — are each supported by either formal proof or experimental ablation. Key Results Titan23 benchmark (22 public chip designs, 92K–1,089K nodes, 84K–1,089K nets; ε = 0.03 balance tolerance): - vs. list scheduling baseline: 22% time step reduction, 70% TOB reduction, 54% runtime reduction - vs. hMetis + partitioning-aware scheduling: 9% time step reduction, 15% TOB reduction - vs. KaHyPar + partitioning-aware scheduling: 13% time step reduction, 35% TOB reduction - Runtime: 1.72× speedup vs. SOTA two-stage approach; 55% CPU time acceleration - All 22 designs achieve zero scheduling-constraint violations Open-source chip benchmark (XiangShan1: 2.6M nodes, XiangShan2: 3.3M nodes, NVDLA: 9.14M nodes): - vs. KaHyPar + scheduling: 12% time step reduction, 74% TOB reduction, 56% runtime reduction, 43% memory reduction - Memory: ParSCo peaks at 8.58 GB for NVDLA vs. 12.45 GB for KaHyPar + scheduling - ParSCo shows better relative improvement on larger designs, consistent with claimed scalability trend vs. conference version [3] + plain scheduling: 15% average time step improvement across Titan23 Ablation (Figure 7): Full ParSCo improves 15% over TopoOrderPart+PS. Global Growing with Fixed Nodes contributes primarily to cut; TopoRefine and partitioning-aware scheduling each contribute to TOB and time step. No single component dominates; joint deployment is necessary. Baseline assessment: hMetis (1997–1999) is dated but remains the industry-standard hypergraph partitioner; KaHyPar (2016) is a strong modern baseline. Both are reasonable comparisons for the partitioning component. List scheduling [34] is the correct industrial scheduling baseline. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contributions are: (1) the first co-optimization framework addressing the full DAH partitioning+scheduling problem under PBE-specific industrial constraints; (2) the O(1) TOB gain calculation theorem enabling scheduling-awareness in FM-style refinement at scale; (3) the decomposed gain storage mechanism that stabilizes initial partitioning under fixed-node constraints. The underlying paradigms — multi-level hypergraph partitioning, FM refinement, ASAP/ALAP scheduling — are standard; the contribution lies in their integration and the algorithmic results that make it tractable. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies are present for all four components (Section 4.4, Figure 7), each component independently replaced by a plain alternative — this is rigorous. Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis covers β ∈ {1/10–4} and ζ ∈ {1–4} (Figure 9); the framework is stable in the middle range but degrades at extremes (β = 4, ζ = 4). No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported. hMetis is stochastic and is run 5 times (best result taken); ParSCo is deterministic. The generated Titan23 DAH benchmark dataset is publicly released [reference 1, Google Drive]. No source code is released. Time step evaluation requires an industrial simulation tool (Sphinx [34]) that is not publicly available, which limits full independent reproducibility. Fixed node (0.01% random) and grouping constraint (1% BRAM random) selections are artificial and may not represent real industrial constraint distributions. Published in ACM Transactions on Design Automation of Electronic Systems (peer-reviewed), April 2026; received July 2025, accepted December 2025. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (C++ implementation evaluated on benchmark suite). - Practical: Runs on a single Linux workstation (Intel Xeon Gold 6248R, 256 GB RAM); the largest test case (NVDLA, 9.14M nodes) uses 2.67 GB peak memory for ParSCo and completes in 2,056 seconds — commercially available hardware is sufficient at tested scale. - Efficient: ParSCo is 55% faster on Titan23 and 56% faster on open-source benchmarks versus the best two-stage competitor; coarsening dominates runtime at ~40%; the O(1) gain theorem is the critical enabler for this efficiency at million-node scale. - Effective: The evidence directly addresses the stated objective — time step reduction, not a proxy — measured via an industrial evaluation tool (Sphinx), achieving 22% reduction on Titan23; this is genuine end-to-end validation rather than a surrogate metric. - Scalable: Tested up to 9.14M nodes, with better relative performance on larger designs suggesting favorable scaling trends, but the billion-gate rack capacity (1.152B gates) that motivates PBE is untested; memory at 9.14M nodes is already 2.67 GB (ParSCo) to 8.58 GB, suggesting 10× scale could be challenging without algorithmic modification. The work delivers a compiled C++ binary; no library, CLI distribution, or public repository is described. Strengths - The O(1) TOB gain calculation (Theorem 2 + Corollary 1, with full proof) is a genuine algorithmic contribution — reducing an O(k) gain scan to a closed-form expression depending only on two partition counts is non-trivial and enables mobility-guided scheduling at scale. - Decomposed gain storage (separate cut priority queues and TOB arrays) cleanly resolves the fixed-node instability that limited the conference predecessor, and the root cause analysis (hyperparameter δ oscillation) is clearly identified. - Evaluation on real open-source processor designs (XiangShan RISC-V, first and second generation; NVDLA) up to 9.14M gates, with conversion from EDF to DAH via commercial tools, is substantially more realistic than synthetic benchmarks alone. - The ablation cleanly demonstrates that TOB-awareness in all three partitioning stages is jointly necessary — no single component produces a result close to the full framework, providing strong evidence for the co-optimization thesis. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors identify simultaneous (non-sequential) partitioning and scheduling as unexplored future work, implicitly acknowledging that the current approach is still sequential at the macro level. They acknowledge that removing parallel nets was skipped due to computational cost at large scale. Hyperparameter sensitivity at extreme values (β = 4, ζ = 4) is acknowledged. The authors do not directly acknowledge the scale gap between the tested designs and the billion-gate deployment scenario. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The largest tested design (9.14M gates) is ~125× smaller than the billion-gate rack-level designs that motivate PBE; the paper provides no analysis of how TOB variance, gain distribution, or convergence behavior would change at that scale — the scalability claim rests entirely on a favorable trend across three designs. - Fixed node (0.01% random) and grouping (1% random BRAM) selections may systematically differ from real industrial constraint distributions, where IP module preassignment is non-uniform and correlated with circuit topology; an adversarial or industry-representative constraint setting would strengthen the evaluation. - Time step measurement relies on the industrial Sphinx tool [34], which is not publicly available; independent verification of the primary metric is therefore impossible without institutional access. - The paper explicitly declines to compare against [41] (ParSGCN, a GNN-based approach bridging partitioning and scheduling) on grounds of different problem formulation; while the justification is reasonable, a partial upper-bound analysis or single-metric comparison would have been informative. - Memory usage grows from 2.67 GB at 9.14M nodes; extrapolating naively to 100M nodes suggests memory may become a practical constraint before billion-gate scale is reached, and the paper offers no mitigation strategy. Bottom Line Practitioners and researchers in EDA hardware emulation compilation will find directly applicable algorithmic contributions — particularly the O(1) TOB gain theorem and constraint-aware co-optimization architecture — but should treat the scalability claims with caution until validated on designs closer to the billion-gate regime.
Citations
#42 — Innovative (72)

LLM-PRISM: Characterizing Silent Data Corruption from Permanent GPU Faults in LLM Training

Abhishek Tyagi, Saurabh Hukerikar, Nirmal Saxena, Yanxiang Huang, Philip Shirvani, Chung-Hsuan Tung, Yuhao Zhu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72): new methodology combining RTL-grounded signatures with intermittent fault model and four-failure-mode taxonomy is meaningful, but builds on known fault-injection tradition. Rigor (70): 7,664 runs is strong statistical coverage, but no error bars/significance tests, no public code, preprint, circular use of commercial EDA tooling. Non-Obviousness (80): fault-phase dominance over fault rate and the gradual-drift/silent-degradation discovery are genuinely surprising findings that reframe monitoring. Completeness (72): limitations are directly acknowledged (GPT-2 scale, single dataset, sequence-parallel only). Resilience (70): honest about extrapolation gap to 405B-scale models. Impact (65): actionable format-selection guidance but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 = 71.9.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLM-PRISM, a methodology and empirical study that characterizes how permanent and intermittent GPU hardware faults propagate as Silent Data Corruption (SDC) through LLM pre-training, using 7,664 complete fault-injected training runs across FP16, BF16, and FP8 formats. - Why it's important: Production LLM training clusters at the scale of tens of thousands of GPUs suffer SDC events regularly (Meta reports 6 unplanned interruptions in 54 days; Google estimates one SDC per week during Gemini training), yet the cumulative effect of permanent faults on model quality and training stability was previously uncharacterized. - How it does it: RTL-level fault simulation on representative GPU functional units (FMA paths, tensor cores, register files, L1/L2 caches) extracts hardware-grounded error signatures that are replayed through a 7-parameter stochastic fault-injection engine embedded in Megatron-LM, sweeping fault rates, injection timing, fault location, and numeric format. - The good: The study identifies four qualitatively distinct failure modes (Spike-and-recover, Spike-and-degrade, Silent Degradation, Gradual Drift), demonstrates that all fault rates produce monotonically increasing weight divergence from the fault-free trajectory even when loss curves appear normal, and shows that fault phase (forward vs. backward) is a stronger predictor of outcome than NaN checking or fault rate alone. - The missing: All experiments use GPT-2 Small (124M) and GPT-2 Medium (355M) on WikiText, so claims about behavior at Llama-3-405B scale (16,000 H100 GPUs, weeks of training) are extrapolation beyond evidence; no tensor-parallel or pipeline-parallel configurations are evaluated. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML infrastructure engineers and hardware reliability researchers designing fault-tolerant training systems; practitioners using BF16 or FP16 at scale should read Section V and VI carefully for actionable format-selection guidance. Problem & Motivation: Large-scale LLM training runs spanning weeks on clusters of tens of thousands of GPUs are increasingly threatened by permanent hardware faults — manufacturing escapes, latent early-life defects, and silicon aging — that produce SDC: computations silently yield wrong numerical values without triggering error signals or program crashes. Unlike transient faults (cosmic ray bit-flips), permanent faults corrupt the same computational paths repeatedly across training steps, potentially accumulating into irreversible divergence from the intended optimization trajectory. Prior fault-resilience studies focused almost exclusively on transient faults and on inference-only or short fine-tuning horizons, leaving the behavior of persistent faults during full pre-training uncharacterized. The specific gap is that no prior work combines hardware-accurate fault models with end-to-end pre-training runs long enough to expose delayed, low-rate failure modes such as gradual drift and silent degradation. Key Idea: The core insight is that permanent GPU faults cannot be studied by injecting a single random bit-flip and observing recovery — instead, a physically grounded fault model must capture the structured, recurring nature of stuck-at defects (same bit positions, same spatial tile, same activating conditions across iterations) and sweep that model across the full fault-rate spectrum from rare intermittent (r ≈ 0.001) to continuous stuck-at (r = 1.0). This Bernoulli activation model, parameterized from RTL simulation of real GPU functional units, is what enables the identification of qualitatively different failure regimes that single-injection studies conflate as "training is resilient." Methodology: LLM-PRISM operates in four stages. Stage 1 extracts computation traces from transformer training workloads (GEMMs, attention, MLP). Stage 2 replays these traces through a commercial EDA tool targeting the GPU functional units most exposed during training — FMA paths in CUDA cores, tensor cores, register files, and L1/L2 SRAM arrays — under realistic toggle conditions, producing error signatures (affected bit positions, corruption magnitude, spatial pattern). Stage 3 samples stuck-at faults in proportion to silicon area of each unit. Stage 4 embeds a software fault-injection engine into Megatron-LM, parameterized by a 7-element tuple: (Rank, Layer, Training Phase [fwd_out / bwd_grad_inp / bwd_grad_wt], Fault Checkpoint, Error Rate r, Fault Density, Bit-Flip Profile). The error rate r ∈ (0,1] models the per-iteration Bernoulli probability that the defect produces a visible corruption, unifying permanent (r=1) and intermittent (r≪1) faults in one framework. Bit-flip profiles are drawn from RTL signatures rather than uniform random assumptions, anchoring the software model in hardware measurement. The objective measured is perplexity (PPL) on WikiText, classified as Unchanged (≤1% deviation from fault-free PPL=28.54), Changed (>1%), or Crashed. All three numeric formats — IEEE FP16, BF16, and FP8 — are swept, holding model architecture and dataset fixed. Critical design decisions: (1) single-fault-at-a-time assumption (justified by realistic fault rates making multi-fault interactions rare, acknowledged as a simplification); (2) GPT-2 scale traded for statistical coverage — 7,664 complete runs rather than a few runs on a large model (justified for characterization but limits extrapolation); (3) WikiText used as a single fixed dataset to eliminate dataset-induced variance as a confound. Key Results: Across all formats, 57–76% of fault-injected runs produce PPL within 1% of the fault-free baseline (28.54), but all fault rates produce monotonically increasing L2 weight divergence once injection begins — the loss curve hides what the parameter vector reveals. Four failure modes are identified: (1) Spike-and-recover: isolated NaN-triggered loss spikes followed by return to baseline trajectory, dominant at r ≤ 0.01; (2) Spike-and-degrade: NaN events so frequent that skip-and-halve recovery fails, loss diverges, prevalent in FP16 at mid-to-high rates; (3) Silent Degradation: no NaN events, loss settles stably above baseline (FP16 GPT-2 Small: 9.5% of runs); (4) Gradual Drift: no NaN events, loss visually indistinguishable from baseline yet PPL meaningfully elevated (FP16: 3.3% of runs). Format effects are pronounced: FP8 produces effectively binary outcomes (recover or crash, zero instances of Silent Degradation or Gradual Drift) because its narrow dynamic range saturates faults harmlessly or causes immediate divergence; FP16 shows the widest PPL distributions with 95th-percentile deviations up to ~600% above baseline among non-diverged runs. BF16 occupies an intermediate risk profile with crash rates rising to 26.9% at r=1 but non-crashing runs silently degrading up to ~400%. Fault phase dominates outcome: fwd_out and bwd_grad_inp faults produce ~60% crash rates at matched fault rates, while bwd_grad_wt faults are largely self-correcting (optimizer momentum smooths corrupted gradients; N-GPU averaging dilutes single-rank corruption by 1/N). Earlier fault injection (step 2571 vs. 7713) yields broader PPL distributions. The NaN check converts otherwise fatal trajectories to Spike-and-recover events for FP16 and BF16 but leaves extreme silent degradation (~600% PPL outliers in FP16) undetected. Downstream task evaluation on Children's Book Test and Winograd Schema Challenge confirms that Spike-and-recover and Gradual Drift models with near-baseline PPL still exhibit measurable accuracy drops, establishing that loss-curve monitoring is insufficient. Baselines are the same architecture trained without fault injection; no comparison against other fault-injection methodologies. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The combination of RTL-derived hardware-grounded error signatures with a probabilistic intermittent fault model embedded in a production training framework for full pre-training characterization is genuinely novel — prior work used idealized bit-flip models or restricted to inference/fine-tuning. The four-failure-mode taxonomy and the quantitative finding that fault phase outpredicts fault rate and NaN checking are new empirical findings with direct engineering implications. The Bernoulli intermittency model itself is a straightforward statistical device; the novelty lies in its grounding in RTL simulation and its application across 7,664 full training runs. Rigor & Reproducibility: The study runs 7,664 complete training runs, providing genuine statistical power for the sweep of fault rates, formats, and injection checkpoints — this is a substantial empirical investment. Ablations are implicit in the structured sweep design (fault rate × format × phase × checkpoint). No error bars or significance tests are reported for individual PPL comparisons; the 1% PPL threshold for "unchanged" is empirically motivated (natural run-to-run variance) but its choice is not validated with bootstrap or permutation tests. Downstream task results (Figure 7) are reported without confidence intervals, on a small model (GPT-2 Medium) that the authors acknowledge has limited task-solving capacity. Code and the modified Megatron-LM framework are not stated to be publicly released; no repository URL is mentioned. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2604.10390v1, 12 Apr 2026) and has not undergone peer review. The RTL fault characterization used a commercial EDA tool, so exact reproducibility depends on access to NVIDIA's internal toolchain. The single-fault-at-a-time assumption is explicitly stated and justified. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems + measurement study). - Practical: The fault injection framework runs on 16× H100 GPUs via modified Megatron-LM; the methodology is implementable on any GPU cluster running Megatron-LM, though RTL characterization requires EDA tool access (likely NVIDIA-internal). - Efficient: The study consumed an estimated ≥11,500 GPU-hours across 7,664 runs; the framework adds negligible per-step overhead (software-level injection), but the characterization campaign itself is expensive to replicate at similar statistical coverage. - Effective: The study convincingly demonstrates that permanent faults induce qualitatively distinct, format-dependent failure modes including silent ones undetectable by NaN checking, directly answering the stated research questions. - Scalable: Results are from GPT-2 (124M–355M parameters); the authors explicitly acknowledge that behavior at Llama-3-405B scale is unknown and left to future work — scalability to modern foundation model training is a stated open question, not a demonstrated result. The work delivers a modified training framework (Megatron-LM with fault injection) but this artifact is not publicly released; the primary deliverable is the empirical characterization itself. Strengths: - Hardware-grounded fault modeling: deriving bit-flip profiles from RTL simulation of actual GPU functional units rather than assuming uniform random bit-flips is a methodologically important distinction that anchors conclusions in physical reality. - Scale of empirical coverage: 7,664 complete pre-training runs is an unusually large experimental campaign for this domain, enabling fine-grained characterization of tail behavior (silent degradation, gradual drift) that short-horizon studies cannot observe. - Discovery of the fault-phase dominance effect: the finding that bwd_grad_wt faults are self-correcting while fwd_out and bwd_grad_inp faults are catastrophic, and that this effect is format-agnostic, provides actionable guidance for fault-tolerant training system design. - Weight divergence as a ground-truth signal: using L2 distance between faulty and fault-free parameter vectors as a complement to loss curves reveals that all fault rates corrupt model state even when PPL appears normal — this methodological contribution has implications for monitoring design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) evaluation on GPT-2 Small and Medium only, which may not generalize to massive foundation models deployed in production; (2) fidelity of software-level injection depends on accuracy of RTL fault models — arbitrary bit-flip patterns would yield misleading results; (3) only sequence parallelism is evaluated, leaving tensor-parallel and pipeline-parallel fault propagation dynamics uncharacterized. The authors frame these as intentional trade-offs enabling statistical rigor, which is credible and appropriately direct. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Single dataset: all experiments use WikiText, which is a relatively simple, domain-homogeneous corpus; whether fault sensitivity differs for instruction-tuning data, code, or multilingual corpora is untested. (2) Single architecture family: GPT-2 uses absolute positional embeddings and pre-LayerNorm; modern architectures with RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU, and different normalization placements may exhibit different fault propagation characteristics through their computational graphs. (3) The downstream evaluation uses only CBT and Winograd — both are relatively simple tasks; harder reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MMLU) where model quality differences would be more discriminative are absent. (4) No ablation on the choice of r distribution (the unimodal PDF centered at low r) — since this governs which fault rates receive most experimental weight, sensitivity of conclusions to this prior is not assessed. (5) The NaN check is the only runtime monitoring mechanism evaluated; gradient norm monitoring, loss smoothing detectors, or weight snapshot comparison are not assessed as alternatives or complements. Bottom Line: Any practitioner responsible for training reliability at GPU-cluster scale should read this paper — it provides the first hardware-grounded, statistically rigorous evidence that permanent GPU faults produce format-dependent failure modes that are invisible to loss-curve monitoring, and delivers concrete guidance on format selection (FP8 for binary fault outcomes, avoid FP16 at high fault rates) and monitoring gaps (NaN checking is necessary but insufficient).
Citations 2604.10390 PDF
#43 — Innovative (72)

Reasoning Resides in Layers: Restoring Temporal Reasoning in Video-Language Models with Layer-Selective Merging

Zihang Fu, Haonan Wang, Jian Kang, Kenji Kawaguchi, Jiaying Wu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) strong — interventional masking provides causal evidence beyond correlation, RANDOM-K variance reported, generalization to four held-out benchmarks. Novelty (65) solid — specific CMA-ES + layer-selective merging + TR/TP tradeoff formulation. Non-obviousness (75) — the layer-sparsity hypothesis with causal validation is genuinely non-obvious. Completeness (75) — authors acknowledge three limitations honestly. Resilience (70) holds up. Impact (60) — delivers weights but no text-only TR benchmark confirmation, no library release. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MERIT, a training-free, task-driven model merging framework that selectively interpolates self-attention layers between a video-language model (VLM) and its paired text-only LLM backbone to restore temporal reasoning degraded by multimodal adaptation. - Why it's important: Multimodal adaptation of LLMs systematically erodes temporal reasoning capabilities that practitioners rely on for video understanding applications such as causal inference, event ordering, and multi-step deduction over sequential visual evidence. - How it does it: CMA-ES evolutionary search optimizes a per-layer binary gating vector over the self-attention parameters of the VLM versus backbone, with the objective maximizing temporal reasoning accuracy while penalizing temporal perception degradation. - The good: MERIT achieves up to +23.9% temporal reasoning gain on Video-MME for LongVA-7B without retraining, generalizes beyond the 232-sample search set to four independent benchmarks, and is backed by interventional layer-masking evidence confirming that the discovered layers are causally critical for reasoning. - The missing: The search signal itself comes from benchmark-defined evaluation, so discovered recipes may implicitly overfit to benchmark conventions; no adversarial or out-of-distribution temporal reasoning evaluation probes whether the recovery is genuine generalization or benchmark alignment. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners working on VLM capability recovery or model merging for multimodal systems should read this; it is less useful for those seeking architectural or training-based solutions. Problem & Motivation: VLMs built by attaching a visual encoder to a pretrained LLM routinely lose the temporal reasoning (TR) capacity of the base language model during multimodal alignment, even though the visual encoder may correctly perceive relevant objects and events. This is not purely a perceptual failure: the adapted backbone fails on text-only temporal logic questions that the unadapted LLM solves correctly, and separately fails to infer causal-temporal structure even when visual perception succeeds. Existing remedies require either expensive retraining (DPO, instruction tuning) or resort to coarse full-model merging that uniformly averages all layers, which unreliably recovers reasoning and often degrades visual perception. The gap motivating MERIT is the absence of a method that recovers reasoning precisely, without retraining, and without sacrificing perceptual competence. Key Idea: Temporal reasoning in VLMs depends disproportionately on a sparse, non-contiguous subset of self-attention layers rather than being uniformly distributed across the backbone. If those layers can be identified by optimizing an explicit TR-versus-TP tradeoff objective, selectively interpolating only those layers toward the text-only backbone restores reasoning more effectively than full-model merging while minimizing interference with perception. Methodology: MERIT defines a family of merging recipes parameterized by a continuous gating vector g ∈ [0,1]^L (one scalar per self-attention layer), thresholded at 0.5 to produce a discrete binary recipe. For each selected layer ℓ, self-attention parameters are blended via directional interpolation: θ_ℓ = α·θ_ℓ^M + (1−α)·θ_ℓ^N when VLM-dominant, or (1−α)·θ_ℓ^M + α·θ_ℓ^N when LLM-dominant, with α ∈ {1.0, 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6, 0.5}. The objective is F(g) = Acc_TR(g) − λ·D_TP(g), where D_TP penalizes any drop in temporal perception below the base VLM's TP accuracy. CMA-ES searches over this space, capping evaluations at 1,600 per α value, with evaluation cached for identical discrete recipes. Three critical design decisions: (1) restricting merging to self-attention parameters only (leaving FFN layers untouched) — justified by the hypothesis that reasoning is encoded there; (2) using CMA-ES rather than gradient-based search because the objective is non-differentiable (accuracy on discrete QA); (3) searching on a small proxy set (55 TP + 177 TR examples from Video-MME) and relying on generalization, rather than full benchmark optimization. The merged model is then fixed; no retraining occurs. Key Results: On Video-MME, MERIT achieves temporal reasoning accuracy of 49.7% (+23.9% relative vs. 40.1% base) for LongVA-7B, 57.6% (+10.8%) for InternVL3-8B, and 46.9% (+3.8%) for Qwen3-VL-4B, while temporal perception either holds or improves. Uniform full-model merging (ALL-LAYER) and RANDOM-K consistently underperform MERIT on TR; RANDOM-K shows high variance (±1.7–2.9 pp std on TR), confirming that layer identity, not just count, drives recovery. The discovered recipes generalize to four benchmarks (LongVideoBench, LVBench, MMBench-Video, Video-Holmes) with consistent TR/reasoning gains. Interventional analysis on LVBench shows that masking MERIT-selected layers at κ=0 causes 51.7% Reasoning drop in LongVA-7B versus 16.8% Overall drop, and 58.2% versus 44.2% in InternVL3-8B, providing causal rather than merely correlational evidence that these layers underlie temporal reasoning. Benchmarks used are broadly recognized (CVPR 2025, NeurIPS 2024, ICCV 2025-level venues); no evaluation is performed on text-only TR benchmarks to confirm the base LLM alignment story quantitatively. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The paper combines CMA-ES black-box optimization with layer-selective model merging under an explicit capability-preservation constraint — the specific formulation (per-layer binary gating + TR-maximize / TP-penalize objective + evolutionary search) is novel, even though model merging and CMA-ES individually are well-established. The application of this formulation to the perception-reasoning tradeoff in VLMs, with interventional validation of the discovered structure, constitutes a genuine methodological contribution beyond prior full-model merging work (Chen et al., 2025). Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations directly compare uniform merging and random layer selection, demonstrating the necessity of selectivity. RANDOM-K is run 5 times with std reported, providing variance estimates. Interventional masking (§4.3) with six κ levels constitutes a non-trivial causal check beyond correlation. Frame-level attribution is grounded in gradient-activation products with a formally stated decision score. However, no code or model weights are released (not mentioned); the paper is a preprint under review as of April 2026. The search is conducted exclusively on Video-MME subsets and generalization to other benchmarks is measured but not stress-tested on domain-shifted or adversarial TR scenarios. The evaluation of MMBench-Video and Video-Holmes uses LLM judges (GPT-4-Turbo, GPT-4o-0125), introducing vendor-dependent scoring that can shift with model updates. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: MERIT modifies only the language backbone's self-attention weights and requires no GPU training — it runs CMA-ES evaluations via standard VLM inference on a small fixed dataset, feasible on a single multi-GPU server. - Efficient: Search caps at 1,600 evaluations per α value (6 values × 3 models), with a reported 2–3× speedup from caching; however, total search wall-clock time is not stated, which matters for practitioners. - Effective: Evidence on five established benchmarks across three VLM architectures, with causal interventional analysis, credibly supports that MERIT recovers genuine temporal reasoning rather than a benchmark artifact — though out-of-distribution validation is absent. - Scalable: MERIT was tested only on 4B–8B parameter VLMs; behavior on models with substantially more layers or parameters, or architectures that do not pair cleanly with a text-only backbone, is untested and not claimed. The work delivers a merged model artifact (weights) per VLM, not a reusable software library; the mergekit-evolve integration is mentioned but not released. Strengths: - The interventional masking experiment (§4.3) goes beyond benchmark number-chasing to establish a causal link between the discovered layers and temporal reasoning, lending mechanistic credibility to the layer-sparsity hypothesis. - The explicit perception-preservation penalty in the search objective operationalizes a non-trivial design constraint and distinguishes MERIT from naive merging approaches that optimize TR alone. - Generalization from a 232-sample search set to four held-out benchmarks across diverse evaluation protocols (accuracy, LLM-judge, relation scoring) is a meaningful test that the method recovers transferable behavior. - Frame-level gradient-activation attribution provides interpretable behavioral evidence of grounding shift, complementing quantitative benchmark results. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly flag three limitations in Appendix A: (1) recipes are benchmark-defined and may depend on how TP/TR are operationalized by specific datasets; (2) the discretized gating-plus-fixed-α parameterization is a tractability compromise relative to a fully continuous L-dimensional joint optimization; (3) optimal recipes vary across VLM architectures, making discovered recipes model-specific and not transferable without re-running search. These are stated directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not evaluate whether the discovered TR gains hold on text-only temporal reasoning benchmarks (e.g., TRAM, TimeBench), which would confirm that the LLM backbone's intrinsic TR is actually being restored versus a video-specific shortcut being reinforced. All three tested VLMs are 4–8B parameter open-source models from Qwen2/Qwen3 lineages — it is unclear whether the layer-sparsity hypothesis holds for architecturally different VLMs (e.g., those with cross-attention rather than unified transformer stacks). The paper also does not ablate the penalty weight λ, so it is unknown how sensitive results are to this hyperparameter. Finally, because Video-Holmes uses LLM judges that rely on GPT-4 variants (themselves updated over time), reported scores may not be reproducible without version-pinned API access. Bottom Line: For researchers building or deploying VLMs who need temporal reasoning recovery without retraining, MERIT is a technically credible, immediately applicable contribution with stronger causal grounding than prior full-model merging work — worth reading in full.
Recommended 2604.11399 PDF
#44 — Innovative (72)

In-Context Learning in Speech Language Models: Analyzing the Role of Acoustic Features, Linguistic Structure, and Induction Heads

Charlotte Pouw, Hosein Mohebbi, Afra Alishahi, Willem Zuidema · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) moderate — new empirical finding (temporal vs spectral asymmetry) plus methodological adaptation of induction-head identification to speech. Rigor (78) strong — 95% CIs, cross-voice replication, graded ablations, random-sequence validation, though no code release and single-model scope. Non-Obviousness (80) high — the temporal-density/prefix-matching linkage between empirical asymmetry and mechanism is genuinely surprising. Completeness (72) — authors confront token-granularity and preliminary-claim limitations directly. Resilience (68) — narrow eval slice (one model, PRIME-LM) is a real vulnerability. Impact (55) — findings and methodology, no artifact. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*55 = 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper systematically measures how acoustic manipulations (speaking rate, pitch, intensity), linguistic structure (lexical overlap, syntactic form, semantic similarity), and induction head ablations affect in-context learning (ICL) performance in SpiritLM, a 7B-parameter interleaved speech-text model, on a text-to-speech (TTS) task. - Why it's important: SpeechLMs are increasingly used for zero- and few-shot TTS and ASR via ICL, yet virtually nothing is known about which demonstration properties drive ICL performance or what internal mechanisms implement it — information critical for prompt engineering and architecture design. - How it does it: Controlled manipulation of acoustic features via Praat (2× fast, 0.5× slow speaking rate; flattened pitch; 30 dB intensity reduction) and linguistic features via the PRIME-LM corpus (graded lexical/semantic overlap, active/passive syntax), followed by causal ablation of attention heads identified as induction heads through modality-specific prefix-matching scores. - The good: Ablating the top-50 speech-prefix induction heads drives Word Error Rate from ~60 to ~100 (near chance), while ablating 50 random heads causes only a small drop, providing clean causal evidence that induction heads implement speech-based ICL. - The missing: Only one SpeechLM (SpiritLM-7B) is evaluated on short, simple controlled sentences from PRIME-LM — the generalizability of all findings to naturalistic long-form speech or other SpeechLM architectures is untested. - Read it or not: Researchers working on mechanistic interpretability of multimodal LLMs or on prompt design for SpeechLMs should read this; it delivers the first controlled causal account of what drives speech ICL. Problem & Motivation ICL is well characterized in text LLMs — demonstration format, label correctness, and distributional properties all modulate performance — but its dynamics in Speech Language Models remain opaque. SpeechLMs such as SpiritLM unify speech and text tokens in a single autoregressive framework, enabling TTS and ASR through ICL without parameter updates. Prior work on speech-based ICL showed that matching speaker identity or semantic content in demonstrations improves ASR, but did not isolate individual acoustic and linguistic features and did not examine whether any demonstrations can trigger ICL when no linguistic overlap exists at all. Furthermore, mechanistic work on induction heads — attention heads that implement a prefix-matching-and-copying mechanism believed to underlie text-based ICL — has not been extended to SpeechLMs, leaving open whether the same circuits operate across modalities.
