#49 — Innovative (72)

From $P(y|x)$ to $P(y)$: Investigating Reinforcement Learning in Pre-train Space (23 ▲ )

Yuqiao Tan, Minzheng Wang, Bo Liu, Zichen Liu, Tian Liang, Shizhu He, Jun Zhao, Kang Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) PreRL input-masking formulation plus NSR-only finding is non-obvious. Non-Obv (80) PSR-collapse-vs-NSR-success asymmetry is genuinely surprising. Rigor (70) gradient-alignment analysis at single checkpoint, single dataset, no error bars. Completeness (65) direct RLPT comparison missing. Resilience (65) 4B/8B only, math-only. Impact (70) code released, cheap warmup. Weighted ≈ 71.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PreRL (Pre-train Space Reinforcement Learning), a paradigm that applies reward-driven online RL to optimize the marginal output distribution P(y) by masking the input question during gradient updates, and proposes Dual Space RL (DSRL), a two-phase strategy that warm-starts with NSR-PreRL before switching to standard conditional RL. - Why it's important: Standard RLVR is bounded by the base model's existing output distribution — models can only reinforce trajectories that already have non-zero probability, capping the reasoning ceiling; pre-train space optimization sidesteps this by encoding reasoning capacity directly into parameters without input conditioning. - How it does it: The core mechanism is removing the input question x from the conditioning context during the policy gradient update, exploiting the fact that model parameters θ simultaneously govern both P(y) and P(y|x), so marginal updates also improve the conditional via gradient alignment — and within this framework, Negative Sample Reinforcement (suppressing wrong trajectories) prunes incorrect reasoning paths globally across all questions. - The good: NSR-PreRL increases transition thoughts 14.89× and reflection thoughts 6.54× over the vanilla model after just 20 steps, and DSRL achieves Avg@32 of 57.54 on Qwen3-4B across six math benchmarks vs. 55.79 for GRPO, with OOD gains of +3.79 on GPQA-Diamond and +5.37 on MMLU-Pro. - The missing: All PreRL training uses a single math dataset (MATH-7500) and only 4B and 8B models are tested, leaving generalization to other domains and larger scales entirely unvalidated. - Read it or not: Researchers working on RLVR training pipelines should read this — the NSR-PreRL warm-up is computationally cheap (10-25 steps), the mechanistic explanation for why positive-sample PreRL collapses while negative-sample PreRL succeeds is directly actionable, and the OOD results suggest genuine capability improvement rather than benchmark overfitting. Problem & Motivation Post-training RL (RLVR) optimizes the conditional policy P(y|x), which is constrained to trajectories that already have non-zero probability under the base model. Prior work (Yue et al. NeurIPS 2025; Zuo et al. ICLR 2026) empirically demonstrates that RLVR-trained models may be fundamentally capped by base model capability rather than the RL algorithm. Pre-train space optimization — targeting P(y) instead — offers broader exploratory capacity by encoding reasoning ability directly into model parameters without input conditioning. However, conventional pre-training relies on static offline corpora and next-token prediction objectives, producing a pronounced distribution shift between pre-trained knowledge and the task distribution encountered during post-training, limiting reasoning enhancement. The problem is: how to bring online, reward-driven RL into the pre-train space to create a stronger initialization for subsequent standard RL, without the pathologies of offline passive learning. Key Idea The single core technical insight is that standard RLVR gradient updates can be redirected to target the marginal distribution P(y) simply by masking (removing) the input question x during the policy gradient computation — the update becomes ∇θ log π_θ(y_t|ẋ, y_{<t}) · R(y) where ẋ denotes the zeroed input. The theoretical basis is Shared Parameter Influence: the same θ governs both distributions, so via first-order Taylor expansion, a PreRL update that improves log π_θ(y) also improves log π_θ(y|x) when their gradients are aligned. A further non-obvious finding is that within this pre-train space, only Negative Sample Reinforcement (suppressing incorrect trajectories, A_i < 0) is beneficial; Positive Sample Reinforcement (reinforcing correct self-generated trajectories) causes on-policy collapse because it accumulates probability mass on the model's own outputs, requiring instead high-quality off-policy demonstrations to function. Methodology The pipeline has three phases: 1. PreRL formulation: GRPO is adapted so that during the gradient update the input x is masked, targeting the marginal distribution. Token-level advantage normalization from GRPO (Shao et al. 2024) and the Yu et al. 2025 token-level policy gradient loss are retained. Groups of G=8 responses are sampled per prompt; group-normalized advantages Â_{i,t} = (R_i − mean(R)) / std(R) determine the sign of each sample's contribution. 2. NSR-PreRL: Only tokens from responses with negative advantages (A_i < 0) receive gradient signal; positive-advantage samples are masked out. This selectively suppresses incorrect reasoning trajectories in the pre-train space, pruning wrong reasoning paths globally (across all input questions), while stimulating endogenous exploration (14.89× more transition thoughts, 6.54× more reflection thoughts relative to the vanilla Qwen3-4B after 20 steps). 3. Dual Space RL (DSRL): A Policy Reincarnation strategy (Agarwal et al. 2022; Liang et al. 2025): the model runs NSR-PreRL for S ∈ [10, 25] steps, then switches to standard GRPO over P(y|x). The unified gradient indicator I[s > S ∨ R(y) < 0] enforces the NSR constraint in phase 1 (s ≤ S) and removes input masking in phase 2 (s > S). Warmup steps follow an inverted-U performance curve: S < 10 provides insufficient stimulation; S > 25 causes excessively long outputs that hinder fine-grained optimization. Training uses MATH dataset (7,500 problems; Lewkowycz et al. 2022), Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B base models, AdamW at 1e-6, batch size 128, 8 rollouts per prompt, max response 8192 tokens, 6 epochs, via the verl framework. Key Results DSRL is evaluated via Avg@32 (Pass@1 averaged over 32 rollouts) and Pass@K (K ∈ [1, 256], n=300 samples per problem) on six math benchmarks: MATH500, AMC23, AIME24, AIME25, Minerva, OlympiadBench. On Qwen3-4B, DSRL achieves 57.54 Avg vs. GRPO at 55.79 (+1.75), Dr.GRPO at 57.04 (+0.50), DAPO at 55.82 (+1.72), PPO at 56.15, Reinforce++ at 49.59. On Qwen3-8B, DSRL achieves 58.47 vs. GRPO 57.00 (+1.47). Pass@K analysis across K ∈ [1, 256] shows DSRL consistently leading GRPO at all K values on both model scales, particularly on AIME24 and AIME25 as K increases, indicating a higher-quality solution distribution rather than merely a Avg@1 artifact. OOD generalization (Qwen3-4B): GPQA-Diamond 43.18 vs. GRPO 39.39 (+3.79); MMLU-Pro 66.49 vs. 61.12 (+5.37); BBH 82.41 vs. 80.37 (+2.04); HumanEval 81.10 vs. 81.10 (+0.00). On Qwen3-8B, HumanEval improves +2.44. NSR-PreRL warm-up ablation: NSR-RL (post-train space) warmup achieves only 54.38 Avg vs. GRPO's 55.79 (underperforms the baseline), while NSR-PreRL (pre-train space) as DSRL achieves 57.54 — confirming the pre-train space is essential, not just the NSR objective. Baselines (PPO, GRPO, Reinforce++, RLOO, Dr.GRPO, DAPO) are current and represent the state of RLVR training methods; all use the same Qwen3 base models, making comparisons clean. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The PreRL formulation (online RL targeting P(y) via input masking during gradient updates) is genuinely new as a fully online reward-driven paradigm in the pre-train space — distinct from prior offline continual pre-training (P(y) via NTP on static corpora) and from Reinforcement Learning Pre-Training (RLPT) methods that use NTP pseudo-rewards on pre-training corpora rather than verifiable task rewards. The identification that NSR succeeds while PSR collapses in pre-train space is a non-obvious empirical finding with mechanistic grounding. DSRL's Policy Reincarnation assembly is a competent synthesis of existing techniques (Agarwal et al. 2022, Zhu et al. 2025) applied to the new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Gradient alignment is validated empirically on Qwen3-4B with 400 rollouts from AMC23 (Figure 2): inner products are non-negative for 100% of samples (average +9.2); cosine similarity distribution is strongly positive. The PSR/NSR decomposition within PreRL is a clean mechanistic ablation. The warmup steps ablation across S ∈ {5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30} is well-motivated and reveals a credible inverted-U curve. Avg@32 and Pass@K with n=300 samples per problem effectively manage evaluation variance on high-variance datasets like AIME. Methodological concerns: (1) The gradient alignment analysis is conducted only at a single training checkpoint; whether alignment degrades during extended PreRL training is not studied. (2) Only one training dataset is used (MATH-7500); the paper does not test whether PreRL constructed with different task types transfers differently. (3) The appendix compares PreRL conceptually to RLPT methods but no direct head-to-head experiments are run against them in Table 1. (4) Error bars are not reported in main tables, though the large-n evaluation design partially mitigates this. Code is stated as available (linked in the abstract). This is an arXiv preprint (April 15, 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Runs on standard GPU hardware using Qwen3-4B/8B models with the verl RLHF framework; no specialized hardware beyond what standard RLVR training requires. - Efficient: The NSR-PreRL warm-up phase requires only 10-25 gradient steps before transitioning to standard GRPO, adding negligible compute overhead relative to the full RL training run. - Effective: DSRL outperforms GRPO and all other baselines on all six in-domain math benchmarks and three of four OOD benchmarks across both model sizes, and Pass@K improvements across the full K range confirm a genuinely better solution distribution, not a threshold artifact. - Scalable: Tested only at 4B and 8B parameters; no experiments above 8B; no ablation on training dataset size or domain breadth; scalability to 70B+ models or non-mathematical reasoning tasks is claimed but undemonstrated. Delivers: code (linked in abstract). Strengths - The theoretical grounding of PreRL via Shared Parameter Influence and first-order Taylor expansion is clean and empirically validated (Figure 2 gradient analysis), giving the method mechanistic credibility beyond an engineering trick. - The PSR vs. NSR decomposition is the paper's most intellectually valuable contribution: the discovery that suppressing wrong trajectories in pre-train space is uniquely effective while reinforcing correct ones causes on-policy collapse is non-obvious, mechanistically explained, and directly actionable in practice. - OOD generalization results (GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, HumanEval) suggest that pre-train space optimization cultivates general reasoning capacity rather than overfitting to the MATH task format — this distinguishes PreRL from domain-specific fine-tuning. - The fully-solved/fully-unsolved question analysis (Figure 6) provides a compelling mechanistic view of how NSR-PreRL eliminates universal failure modes before standard RL refines problem-specific nuances. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) PSR-PreRL suffers from on-policy learning collapse, requiring off-policy expert data to function — a significant practical limitation they confront directly rather than minimize. (2) Excessive NSR-PreRL warm-up steps generate excessively long outputs that hinder subsequent fine-grained optimization, making the transition point S ∈ [10, 25] a sensitive hyperparameter. These are presented as genuine constraints on the method's operating envelope. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All PreRL training is performed exclusively on MATH-7500 (competition math); whether the pre-train space paradigm generalizes to other domains (code, scientific reasoning, multi-hop QA) is entirely untested. - No experiments exceed 8B parameters; whether DSRL's advantages persist or shrink at 70B+ scales is unknown and not claimed to be known. - The gradient alignment analysis is performed at a single training checkpoint; the paper does not track whether alignment degrades over the course of extended PreRL, which would undermine the theoretical justification for the approach after early steps. - Direct experimental comparison against RLPT methods (Hatamizadeh et al. 2025, Dong et al. 2025, Xing et al. 2025) is absent from the main tables; the distinction is made only in a conceptual comparison table in the appendix. - The frequency and impact of token-probability misalignment cases (Figure 11) — where P(y) and P(y|x) diverge significantly — is not quantified, leaving open the question of how often the gradient alignment assumption fails in practice. Bottom Line Practitioners training RLVR models on mathematical reasoning should read this paper — the NSR-PreRL warm-up is computationally cheap, delivers consistent improvements over GRPO across both in-domain and OOD benchmarks, and the mechanistic account of why positive-sample PreRL collapses while negative-sample PreRL succeeds is immediately actionable for anyone designing training curricula.
Recommended 2604.14142 PDF
#50 — Innovative (72)

RAD-2: Scaling Reinforcement Learning in a Generator-Discriminator Framework (21 ▲ )

Hao Gao, Shaoyu Chen, Yifan Zhu, Yuehao Song, Wenyu Liu, Qian Zhang, Xinggang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) drives the score — RAD-2 delivers a trained planner, BEV-Warp simulation pipeline, and real-vehicle deployment, with ~56-65% closed-loop collision reductions across two simulators. Rigor (65) solid — extensive ablations across 7+ tables and cross-environment validation, but no error bars on small 256-512 clip eval sets, no public data, real-world results only qualitative. Novelty (70) — generator-discriminator decoupling redirecting RL to low-dim score space is principled and non-obvious. Non-Obviousness (75) — reformulating planning as preference learning to dodge high-dim credit assignment is a strong conceptual move. Completeness (70), Resilience (70) — limitations confronted directly, but proprietary data and no quantitative on-road metrics leave deployment claims partially unsupported. Weighted (systems 15/15/10/15/15/30): 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*80 ≈ 72.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RAD-2, a generator-discriminator framework for autonomous driving motion planning that trains an RL-optimized trajectory discriminator to rerank candidates from a diffusion generator, alongside Temporally Consistent GRPO (TC-GRPO) and On-policy Generator Optimization (OGO) to stabilize training and a BEV-Warp simulation environment to enable high-throughput closed-loop RL. - Why it's important: Directly applying RL to high-dimensional continuous trajectory spaces suffers from severe credit assignment failures and optimization instability, blocking scalable closed-loop training of diffusion planners that are otherwise strong at modeling multimodal driving distributions. - How it does it: RL is redirected from optimizing trajectories directly to optimizing a low-dimensional discriminator score, then structured longitudinal feedback (OGO) progressively shifts the generator's probability mass toward high-reward manifolds without destabilizing diffusion sampling. - The good: In BEV-Warp closed-loop evaluation on 512 safety-critical scenarios, RAD-2 reduces collision rate to 0.234 and at-fault collision rate to 0.092 — roughly 56% and 65% reductions versus the ResAD diffusion baseline (0.533 / 0.264), with consistent gains confirmed in an independent 3DGS photorealistic benchmark and real-vehicle testing. - The missing: Real-world deployment evidence is purely qualitative (no collision statistics or quantitative safety metrics from on-road runs), and all closed-loop training and evaluation data are collected internally with no public dataset release, making independent reproduction difficult. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers working on closed-loop RL training for diffusion-based planners should read this — it provides a concrete, well-ablated solution to the credit assignment problem and introduces a practical high-throughput simulation mechanism; those focused on world-model-based or non-BEV-centric architectures may find limited direct applicability. Problem & Motivation High-level autonomous driving demands planners capable of modeling multimodal future trajectories (e.g., yielding vs. proceeding at intersections) while remaining robust in closed-loop interaction. Diffusion-based imitation learning (IL) planners can represent rich trajectory distributions but suffer from two compounding failures: (1) stochastic training instability from the diffusion process, and (2) causal confusion from open-loop training, where the model learns correlations between states and actions rather than causal driving intent. Prior work combining RL with IL is hampered by the mismatch between sparse scalar RL rewards and the high-dimensional, temporally structured trajectory action space — a severe credit assignment problem. Existing simulation environments (game engines, reconstruction-based, learned world models) each fail on at least one of: realism, scalability, or long-horizon temporal stability. Key Idea Rather than applying RL directly to the high-dimensional diffusion trajectory space (which produces unstable gradients), restrict RL to train a low-dimensional discriminator that scores candidate trajectories by their expected long-term closed-loop outcomes; then use the discriminator's structured feedback to indirectly shift the generator's distribution via longitudinal trajectory adjustments (OGO). The generator and discriminator co-adapt iteratively on shared rollout data, with temporal coherence in trajectory sampling (TC-GRPO's latched execution) denoising the policy gradient signal. Methodology The pipeline has four stages operating on BEV-encoded scene representations: (1) Diffusion Generator (G): A DiT-based diffusion model conditioned on BEV scene features (map, agents, navigation) samples M=32 candidate (x,y) trajectories over horizon H via iterative denoising. Pre-trained on ~50,000 hours of real-world ego-trajectory data via imitation learning. (2) RL Discriminator (D): A Transformer encoder embeds each candidate trajectory via MLP point embeddings prepended with a learnable [CLS] token, then cross-attends to scene features (map BEV, dynamic agents, fused agent-map interaction). A sigmoid head produces a scalar score in [0,1]. The discriminator is initialized from the generator's planning head weights. Trained via TC-GRPO with two reward signals: (i) Safety-Criticality Reward based on worst-case Time-To-Collision (TTC) margin across a rollout horizon; (ii) Navigational Efficiency Reward as a stability-window penalty anchoring ego progress ρ ∈ [1.05, 1.10] relative to the clipped expert trajectory. The RL objective (Eq. 18) integrates a clipped importance-sampling loss with adaptive entropy regularization that activates only when batch entropy falls below a learnable target — preventing score collapse to 0/1 extremes. (3) TC-GRPO: Addresses the credit-assignment mismatch between high-frequency replanning and sparse rollout rewards. A latched execution strategy commits to a selected trajectory for H_reuse=8 steps, ensuring the cumulative reward is attributed to that specific trajectory hypothesis. Groups of G=4 rollouts from the same initial state form the normalization baseline for advantage estimation (Eq. 15). Advantages are computed only at sparse decision points K_i (trajectory-resampling moments), not at every timestep, preventing dilution by high-frequency mode-switching. (4) On-policy Generator Optimization (OGO): At each closed-loop step, the raw lookahead trajectory is longitudinally adjusted based on reward signals — safety-driven deceleration (travel distance compressed by ratio ρ < 1 when TTC < γ_safe) or efficiency-driven acceleration (travel distance expanded by ρ' > 1 when progress lags and no collision risk). These optimized trajectory segments D_opt supervise the generator via MSE over the prediction horizon (Eq. 19), gradually shifting its output distribution. (5) BEV-Warp Simulation: A closed-loop environment that warps reference BEV feature maps using SE(2) transformation matrices derived from the deviation between simulated and logged ego poses, bypassing image rendering. A FIFO replay buffer of 8 batches maintains a 8:1 discriminator-to-generator training frequency ratio. Scenario composition mixes safety-critical and efficiency-oriented clips (10K each) with reward-variance-based filtering to discard low-information clips. Critical design decisions: (a) Restricting RL to the discriminator (rather than the generator) is well-justified — it avoids direct gradient flow through the high-dimensional trajectory diffusion process. (b) Latched execution for TC-GRPO is well-motivated by the credit assignment analysis but introduces a fixed H_reuse hyperparameter that trades reactivity for consistency (ablated in Table 5, H_reuse=8 optimal). (c) OGO's longitudinal-only optimization constrains generator updates to the temporal axis, preserving spatial path diversity — a defensible choice for stability but it means the generator cannot learn new spatial maneuvers from RL feedback alone. Key Results BEV-Warp closed-loop (512 safety-critical + 512 efficiency-critical held-out scenarios): - RAD-2: CR=0.234, AF-CR=0.092, Safety@1=0.730, Safety@2=0.596, EP-Mean=0.988, EP@1.0=0.736, EP@0.9=0.984 - Best prior: ResAD — CR=0.533, AF-CR=0.264, Safety@1=0.418, EP@1.0=0.516 - RAD-2 reduces CR by 56% and AF-CR by 65% versus ResAD 3DGS photorealistic benchmark (256 safety-critical evaluation clips): - RAD-2: CR=0.250, AF-CR=0.078, Safety@1=0.723, Safety@2=0.644 - Best prior (Senna-2): CR=0.269, AF-CR=0.077 - RAD-2 improves CR by 7% while matching AF-CR Open-loop Senna-2 benchmark (6 driving scenarios): - RAD-2: FDE=0.553m, ADE=0.208m, CR=0.142%, DCR=0.138%, SCR=0.004% - Best prior (Senna-2): FDE=0.597m, ADE=0.225m, CR=0.288% Inference-time scaling: increasing candidate count M from 8 to 64 improves EP@1.0 from 0.667 to 0.816 while CR stays near 0.234, confirming the discriminator effectively leverages a larger search space. Baselines (TransFuser, VAD, VADv2, GenAD, ResAD, Senna, Senna-2, RAD) are generally current and competitive, with Senna-2 and RAD being the most recent strong comparators. All baselines use the same perception backbone for fair planning head comparison. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the decoupled RL-in-discriminator-space approach that reformulates autonomous driving planning as a preference learning problem, sidestepping direct RL in high-dimensional diffusion trajectory space, combined with TC-GRPO adapting GRPO's group-relative advantage framework to temporal motion planning coherence requirements. BEV-Warp is a novel engineering contribution for high-throughput feature-level closed-loop simulation. OGO's structured longitudinal optimization is a non-obvious bridge between scalar RL signals and generator distribution updates. What is not novel: diffusion trajectory generation, GRPO itself (adapted from LLM post-training), discriminator/scoring for trajectory selection (established in VAD, DriveSuprim, etc.), and BEV feature encoding. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: training pipeline components (Table 4, 5 configurations), execution horizon H_reuse (Table 5), clip filtering (Table 6), discriminator initialization (Table 7), TC-GRPO group size (Table 8), entropy regularization (Table 9), scenario composition (Fig. 10), and inference-time candidate count (Table 10). Two distinct simulation environments (BEV-Warp and 3DGS) provide cross-environment validation. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported in any table — all numbers are point estimates, raising questions about variance, especially given the relatively small evaluation sets (512 clips per scenario type). Real-world deployment is described qualitatively without quantitative collision or intervention metrics. Code and model weights are not explicitly stated to be released; a project page is listed (https://hgao-cv.github.io/RAD-2) but no repository URL is provided in the paper. This is an arXiv preprint, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The system uses multi-view camera inputs encoded into BEV features on standard GPU hardware; real-vehicle testing is reported, confirming physical deployability. - Efficient: BEV-Warp eliminates image rendering, enabling high-throughput closed-loop iteration; inference with M=32 candidate trajectories and iLQR tracking adds modest overhead but is not quantified in latency terms. - Effective: Closed-loop evaluation in two independent simulation environments plus qualitative real-world results provide multi-layer evidence that the method improves safety and navigation efficiency, not just proxy metrics. - Scalable: Inference-time scaling is demonstrated from M=8 to M=128; training data scale is noted (~50K hours pretraining, 50K clips RL training) but no ablation on training data size is provided, and the 3DGS evaluation uses only 256 clips — a small stress test. The work delivers a trained system and the BEV-Warp simulation pipeline as engineering artifacts; code and weights are not confirmed released. Strengths - The generator-discriminator decoupling that redirects RL to a low-dimensional score space is a principled and effective solution to the high-dimensional credit assignment problem — not just a heuristic patch but a formally motivated reformulation as preference learning (KL minimization objective, Eq. 11). - TC-GRPO's latched execution mechanism addresses the specific temporal structure of motion planning in a way that standard GRPO does not, and the ablations (Tables 5, 8) demonstrate each component contributes measurably. - BEV-Warp is a practical and lightweight closed-loop simulation mechanism verified for spatial equivariance (Fig. 5) that enables scale of training not achievable with reconstruction-based simulators. - Cross-environment validation (BEV-Warp and independent 3DGS benchmark) and an open-loop benchmark together provide convergent evidence rather than single-metric optimization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge two limitations: (1) BEV-Warp's geometric warping is specific to BEV-centric perception and does not generalize to raw pixel or unified latent embedding architectures without a more general world model; (2) generative world models offer superior flexibility and photorealism but suffer from computational overhead and temporal drift at long horizons, making them impractical for large-scale RL training currently. The authors frame the transition to generative world models as the primary future direction. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No quantitative metrics from real-world vehicle testing — the paper claims "improved perceived safety and driving smoothness" but provides zero on-road collision rates, disengagement rates, or intervention counts, which are the only metrics that matter for deployment claims. - Evaluation sets for closed-loop benchmarks (512 clips per type for BEV-Warp, 256 for 3DGS) are small enough that point-estimate comparisons without confidence intervals are unreliable — a 5% CR improvement on 256 clips is statistically weak. - OGO only modifies the longitudinal (temporal speed) component of trajectories; it cannot teach the generator new spatial maneuvers (e.g., novel lane changes or obstacle avoidance paths). This fundamentally limits how much the generator can improve beyond its IL pretraining distribution in spatial complexity. - All training and evaluation data are proprietary internal datasets. No public dataset or benchmark is used for closed-loop training evaluation, making independent reproduction impossible. - The 8:1 discriminator-to-generator training frequency ratio is presented as a design choice but not ablated — it could be a sensitive hyperparameter. - The paper does not report model parameter counts for G and D or total training compute, making it impossible to assess reproducibility at scale. Bottom Line Practitioners working on closed-loop RL training for diffusion-based autonomous driving planners will gain concrete, well-ablated methods from this work — the generator-discriminator decoupling and TC-GRPO are genuine contributions — but the absence of quantitative real-world results and the closed proprietary data pipeline mean the collision rate claims cannot be independently validated.
Recommended 2604.15308 PDF
#51 — Innovative (72)

KumoRFM-2: Scaling Foundation Models for Relational Learning

Valter Hudovernik, Federico López, Vid Kocijan, Akihiro Nitta, Jan Eric Lenssen, Jure Leskovec, Matthias Fey · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (85) high — deployed Python SDK, 41 tasks, production-scale databases. Novelty (75) — first few-shot foundation model surpassing supervised on relational benchmarks; architectural contribution meaningful. Rigor (55) — vendor self-report, closed weights, no error bars/significance tests, no model size/compute disclosed. Non-Obv (70), Completeness (60), Resilience (60) — missing cost/latency numbers, independent replication impossible. Weighted: 0.15*75+0.15*55+0.10*70+0.15*60+0.15*60+0.30*85 ≈ 69.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KumoRFM-2, a pre-trained relational foundation model that performs in-context learning and fine-tuning directly on multi-table relational databases without manual table flattening or task-specific training. - Why it's important: Deploying predictive models on relational databases currently requires hours to days of expert feature engineering or full supervised retraining per task; a zero-shot foundation model that matches or exceeds these pipelines would eliminate that bottleneck entirely. - How it does it: A two-stage hierarchical attention mechanism first processes intra-table row/column relationships with task-conditioned targets injected early, then aggregates information across tables via foreign-key graph attention and cross-sample attention — enabling the model to learn which columns and relationships are predictive without any task-specific gradient updates. - The good: On RelBenchV1, KumoRFM-2 achieves avg AUROC 79.60 with best overall rank 3.08, surpassing the strongest supervised relational baseline (RelGNN, 78.06/3.75) using as little as 0.2% of available training data as context examples — the paper's claimed first demonstration of a few-shot foundation model exceeding supervised approaches on relational benchmarks. - The missing: The paper does not report model size, pre-training compute, or inference latency comparisons against baselines, making it impossible to assess cost-benefit trade-offs for practitioners; and all results are self-reported by the commercial vendor with no independent replication. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners and researchers in relational/tabular ML and database-native AI, as it establishes a new empirical baseline for what zero-shot prediction on multi-table data can achieve; can be skipped by those uninterested in the relational setting. Problem & Motivation: Predictive modeling on relational databases — churn, LTV, fraud, conversion rate — is pervasive in industry but requires either expensive manual feature engineering (hours to days per task) or task-specific model training on large labeled datasets. Existing tabular foundation models operate on single flat tables and extend to multi-table settings only by pre-aggregating relational subgraphs into wide feature vectors via fixed, column-wise encoders (deep feature synthesis). This flattening destroys row-level interaction signals: any label that depends on the co-occurrence of two features within a row (rather than their marginals) is invisible to such encoders. Moreover, temporal consistency — ensuring predictions use only data available at the anchor timestamp — is difficult to enforce post-hoc when tables are materialized offline. Key Idea: The key technical insight is that task-relevant feature selection in relational data is fundamentally a row-level, cross-table reasoning problem that cannot be decomposed into column-wise encodings. KumoRFM-2 solves this by injecting context targets (the known labels from in-context examples) directly into the input representations before any attention is computed, creating task-conditioned row embeddings that distinguish predictive from uninformative columns adaptively. A two-stage hierarchy then aggregates these embeddings first within each table (row + column attention) and then across tables (graph + cross-sample attention), maintaining temporal consistency throughout via the temporal heterogeneous graph formalism. Methodology: The pipeline operates in three stages. (1) Intra-table encoding: For each table in the relational subgraph, a lightweight network computes row embeddings via alternating column attention (across features within a row) and row attention (across rows within the table). Context targets are appended to input tokens before this stage, making representations task-conditioned. This avoids attending over all cells jointly (quadratic in rows × columns) while preserving intra-row dependencies. (2) Inter-table aggregation: A larger graph attention network propagates embeddings across primary-foreign key edges, effectively performing multi-hop message passing over the relational schema graph. A cross-sample attention layer additionally allows the query entity to attend to context examples, implementing in-context learning analogously to how transformers implement it for sequences. (3) Prediction readout: The final embedding of the query entity is decoded to the target variable (classification or regression). The model is pre-trained on a combination of synthetic tables/graphs generated via Structural Causal Models (SCMs) and real-world public relational databases, in a curriculum from single tables to multi-table structures. At inference, context examples are generated automatically by replaying historical database states up to anchor timestamps, preventing data leakage. Ensembling randomizes column and class order shuffles and aggregates over subgraph sampling configurations. Three critical design decisions: (a) hierarchical rather than flat attention — justified on quadratic complexity grounds and empirically shown to improve robustness to noise; (b) early target injection for task-conditioning — justified by an expressivity argument showing column-wise encoders achieve AUROC=0.5 on a synthetic co-occurrence task where KumoRFM-2 achieves AUROC=1.0; (c) structured local+global context selection combining the entity's own temporal history with recent database snapshots. Key Results: Evaluated on 41 tasks across 15 relational databases from four benchmarks (RelBenchV1, RelBenchV2, SALT, 4DBInfer), covering 2.35 billion rows and 914 columns. On RelBenchV1 binary classification (12 tasks, AUROC metric): KumoRFM-2 avg 79.60 AUROC with rank 3.08, outperforming KumoRFM-1 (76.71/8.33), the best supervised relational model RelGNN (78.06/3.75), and the best feature-engineered tabular approach DS+AutoGluon (75.36/8.50). On RelBenchV1 regression (MAE, normalized): KumoRFM-2 normalized score 0.822 with rank 2.67 vs. RelGNN's 0.861/4.44. On SALT multi-class classification (MRR): KumoRFM-2 ICL 0.83 (rank 1.88) vs. DS+AutoGluon 0.77/3.75 and DS+CARTE 0.79/3.88; fine-tuning lifts to 0.89 (rank 1.38). On RelBenchV2 regression: normalized MAE 0.601 with rank 1.0. On 4DBInfer binary classification: avg AUROC 79.96 (rank 3.6), matching the best supervised models. Notably, KumoRFM-2 uses at most 0.2% of available training data as context in the most data-rich settings. Robustness studies show performance stable up to 75% feature removal, 75% edge removal, and heavy injection of noisy columns. Subgraph depth ablations show a sharp performance gain from 0 to 1 hops, saturating around 4 hops. Fine-tuning completes in under 2 minutes per task. Baselines are current and strong, including recent competitors RT_zero (ICLR 2026), RDBLearn (2026), Griffin (ICML 2025), and TabICLv2 (2026). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The hierarchical two-stage attention with early task-conditioning for native relational in-context learning is a genuine architectural contribution not reducible to prior tabular or graph foundation models. The empirical finding — that a few-shot foundation model surpasses carefully tuned supervised pipelines on standardized relational benchmarks — is reported as a first-of-kind result. The pre-training curriculum and SCM-based synthetic data generation build on prior work (KumoRFM-1, Hoppe et al. 2025) rather than introducing new data generation methodology. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover context size, subgraph depth, subgraph breadth, feature scarcity, link scarcity, and noise injection — this is a thorough sensitivity analysis. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; all results are single-run point estimates. Benchmark evaluation scripts are publicly available at https://github.com/kumo-ai/kumo-rfm, and the model is accessible via `pip install kumoai`. However, the model weights are served via a proprietary inference endpoint (not openly released), so independent reproduction of the pre-trained model is not possible. All results are from the authors' own system (vendor self-report), and the pre-training dataset and model architecture hyperparameters (size, depth, head count) are not disclosed. None of the benchmark datasets were used during pre-training (explicitly stated), which guards against Goodhart's Law contamination. The paper's claimed first surpassing of supervised methods on relational benchmarks is a strong claim that warrants independent replication. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — KumoRFM-2 is a deployed system evaluated on production-scale databases. - Practical: The inference endpoint is accessible via Python SDK (`pip install kumoai`) from Python 3.10+; no local GPU required for ICL inference; fine-tuning is claimed to complete in under 2 minutes per task, though hardware specs for this claim are not stated. - Efficient: Inference cost is not quantified in absolute terms (latency, GPU-hours, cost per prediction); the SSD-based graph engine supports 5 GB/sec bandwidth and 20M lookups/sec for 500B-row databases, but no comparison to supervised pipeline inference cost is provided. - Effective: On 41 tasks across four benchmarks, the model achieves best-overall rank on RelBenchV1 and RelBenchV2, outperforming supervised baselines on average — evidence that it solves the stated problem, not merely a proxy, though task-level variance is high and supervised methods retain advantages on some large-dataset tasks. - Scalable: Tested at database scale up to 2.35 billion rows across 15 databases; the graph engine is documented to handle 500B+ rows via memory-mapped SSD I/O, but no empirical latency or throughput numbers at that scale are reported in the paper — this remains a claim rather than a measured result. The work delivers a software artifact (Python SDK, public inference endpoint, benchmark scripts); model weights are not openly released. Strengths: - The expressivity argument with the formal co-occurrence example (AUROC 0.5 for column-wise encoders vs. 1.0 for KumoRFM-2) provides a crisp, theoretically grounded motivation for the architectural design rather than relying purely on empirical ablations. - Evaluation scope is unusually broad: 41 tasks, 15 databases, 4 benchmark suites, with a rich and current set of ~20 baseline methods spanning supervised tabular ML, relational GNNs, LLMs, tabular foundation models, and relational foundation models. - The robustness studies (feature scarcity, link scarcity, noise injection) directly address practical deployment concerns and show KumoRFM-2 degrades substantially more gracefully than tabular baselines under data quality issues. - The temporal consistency enforcement via database snapshot replay at context generation time is a principled solution to data leakage that prior approaches handle informally or ignore. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that in large-scale supervised training regimes (e.g., amazon, stack, hm tasks), supervised relational models retain slight advantages that KumoRFM-2 has not yet closed; they note context size limitations as the primary bottleneck and flag fine-tuning as the path to close this gap. They note the optimal subgraph neighborhood size is task-dependent. These are stated directly and credited credibly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper omits model size, FLOPs, and inference latency numbers entirely — a significant gap for practitioners weighing deployment cost against accuracy gains, especially since competing supervised pipelines (LightGBM, AutoGluon) are extremely cheap to run. All numbers are self-reported by Kumo AI (a commercial entity with direct financial interest in favorable results); the open-sourced benchmark scripts allow independent evaluation of the model via the inference endpoint, but independent replication of the pre-training is impossible without released weights. The fine-tuning results on SALT are the strongest in the paper (avg MRR 0.89 vs. 0.77 for DS+AutoGluon), but fine-tuning ablations are limited to a single benchmark; it is unclear whether fine-tuning generalizes across all 41 tasks or specializes well only in the SALT domain. The comparison to LLM baselines is brief and uses two specific prompting strategies; a more carefully prompted LLM (e.g., with schema-aware chain-of-thought) might be more competitive. The pre-training data composition and curriculum are described at a high level only. Bottom Line: A practitioner building predictive pipelines on relational databases will find this work directly actionable: it demonstrates that zero-shot foundation model inference can replace multi-day feature engineering workflows and match or beat supervised approaches on standardized benchmarks, with a usable Python SDK — but the closed model weights and missing cost/latency numbers require treating vendor-reported numbers with appropriate caution until independent replication.
Search Results 2604.12596 PDF
#52 — Innovative (72)

AI-Driven Research for Databases

Audrey Cheng, Harald Ng, Aaron Kabcenell, Peter Bailis, Matei Zaharia, Lin Ma, Xiao Shi, Ion Stoica · arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (85) is the largest weighted contributor — deployable white-box PostgreSQL policies with <1ms overhead and public code beating current baselines (19.8% hit rate, 5.4×/6.8× latency reductions). Novelty (75) — co-evolutionary evaluator framing is genuinely new relative to AlphaEvolve/OpenEvolve. Non-Obviousness (70) boosted by empirical discovery that cost is a misleading proxy. Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) materially reduced: the central causal claim (co-evolution vs. static expert evaluator) is never isolated via ablation, and R-Bot comparison mixes different LLMs asymmetrically. Completeness (65) — limitations honestly stated, but single-DBMS/SF=10 scope limits scope honesty. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*85 = 70, round to 72 considering impact strength.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a co-evolutionary framework for AI-Driven Research for Systems (ADRS) that automatically designs database evaluators alongside candidate algorithms, enabling LLM-driven optimization to discover novel, deployable PostgreSQL components for buffer management, index selection, and query rewriting. - Why it's important: The evaluation pipeline — not solution generation — is the binding constraint on applying ADRS to databases: testing one LLM-generated candidate requires recompiling PostgreSQL (~3 min), loading data, and running benchmarks (~30 min), making the hundreds of iterations required for convergence computationally infeasible. - How it does it: An outer LLM loop continuously refines the evaluator (simulator, performance model, or workload/search space boundary) using performance signals from an inner algorithm-generation loop, treating the evaluator as an evolvable artifact rather than a fixed, manually engineered component. - The good: Three end-to-end case studies on real PostgreSQL components yield deployable white-box code that beats strong current baselines: 19.8% higher buffer cache hit rate, 6.3% latency reduction in index selection, and 5.4×/6.8× average query latency reduction in rewriting vs. PostgreSQL baseline — with <1ms runtime overhead. - The missing: All experiments run at TPC-H/TPC-DS scale factor 10 on a single DBMS (PostgreSQL); no ablation isolates the co-evolutionary outer loop's contribution versus simply running the inner ADRS loop with a better initial evaluator. - Read it or not: Database systems researchers and practitioners interested in applying LLM-driven algorithm discovery to specific DBMS components should read this; those seeking cross-DBMS generality or formal treatment of the co-evolution dynamics will find the paper's scope insufficient. Problem & Motivation: Database performance optimization — buffer cache eviction, index selection, query rewriting — has long required expert-designed algorithms. ADRS frameworks address this by replacing manual design with LLM-driven code generation in an iterative loop, but their practical application to databases is blocked by evaluation cost: each candidate algorithm must be assessed for correctness and performance, which at full system fidelity requires PostgreSQL recompilation, data loading, and full benchmark execution, making the hundreds of iterations required for convergence infeasible. Existing mitigation techniques (simulation, performance modeling, workload selection, search space pruning) each require substantial domain expertise to implement correctly and individually address only one of three evaluation axes (system, workload, search space). The evaluation setup is itself a research problem that prior ADRS work treats as a static, human-authored prerequisite. Key Idea: Treat the evaluator as a first-class evolvable artifact: place a second LLM loop (the outer loop) around the standard ADRS algorithm-generation loop (the inner loop) that continuously refines the evaluator configuration — simulator structure, fitness function formulation, workload composition, search space boundaries — using performance discrepancies between the evaluator's predictions and observed full-system behavior as the signal for refinement. The evaluator and the candidate algorithms co-evolve in tandem, eliminating the need for a human to manually engineer the evaluation pipeline. Methodology: The framework instantiates three problem-specific co-evolutionary loops, each targeting a different evaluation axis: 1. Buffer cache (simulator axis): The outer loop alternates between mutating the simulator configuration and evolving policies in the inner loop using OpenEvolve (50 iterations per outer cycle). Starting from a structurally naive simulator generated by Cursor from PostgreSQL source, the outer loop calibrates the simulator against multiple full-system baselines (Clock, PBM-PQ, PBM-Sampling) — the "more is more" insight — iteratively exposing metadata (scan context, I/O latency, dirty-page write cost ~200μs vs. ~20μs for reads, lock contention) that the naive simulator omitted. The scoring function evolves from pure hit rate to a cost-weighted latency metric. GPT-5 drives solution generation; outer loop implemented in 5K lines of Python. 2. Index selection (performance model axis): The outer loop co-evolves the fitness function alongside index policies. It begins with computational cost estimation (the standard proxy), but discovers empirically that this metric is unreliable: DB2Advis achieves the highest cost reduction (49.7%) but worst actual latency (8% worse than Extend). The outer loop introduces statistical denoising (executing each query in a fixed, isolated order to eliminate cache interference between queries), discovering that run-to-run variance from standard sequential execution often exceeds inter-algorithm differences. The final fitness function uses denoised latency with a novel table-importance weighting (summing baseline query costs referencing each table, scaled by a square-root multiplier to prioritize dimension-style tables). GPT-5 drives inner loop; outer loop runs 5 iterations of 100 inner iterations; 1.2K lines of Python. 3. Query rewrite (workload and search space axes): The outer loop constructs targeted empirical datasets mapping query structural features to performant rewrite rule sequences, dynamically selecting which queries to test (workload selection) and pruning invalid or regressive rule combinations (search space pruning). Three static pruning mechanisms are applied: removing rules inapplicable to a query's structure, enforcing empirically discovered rule ordering phases, and blacklisting rule combinations causing regressions on similar queries. The inner loop generates a single unified rewrite policy per workload, implemented as Java code in Apache Calcite's HepPlanner. Claude Sonnet 4.5 drives the inner loop; 7K lines of Python + Java in the outer loop; 5 outer iterations. Critical design decisions: (1) Multiple calibration baselines in simulation vs. a single baseline — well-justified: single-baseline calibration traps evolution in local optima where the simulator cannot expose metadata needed for more sophisticated policies. (2) Statistical denoising via ordered isolated execution rather than median-across-runs — empirically discovered when run-to-run variance exceeded inter-algorithm latency differences on TPC-H. (3) Targeted workload construction over full benchmark evaluation for query rewrite — justified by the NP-Hard combinatorial structure of the rewrite rule search space, which makes exhaustive ADRS application computationally intractable. Key Results: Buffer cache (PostgreSQL 14.0, AWS c5.18xlarge, 144 GiB RAM, 200GB NVMe SSD, TPC-H SF=10, 3 independent runs): - 19.8% higher hit rate vs. PBM-Sampling (best baseline) at 32 parallel streams - 11.4% I/O volume savings vs. best baseline - Baselines: Clock, PBM-PQ, PBM-Sampling, PBM-Sampling full Index selection (PostgreSQL 14.0, same hardware, TPC-DS and TPC-H SF=10, 500MB budget, median over 5 runs ±1 std dev): - 6.3% latency reduction on TPC-DS vs. Extend (37,275s vs. 39,791s) - 5.8% latency reduction on TPC-H vs. baselines - 2.2× lower selection time on TPC-H (3.4s vs. 7.3s for Extend) - Policy evolved exclusively on TPC-DS, generalizes to TPC-H without re-training - DB2Advis achieves highest cost reduction (49.7%) but worst latency — confirming cost as unreliable proxy - Baselines: AutoAdmin, Anytime, DB2Advis, Extend Query rewrite (PostgreSQL 17, Apache Calcite HepPlanner, same hardware, TPC-H and DSB SF=10, separate train/test query variants): - Average query latency: 8.78s vs. PostgreSQL baseline 47.56s (5.4× faster on TPC-H) - 6.8× faster on DSB vs. PostgreSQL baseline - 2.6× faster query latency than R-Bot (GPT-4o) on TPC-H; 4.0× faster on DSB - Total execution latency (rewrite + query): Evolved 387s vs. R-Bot GPT-4o 3,494s on TPC-H (9.0× faster) - <1ms policy execution overhead at runtime vs. >1 min/query for R-Bot - Correctness verified: every rewritten query produces identical rows to original - Baselines: PostgreSQL native optimizer, R-Bot (GPT-4o), R-Bot (GPT-5) Counter-intuitive finding: the policy that minimizes computational cost (DB2Advis) has the worst actual runtime latency — a concrete empirical demonstration that cost-based evaluation metrics actively mislead ADRS optimization. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The co-evolutionary framing — treating the evaluator as a first-class LLM-generated artifact that evolves alongside candidate algorithms — is genuinely novel; prior ADRS work (AlphaEvolve, GEPA, OpenEvolve) treats evaluators as static, manually authored components. The three problem-specific instantiations demonstrate distinct structural approaches (simulator co-evolution, performance model co-evolution, workload/search space co-evolution) rather than a single technique applied uniformly. The application domain is not novel — database component optimization is a crowded field — but the evaluator automation mechanism and its concrete database instantiations are new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Source code is publicly available at https://github.com/audreycheng/adrs. Buffer cache results report averages over 3 independent runs; index selection reports median ±1 standard deviation over 5 runs; query rewrite runs each experiment 3 times. The query rewrite evaluation uses separate training and test query variants (two variants per template in each benchmark) to prevent overfitting, and verifies correctness via row-level equivalence. The index selection case provides a strong generalization test (evolve on TPC-DS, evaluate on TPC-H). However: no ablation formally isolates the outer co-evolutionary loop's contribution versus running the inner loop only with a hand-designed better evaluator — the core causal claim of co-evolution is not experimentally supported. The three case studies use different LLMs (GPT-5 for buffer cache and index selection, Claude Sonnet 4.5 for query rewrite), with no discussion of whether LLM choice affects results. The R-Bot comparison in query rewriting is asymmetric: R-Bot uses GPT-4o/GPT-5 while the evolved policy uses Claude Sonnet 4.5 for generation, an inconsistency not acknowledged. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.06566v1, April 8, 2026); the PVLDB reference format appears with a placeholder DOI, suggesting the paper is submitted or conditionally accepted but not yet published with a final DOI. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: The evolved policies deploy as standard PostgreSQL C code (buffer cache), Python policy scripts (index selection), or Java/Calcite HepPlanner code (query rewrite) on commodity AWS c5.18xlarge instances; no specialized ML infrastructure or GPU required at runtime. - Efficient: The co-evolutionary outer loop is compute-intensive during policy discovery (full PostgreSQL compilation and benchmark runs are required for buffer cache evaluation), but the discovered policies impose <1ms runtime overhead — dramatically more efficient than R-Bot's >1 min/query inference cost. - Effective: Evidence shows evolved algorithms improve on multiple concrete metrics (hit rate, I/O volume, query latency, selection time) vs. current baselines on TPC-H and TPC-DS, with correctness verified at row level for query rewriting. - Scalable: All experiments use scale factor 10; no experiments at larger scale. The claim that discovered heuristics (e.g., table-importance weighting, clean/dirty tracking) generalize beyond SF=10 or to different hardware configurations is not supported by experiment — only asserted. The paper delivers runnable software artifacts (outer loop code, evolved policies integrated into PostgreSQL). Strengths: - The co-evolution framing addresses a specific, concrete, well-articulated bottleneck (automated evaluator design) that has blocked practical ADRS application to databases — the contribution is directly actionable, not merely descriptive. - Three structurally distinct case studies spanning simulation, performance modeling, and workload/search space design demonstrate that the outer-loop approach generalizes across the main evaluation axes within the database domain. - The discovered policies are interpretable white-box code, directly deployable in production PostgreSQL without runtime ML infrastructure — a meaningful practical advantage over black-box learned approaches. - The empirical discovery that computational cost estimation is an unreliable (and actively misleading) fitness proxy for index selection is a concrete, well-evidenced finding with immediate implications for practitioners using standard ADRS frameworks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly restrict scope to performance problems where solution correctness can be verified without altering semantics, excluding concurrency control, write-ahead logging, and crash recovery because ADRS cannot yet generate evaluators with formal correctness guarantees. They acknowledge that extending ADRS to workload optimization introduces a specification problem since application correctness is often expressed implicitly in application code rather than through formal database constraints. They note that future database architectures may need to be redesigned for AI-driven search — prioritizing modularity and predictability over raw performance. The authors directly confront these limitations rather than minimizing them, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The central causal claim — that co-evolving the evaluator is what enables discovery of superior algorithms — is never directly tested. No experiment compares "co-evolved evaluator + inner loop" vs. "expert-designed evaluator + same inner loop with same compute budget." The paper does not rule out the possibility that a well-designed static evaluator would produce equivalent or better results. - All three case studies use PostgreSQL exclusively. Whether the co-evolution methodology or the discovered policies transfer to other DBMS architectures (MySQL, SQL Server, DuckDB, columnar systems) is not discussed. - The use of GPT-5 for two case studies and Claude Sonnet 4.5 for the third, without explanation or sensitivity analysis, makes it impossible to separate the effect of LLM capability from co-evolutionary methodology across case studies. - The R-Bot baseline comparison is asymmetric: R-Bot uses GPT-4o/GPT-5 while the evolved policy system uses Claude Sonnet 4.5 — different-capability LLMs compared in the same table without acknowledgment. - No experiments at scale factor above 10; evolved heuristics (table-importance weighting, clean/dirty tracking) may not remain optimal at production-scale data distributions. - The outer loop's own computational cost (how many total CPU-hours the co-evolutionary discovery process requires) is not reported, making it impossible to assess whether the approach is practical beyond the research setting. Bottom Line: A practitioner building database components or researching ADRS application to systems will gain a concrete, implemented framework and empirical evidence that automating evaluator design is both feasible and critical to making LLM-driven algorithm discovery work in practice — but should treat the scale-up and cross-DBMS generalization claims as open questions requiring further validation.

#53 — Innovative (72)

Accelerating Triangle-Connected Truss Community Search Across Heterogeneous Hardware

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (80) high — the internal/marginal/external triangle classification with Theorem 1 order-independence is an elegant, genuinely non-obvious restructuring that eliminates the per-k iteration bottleneck. Impact (75) — 336× construction and 108× search speedups on graphs to 261M edges, cross-vendor (NVIDIA + AMD) portability via PyTorch TCR. Rigor (65) — targeted ablations (W/O-BM, Basic vs advanced, 3 search strategies) present, but single-run timings, no significance tests, and evaluation fixes k=4 for the main table. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) — dynamic maintenance only compared to rebuild-from-scratch, no incremental CPU baseline, no public code URL. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*75 = 69, adjusted to 72 for the elegance and proof-backed theoretical insight.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TETree/TETruss, a tensor-based framework that constructs, queries, and maintains EquiTree/EquiTruss indices for triangle-connected k-truss community (k-TTC) search, deployable across heterogeneous hardware accelerators (NVIDIA and AMD GPUs) via Tensor Computation Runtimes. - Why it's important: Sequential CPU-based k-TTC index construction on billion-edge graphs takes hours, making real-time community search impractical for large social and biological networks where query latency matters. - How it does it: It classifies every triangle into one of three types (internal, marginal, external) based on the trussness of constituent edges, proving that triangles can then be processed in any order and simultaneously across all truss levels, eliminating the costly per-k iteration that prevents parallelism. - The good: TETree achieves a 336.26× average speedup over the sequential SETree baseline on GPU for index construction, and 108× average speedup on k-TTC search, validated on graphs up to 261M edges across NVIDIA A100, RTX 3090, and AMD 7700XT. - The missing: All k-TTC search evaluations use a fixed default trussness k=4, leaving the performance profile for high-k queries (where community size shrinks dramatically) understated; the per-k ablation covers construction sensitivity but not end-to-end search latency under varying k. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers or engineers building GPU-accelerated graph analytics systems — the triangle-type classification trick is clean and transferable; practitioners who only need k-TTC at modest scale can safely skip it. Problem & Motivation: In large-scale graphs such as social networks (Weibo: 261M edges) and protein-interaction networks, k-truss community search (k-TCS) requires finding all subgraphs where every edge participates in at least k-2 triangles. Triangle-connected k-truss community (k-TTC) strengthens this by additionally requiring triangle connectivity among edges. The state-of-the-art index, EquiTree, supports time-optimal k-TTC search but its sequential CPU construction takes hours on billion-edge graphs, and existing parallel algorithms (EquiTruss via multi-core CPUs) still require seconds to minutes. The root cause is that both construction and search are organized around iterating over each trussness level k, which produces many small tensor operations with high kernel-launch overhead rather than a few large batched operations that hardware accelerators are designed to exploit. Key Idea: The central insight is that every k-triangle (a triangle containing at least one k-truss edge) can be classified as internal (all three edges have equal trussness), marginal (two edges have equal trussness, one has higher), or external (all three differ). These three types correspond to exactly the roles each triangle plays in creating supernodes, connecting supernodes via superedges, and merging conflicting supernodes during index construction — and critically, Theorem 1 proves that the processing order of triangles does not affect the resulting EquiTree structure. This order-independence means all triangles across all k-levels can be batched and processed simultaneously in a single tensor pass, eliminating the per-k outer loop entirely. Methodology: The pipeline has two algorithmic phases. During creation, triangles are computed and classified into (E_s, E_l, E_r) sets; internal triangles merge their three edges into the same supernode (via tensor-parallel Link/Compress operations implementing Union-Find); marginal triangles additionally connect the resulting supernode to the supernode of the third edge; external triangles create superedges between distinct supernodes. This uses standard tensor operators (sort, diff, unique, mask-based selection) in O(log m) steps. During refinement, conflicting supernodes (same trussness connected to the same higher-trussness child) are merged in batches. The algorithm identifies maximal active supernodes — those with no active neighbors of larger trussness — and merges them in parallel, iterating until all supernodes become static. Theorem 2 proves each round strictly reduces the active set. The batch merging avoids the layer-by-layer k iteration of Algorithm 1 (basic construction). Algorithm 3 implements k-TTC search via Optimized Parallel CC (OPCC), which propagates labels directly over superedges rather than traversing the tree layer-by-layer (as in TF or LP strategies), in O(log(n_V_k)) steps. Algorithm 4 handles dynamic graph maintenance by identifying affected edges, recomputing their trussness in a local subgraph, deleting stale supernodes, and re-running the creation/refinement algorithms on the affected subgraph only. Key Results: On index construction, TETree on A100 GPU achieves 336.26× average speedup over sequential SETree and 16.76× over TETree-Basic (same tensor framework but with per-k iteration). TETruss on A100 achieves 154.15× speedup over multi-core MCETruss and 19.59× over a native CUDA implementation of MCETruss. The Orkut dataset (117M edges, k_max=78) goes from ~32,602s (SETree) to 11.056s (TETree on A100). Weibo (261M edges) exceeds SETree/MCETruss's 2-hour limit while TETree completes in 12.178s. For k-TTC search, TETree achieves 108× average speedup over SETree; TETruss achieves 100× over SETruss. The TETree-W/O-BM ablation (disabling batch merging) is consistently surpassed by TETree with up to 6.15× improvement, validating batch merging. On AMD 7700XT, TETree still substantially outperforms all CPU baselines, though performance is lower than NVIDIA due to ROCm backend limitations. All seven datasets are publicly available (SNAP, Network Repository). Iteration count analysis (Table 3) shows TETree reduces total iterations by 2.7–22.5× versus TETree-Basic depending on dataset. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The triangle-type classification (internal/marginal/external) enabling order-independent, k-iteration-free index construction is genuinely novel. The OPCC-based search algorithm tailored for tree-structured indices is new. The rest — tensor representation via CSR/COO, Union-Find via Link/Compress, k-truss decomposition itself — are standard combinations of prior ideas (EquiTree [47], MCETruss [19], TenGraph [28]) adapted to the TCR execution model. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and targeted: TETree-W/O-BM isolates batch merging (up to 6.15× benefit), and comparison of TETree vs TETree-Basic isolates the triangle-type creation strategy. Three search strategies (OPCC, LP, TF) are compared. Sensitivity analyses vary batch size, average degree, and k_max. Error bars and statistical significance tests are absent — all results are single-run measurements. No code repository or artifact URL is mentioned; the CPU baselines' C++ code was obtained from original authors, and the GPU implementation is in Python/PyTorch. The evaluation uses only k=4 as the default search parameter for the overall comparison table (Table 5), though varying-k plots are included separately. The framework relies on PyTorch 2.2.2 with specific hardware configurations (A100+Xeon 8358P, RTX 3090+Xeon 6142) that are documented precisely. Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: Runs on commodity GPU servers (RTX 3090 with 24 GB VRAM) and AMD 7700XT desktop GPU via PyTorch TCR, requiring no custom CUDA kernels. - Efficient: Peak GPU memory for TETree on Weibo is ~20,000 MB (A100 80 GB), but can be reduced to ~5,000 MB by halving the batch size (from 2×10^7 to 4×10^6 triangles) at modest time cost; MCETruss runs out of memory on OK and WB datasets entirely. - Effective: Index construction and k-TTC search correctness are validated by comparison against SETree/SETruss on all seven datasets; the dynamic maintenance algorithm is validated against rebuild-from-scratch across edge counts 10^0 to 10^4. - Scalable: Tested up to 261M edges (Weibo), the largest graph in the Network Repository collection used; batch processing for graphs that exceed device memory is implemented and evaluated, but no theoretical analysis of scaling beyond GPU memory capacity is provided. The paper delivers a working software artifact (Python/PyTorch implementation); no public repository is stated. Strengths: - The triangle-type classification yielding provable order-independence (Theorem 1) is an elegant insight that cleanly restructures a fundamentally iterative algorithm into a batch-parallel form. - Cross-hardware validation on both NVIDIA (A100, RTX 3090) and AMD (7700XT) GPUs genuinely demonstrates TCR portability, not just a claim. - The scale of evaluation — seven datasets up to 261M edges including Weibo — is substantial; the fact that CPU baselines time out on the two largest graphs while TETree handles them in tens of seconds is compelling evidence. - The ablation study (TETree-W/O-BM, TETree-Basic vs TETree, three search strategies) cleanly isolates the contribution of each technical component. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that AMD 7700XT performance is lower than NVIDIA A100 due to physical hardware limits and inadequate PyTorch operator optimization on ROCm backends. They acknowledge the basic algorithm has high kernel-launch overhead on small tensors (the per-k iteration problem that motivates the advanced algorithm). They note that for extremely large graphs that cannot fit in device memory, triangles must be partitioned and processed in batches, and memory overhead for the advanced algorithm is larger than the basic algorithm. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The k-TTC search evaluation in Table 5 fixes k=4 for the primary comparison; k=4 is the minimum non-trivial value and corresponds to the largest communities, which are easiest to find. High-k search (tight communities in dense subgraphs) may behave differently, and the paper does not report overall speedup for k>6 in the search comparison tables. No significance tests or confidence intervals are reported for any timing result. The dynamic maintenance evaluation (Fig. 10) compares only against rebuild-from-scratch rather than against any incremental CPU baseline (e.g., the dynamic EquiTree algorithm from [47]), making it impossible to assess how much of the maintenance benefit is from the tensor approach versus the incremental strategy itself. The implementation is in Python/PyTorch, which introduces Python overhead; the CPU baselines are in optimized C++, so the comparison is not apples-to-apples at the language level (though the orders-of-magnitude speedup makes this unlikely to explain the gap). Bottom Line: A practitioner building GPU-accelerated graph community search infrastructure gains concrete, deployable algorithms here — the triangle-type classification trick and the OPCC search are non-obvious contributions backed by strong empirical results at scale.
Citations
#54 — Innovative (72)

BERT-as-a-Judge: A Robust Alternative to Lexical Methods for Efficient Reference-Based LLM Evaluation

Hippolyte Gisserot-Boukhlef, Nicolas Boizard, Emmanuel Malherbe, Céline Hudelot, Pierre Colombo · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical+systems. Novelty (55) — known encoder technique applied at scale; contribution is recipe and empirical case. Rigor (75) — extensive ablations, OOD tests, but single seed and circular ground-truth against Nemotron. Non-Obviousness (60) — regex distortion quantification is the non-obvious part. Completeness (75) — limitations acknowledged directly. Resilience (65) — circular oracle weakens claims. Impact (90) — immediately deployable encoder, code+data+weights released, practical CPU inference. Weighted composite lands around 72. Strong practical contribution, moderate novelty discount.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 90
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BERT-as-a-Judge, a fine-tuned 210M-parameter encoder model for reference-based LLM evaluation that replaces regex/lexical matching with semantic correctness assessment, and quantifies the distortions that regex-based evaluation imposes on leaderboard rankings across 36 models and 15 tasks. - Why it's important: Regex-based evaluation confounds formatting compliance with problem-solving ability, causing models like Qwen-3 32B to drop 18 rank positions and Llama-3 70B to drop 13.3 rank positions — artifacts of formatting quirks rather than true capability differences that mislead benchmark-driven model selection. - How it does it: EuroBERT 210M is fine-tuned for one epoch on ~1M synthetically labeled question-candidate-reference triplets (labeled by Nemotron-Super-v1.5 at 97.5% human agreement) using binary cross-entropy, requiring only 20 GPU hours to train and ~200ms/sample at inference on a CPU. - The good: BERT-as-a-Judge consistently outperforms regex on every benchmark tested, often by large margins (+20.3% on CoQA, +10.4% on ARC-Challenge, +20.5% on MATH), while also dominating LLM-as-a-Judge methods with comparable inference FLOPs across multiple model families and task types. - The missing: Ground-truth labels are themselves generated by a large LLM (Nemotron-Super-v1.5), introducing circularity — the encoder is trained to mimic synthetic labels from a model that is not evaluated for its own correctness on these tasks, and there is no independent oracle validation beyond the 3,212-instance human spot-check. - Read it or not: Read it if you train or evaluate open-weight LLMs on benchmark suites — the empirical case against regex evaluation is the strongest systematic treatment to date, and the released encoder is immediately deployable as a drop-in replacement. Problem & Motivation Evaluating instruction-tuned LLMs in zero-shot generative settings requires two steps: extracting the predicted answer from free-form model output, then comparing it to a reference. Both steps are currently dominated by regex parsing followed by exact match, ROUGE-L, or Math-Verify, all of which tie evaluation accuracy to formatting compliance. Models that produce verbose, chain-of-thought, or differently formatted but semantically correct answers are penalized. This is not a minor inconvenience: across 36 models on 15 tasks, the authors show that regex-based measurement substantially underestimates true performance and distorts rankings by up to 18 positions, with smaller and more diverse-output models suffering most. LLM-as-a-Judge methods address format sensitivity but introduce high computational cost and sensitivity to judge model and prompt design. The gap to fill is a lightweight evaluator that is robust to format variation while remaining compute-competitive with regex. Key Idea A bidirectional encoder fine-tuned to classify whether a candidate answer is semantically equivalent to a reference answer, given the question as context, captures the relevant signal more directly than pattern-matching heuristics and at orders of magnitude less cost than generative LLM judges. The key insight is that answer equivalence is a binary classification problem well-suited to encoder architectures — not a generation task — and that 100K–1M synthetically labeled triplets are sufficient to train a generalizable classifier from a strong encoder initialization. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. (1) Data generation: 36 open-weight models (135M–70B parameters, 12 families including Llama-3, Qwen-3, Gemma-3, Phi-4, Mistral-3, OLMo-3, EuroLLM, Apertus, SmolLM-2/3, Falcon-3, LFM-2) are prompted zero-shot with greedy decoding on 15 benchmarks across three categories — multiple-choice (MMLU, MMLU-Pro, TruthfulQA, ARC-Easy/Challenge, GPQA), context extraction (SQuAD-v2, HotpotQA, DROP, CoQA), and open-form math (GSM8K, MATH, ASDiv, AIME24, AIME25) — producing approximately 1M question-candidate-reference triplets. (2) Labeling: Nemotron-Super-v1.5 annotates each triplet with a True/False correctness judgment; 11 human annotators independently re-labeled 3,212 instances, yielding 97.5% agreement with synthetic labels. (3) Training: EuroBERT 210M is initialized and fine-tuned for one epoch on a balanced training mixture drawn from MMLU, ARC-Easy/Challenge, SQuAD-v2, HotpotQA, GSM8K, and MATH using binary cross-entropy, learning rate 2×10⁻⁵, batch size 32, on 8 MI250x GPUs (~20 GPU hours). Critical design decisions: (a) encoder rather than decoder, justified empirically by showing 0.6B generative judges underperform even regex on most tasks; (b) synthetic rather than human-only labels, validated by 97.5% human agreement; (c) balanced task-family sampling in training, shown to produce strong OOD generalization. Regex baseline uses the lm-evaluation-harness regex rules with exact match for multiple choice, ROUGE-L for context extraction, and Math-Verify for open-form math. LLM-as-a-Judge baseline is Qwen-3 0.6B (matched inference scale). Key Results Against Nemotron-Super-v1.5 synthetic labels as ground truth, BERT-as-a-Judge achieves: ARC-Challenge 99.4% (vs. regex 89.0%, LLM-Judge 50.2%), ARC-Easy 99.7% (vs. 54.0%, 50.2%), MMLU 98.5% (vs. 88.1%, 66.2%), CoQA 88.1% (vs. 67.0%, 75.2%), GSM8K 98.8% (vs. 94.4%, 71.3%), MATH 93.7% (vs. 73.4%, 58.9%), AIME24 90.0% (vs. 87.8%, 77.9%). The encoder achieves these results at ~200ms/sample on an Apple M1 CPU — roughly 3–4 orders of magnitude fewer FLOPs than a 7B LLM judge. OOD generalization is strong: accuracy on TruthfulQA (excluded from training) is 98.6%, CoQA is 88.1%, ASDiv is 95.3%. Removing model families from training has minimal impact on held-out model assessment (Table 3), confirming generalization to unseen model families. The ranking distortion from regex is documented in detail: Qwen-3 32B drops 17.6 positions on multiple-choice tasks; Llama-3 70B drops 13.3 on context extraction. Training data efficiency: 100K samples suffice for multiple-choice and math (2 GPU hours), with context extraction benefiting from more data due to requiring contextual reasoning beyond candidate-reference matching. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The systematic, multi-model, multi-task characterization of regex failure modes and their ranking consequences is the most comprehensive treatment of this problem in the literature. The encoder itself is a known architectural idea (fine-tuned BERT/encoder for classification) applied to the answer equivalence task — the contribution is the dataset at scale, the training recipe, and the empirical proof that this beats both alternatives in its FLOP budget.
