This is competent, well-executed engineering of known techniques rather than a novel architectural or algorithmic invention. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper provides training efficiency ablations (100K vs. 200K vs. 500K vs. 1M samples), formatting robustness experiments (cross-format evaluation in Table 6), question inclusion/exclusion ablation (Table 5), hybrid regex+BERT-J evaluation (Table 4), and decision threshold sensitivity (Figure 5). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — a single training run is described. The ground-truth oracle is Nemotron-Super-v1.5 (a large generative model), which introduces a methodological concern: BERT-as-a-Judge is trained to reproduce Nemotron's judgments, and its accuracy is also measured against Nemotron's judgments, so the reported accuracy numbers partly reflect how well it mimics the teacher rather than independent correctness. The 97.5% human-synthetic agreement partially mitigates this, but human spot-checks cover only 3,212 of the ~1M+ triplets and are not disaggregated by task or model family. Code is released at https://github.com/artefactory/BERT-as-a-Judge; models and data at https://hf.co/collections/artefactory/bert-as-a-judge. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.09497v1, April 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: The encoder runs at ~200ms/sample on a standard Apple M1 CPU with no GPU required, making it deployable in any evaluation pipeline on commodity hardware. - Efficient: At 210M parameters with binary cross-entropy inference, BERT-as-a-Judge uses roughly 3–4 orders of magnitude fewer FLOPs than a 7B LLM judge and matches the inference cost of a 0.6B LLM judge while substantially outperforming it. - Effective: The method demonstrably corrects ranking distortions that regex introduces and achieves >97% accuracy against human-validated labels on multiple-choice tasks, though effectiveness on open-ended generation (summarization, code) is not tested and is explicitly out of scope. - Scalable: The approach is tested at up to 1M training triplets and 36 evaluated models up to 70B parameters; scaling to additional model families is experimentally supported, but extension to multimodal inputs, multilingual settings, or open-ended generation requires future work the authors acknowledge. The work delivers a trained model (multiple checkpoints) and labeled dataset as software artifacts, fully released. Strengths - The empirical documentation of regex-induced ranking distortions across 36 models and 15 tasks is thorough and practically important; the rank-shift data (e.g., Table 1, Tables 16–18) provides a convincing case that current benchmarking practice is systematically biased. - The inference efficiency advantage over LLM-as-a-Judge at matched compute is demonstrated rigorously via FLOPs comparison across model sizes and families (Figure 3), not just asserted. - OOD generalization is tested both on held-out benchmarks (TruthfulQA, CoQA, ASDiv) and on held-out model families (Table 3), providing credible evidence that the encoder does not merely memorize model-specific formatting artifacts. - Full release of code, data, and model checkpoints enables immediate adoption and reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly confine the paper to English benchmarks with objectively verifiable reference answers, noting that open-ended generation tasks (summarization, translation, code generation, instruction following), multilingual settings, and multimodal inputs are out of scope. They identify these as natural directions for future work. The authors engage with these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular ground truth: BERT-as-a-Judge is both trained on and evaluated against Nemotron-Super-v1.5 labels. Its reported accuracy numbers are partially self-referential; a truly independent evaluation would require either full human annotation or a different oracle. The human spot-check covers only 0.3% of total examples and is not stratified by the hardest or most disputed cases. - The LLM-as-a-Judge baseline in the main Table 2 uses Qwen-3 0.6B, a model so small it falls below regex on most benchmarks — it is a weak baseline that makes the encoder look better by comparison. The more competitive comparison is Figure 3, where the encoder matches but does not conclusively dominate large LLM judges (7B–32B) on open-form math tasks. - No calibration analysis: the encoder outputs sigmoid probabilities, but there is no evaluation of whether these probabilities are well-calibrated (i.e., does a score of 0.7 actually correspond to 70% chance of correctness?), which matters for downstream use in ranking or confidence-weighted evaluation. - The benchmark set skews toward tasks with short, unambiguous reference answers; context extraction tasks already assume a single gold span, and multiple-choice tasks have letter answers. The most challenging evaluation setting — multi-reference, partial-credit, or abstractive answers — is not addressed. Bottom Line Any practitioner running open-weight model benchmarks should replace regex evaluation with BERT-as-a-Judge immediately — the distortion evidence is convincing, the encoder is cheap and released, and the accuracy numbers are substantially better across the board, even if the self-referential ground truth warrants a modest confidence discount.
Recommended 2604.09497 PDF
#55 — Innovative (72)

On Optimizing Multimodal Jailbreaks for Spoken Language Models

Aravind Krishnan, Karolina Stańczak, Dietrich Klakow · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — first joint GCG+PGD multimodal jailbreak for SLMs, combining existing components in a non-obvious way. Rigor (80) strong: 5 seeds with standard errors, two evaluation metrics, public code release, matched-hyperparameter ablations, SAMA comparison. Non-Obviousness (75) — the mechanistic analysis showing text/audio perturbations occupy partially independent representation subspaces (PCA linear separability, gradient energy) adds genuine insight beyond attack numbers. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) limited by white-box-only threat model, no transferability tests, AdvBench saturation on Qwen2.5 Omni, and no defense evaluation. Impact (70) reasonable — code released, clear message for SLM red-teaming. Preprint. Weighted composite ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack), a white-box gradient-based framework that simultaneously optimizes adversarial text suffixes (via GCG) and audio perturbations (via PGD) to jailbreak Spoken Language Models across both modalities at once. - Why it's important: Safety alignment of SLMs has been evaluated almost exclusively in the unimodal setting, which this work shows is insufficient — an adversary who jointly exploits both modalities can achieve up to 10× higher jailbreak rates than the best single-modality attack, meaning current robustness benchmarks may severely overstate SLM safety. - How it does it: JAMA couples GCG's discrete token search and PGD's continuous audio perturbation into a single joint optimization loop, computing a shared loss over a batch of 8 malicious queries and alternating gradient updates so both perturbations co-evolve to push the model's last-layer representations into a region far from the benign decision boundary. - The good: Across 4 state-of-the-art SLMs (Audio Flamingo 3, Qwen2 Audio, Qwen2.5 Omni, Gemma 3N) and 4 audio types, JAMA consistently outperforms unimodal baselines, with the clearest gains on models that individually resist one modality, demonstrating genuine cross-modal interaction effects rather than mere additive improvement. - The missing: All attacks are white-box (require full gradient access); there is no evaluation of transferability across models, no black-box approximation, and AdvBench alone is a narrow harmful-content benchmark that may not capture the breadth of real safety failures. - Read it or not: Security researchers working on multimodal LLM safety and practitioners responsible for deploying SLMs should read this; it provides the first rigorous multimodal gradient attack framework for SLMs, but readers interested only in defenses will find little actionable mitigation guidance. Problem & Motivation: Spoken Language Models inherit both the safety vulnerabilities of their LLM backbone and an expanded attack surface through speech input. Prior jailbreaking work optimizes attacks on one modality (text or audio) while leaving the other unoptimized. In realistic adversarial settings, an attacker will naturally seek to maximize malicious signal across all available input modalities simultaneously. The question the paper addresses is whether unimodal robustness evaluations accurately predict multimodal robustness — i.e., does robustness to a text-only or audio-only attack imply robustness to a joint attack? The practical stakes are high: SLMs are being deployed in dialogue and speech translation pipelines, and a model certified safe under unimodal evaluation may remain trivially jailbreakable when both channels are perturbed. Key Idea: The core insight is that discrete text perturbations (GCG) and continuous audio perturbations (PGD) occupy partially independent subspaces in the model's last-layer representation space. Because they perturb complementary components of the model's decision boundary, their combination can expose vulnerabilities invisible to either modality alone. This is operationalized by coupling GCG and PGD into a single joint optimization loop that shares a common loss over a batch of malicious queries, so the audio perturbation conditions on the current text suffix at each step and vice versa. Methodology: The pipeline has two interlocked components. (1) PGD for audio: an imperceptible perturbation δ is added to a base audio signal x; at each step, the normalized gradient of the joint cross-entropy loss L(y | q, x+δ) with respect to δ is computed, and δ is updated via projected gradient descent with L2 normalization, learning rate lr=0.01, and clipping ε=0.001 to enforce perceptual similarity. (2) GCG for text: a suffix S = (s₁,...,sₙ) of N discrete tokens is optimized by computing per-token gradients, identifying Top-K candidate substitutions, building a candidate set C by randomly replacing one position with its Top-K alternatives, and selecting the candidate minimizing the joint loss. The joint loss averages over Q=8 malicious query–response pairs. Both components run simultaneously for T=1000 steps with the forward pass conditioning text tokens on the currently perturbed audio. Critical design decisions: (a) using a batch of Q queries to improve generalization (well-justified by transfer literature); (b) clipping ε=0.001 for audio, matched to prior PGD work on SLMs; (c) masking special control tokens during GCG gradient computation to prevent model-specific control character exploitation — each decision is explicitly motivated. Evaluation uses string matching (following GCG) and LLaMA Guard 3, across 5 random seeds on 480 AdvBench test samples. A sequential approximation (SAMA) runs GCG first without audio, then freezes the suffix and optimizes PGD conditioned on the fixed suffix, achieving 4×–6× speedup. Key Results: JAMA surpasses unimodal jailbreak rates by 1.5×–10× depending on model and configuration. For Qwen2.5 Omni, the GCG-only baseline achieves 59%–90.9% at length 16 (already high), and JAMA primarily amplifies these; for Gemma 3N, which resists GCG with only 3% success at 4 tokens, JAMA induces jailbreak rates visible only with music-initialized PGD at longer durations. Audio Flamingo 3 reaches jailbreak rates above 80% under JAMA with 32 GCG tokens and 4s PGD. The most striking finding is that for models where unimodal attacks underperform, joint optimization shows clear interaction effects — short GCG suffixes or weak PGD perturbations that independently fail can together elicit harmful outputs. SAMA achieves rates within ~10% of JAMA on average, with the gap shrinking at larger GCG/PGD lengths. A linear classifier trained on PCA-reduced last-layer embeddings achieves 99% accuracy distinguishing benign, GCG-only, PGD-only, and joint attack inputs using only 2 principal components, confirming that joint attacks occupy a distinct representation subspace. Gradient energy analysis shows the GCG component dominates early optimization (sharp loss reduction on the discrete landscape), followed by sustained PGD refinement. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The simultaneous joint optimization of GCG (discrete) and PGD (continuous) for multimodal jailbreaking is a novel combination not previously demonstrated for SLMs. The empirical finding that text and audio adversarial perturbations occupy partially independent representation subspaces whose combination surpasses either alone is a genuine discovery. The component methods (GCG, PGD) are existing algorithms applied to a new composite setting; novelty lies in the joint formulation and the mechanistic analysis of why it works. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present: PGD-only and GCG-only baselines at matched hyperparameters are evaluated alongside JAMA, and SAMA is compared against JAMA with the same number of total optimization steps. Results are reported across 5 random seeds with standard errors. Two evaluation metrics are used (string matching and LLaMA Guard 3), and the paper notes that string matching results follow the same trends as LLaMA Guard 3. Code and data are publicly released at https://repos.lsv.uni-saarland.de/akrishnan/multimodal-jailbreak-slm. The paper is an arXiv preprint (2603.19127v1, March 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. One methodological concern: AdvBench is a narrow benchmark developed in the adversarial NLP community and has known saturation issues for strong attacks on capable models; results on Qwen2.5 Omni (GCG-only already at 90.9%) leave limited headroom to distinguish attack quality. No evaluation on out-of-distribution harmful categories or real-world deployment scenarios is provided. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments run on a single NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU node; SAMA reduces this further, making the attack practical for well-resourced adversaries and security researchers with access to one high-end GPU. - Efficient: JAMA requires ~1.5× the compute of unimodal JAMA due to audio forward passes during GCG candidate evaluation; SAMA eliminates this overhead and runs 4×–6× faster than JAMA. - Effective: The attack demonstrably elicits harmful completions (Tables 1–4 show verbatim jailbroken outputs including malware code, poisoning instructions, and disinformation templates) across all four tested SLMs, confirming the method solves the stated problem rather than a proxy. - Scalable: Tested only at the scale of 4 open-weight SLMs up to 7B parameters; no evaluation on larger models or proprietary SLMs, and no claim about whether the gradient-based approach transfers to black-box systems. The paper does not test at 10×–100× model scale. The work delivers code, attack scripts, and data (not a trained model or defense tool). Strengths: - The mechanistic analysis (gradient energy ratio, t-SNE embedding, PCA linear separability) goes beyond reporting attack success rates and provides genuine insight into why joint optimization works — a rare contribution in jailbreaking papers. - The SAMA sequential approximation is practically valuable: it achieves comparable jailbreak rates at 4×–6× lower compute without modifying the core insight, making the attack reproducible on broader hardware. - Evaluating across four models with meaningfully different unimodal robustness profiles (near-saturated Qwen2.5 Omni vs. highly resistant Gemma 3N) stress-tests whether the joint attack claim is general or model-specific. - Public code release with full hyperparameter specification supports reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors restrict their scope to white-box gradient-based attacks and explicitly call for "strong guardrails in the composite attack space" as future work. They note that the primary aim is identifying safety risks to promote model defense, not enabling misuse. The paper acknowledges that the Gemma 3N anomaly (SAMA outperforming JAMA by ~20% on switchboard) is likely a gradient interaction artifact and is left open. The string matching evaluation metric is noted as imperfect, with LLaMA Guard 3 results reserved for the appendix. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The white-box restriction is a significant boundary the authors mention but do not attempt to bridge — no transferability experiment tests whether a JAMA-optimized perturbation on Qwen2.5 Omni transfers to Audio Flamingo 3, which would substantially increase real-world threat modeling relevance. AdvBench covers only English harmful prompts; no multilingual or domain-shifted evaluation is attempted despite Qwen models' multilingual capabilities. The paper evaluates no defenses — adversarial training, input preprocessing, or multimodal safety classifiers — meaning the practical defensive takeaway is limited to "unimodal evaluation is insufficient" without specifying what a sufficient evaluation or mitigation looks like. The ε=0.001 PGD clipping constraint is taken from prior work without ablation, leaving it unclear whether the jailbreak success differential between JAMA and unimodal baselines is sensitive to this choice. Bottom Line: Security researchers and SLM deployers gain a concrete, reproducible demonstration that unimodal safety evaluation is structurally insufficient, backed by mechanistic analysis, making this paper a required read for anyone responsible for red-teaming or certifying multimodal speech AI systems.
Recommended 2603.19127 PDF
#56 — Innovative (72)

Lineage-based Reuse and Memory Management for Multi-backend ML Systems

sigmodrecord.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (85) dominates — integrated into open-source Apache SystemDS, real 8-node A40 cluster, concrete 1.2x-9.6x speedups on representative pipelines against strong baselines (LIMA, HELIX, VISTA, PyTorch). Novelty (65) — unified lineage cache spanning eager CPU, lazy Spark, async GPU with MAXPARALLELIZE is a substantive system-level contribution beyond LIMA extension. Rigor (65) — strong multi-baseline comparison but no per-optimization ablation isolating delayed caching vs. eviction injection vs. MAXPARALLELIZE; SIGMOD Record minor revision (lower review bar). Non-Obviousness (70) — tight coupling of reuse with memory management is the key insight. Completeness (75) — limitations confronted directly. Resilience (70). Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*65+0.10*70+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.30*85 = 72.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MEMPHIS, a holistic framework for multi-backend reuse of ML intermediates and integrated memory management across CPU, distributed Spark, and GPU backends, implemented inside Apache SystemDS. - Why it's important: ML pipelines exhibit high computational redundancy from hyperparameter tuning, data augmentation, and incremental modifications, but existing reuse systems (LIMA, HELIX, VISTA) are backend-specific and cannot eliminate fine-grained cross-backend redundancy without tight memory-management integration. - How it does it: A hierarchical lineage-based reuse cache provides a unified identity for intermediates across all backends via fine-grained DAG hashing, extended by backend-specific cache and memory management (lazy GC for Spark, pointer recycling for GPU) and compiler rewrites for asynchronous data exchange, delayed caching, eviction injection, and inter-backend parallel operator scheduling. - The good: End-to-end speedups reach 9.6x over baseline SystemDS on a real 8-node cluster with NVIDIA A40 GPUs on an HCV hyper-parameter tuning pipeline at 100GB, with 2.6x–3x on model selection and transfer learning pipelines. - The missing: No ablation isolating individual compiler optimizations (delayed caching vs. eviction injection vs. MAXPARALLELIZE), and no evaluation at LLM-scale workloads despite their explicit mention as motivation. - Read it or not: ML systems researchers and SystemDS/Spark practitioners building multi-backend ML pipelines should read this; researchers on PyTorch/JAX stacks will find the design principles instructive but the artifact non-transferable without significant porting effort. Problem & Motivation Modern ML pipelines that use compilation-based systems (SystemDS, MLlib, SageMaker) are compiled into hybrid execution plans spanning in-memory CPU operations, distributed Spark RDDs, and GPU CUDA kernels. Exploratory data science workloads—grid search, hyperparameter tuning, iterative learning, AutoML—exhibit high redundancy at fine granularity (e.g., repeated X^T X within a linear regression pipeline across parameter configurations, repeated feature extraction across transfer learning trials). Prior reuse systems address only a single backend: LIMA handles fine-grained CPU reuse, HELIX handles coarse-grained Spark RDD caching, and VISTA exploits GPU CSE. None integrates reuse with memory management for Spark's lazy evaluation (dangling RDD references), GPU memory pressure (cudaMalloc overhead, fragmentation), and asynchronous cross-backend data transfer. This tight coupling makes holistic redundancy elimination infeasible with backend-specific tools. Key Idea Hierarchical fine-grained lineage tracing — incrementally building a DAG of operations over every intermediate at runtime — provides a unified, backend-agnostic identity that enables the same REUSE abstraction to detect cache hits, drive eviction decisions, support lazy garbage collection, and guide compiler rewrites uniformly across CPU, Spark, and GPU, without requiring user code changes. Methodology MEMPHIS is structured around three integrated components. First, the lineage cache: a LineageMap (hash map from live variable names to lineage DAG nodes) is built incrementally at runtime by invoking TRACE() before each linear algebra instruction. Cache lookups use sub-DAG memoization with early abort; on a hit, REUSE() skips computation and returns the cached object. Cache compaction replaces LineageMap entries with lineage keys of cached objects on hit to increase shared sub-DAG references and reduce memory footprint. Second, backend-local cached objects: wrappers around backend-specific pointers (in-memory matrices, Spark RDDs with lazy materialization state, GPU CUDA pointers) enable seamless data movement across backends. Spark reuse probes the lineage cache before triggering a Spark job; if the RDD is cached, it is returned directly, bypassing job submission. Lazy GC tracks child RDDs and pending consumers to reclaim dangling broadcast and RDD memory asynchronously. GPU memory is managed via Live and Free pointer lists; memory recycling reuses Free pointers of matching size to avoid cudaMalloc overhead, benefiting mini-batch DNN workloads. Eviction for Spark uses a cost-based scoring function: argmin_{o∈Q} (r_h + r_m + r_j) · c(o)/s(o), weighting hit-rate ratio, miss ratio, and job count against compute cost and object size; GPU eviction uses argmin_{o∈Q} T_n(o) + 1/h(o) + c(o), favoring recently accessed and expensive-to-recompute objects. Third, compiler integration: six program-level rewrites are applied — (1) a prefetch operator triggers remote Spark/GPU jobs asynchronously, enabling CPU–Spark–GPU pipeline parallelism; (2) an async broadcast rewrite transfers local results to distributed workers without blocking; (3) delayed caching defers Spark RDD materialization until the n-th cache hit (n estimated from loop structure and reuse potential) to avoid unnecessary persistence overhead; (4) eviction injection identifies allocation-pattern loops and inserts evict instructions for periodic GPU memory cleanup; (5) two workload-aware checkpoint placement rewrites eliminate RDD recomputation from overlapping Spark jobs and iterative algorithms; (6) MAXPARALLELIZE operator ordering (Algorithm 1) linearizes remote operator chains in descending length order to maximize concurrent execution of Spark and GPU pipelines. Key Results All experiments run on an 8+1-node cluster with NVIDIA A40 GPUs (48 GB), 38 GB Spark driver memory, and 230 GB executor memory. Baselines include vanilla SystemDS (Base), async-enabled SystemDS (Base-A), LIMA, HELIX, CoorDL, Clipper, VISTA, and PyTorch. Reuse overhead: for inputs ≥8 MB, tracing and probing add negligible overhead; cache hits yield 1.3x–3x speedup. For inputs 800B–8 MB, overhead is 1.3x–2x execution time increase, dominated by interpretation and bookkeeping. A 900 MB cache consistently achieves 1.2x speedup; 5 GB and 30 GB caches yield progressively higher returns. HCV (hyper-parameter tuning of linear regression, 5–100 GB synthetic data): MEMPHIS achieves up to 9.6x speedup over Base by reusing X^T X and X^T y and exploiting prefetch-enabled parallel execution. HELIX and LIMA plateau at ~20 GB (local intermediates only); MEMPHIS continues scaling to 100 GB via distributed reuse. MEMPHIS is ~20% faster than MPH-NA due to MAXPARALLELIZE. HBAND (Hyperband model selection, L2SVM + multi-class logistic regression, 5–20 GB): MEMPHIS achieves 2.6x/2.5x speedups for 5 GB/20 GB inputs over Base; outperforms HELIX and LIMA by ~40%; delayed caching is critical for this workload. TLVIS (transfer learning with vision models, AlexNet/VGG16/ResNet18, CIFAR-10 and ImageNet at 10K images each): MEMPHIS achieves 2x speedup on CIFAR-10 and 3x on ImageNet by reusing feature extraction intermediates and recycling 30K–17.5K GPU pointers. VISTA performs similarly via CSE; PyTorch-Clr runs out of memory on ImageNet; PyTorch is 1.9x faster than Base-G (SystemDS-GPU) for TLVIS but 1.5x slower than MEMPHIS. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. Genuinely novel contributions: (1) unified hierarchical lineage cache abstraction spanning eager CPU, lazy distributed Spark, and asynchronous GPU backends in a single system; (2) GPU pointer recycling integrated with lineage-based eviction scoring; (3) MAXPARALLELIZE operator ordering for inter-backend pipeline parallelism; (4) delayed caching with automatic parameter tuning driven by reuse potential estimation. The lineage tracing substrate extends LIMA [71] — not entirely new — but extension to Spark and GPU with compiler co-design constitutes a substantive system-level contribution beyond a standard application of known techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility The evaluation scales data size, task complexity, and instruction counts systematically across three end-to-end ML pipelines. Multiple SystemDS configurations (Base, Base-A, MPH-NA, MPH) are compared to attribute gains to async vs. reuse components. However, no ablation table isolates individual compiler rewrites (delayed caching, eviction injection, async prefetch, MAXPARALLELIZE) independently — the reader cannot determine which contributes most. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported (common for systems papers, but worth noting). MEMPHIS is integrated into open-source Apache SystemDS (https://github.com/apache/systemds), making it reproducible within that stack. This paper is a stated minor revision of EDBT 2025 [69], published in SIGMOD Record — a lower peer-review bar than a full conference or journal submission. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built framework evaluated by deployment on a real multi-node cluster. - Practical: Runs on commodity clusters with NVIDIA A40 GPUs and Apache Spark; no special hardware required beyond what production ML clusters commonly have. - Efficient: Lineage tracing overhead is negligible for inputs ≥8 MB; GPU pointer recycling avoids cudaMalloc for fixed-size mini-batch intermediates; Spark lazy GC reclaims dangling RDDs asynchronously without blocking execution. - Effective: Evidence from three representative end-to-end pipelines shows 1.2x–9.6x speedups over strong baselines (Base, LIMA, HELIX, PyTorch), directly solving redundant recomputation for iterative ML workloads. - Scalable: Tested up to 100 GB for distributed workloads (HCV) and 20 GB for model selection (HBAND); GPU experiments use 10K image batches. The paper does not test at 10x–100x these scales, and LLM-scale workloads are mentioned as motivation but not evaluated. Delivers a software artifact integrated into Apache SystemDS; no trained model weights or standalone library release. Strengths - The unified lineage abstraction spanning three fundamentally heterogeneous backends (eager CPU, lazy distributed, asynchronous GPU) without user-code changes is a non-trivial design achievement that prior backend-specific systems cannot replicate. - Integration of reuse with memory management (lazy GC for dangling Spark RDDs, GPU pointer recycling, cost-based eviction) addresses the tight coupling that makes piecemeal solutions insufficient — this holistic view is the paper's clearest intellectual contribution. - MAXPARALLELIZE delivers concrete inter-backend pipeline parallelism (~20% additional speedup over MPH-NA in HCV) that existing operator schedulers cannot exploit without the lineage substrate. - Delayed caching with automatic parameter tuning is a pragmatic solution to Spark's lazy evaluation problem that avoids both under-caching (repeated job triggers) and over-caching (unnecessary materialization overhead). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) reuse potential is workload-dependent, so lineage tracing benefits vary; (2) fine-grained lineage tracing requires control-flow access, limiting applicability to Python-hosted libraries (e.g., LIMA's Python mode) without compilation; (3) non-deterministic primitives may yield slightly different results under varying configurations; (4) memory management and reuse in Spark applies to lazily-evaluated frameworks, not natively to Dask or Ray without extensions; (5) a cost-based, reuse-aware optimizer jointly optimizing query planning and operator scheduling is identified as important future work. The authors confront these limitations directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No per-optimization ablation: the paper compares MPH-NA vs. MPH but does not isolate delayed caching, eviction injection, async prefetch, and MAXPARALLELIZE individually. Given that these rewrites interact, the contribution of each is unclear. - Generalizability is claimed for TensorFlow, JAX, and PyTorch-based stacks (Section 5.4) but demonstrated only within the SystemDS/Apache ecosystem; the claim rests on architectural argument, not experimental evidence. - The eviction scoring functions (Eqs. 1–2) are heuristic with no theoretical bound on suboptimality or miss rate; comparison with an optimal offline policy is absent. - The GPU evaluation uses only 10K image batches (a small-scale ImageNet subset), not full ImageNet-scale training — the scalability claim for DNN training is not stress-tested. - SIGMOD Record explicitly notes this is a minor revision of EDBT 2025 [69]; the delta from the conference version is not stated, making it unclear what is new relative to the published conference paper. Bottom Line ML systems researchers and SystemDS practitioners gain a deployable, open-source framework with well-evidenced 1.2x–9.6x speedups from principled multi-backend reuse; those working outside the SystemDS/Spark ecosystem will find the design valuable conceptually but cannot adopt the artifact without substantial porting effort.
Citations
#57 — Innovative (72)

Turing Test on Screen: A Benchmark for Mobile GUI Agent Humanization (19 ▲ )

Jiachen Zhu, Lingyu Yang, Rong Shan, Congmin Zheng, Zeyu Zheng, Weiwen Liu, Yong Yu, Weinan Zhang, Jianghao Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (80) high — new problem formulation (agent humanization vs biometrics) plus new dataset and counter-intuitive empirical findings. Rigor (60) limited by single device/OS/cultural context, no error bars, undescribed train/test split risking user-identity leakage, though theorems are proved and code/data released. Non-Obviousness (75) — Fake Action increasing detectability is genuinely surprising. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) — author limitations are honest but the single-device evaluation plus untested temporal-sequence detectors leave the central numbers exposed. Impact (75) — released benchmark and wrappers, but humanization fails to reach random-chance against ML detectors so practical evasion utility is bounded. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 71.5 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Agent Humanization Benchmark (AHB), which formalizes the adversarial interaction between mobile GUI agents and platform bot-detectors as a MinMax optimization problem, collects a large-scale touch-dynamics dataset, proves three theorems bounding detector efficacy, and evaluates four humanization strategies on the imitability-utility Pareto tradeoff. - Why it's important: LMM-based GUI agents are trivially detectable by behavioral biometrics (XGBoost achieves 0.995–1.000 accuracy against raw agents), causing platforms to block legitimate users who deploy AI assistants — a real-world failure mode documented by the Doubao/WeChat incident in late 2025. - How it does it: An external post-processing wrapper replaces mechanically-generated touch trajectories and timing with samples drawn from (or affine-matched to) a database of real human gestures, formally reducing the Jensen-Shannon divergence between agent and human distributions and thereby bounding any detector's accuracy near random chance. - The good: History Matching reduces individual maxDev detectability from 0.9969 to 0.5515 (near random) while preserving task utility, and the empirical finding that naive Fake Action injection increases ML detectability even while defeating interval rules is non-obvious and actionable. - The missing: The entire evaluation runs on a single Xiaomi Mi Max 2 running MIUI 11.0.2.0 with exclusively Chinese users and Chinese apps, making cross-device, cross-OS, and cross-cultural generalizability entirely untested. - Read it or not: Essential for researchers working on GUI agent deployment, behavioral biometrics, or adversarial agent-platform dynamics; can be skipped by those focused solely on task-performance benchmarks who have no interest in detection evasion. Problem & Motivation LMM-based GUI agents executing mobile tasks produce touch trajectories that are kinematically distinct from human input: near-perfect straight-line swipes (raw agents cluster at maxDev ≈ 0 versus a broad human distribution spanning 0–100), near-zero tap durations (instantaneous event injection vs. human Gaussian 0.05–0.10s), and inference-induced action intervals of 5–80 seconds versus sub-second human baselines. Mobile platforms that depend on the attention economy (advertising impressions, engagement metrics) deploy behavioral bot-detection that trivially flags these agents, blocking service and harming legitimate users who deploy AI assistants — concretely illustrated by the Doubao Mobile Assistant incident in late 2025, where WeChat and banking apps triggered security blocks due to INJECT_EVENTS-based automation. The LMM-agent research community has focused exclusively on task utility and adversarial robustness against content injections (jailbreaks, prompt injections), overlooking the prerequisite gating problem: platforms will filter agents on behavioral signals before any content-level interaction occurs. No systematic framework existed to study or benchmark the trade-off between agent survivability (imitability) and task performance (utility). Key Idea The core technical insight is that detector performance is upper-bounded by the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the human and agent behavioral distributions, so reducing JS divergence — by replacing mechanical agent trajectories with real human gesture samples via affine transformation (rotation + scaling) to match the agent's intended action vector — directly and provably reduces the maximum achievable detection accuracy for any Lipschitz-bounded classifier, with the bound approaching random chance as the empirical history distribution converges to the true human distribution. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Data collection: Interactions across 21 Chinese apps in 5 task clusters (Social Media, Shopping, Video Streaming, Trip Planning, Office & Learning) are recorded on a single Xiaomi Mi Max 2 (MIUI 11.0.2.0) from 4 human sub-populations (young male/female, middle-aged, elderly) and 4 GUI agents (UI-TARS, MobileAgent-E with GPT-4o/Claude-3.5-Sonnet, AgentCPM, AutoGLM), yielding 37.7M human and 243M agent MotionEvents. (2) Feature extraction: 24 statistical features per action covering velocity percentiles (v20/50/80), acceleration percentiles (a20/50/80), deviation-from-linearity metrics (dev20/50/80, maxDev), geometric features (meanResultantLength, direction, ratio_end_to_length), and temporal features (duration, startX/Y, endX/Y); information gain is computed as normalized mutual information I(F;U)/H(U). (3) Detection hierarchy: Rule-based detectors threshold on swipe accuracy, time-interval accuracy, and tap-duration accuracy; learning-based detectors use SVM and XGBoost on the 24-feature vector. The choice of SVM/XGBoost over sequence models is justified by observing near-perfect accuracy saturation for raw agents, making deeper architectures redundant. (4) Humanization strategies: Four external-wrapper methods are evaluated — B-Spline noise injection (curved paths via normally-distributed control points around the direct chord); History Matching (affine-transforms a retrieved human reference trajectory via p' = s·R(θ)·(p − p_ref_start) + P_start to match the agent's intended swipe vector); Fake Action injection (inserts micro-scrolls at Poisson rate λ=0.9 Hz during inference idle periods to mask the long-tail interval distribution); and Longer Presses (samples tap durations from a Gaussian fitted to human data). Critical design decisions: (a) external wrapper over internal fine-tuning is justified by model-agnostic compatibility — well-motivated; (b) restricting focus to MotionEvents rather than SensorEvents is explicitly scope-limited, not fully justified as sufficient for real-world evasion; (c) SVM/XGBoost as detector baseline is justified by saturation but leaves the paper untested against temporal sequence models. Key Results Raw agents are trivially detectable: XGBoost achieves 0.995–1.000 and SVM achieves 0.98–1.000 across all 5 task clusters. History Matching (Online Rotation & Match) reduces maxDev single-feature detectability from 0.9969 to 0.5515 in Social Media and meanResultantLength from 0.9878 to 0.6818, but full-feature SVM accuracy only drops from 0.9817 to 0.8750 and XGBoost from 1.000 to 0.9773 — humanization against multi-feature classifiers remains substantially incomplete. Fake Action injection reduces interval-rule accuracy to ~0.52 (near random) but causes Trip Planning task success to collapse from 0.75 to 0.15 in the Online + Fake configuration, confirming a hard imitability-utility Pareto tradeoff. Long Press reduces tap-duration detection from 0.9840 to 0.6133. A key counter-intuitive finding: naive Fake Action injection increases overall ML detectability despite defeating the interval rule, because hard-coded micro-gestures introduce new, predictable mechanical artifacts detectable in other feature dimensions. The combined best offline configurations achieve SVM accuracy of 0.87–0.94 across clusters — above the 0.5 random-chance target, so no configuration fully passes the Turing Test on Screen against ML detectors. Feature importance: maxDev (IG=0.6726) and meanResultantLength (IG=0.6581) are most discriminative; acceleration statistics (a50 IG=0.3583) and coordinates (endX IG=0.5160) are least. Baselines are current (UI-TARS 2025, MobileAgent-E with GPT-4o/Claude, AgentCPM, AutoGLM), but all tested on one device. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formal framing of GUI agent survivability as a MinMax adversarial game against behavioral biometric detectors is genuinely new; the AHB dual-axis benchmark (imitability × utility) is a novel evaluation framework with no prior equivalent; the empirical discovery of the non-monotone interaction between humanization strategies (Fake Actions defeat interval rules while increasing ML detectability) is non-obvious. The individual component techniques — B-splines, affine trajectory matching, Poisson fake-action injection — are applications of standard signal processing and stochastic processes to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at github.com/Gebro13/Passing-the-Turing-Test-on-Screen-Agent-Humanization-Benchmark and data at huggingface.co/datasets/lyyang2766/Passing-the-Turing-Test-on-Screen-Agent-Humanization-Benchmark. No ablations isolating which subcomponents of History Matching (rotation vs. scaling vs. reference selection) drive performance improvements. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-run seeds are reported for any result. The three theorems are complete and proofs are provided in the appendix; assumptions (human distribution absolutely continuous, agent distribution on lower-dimensional manifold, Lipschitz-bounded detector class) are stated explicitly. The evaluation is restricted to one device and one OS version — a significant reproducibility limitation for a benchmark paper. Train/test split methodology for the SVM/XGBoost classifiers is not described, raising the possibility of within-user data leakage inflating detection accuracy estimates. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (built and run the wrapper on a real device with real agents). - Practical: B-Spline and History Matching wrappers require only standard linear algebra; they run on the phone in real time during agent action execution on commercial Android hardware. - Efficient: History Matching introduces retrieval and affine transformation latency that the authors acknowledge causes agents to miss transient UI events (e.g., closing popup windows); Fake Action injection adds a constant background load of ~0.9 Hz micro-gestures, and the latency cost is not quantified in milliseconds. - Effective: Strategies demonstrably reduce individual feature detectability and defeat rule-based detectors, but no configuration reduces SVM/XGBoost accuracy below 0.87 across all clusters — the stated goal of reaching ~0.5 (random guessing) is not achieved against multi-feature ML detectors. - Scalable: Tested exclusively on one device; no experiments across iOS, stock Android, different screen resolutions, or OEM skins with different touch sampling rates. The software artifact (wrapper + benchmark harness) is released but validated only in this narrow hardware context. Strengths - The MinMax formalization with three proved theorems provides a rigorous theoretical foundation that elevates the paper beyond a purely empirical benchmark; the proofs are clean and the connection between JS divergence and detector accuracy bounds is directly actionable. - The dataset scale (37.7M human events, 243M agent events, 4 demographic sub-populations, 4 state-of-the-art agents, 21 apps) represents genuine infrastructure investment that will benefit follow-on work. - The empirical identification of the imitability-utility Pareto frontier — with concrete numbers showing task success collapsing from 0.75 to 0.15 under naive temporal humanization in Trip Planning — is a specific, quantified finding that directly informs the next generation of agent design. - Framing the problem as a "Turing Test on Screen" and connecting it to real-world deployment failures (Doubao incident) grounds an otherwise abstract benchmark in practical stakes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) scope is intentionally restricted to MotionEvents, excluding SensorEvents (gyroscope, magnetometer) that real anti-bot systems also use, because simulating sensor signals requires kernel-level intervention outside agent policy design; (2) the External Wrapper introduces latency that can cause missed UI events; (3) History Matching requires an offline human trajectory database; (4) future work is needed for end-to-end humanization, personalized humanization, and cross-modal extension (typing dynamics, reading/scrolling behavior). The authors confront the ethical dual-use concern directly, arguing the work serves long-term platform security through Red Teaming transparency and releasing under a restrictive research license. This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps The single-device evaluation on MIUI 11.0.2.0 is the most serious limitation: touch event sampling rates, event injection APIs, and anti-bot heuristics differ substantially across iOS, stock Android, and OEM skins — the quantitative detection accuracy numbers cannot be assumed to transfer. All human data comes exclusively from Chinese users on Chinese apps (Taobao, WeChat, Toutiao, iQIYI, etc.), so biomechanical feature distributions may differ for Western users on different app layouts and interaction patterns. The paper justifies SVM/XGBoost over LSTM/Transformer by pointing to raw-agent saturation, but this justification does not hold for humanized agents where the distributional gap is reduced — humanized agents may be detectable by temporal sequence models while evading feature-statistic classifiers. No person-level cross-validation procedure is described for the training/test split of the supervised detectors, which risks inflating detection accuracy through user-identity leakage. The rate and type of unintended state changes caused by Fake Action injection are not measured directly — the utility collapse is captured by final task success but the mechanism (how many fake actions triggered unintended navigation) is not instrumented. Bottom Line This paper delivers a timely, well-grounded formalization of a genuinely important new problem — GUI agent behavioral survivability — with a publicly released benchmark, substantial dataset, and the concrete empirical finding that naive humanization strategies fail against multi-feature detectors while introducing severe utility regressions; its primary weakness is that the entire quantitative evaluation rests on a single device and a single cultural ecosystem, limiting how far the specific numbers can be trusted beyond that narrow context.
Recommended 2604.09574 PDF
#58 — Innovative (71)

HY-World 2.0: A Multi-Modal World Model for Reconstructing, Generating, and Simulating 3D Worlds (68 ▲ )

Team HY-World, Chenjie Cao, Xuhui Zuo, Zhenwei Wang, Yisu Zhang, Junta Wu, Zhenyang Liu, Yuning Gong, Yang Liu, Bo Yuan, Chao Zhang, Coopers Li, Dongyuan Guo, Fan Yang, Haiyu Zhang, Hang Cao, Jianchen Zhu, Jiaxin Lin, Jie Xiao, Jihong Zhang, Junlin Yu, Lei Wang, Lifu Wang, Lilin Wang, Linus, Minghui Chen, Peng He, Penghao Zhao, Qi Chen, Rui Chen, Rui Shao, Sicong Liu, Wangchen Qin, Xiaochuan Niu, Xiang Yuan, Yi Sun, Yifei Tang, Yifu Sun, Yihang Lian, Yonghao Tan, Yuhong Liu, Yuyang Yin, Zhiyuan Min, Tengfei Wang, Chunchao Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (55) moderate — mostly integration of prior components with incremental extensions (Keyframe-VAE, normalized RoPE, SSM++). Rigor (70) thorough ablations across 4 stages but no seeds/error bars, preprint, author-designed trajectories introduce benchmark bias. Non-Obviousness (65) — Keyframe-VAE spatio-temporal-compression diagnosis is non-obvious. Completeness (65) — honest limitation acknowledgment but Marble comparison is qualitative only. Resilience (60) — the headline open-vs-Marble claim is unverified numerically. Impact (90) — full open release of code, weights, WorldLens platform is highly consequential for the open-source 3D world ecosystem. Weighted: 0.15×55+0.15×70+0.10×65+0.15×65+0.15×60+0.30×90 ≈ 71.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 90
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HY-World 2.0, an open-source multi-modal framework that unifies 3D world generation (from text or single images) and reconstruction (from multi-view images/video) into a single four-stage pipeline producing navigable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes. - Why it's important: No prior open-source system bridges generative and reconstructive 3D world modeling at quality competitive with closed-source commercial products like Marble, leaving robotics simulation, game development, and embodied AI research without a reproducible foundation. - How it does it: A four-stage pipeline — panorama synthesis via MMDiT (HY-Pano 2.0), geometry-aware trajectory planning (WorldNav), memory-augmented keyframe-latent video diffusion (WorldStereo 2.0), and feed-forward reconstruction with 3DGS composition (WorldMirror 2.0 + MaskGaussian) — where the key enabling trick is operating in a keyframe latent space that avoids spatio-temporal VAE compression artifacts that otherwise destroy geometric fidelity. - The good: WorldMirror 2.0 achieves the best surface normal estimation on ScanNet (mean 12.3), NYUv2, and iBims-1 at medium resolution, surpassing dedicated single-task methods, while the full pipeline generates a complete 3D world in ~712 seconds on NVIDIA H20 GPUs with MaskGaussian reducing Gaussian count by 77% at only −0.14 dB PSNR cost. - The missing: Comparison against the closed-source Marble system is qualitative only — no quantitative end-to-end benchmark measures the generative path's 3DGS quality, leaving the headline competitive claim unverified by numbers. - Read it or not: Read if you work on 3D scene generation, open-source world models, or reconstruction pipelines — this is a well-ablated, fully released system with immediate practical value; skip if you need peer-reviewed theoretical results or focused single-contribution novelty. Problem & Motivation The 3D world modeling field is bifurcated: generative methods synthesize visually compelling, explorable scenes from sparse inputs (text, single images) but hallucinate geometry in unseen regions; reconstruction methods recover accurate 3D from dense multi-view inputs but cannot extrapolate beyond observed views. The one commercial system (Marble) that credibly bridges this gap is closed-source. Practitioners in robotics simulation, game development, and embodied AI consequently lack an open, reproducible framework covering text-to-3D, image-to-3D, and multi-view-to-3D reconstruction under a unified model. Prior open-source approaches (HY-World 1.0, WorldStereo 1.0, WorldMirror 1.0) each addressed only a slice of this problem. Key Idea The central technical insight is that standard video VAEs apply spatio-temporal compression that causes motion blur and geometric distortion under large camera movements — exactly the large-baseline trajectories needed for reliable 3D reconstruction. Replacing the video latent space with a keyframe latent space (Keyframe-VAE: spatial-only image encoding applied independently to sparse keyframes) preserves high-frequency appearance and geometry cues, enabling downstream reconstruction to work on generated views nearly as well as on real captures. This design change unlocks the rest of the pipeline: precise camera control, multi-trajectory consistency via memory modules, and high-quality 3DGS composition. Methodology Stage 1 — HY-Pano 2.0: A Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer (MMDiT) processes text or perspective image inputs concatenated with panoramic noise latents as a unified token sequence, learning an implicit perspective-to-equirectangular (ERP) projection without requiring camera intrinsics. Circular padding in latent space and linear pixel blending at 360° boundaries eliminate seam artifacts. Training data mixes real-world high-resolution panoramas with Unreal Engine synthetic renders. Stage 2 — WorldNav: Given the panorama, constructs a global point cloud via MoGe2 + GPU-accelerated LSMR (48 perspective views sampled from ERP), semantic segmentation via Qwen3-VL + SAM3, and a NavMesh via Recast Navigation with KD-Tree boundary erosion. Five heuristic trajectory modes (regular, surrounding, reconstruct-aware, wandering, aerial; up to 35 paths total) are computed via Dijkstra shortest paths and ray-casting collision detection. No learned trajectory planning is used. Stage 3 — WorldStereo 2.0: Three training stages: (a) domain-adaption fine-tunes a pretrained video DiT with Keyframe-VAE and explicit camera control via Plücker rays + warped reference point cloud, freezing cross-attention and FFN layers to balance camera precision and generalization; (b) middle-training adds Global-Geometric Memory (GGM: extended point clouds rendered as geometric video guidance) and Spatial-Stereo Memory++ (SSM++: retrieval-based horizontal stitching where each target keyframe is spatially concatenated with its nearest retrieved reference, RoPE modified to share temporal indices); (c) post-distillation via Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) to a 4-step inference model. Loss: photometric (L1 + SSIM + LPIPS), no adversarial term. Critical design decisions: (1) Keyframe-VAE — well-justified by ablation showing Video-VAE yields 64.39% user quality preference vs 60.95% for partially-frozen Keyframe-VAE; (2) SSM++ spatial stitching vs temporal concatenation — ablation (Tab. 8, Config A*) shows temporal concatenation degrades all metrics; (3) freezing cross-attention + FFN — ablation isolates this as the optimal operating point (RotErr 0.492 vs 0.578 for fully unfrozen). Stage 4 — World Composition: WorldMirror 2.0 estimates depth and normals for generated keyframes; depths are RANSAC-linearly-aligned to the panoramic point cloud with outlier detection via 90th-percentile relative deviation across 9 anchor depth values. Back-projected aligned depths expand the point cloud, which initializes 3DGS. 3DGS optimization uses: photometric loss (L1+SSIM+LPIPS), geometric loss (sparse depth + dense normal cosine similarity), scale regularization, and MaskGaussian (Gumbel-Softmax per-Gaussian binary masks + squared average mask activation for sparsity). Sky regions are excluded from adaptive densification to suppress floaters. WorldMirror 2.0 key improvements: normalized RoPE (patch indices mapped to [−1,1] regardless of resolution, converting position extrapolation to interpolation), depth-to-normal loss (L_d2n: back-projects predicted depth to normals via cross-products, supervises against GT normals or monocular pseudo-normals), explicit depth mask prediction head (binary cross-entropy for per-pixel validity). Key Results HY-Pano 2.0 achieves best CLIP-T (0.258 vs 0.250 Matrix3D) and best Q-Align quality/aesthetics on both T2P and I2P tasks across DiT360, Matrix3D, HY-World 1.0, CubeDiff, GenEx baselines (Tab. 4). WorldStereo 2.0 achieves highest point cloud AUC on Tanks-and-Temples (60.09 vs 58.19 FlashWorld) and MipNeRF360 (65.64 vs 65.32 Gen3C), and best camera control metrics vs SEVA, Gen3C, WorldPlay, WorldCompass, WorldStereo 1.0 (RotErr 0.492 / TransErr 0.968 / ATE 1.768 vs WorldStereo 1.0's 0.762/1.245/2.141; Tab. 5–6). WorldMirror 2.0 surpasses all baselines (Fast3R, CUT3R, FLARE, VGGT, π³, WorldMirror 1.0) on 7-Scenes, NRGBD, DTU at medium resolution; at high resolution (756×1036), where WorldMirror 1.0's AUC@30 drops from 86.13 to 66.29, WorldMirror 2.0 maintains 86.89. Surface normal mean error on ScanNet: 12.3 at medium (vs 13.8 WorldMirror 1.0, 16.0 StableNormal). Novel view PSNR: WorldMirror 2.0 stable at ~20 across resolutions; WorldMirror 1.0 collapses to 17.78 at high resolution. MaskGaussian ablation (Tab. 9, 10 scenes): reduces Gaussian count from 5.254M (adaptive densification without mask) to 1.383M while maintaining PSNR 25.017 (vs 25.158 baseline). Full pipeline runs in 712 seconds on H20 GPUs. Vs Marble: qualitative comparison only (Figs. 23–24). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD (multiple components). The unified generation+reconstruction framework is genuinely novel as an open-source system. Individual contributions — Keyframe-VAE (builds on FlashWorld), SSM++ (extends WorldStereo 1.0's SSM), normalized RoPE (adapts DINOv3's approach), depth-to-normal loss (standard technique applied in this context), MaskGaussian (Gumbel-Softmax masking for 3DGS) — each represent incremental extensions of prior ideas. The combination and its open-source release constitute the main novelty; no single component is a fundamental algorithmic invention. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation coverage is thorough across all four stages: 8 configurations for WorldStereo 2.0 memory/distillation (Tab. 8), qualitative trajectory planning ablation (Fig. 19), 5-row 3DGS component ablation (Tab. 9), 7-row camera control ablation (Tab. 7). Error bars and multiple seed reporting are absent throughout. Code and weights are publicly released at github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/HY-World-2.0. The paper is a preprint — not peer-reviewed. A methodological concern: the WorldStereo 2.0 single-view benchmark (Tab. 5) uses pseudo-GT point clouds from multi-view stereo, which introduces noise into the evaluation standard. The WorldStereo evaluation uses trajectories designed by the authors ("more challenging camera trajectories than the original benchmark"), not purely independent benchmarks. The end-to-end world generation quality is compared to Marble qualitatively only, citing version-locked Marble 1.0 (as of March 30, 2026). Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA H20 GPUs; the 4-GPU SP+BF16+FSDP configuration handles 256-view WorldMirror inference; single H20 suffices for panorama and WorldStereo. H20s are commercially available datacenter GPUs, not consumer hardware; consumer-grade performance is not reported. - Efficient: End-to-end world generation in 712 seconds on H20; 4-step DMD inference for WorldStereo 2.0 substantially reduces per-generation cost; BF16+SP+FSDP reduce WorldMirror 2.0 memory from 59.26 GB to 42.71 GB (128 views, 4 GPUs) and wall-clock from 18.00s to 6.27s. - Effective: Consistently outperforms open-source alternatives on all sub-benchmarks; the generative pipeline outperforms Marble qualitatively for input fidelity and geometric consistency across viewpoints, but this is a vendor-self-report without quantitative validation. - Scalable: Tested at up to 256 views for WorldMirror 2.0 and 1036×1036 resolution; beyond this scale is not evaluated. Error accumulation in multi-trajectory generation is acknowledged but not stress-tested. Delivers: code + weights (GitHub), WorldLens interactive rendering platform, full sub-model weights for HY-Pano 2.0, WorldStereo 2.0, WorldMirror 2.0. Strengths - Keyframe-VAE motivation and validation: the insight that spatio-temporal compression systematically destroys geometry for reconstruction is well-articulated, and the ablation in Tabs. 7–8 + Fig. 8 provides clear causal evidence. - Normalized RoPE: the resolution-extrapolation problem in WorldMirror 1.0 is diagnosed precisely (cosine similarity drops to 0.23 at 2048 resolution with standard RoPE), and the fix is principled and analytically verified (Fig. 13). - Depth-to-normal loss: using back-projected normals from predicted depth as an auxiliary supervision signal, applied on all datasets regardless of normal GT availability, is a practical design that demonstrably improves geometric coupling (WorldMirror 2.0 achieves best normal estimation despite WorldMirror being primarily a reconstruction model, not a normal estimation model). - Open release scope: releasing code, all sub-models, WorldLens platform, and technical details provides a reproducible foundation that Marble and similar systems do not offer. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) WorldMirror 2.0 still struggles in highly challenging outdoor open scenes (explicitly noted in Sec. 7.1.2); (2) sky regions lack depth supervision, causing floaters addressed only heuristically; (3) comparison with Marble is locked to version 1.0 as of March 30, 2026, and qualitative only. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps The most significant gap is the absence of a quantitative end-to-end benchmark for the generative path: no FID, no FVD on novel views, no user study for the complete text/image-to-3DGS pipeline quality vs. Marble — only qualitative figures, which cannot be reproduced or challenged by third parties. WorldNav trajectory planning is entirely heuristic (five hand-designed modes) with no learned component and no quantitative evaluation of whether specific trajectory choices improve downstream reconstruction quality beyond the ablation in Fig. 19. Hardware reporting is H20-exclusive; since H20 is a high-memory datacenter GPU, practitioners on A100 or consumer GPUs cannot predict their experience. No evaluation of temporal consistency of generated scenes is reported; only spatial frame-level metrics appear. The depth alignment procedure (RANSAC + per-frame linear scale/shift) assumes a linear relationship between WorldMirror depth and panoramic point cloud depth — this assumption fails in outdoor scenes with large depth ranges, consistent with the acknowledged outdoor limitation. Bottom Line Practitioners building open-source 3D world generation or reconstruction pipelines gain immediate, direct value from HY-World 2.0 as a deployable system with well-ablated design choices and full code release — but the critical claim of competitive quality vs. closed-source Marble rests entirely on qualitative figures, and no stronger alternative covering this exact scope currently exists in the open-source ecosystem.
Recommended 2604.14268 PDF
#59 — Innovative (70)

SpecMoE: A Fast and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Inference via Self-Assisted Speculative Decoding

Jehyeon Bang, Eunyeong Cho, Ranggi Hwang, Jinha Chung, Minsoo Rhu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (70) drives via 30% weight — concrete training-free MoE serving optimization with measured 4.30× throughput and 76.73% PCIe traffic reduction at batch 256, but no public code release caps it. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) reflect a clean adaptation of speculative decoding to CPU-offloaded MoE using in-situ hot experts as draft — non-trivial insight, not a new formulation. Rigor (72) is solid: oracular MoE-Overlap upper bound, multi-knob ablations on N and γ, three model families, but no seeds/error bars, no BLEU/ROUGE for sampling-based acceptance, preprint status. Completeness (68) and Resilience (70) hold up but are bounded by single-GPU evaluation when target deployments are multi-GPU TP/PP. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*72+0.10*70+0.15*68+0.15*70+0.30*70 ≈ 70.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 68 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SpecMoE, a CPU-offloaded MoE inference system that uses a self-assisted speculative decoding algorithm — constructing the draft model from hot experts already resident in GPU memory — to reduce CPU-to-GPU expert migration traffic and increase inference throughput by up to 4.30× without any additional model training. - Why it's important: In production MoE inference, expert migration over the narrow PCIe channel (up to 90.11% of total decoding latency) is the dominant bottleneck, and existing overlap and caching strategies break down at large batch sizes where many distinct experts must be fetched simultaneously. - How it does it: During the speculation phase, a draft sub-model formed from non-expert (always-GPU) layers plus N temporally hot experts per MoE block generates γ tokens entirely in GPU memory; the target model then verifies all draft tokens in a single batched pass, coalescing and minimizing expert migrations to once per verification round. - The good: At batch size 256 on NLLB-MoE, SpecMoE reduces CPU-to-GPU data transfer by 76.73% vs. MoE-OnDemand and 71.89% vs. MoE-Caching, achieving 4.30× throughput improvement, while using far fewer cached experts (N=4 vs. MoE-Caching's 13 out of 128). - The missing: Evaluation is conducted on a single NVIDIA H100 with a single PCIe link; multi-GPU tensor- or pipeline-parallel deployments — increasingly the practical production setting for 94–218 GB models — are not evaluated, and the results may not generalize to distributed inference topologies. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers building or optimizing CPU-offloaded MoE serving stacks, particularly those targeting large-batch throughput; practitioners deploying small-batch or uniformly-activated MoE models will see more modest gains. Problem & Motivation: Serving large MoE models (e.g., Llama-4, DeepSeek-R1, Mixtral) on memory-constrained systems requires offloading sparse expert parameters to CPU DRAM, since model capacity (54–218 GB) far exceeds single-GPU HBM. Each autoregressive decoding step must fetch the sparsely activated expert subset from CPU over PCIe, where expert migration accounts for up to 90.11% of end-to-end decoding latency on a single H100 connected via PCIe 5.0 at 64 GB/s. This bottleneck worsens at larger batch sizes: activating more experts per step means proportionally more PCIe traffic, reaching over 28 GB transferred per decoding step at batch size 256 for NLLB-MoE. Prior approaches — compute-communication overlap (MoE-Overlap) and hot-expert caching (MoE-Caching) — provide diminishing returns at larger batches precisely when serving economics demand them most. Key Idea: The core insight is that the target MoE model already contains a small subset of "hot" experts (whose activation is highly skewed and temporally local) that can serve as a lightweight draft model without any training, if those experts are pinned in GPU memory. By running speculative decoding with this self-constructed draft, multiple output tokens can be generated per expert migration event, amortizing PCIe communication over several tokens rather than incurring it once per token. Methodology: SpecMoE operates in two interleaved phases. In the speculation phase, a draft model — comprising all non-expert layers (MHA, layer norms, always GPU-resident) plus N=4 hot experts per MoE block — generates γ=10 draft tokens autoregressively entirely within GPU memory, incurring zero CPU-to-GPU transfers. Because the gating function may route tokens to experts not in GPU memory, an affinity-based expert selection mechanism redirects such tokens to the most similar available draft expert using a precomputed offline L2-distance affinity table (under 200KB for NLLB-MoE). In the verification phase, the full target model processes all γ draft tokens in parallel, fetching any required experts from CPU in a single coalesced batch; only experts activated during verification migrate over PCIe, and because multiple tokens are verified simultaneously, redundant migrations for the same expert across tokens are eliminated. An expert replacement policy updates which N experts are pinned as draft experts after each verification step by tracking temporal hotness: the N most frequently activated experts during the just-completed verification round replace any draft experts not in that set, with no additional PCIe cost since newly hot experts were already fetched during verification. The acceptance criterion uses greedy matching (speculated tokens must exactly match the target model's greedy output) for NLLB-MoE and sampling-based acceptance for Mixtral-8x7B and Llama-4-Scout. The system is implemented on top of a CPU offloading-based open-source inference framework using PyTorch 2.5.0 with CUDA 12.6 and HuggingFace Transformers 4.51.0. Critical design decisions: (1) N=4 draft experts per block — the paper ablates N from 2 to 16 and finds N=4 optimal for NLLB-MoE (batch 256), since larger N increases draft computation latency without proportionally improving token acceptance; this is well-justified. (2) Affinity table for approximate expert routing — redirecting tokens to the most L2-similar expert approximates the target expert's output, validated empirically by generating 7.23% more tokens per step on average vs. random draft expert selection; the approximation quality is not evaluated on output accuracy metrics. (3) HotTemporal replacement policy — exploiting temporal locality of activation patterns rather than global historical frequency, yielding 1.143× speedup over random selection; justified by the temporal shift property observed in expert activation across inference runs. Key Results: On NLLB-MoE (English-French translation, WMT-14) at batch size 256 on a single H100: - End-to-end latency: 23% of MoE-OnDemand (77% reduction), outperforming MoE-Overlap and MoE-Caching at all batch sizes ≥4. - Throughput: 123.0 tokens/sec vs. ~28.6 for MoE-OnDemand, representing 4.30× speedup. - CPU-to-GPU data transfer: 76.73% reduction vs. MoE-OnDemand; 71.89% vs. MoE-Caching; 10.96× batch-size scaling in data transfer vs. 45.28–50.97× for baselines when going from batch 1 to 256. - Token acceptance: HotTemporal generates 7.265 tokens/step vs. 6.812 for random draft selection (γ=10). On Mixtral-8x7B (CNN-DailyMail text completion): up to 2.17× throughput over MoE-OnDemand (vs. 1.53× for MoE-Overlap/Caching peak). On Llama-4-Scout: up to 1.42× (vs. 1.15× for baselines). The lower gains on these models are attributed to less skewed expert activation (skewness 0.32 and 0.59 vs. 0.84 for NLLB-MoE), meaning the hot-expert assumption holds less strongly. With SSD offloading, SpecMoE achieves 2.25× throughput vs. MoE-OnDemand's 1× (MoE-Overlap: 1.05×, MoE-Caching: 1.29×). MoE-Caching slightly outperforms SpecMoE at batch size 1 for NLLB-MoE, a known limitation. Baselines are current and appropriate; the oracular version of MoE-Overlap (perfect latency hiding) is included for a conservative comparison ceiling. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is applying speculative decoding to CPU-offloaded MoE inference without a separately trained draft model, by exploiting the skewed expert activation distribution inherent in MoE models to construct the draft in situ. The affinity-based expert routing for mismatched gating decisions is also new. The underlying speculative decoding formalism and CPU-offloaded inference framework are existing techniques applied to a new setting with a non-trivial adaptation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for the expert replacement policy (Random vs. HotGlobal vs. HotTemporal, Table II), number of draft experts N (Table III), and draft token count γ (Figure 13). Error bars and significance tests are absent throughout; no multiple random seeds are reported. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.10152v1) accepted at DAC 2026 — peer-reviewed but not yet in final form. Code is not released (no repository URL stated). Evaluation uses publicly available model weights (NLLB-MoE, Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-4-Scout) and standard benchmarks (WMT-14, CNN-DailyMail). The oracular MoE-Overlap baseline is a methodological strength, preventing the authors from comparing against an artificially weak version of the prior approach. Output quality of the speculative decoding (i.e., whether accepted tokens match the target model's output distribution) is asserted for greedy decoding (exact match by construction) but not measured for sampling-based acceptance on Mixtral/Llama-4-Scout tasks via ROUGE or BLEU. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems paper evaluated by deployment measurements). - Practical: Runs on a single commodity server with one NVIDIA H100 and CPU DRAM; requires no changes to the MoE model architecture or additional GPU hardware, though H100 with 96 GB HBM3 is high-end hardware. - Efficient: Draft model uses N=4 experts per block pinned in GPU (no extra GPU memory overhead relative to non-expert parameters already resident); affinity table is under 200KB; no additional training compute required; speculation adds overhead at small batch sizes where it is less beneficial. - Effective: Demonstrates 4.30× throughput improvement at batch 256 on NLLB-MoE and 2.17× on Mixtral-8x7B; gains are driven by the measurable reduction in PCIe traffic (76.73%) rather than by a proxy metric, validating the mechanism. - Scalable: Benefits scale favorably with batch size (the key bottleneck regime) and hold under SSD offloading, but multi-GPU scale is not evaluated; the system targets single-GPU CPU-offloaded inference and has not been tested on distributed tensor-parallel or pipeline-parallel deployments that handle the 94–218 GB models in practice. SpecMoE delivers a software system (custom inference implementation) but no open-source library or trained model release is stated. Strengths: - The self-assisted draft construction is architecturally clean: it requires no training, no additional GPU memory for a separate model, and is compatible with any MoE architecture, making it immediately deployable to existing offloaded serving stacks. - The coalescing of expert migrations to the verification phase is a mechanically sound and well-analyzed optimization — the paper formally derives the expected speedup and shows it grows with batch size, matching empirical results. - Evaluating an oracular MoE-Overlap baseline that perfectly hides all migration latency establishes a credible upper bound for overlap strategies, making SpecMoE's advantage persuasive rather than contingent on a weak baseline. - The SSD-offloading experiments (Figure 16) are a valuable addition showing robustness to even higher-latency storage hierarchies, extending applicability to deployment scenarios beyond DRAM-offloading. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) SpecMoE provides smaller gains for models with less skewed expert activation (Mixtral-8x7B skewness 0.32, Llama-4-Scout 0.59 vs. NLLB-MoE 0.84); they adjust γ downward to 5 for these models to compensate. (2) At batch size 1, MoE-Caching slightly outperforms SpecMoE. (3) Self-assisted speculative decoding cannot be trivially applied to Llama-4-Scout because no smaller model shares its tokenizer, making traditional speculative decoding approaches dependent on training a new draft model — a limitation SpecMoE avoids but which is acknowledged as a challenge for the broader ecosystem. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All experiments use a single NVIDIA H100 with a single PCIe link. Production deployments of 94–218 GB MoE models (Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-4-Scout) typically use multi-GPU tensor-parallel or pipeline-parallel configurations, where PCIe bandwidth per GPU differs and expert distribution strategies change fundamentally. Whether SpecMoE's gains transfer to multi-GPU topologies is unaddressed. - Output quality (BLEU, ROUGE) is not reported for any configuration; while greedy acceptance is lossless by construction, sampling-based acceptance (used for Mixtral and Llama-4-Scout) introduces distributional error not quantified here. - The affinity table approximation quality — i.e., how much output fidelity degrades when tokens are re-routed to a similar but wrong expert — is not ablated against a no-affinity baseline in terms of generation quality metrics. - Energy and power consumption are not reported, which matters for server operators optimizing for performance-per-watt. - The paper does not address the interaction between SpecMoE and quantized expert weights (e.g., INT4/INT8 expert offloading), which is a common technique in practice to reduce PCIe traffic independently. Bottom Line: SpecMoE makes a concrete, training-free systems contribution that meaningfully reduces the dominant bottleneck in CPU-offloaded MoE inference for highly skewed expert activation models, and any team building or evaluating CPU-offloaded MoE serving infrastructure should read it — though teams running distributed multi-GPU deployments will need to validate the gains independently.
Search Results 2604.10152 PDF
#60 — Innovative (70)

RAG or Learning? Understanding the Limits of LLM Adaptation under Continuous Knowledge Drift in the Real World

Hanbing Liu, Lang Cao, Yang Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strong on multiple dimensions: Novelty (70) — both a new benchmark targeting continuous drift (a real evaluation gap) and EEG synthesis are meaningful contributions. Rigor (70) — thorough component-wise ablation (the 57.7-point history-reconstruction drop is informative), three runs with std reported, code released; pulled down by preprint status, small benchmark (513 quadruples), opaque human-verification protocol, and unmeasured EEG-F hallucination accuracy. Non-Obviousness (70) — the failure analysis of knowledge editing collapsing under continuous drift is a falsifiable, specific empirical insight. Completeness (70) — limitations are acknowledged honestly including non-guaranteed correctness. Resilience (65) — exact-match scoring and fixed weak retriever are vulnerabilities under adversarial review. Impact (65) — code+benchmark released, but ~4 LLM calls/query with no cost analysis. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*70+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*65 = 68.75 ≈ 69.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
