TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a benchmark of real-world dynamic events (Jan 2024–Oct 2025) and Chronos, a time-aware retrieval framework that organizes retrieved evidence into an Event Evolution Graph to enable temporally consistent LLM reasoning under continuous knowledge drift. - Why it's important: Deployed LLMs have fixed knowledge cutoffs, and the field lacks evaluation settings that test whether adaptation methods—RAG, continual fine-tuning, knowledge editing—actually maintain coherent temporal reasoning as facts evolve over months and years, not just at isolated update points. - How it does it: Chronos decomposes queries into entities and time windows, retrieves quadruples scored by a combined semantic-similarity and exponential-decay temporal relevance score, then progressively builds an Event Evolution Graph (EEG) through temporal ordering, history reconstruction, and entity-centric linking before serializing multi-perspective views for final generation. - The good: Chronos_GPT-4o achieves 73.86% overall accuracy versus 56.94% for the best baseline (ReAct RAG_Claude), with particularly large gains on multi-timestamp (C2: 66.51% vs. 59.63%) and multi-source (C3: 71.19% vs. 47.46%) contemporary queries; ablations confirm every pipeline component contributes non-trivially. - The missing: Chronos requires approximately four sequential LLM calls per query versus one for vanilla RAG, but the paper reports no latency, token cost, or throughput comparison, making the practical cost-benefit case unquantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on knowledge-grounded generation, temporal QA, or continual learning benchmarking — the benchmark design and the systematic failure analysis of knowledge editing under continuous drift are the most reusable contributions; practitioners needing a deployment-ready solution should note this is a preprint with a small-scale benchmark. Problem & Motivation: Large language models acquire factual knowledge at training time and cannot directly update that knowledge as the world evolves. This knowledge cutoff problem goes beyond stale facts: when multiple temporal states of the same entity exist (e.g., who was the world's richest person at each of five points in 2024), models must also reason consistently about the ordering and transitions among those states. Existing adaptation paradigms — continual pretraining, knowledge editing (ROME, MEMIT, WISE), and retrieval-augmented generation — have mostly been evaluated on isolated, single-point updates or static corpora, leaving open whether they can maintain temporal coherence across continuously evolving, interrelated real-world facts. No benchmark with time-stamped, evolving evidence across multiple domains existed to expose these gaps systematically. Key Idea: The core insight is that knowledge drift requires explicitly modeling entity state trajectories over time, not merely retrieving relevant documents. By organizing retrieved evidence as an Event Evolution Graph — where nodes are time-stamped knowledge quadruples and edges encode both global chronological ordering and entity-specific succession — the framework gives the model a structured, multi-perspective view of how facts change, enabling it to distinguish what was true at T1 from what was true at T2 without any parameter updates. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Query Analysis: an LLM extracts entities, infers a time window [t_s, t_e], and rewrites a time-agnostic version of the query. (2) Time-aware Retrieval: for each entity, candidate quadruples from an external knowledge base are scored by Score(k_i) = α·sim_i + (1−α)·exp(−Δ(t_i,t_q)/τ), where Δ is zero inside the query time window and grows linearly outside it; top-n quadruples form the retrieved set K_r. (3) Event Evolution Graph Construction: EEG-I builds a timeline by sorting K_r by timestamp; EEG-F augments with LLM-generated historical quadruples for pre-cutoff context; EEG-A enriches the graph with additional retrieved or LLM-inferred quadruples and adds entity-centric directed edges linking successive events for each entity. (4) Prediction: the augmented graph is serialized into a temporal view (global chronological subgraph within the query window) and per-entity views, all passed to the LLM for final answer generation. Key design decisions: (a) exponential temporal decay hyperparameter τ (set to 180 days) — justified empirically on a validation set but sensitivity is acknowledged as low; (b) LLM-generated history reconstruction rather than a separate pre-cutoff corpus — convenient but introduces hallucination risk; (c) entity-centric edges explicitly added on top of global temporal edges — well-motivated by the multi-source C3 task, and confirmed by ablation. Key Results: Evaluation uses exact-match accuracy across five QA categories on three LLMs (GPT-4o gpt-4o-2024-11-20, Claude claude-3-haiku-20240307, LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct). All have pre-2024 knowledge cutoffs, matching the benchmark's 2024–2025 time range. Chronos_GPT-4o reaches 63.96% historical, 96.30% C1 (single-timestamp contemporary), 66.51% C2 (multi-timestamp), 71.19% C3 (multi-source), 71.36% commonsense, and 73.86% overall — versus the strongest non-Chronos method ReAct RAG_Claude at 56.94% overall. Knowledge editing methods (ROME, MEMIT) on LLaMA-3.1 collapse to near 0% on C2 (ROME: 0.92%, MEMIT: 1.38%), substantially below even the unaugmented LLaMA-3.1 baseline (1.38%–11.31%), confirming catastrophic forgetting. LoRA fine-tuning performs better (32.17% overall) but still degrades historical accuracy relative to direct generation. The benchmark comprises 513 knowledge quadruples, 111 historical questions, 513/218/59 contemporary C1/C2/C3 questions, and 817 commonsense questions from TruthfulQA — a modest scale. Retrieval uses all-mpnet-base-v2 uniformly across all methods; this is a lightweight model and may disadvantage retrieval-based approaches relative to stronger retrievers. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The benchmark's explicit time-stamped quadruple structure spanning 10 domains over 22 months with five QA task types, including multi-timestamp and multi-source categories, is genuinely novel and fills a gap in the evaluation landscape. The EEG construction as applied to temporal QA under knowledge drift is a meaningful methodological contribution, though its individual components (temporal scoring in retrieval, graph-based evidence organization, entity-centric linking) each draw on prior work. The combination and the specific application to the continuous drift problem is the novel synthesis. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 are thorough and cover all five EEG components independently; results show that removing history reconstruction collapses historical accuracy from 63.96% to 6.31%, the largest single-component effect, directly validating a non-obvious design choice. Standard deviations across three runs are reported inline (e.g., Chronos_GPT-4o: 73.86 ±19.3), though with only three backbone models and a small benchmark, these are not full significance tests. QA generation used LLMs with human post-hoc verification; the verification protocol (inter-annotator agreement, rejection rate) is not specified, leaving benchmark quality partially opaque. Code is released at https://github.com/hbing-l/chronos. The history reconstruction step (EEG-F) asks the LLM to hallucinate plausible historical quadruples — accuracy of this step relative to ground truth is not evaluated separately, which is a meaningful gap. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (RAG-style inference system, evaluated on a constructed benchmark). - Practical: Chronos runs entirely at inference time via prompting and a standard dense retriever (all-mpnet-base-v2); it requires no GPU fine-tuning and runs on A800 80GB for heavier LLMs, but the LLM backbone is the dominant cost. - Efficient: Each query requires approximately four sequential LLM calls (query analysis, history reconstruction, event augmentation, final response) plus parallel entity retrievals, versus one call for vanilla RAG; the paper does not report per-query latency, total token cost, or throughput — the efficiency overhead is unquantified. - Effective: The benchmark evidence supports that Chronos solves the stated problem of temporally consistent QA: 71.19% vs. 47.46% on the hardest multi-source contemporary task, with ablation confirming each component contributes. - Scalable: Tested on a benchmark of 513 quadruples and at most tens of retrieved items per query; behavior on a corpus of millions of quadruples with thousands of entities, where retrieval noise and graph density would grow substantially, is not evaluated or discussed. The work delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) and a dataset, in addition to paper form. Strengths: - The taxonomy of QA task types (historical, C1 single-timestamp, C2 multi-timestamp, C3 multi-source, commonsense) is carefully designed to stress-test different aspects of temporal reasoning, moving well beyond prior single-update settings. - The failure analysis of knowledge editing under continuous drift (Table 4) concretely demonstrates that parameter-level methods collapse multiple temporal states into a single answer — this is a specific, falsifiable finding with direct implications for practitioners who deploy ROME/MEMIT in production. - Ablation in Table 3 is specific and quantitatively informative; the 57.7-point drop in historical accuracy upon removing history reconstruction justifies a design choice that might otherwise seem expensive or risky. - The entity-centric view contribution to C2 accuracy (+17.0 points when included vs. removed) demonstrates that entity-level temporal tracking is not redundant with global temporal ordering — a non-obvious finding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that the fixed 2024–2025 evaluation window may not match the temporal scope needed in other domains or deployments. They acknowledge the benchmark captures simpler, more structured knowledge dynamics than real industrial scenarios and call for richer future test cases. They explicitly flag that Chronos does not guarantee correctness and requires human oversight for high-stakes deployment. These limitations are stated directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The history reconstruction step (EEG-F) prompts the LLM to generate pre-cutoff quadruples from memory — this reintroduces the parametric knowledge the paper otherwise argues is unreliable, and the accuracy of these generated quadruples relative to ground truth is never measured. The benchmark, at 513 quadruples derived from Wikipedia, is small enough that performance differences could partly reflect coverage artifacts in the knowledge base rather than generalizable system behavior. Exact-match evaluation penalizes valid paraphrases (e.g., "Oracle" vs. "Oracle Corporation"), likely systematically under-reporting all methods' true accuracy. The retriever (all-mpnet-base-v2) is held fixed across methods, which controls for retrieval quality but may set a low ceiling for all retrieval-based methods; Chronos's gains could partly vanish with a stronger retriever applied to vanilla RAG as well. The paper tests LLaMA-3.1-8B as the only open-source model — an 8B model is a poor representative of what practitioners actually deploy, and results on LLaMA may reflect capacity limitations rather than paradigm limitations. Bottom Line: A practitioner designing or evaluating LLM systems that must track evolving real-world facts gains concrete, evidence-backed guidance on which adaptation paradigms fail and why, plus a usable retrieval framework and benchmark — but should note the small benchmark scale, unquantified inference cost overhead, and preprint status before adopting Chronos in production.
Recommended 2604.05096 PDF
#61 — Innovative (70)

CLEAR: Context Augmentation from Contrastive Learning of Experience via Agentic Reflection

Linbo Liu, Guande Wu, Han Ding, Yawei Wang, Qiang Zhou, Yuzhe Lu, Zhichao Xu, Huan Song, Panpan Xu, Lin Lee Cheong · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — decoupling CAM from execution agent with contrastive multi-trajectory reflection is a genuine method. Rigor (70) has clean ablations, 3 seeds, public code, but WebShop-40k is a self-constructed subset and combinatorial data augmentation raises overfitting concerns. Non-Obviousness (70) — contrastive reflection for context distillation is non-trivial. Completeness (65) weak on benchmark breadth (two sequential-action tasks only). Resilience (60) — transferability gain to DeepSeek is small. Impact (75) — code released, practically useful for proprietary-model deployment. Weighted ≈70.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLEAR, a framework that trains a lightweight Context Augmentation Model (CAM) to generate task-specific guidance for LLM agents by learning from contrastive analysis of multiple past execution trajectories, improving agent task completion without modifying the execution agent's weights. - Why it's important: LLM agents built on proprietary models (Claude, GPT) cannot be weight-fine-tuned, yet existing context engineering methods like RAG require the execution agent to perform additional in-context reasoning to adapt retrieved static instructions — a burden that causes degradation when the base agent is weak. - How it does it: A reflection agent analyzes m=6 rollouts of each training task contrastively (success vs. failure), distills task-specific guidance as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data, trains the CAM on that data, then further refines via GRPO reinforcement learning using the execution agent's task-completion reward as the signal — keeping only CAM parameters trainable. - The good: On AppWorld TEST-N (Claude-Sonnet-4 as execution agent), CLEAR improves Task Goal Completion from 72.62% (baseline) to 81.15% and Scenario Goal Completion from 52.38% to 66.67%, beating ACE — a competitor with benchmark-specific prompt engineering — by +6.75 and +7.74 points respectively, with only 1.21 seconds of added inference latency. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to two benchmarks in the app-control/web-shopping domain; WebShop is evaluated on a self-constructed 40k-item subset (not the original full benchmark), and there is no evaluation on code agents, reasoning tasks, or other agentic domains — whether CLEAR generalizes is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers deploying LLM agents on top of proprietary or black-box execution models who need a training framework that avoids weight access; can be skipped by those focused on prompting-only methods or domains beyond sequential web/app interaction tasks. Problem & Motivation LLM agents performing sequential decision-making (e.g., controlling apps via APIs, web shopping) depend critically on context engineering to supply task-relevant knowledge that is absent from model weights. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) retrieves static instructions from a knowledge base, but the agent must then reason over them to adapt to the current task instance — a burden that degrades performance when the execution model is weak. Methods like ACE and GEPA learn universal playbooks or optimized prompts, but these are static, general-purpose artifacts that do not adapt to specific task instances at inference time. The fundamental gap is the absence of a trainable, instance-level context generator that works without access to the execution model's parameters — which is a hard constraint when deploying agents on top of proprietary APIs (Claude, GPT-4). Key Idea The core insight is to separate the context generation problem from the execution problem: train a small, open-source model (CAM) whose sole job is to generate a task-specific instruction snippet prepended to the task description. The trick enabling high-quality training data is contrastive multi-trajectory reflection — running the execution agent multiple times per training task, then using a reflection agent equipped with shell tools to analyze the grouped trajectories, identifying what differentiating behaviors led to success vs. failure, and distilling these into actionable instructions. This shifts reasoning burden from the execution agent at inference time to the CAM, which has already internalized task-specific patterns. Methodology CLEAR is a three-phase pipeline. Phase 0 (Agentic Reflection): for each training task q_i, run the execution agent π^E m=6 times. A reflection agent π^R (Claude-Sonnet-4 with shell tools for selective trajectory file inspection) performs contrastive analysis over the grouped replay buffer Γ_i = {(τ_i^1, r_i^1), ..., (τ_i^6, r_i^6)}, producing a task-specific instruction c_i. To expand dataset size from n to 20n, combinatorial subsampling selects C(6,3)=20 subsets of 3 trajectories per task, each yielding a distinct reflection pass. The resulting (q_i, c_i) pairs form the SFT dataset D_SFT. Phase 1 (SFT): a Qwen3-32B model is full-parameter fine-tuned on D_SFT for 5 epochs on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs (bf16, LR=1e-5, warm-up ratio 0.05) using LlamaFactory. Phase 2 (RL with GRPO): the SFT model is further optimized via GRPO for 15 epochs; rewards are computed by running the execution agent π^E on tasks augmented with CAM-generated context, using the benchmark's outcome reward function; only CAM parameters are updated. At inference, given q_new, CAM generates c_new, which is concatenated to form q_new ⊕ c_new as the augmented task description. Three design decisions are critical: (1) contrastive multi-trajectory reflection rather than single-trajectory reflection — ablation validates this removes a key data quality step; (2) decoupling CAM from execution agent — the objective in Equation 3 maximizes E[R(τ)] under CAM-generated context, allowing CAM to be trained independently; (3) using GRPO (rather than PPO or DPO) for RL — the paper is agnostic about RL algorithm choice but justifies RL necessity by ablation. Key Results On AppWorld TEST-N with Claude-Sonnet-4 as the execution agent (Table 1, 3 runs): CLEAR achieves TGC Avg=81.15% (±2.48) vs. baseline 72.62% (±2.59), RAG 72.02% (±2.15), and ACE 74.40% (±3.57); SGC Avg=66.67% (±4.49) vs. baseline 52.38%, RAG 54.67%, ACE 58.93%. CLEAR's Pass@3 TGC is 91.67% vs. ACE's 85.71%. Notably, RAG barely matches the baseline on TGC Avg (72.02 vs. 72.62), illustrating that retrieval of static context without adaptation does not help strong execution agents, and hurts weaker ones: with DeepSeek-V3.1 as π^E, RAG TGC drops to 33.33% vs. a 41.27% baseline. On WebShop-40k (Table 2): CLEAR Avg. Reward=0.7406 (±0.0044) vs. baseline 0.6799 (±0.0119) and RAG 0.7252 (±0.0076). Ablation (Table 3): removing RL drops TGC from 81.15% to 74.21%; removing contrastive learning drops it to 68.65%; removing SFT entirely (raw Qwen3-32B as CAM) drops to 67.40% — all three phases contribute non-trivially. CAM transferability: the Claude-trained CAM applied to DeepSeek-V3.1 execution agent yields TGC=42.95% vs. 41.27% baseline (+1.68) and SGC=24.40% vs. 19.05% baseline (+5.35) — positive but substantially smaller than same-model gains. Latency: CLEAR adds 1.21s CAM inference and ~1.2 additional turns, totaling 13.8s additional wall-clock time per task on AppWorld (63.4s→77.2s baseline→CLEAR). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is the architecture of a dedicated, trainable context augmentation model decoupled from the execution agent, combined with contrastive multi-trajectory agentic reflection for SFT data generation. The SFT+RL training pipeline itself is a standard combination of established techniques (SFT, GRPO); the contrastive learning principle applied to agent trajectories for context distillation (rather than direct policy improvement) is the distinguishing mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative — Table 3 isolates the contribution of each phase (contrastive learning, SFT, RL) with consistent evaluation on AppWorld TEST-N. Results are averaged over 3 independent runs with standard deviations reported. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/awslabs/CLEAR. The AppWorld evaluation uses the official train/test split (disjoint) and the official evaluation harness. One methodological concern: the WebShop-40k benchmark is a self-modified subset of the original WebShop (40k vs. millions of products) due to Docker image size constraints on Amazon Bedrock AgentCore — the authors acknowledge this may be inherently easier than the original and results may not be comparable to prior WebShop literature. The combinatorial data augmentation (20 subsets of 3 from 6 rollouts per task) introduces correlated training examples that share trajectory data; no discussion of this potential source of overfitting is provided. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: CAM training requires 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs and large-scale parallel rollout infrastructure (Amazon Bedrock AgentCore for containerized concurrent agent execution); inference requires a GPU server running vLLM with the Qwen3-32B model — not deployable on commodity hardware without significant cloud budget. - Efficient: CAM adds 1.21s per task at 69.5 tokens/s throughput, representing approximately 22% wall-clock overhead relative to the baseline execution time (13.8s additional / 63.4s baseline); the Qwen3-32B model is large but inference is batched via vLLM. - Effective: Evidence on AppWorld is solid and uses disjoint official splits (+8.53 TGC Avg over strong baseline); WebShop-40k gains are real but the non-standard subset weakens confidence in generalization to the original benchmark formulation. - Scalable: Performance at larger task distributions, longer-horizon tasks, or more complex agentic domains is not tested; the paper provides no analysis of how CAM quality scales with training set size or number of rollouts per task. Delivers: code at https://github.com/awslabs/CLEAR; training scripts for SFT (LlamaFactory) and RL (Verl framework with GRPO); no trained model weights released. Strengths - Principled decoupling of context generation from execution: CAM can augment any black-box execution agent without parameter access, directly addressing the proprietary model constraint that renders prior fine-tuning approaches inapplicable. - Contrastive multi-trajectory reflection produces richer, higher-quality SFT data than single-trajectory methods — the ablation (SFT with CL vs. without: TGC 74.21% vs. 68.65%) provides clear quantitative support for this design choice. - CAM transferability is a practically useful property: a CAM trained entirely on Claude-Sonnet-4 trajectories provides improvements for a different execution model (DeepSeek-V3.1) without retraining, suggesting the generated context captures task-structural knowledge not specific to one model's reasoning style. - The latency overhead analysis (Table 4) is concrete and honest — the 22% wall-clock increase is modest relative to the performance gain, and the authors frame it as a cost rather than ignoring it. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge that WebShop-40k is a self-constructed reduced-scale variant of the original benchmark due to infrastructure constraints, and that the reduced search space may make it inherently easier — reducing comparability to prior WebShop literature. They also note that ACE results are omitted for WebShop because ACE requires benchmark-specific prompt templates. These limitations are confronted openly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation breadth is narrow: both benchmarks (AppWorld, WebShop) are sequential web/app interaction tasks with discrete action spaces; there is no evaluation on code execution agents, scientific reasoning agents, or document-centric tasks — it is unknown whether CLEAR generalizes beyond this task family. - Total training cost is not reported: the data collection phase involves 6 rollouts × |D_train| tasks × reflection agent calls (Claude-Sonnet-4, a paid API), followed by SFT and RL on 8 B200 GPUs — this could be prohibitively expensive for most practitioners, and the omission of cost figures harms deployability assessment. - The CAM transferability gains to DeepSeek-V3.1 are substantially smaller than same-model gains (+1.68 TGC vs. +8.53 for Claude), suggesting meaningful model-specificity that the paper's positive framing partially obscures — the gap may widen for models more architecturally distant from Claude. - No comparison against directly fine-tuning an open-source model as the execution agent using RL: when the execution model is open-source, CLEAR's approach (training a separate CAM) competes against simply fine-tuning π^E directly, and this baseline is absent. - Combinatorial data augmentation (20 correlated subsets per task) artificially inflates the training set size while introducing intra-task correlations; no cross-validation or held-out task analysis is reported to confirm this does not overfit to training task distributions. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying LLM agents on proprietary execution models gains a concrete, code-available technique that delivers 8–15 percentage-point improvements on sequential interaction benchmarks without touching execution model weights — the narrow benchmark coverage and high training infrastructure requirements are real limitations, but the method is solid enough that agentic systems researchers should read it.
Recommended 2604.07487 PDF
#62 — Innovative (70)

Do diacritics matter? evaluating the impact of arabic diacritics on tokenization and llm benchmarks

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (65) — first systematic cross-model diacritization study, new empirical finding that overturns a preprocessing default. Rigor (75) — McNemar's throughout, code released, 9 models × 3 benchmarks. Non-Obviousness (70) — non-monotonic tokenizer-dependent finding is genuinely surprising. Completeness (70) — limitations directly acknowledged, but diacritizer error confound weakens full* claim. Resilience (60) — ~11.5% WER confound is a real vulnerability to adversarial review. Impact (65) — actionable negative result, public artifact, no new tokenizer. Weighted: ~69. Solid, honest work; confound limits top tier.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents the first systematic empirical study quantifying how three degrees of Arabic diacritization (removed, naturally occurring, and fully diacritized) affect tokenization fragmentation and zero-shot benchmark accuracy across nine major LLMs. - Why it's important: Arabic NLP pipelines universally strip diacritics during preprocessing, yet diacritics are pervasive in children's literature, religious texts, and educational materials—exactly the domains where Arabic LLMs are increasingly deployed—making this a blindspot with real downstream consequences. - How it does it: Three diacritization variants of three standard Arabic benchmarks are constructed and evaluated on nine models using tokenization metrics (fertility, Jaccard similarity, layer-wise cosine similarity) and McNemar-tested zero-shot multiple-choice accuracy. - The good: A consistent, statistically validated finding across 9 models and 3 benchmarks: wild (naturally sparse) diacritics are benign or slightly helpful, while full diacritization causes significant accuracy drops—e.g., Jais Chat 13B falls 14.4 points on ArabicMMMLU and 26.2 points on AraTrust relative to wild. - The missing: The full-diacritization condition uses an automatic diacritizer with ~11.5% word-level error rate, conflating the effect of dense diacritics with the effect of diacritization errors; no manually diacritized control exists to disentangle the two. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or evaluating Arabic NLP systems, particularly for high-diacritization domains; the results directly falsify the dominant preprocessing assumption that diacritic removal is always safe. Problem & Motivation: Arabic script is optionally diacritized, with coverage ranging from over 80% of lines in children's and religious texts to under 15% in news and user-generated content. Current LLM tokenizers are predominantly trained on undiacritized or lightly diacritized text; when diacritized input is encountered, characters are treated as out-of-vocabulary or are byte-encoded, causing subword fragmentation and potentially degrading representations. The dominant practice of stripping diacritics as a preprocessing step was never rigorously validated for modern LLMs—it was inherited from older pipeline decisions. No prior work had measured the effect of varying diacritization degrees on LLM benchmark performance across multiple model families. Key Idea: The performance impact of Arabic diacritics is non-monotonic and tokenizer-dependent: sparse wild diacritics, as they naturally appear in real text, cause minimal disruption and are often tolerated or helpful; but near-complete (full) diacritization pushes tokenizers into character-level fragmentation because their vocabularies contain almost no diacritic-bearing subword types, destroying the subword representations the models learned during training. Methodology: The study has two tracks. For tokenization analysis, nine model tokenizers (AceGPT-v2 8B, ALLaM 7B, Fanar 9B, Jais 13B, Jais-2 8B, Aya-Expanse 8B, Llama-3.1 8B, Qwen3 8B, GPT-4o) are measured on the Wild2MaxDiacs corpus across undiac/wild/full settings, reporting subword fertility (#subwords/#words), character density (#chars/#subword), percentage of words with equal subword counts across condition pairs, and Jaccard similarity of subword sets. Layer-wise cosine similarities of mean-pooled word representations are computed across all transformer layers for 3,000-word subsets. For benchmark analysis, three versions of ArabicMMMLU (n=14,455), ArabCulture (n=3,482), and AraTrust (n=522) are constructed: undiac via CAMeL Tools' `dediac_ar`, wild (original), and full* via a BERT-based morphological disambiguation diacritizer (88.9% word-level accuracy, meaning ~11.5% error rate). Open-weight models are run zero-shot on LM Evaluation Harness with greedy decoding; GPT-4o uses the Batch API with temperature=0. Statistical significance is assessed via McNemar's test (p<0.05). Critical design decisions: (1) three discrete conditions matching naturally occurring diacritization levels—well-motivated but leaves the continuous spectrum unsampled; (2) zero-shot only—not tested in few-shot or fine-tuned regimes; (3) automatic diacritization conflates the diacritization treatment with diacritizer errors, acknowledged but not experimentally controlled. Key Results: Full diacritization (full) degrades accuracy significantly across most models and benchmarks by McNemar's test. GPT-4o is the most robust: 80.7/88.3/92.9 (wild) vs. 79.4/87.2/92.9 (full) on ArabicMMMLU/ArabCulture/AraTrust—small but significant drops on ArabicMMMLU and ArabCulture. Jais Chat 13B is the most fragile: −14.4 points on ArabicMMMLU, −9.7 on ArabCulture, −26.2 on AraTrust (wild→full*), directly traceable to its tokenizer having only 7 diacritic vocabulary entries, causing character-level fragmentation. Diacritization removal (undiac) produces mostly insignificant differences vs. wild, with five exceptions where removing diacritics causes significant drops (e.g., Aya-Expanse and Llama-3.1 on ArabicMMMLU). Best open-weight models: ALLaM 7B on ArabicMMMLU (70.0 wild), Fanar 9B on ArabCulture (44.9 wild), Aya-Expanse on AraTrust (85.4 wild). Tokenizer fertility increases monotonically undiac < wild < full across all nine tokenizers; in Jais models, a single diacritic character expands to a multi-byte token sequence. Layer-wise cosine similarity shows undiac-full pairs have lowest embedding similarity at layer 0, converging to ~1.0 in middle layers, indicating partial representational recovery in deeper layers. Subsets containing wild diacritics show the same patterns as the full benchmarks, confirming results are not driven by the diacritic-absent majority. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components—Arabic diacritization, LLM tokenization analysis, Arabic benchmark evaluation—are all established; the genuine novelty is the first systematic cross-model, cross-benchmark, cross-diacritization-level measurement that yields a concrete empirical correction to the field's dominant preprocessing assumption. The finding that retaining wild diacritics is neutral-to-beneficial, while automatic full diacritization is reliably harmful, is a specific, falsifiable empirical claim not previously established. Rigor & Reproducibility: McNemar's test is applied consistently for all pairwise benchmark comparisons—a correct choice for paired accuracy differences. No error bars on tokenization metrics, but the corpus size makes single-pass estimates stable. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/do_diacritics_matter. All open-weight experiments run on a single A100 80GB for at most 24 GPU hours. GPT-4o costs under $15 USD. Single-seed zero-shot evaluation; no variance estimates across prompt orderings or seeds. The key methodological concern is the automatic diacritizer's ~11.5% word error rate in the full* condition: observed performance drops cannot be cleanly attributed to dense diacritics alone versus to introduced diacritization errors. This is the primary confound the authors acknowledge but cannot resolve without manually diacritized gold data. Evaluation is restricted to multiple-choice tasks; open-ended generation is untested. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: All open-weight experiments run on a single A100 80GB GPU with under 24 GPU hours; straightforward to reproduce. - Efficient: Evaluation overhead is identical to standard benchmark runs; the diacritization preprocessing adds negligible cost. - Effective: The evidence clearly identifies which models degrade under full diacritization and why (tokenizer vocabulary gap), providing actionable guidance: keep wild diacritics, avoid auto-diacritization pipelines, and audit tokenizer diacritic coverage. - Scalable: Tested up to 13B parameters; no experiments on larger models (70B+ class) and no dialectal Arabic; behavior at larger scale or across Arabic dialects is unverified. The work delivers a public code/data artifact and a clear negative result (auto-diacritization preprocessing is harmful for current tokenizers), but no trained model or improved tokenizer. Strengths: - Covers nine models across Arabic-centric, multilingual, and proprietary families simultaneously, making the generalization much stronger than single-model studies. - Uses McNemar's test throughout rather than reporting raw accuracy deltas—a methodological rigor not universal in benchmark comparison papers. - Directly contradicts the entrenched diacritic-stripping practice with quantitative evidence showing it can reduce performance, and provides a mechanistic explanation tied to tokenizer vocabulary statistics. - Layer-wise cosine similarity analysis is a mechanistic lens that goes beyond accuracy numbers, revealing that representational damage from full diacritization partially self-corrects in middle layers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state four limitations: (1) zero-shot evaluation only, potentially not generalizing to few-shot or fine-tuned settings; (2) automatic diacritization introduces errors that could drive the observed degradation; (3) limited model coverage due to compute constraints; (4) analysis targets MSA and does not address dialectal Arabic. These are confronted plainly, which is appropriate. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The full confound is more serious than the authors frame it: if the automatic diacritizer introduces systematic errors (not just random noise), the performance drop may be driven by morphologically incorrect forms rather than by diacritization density. A small manually diacritized control set—even 200 items—would resolve this. Additionally, the study evaluates only multiple-choice benchmarks; generation tasks are likely more sensitive to tokenization fragmentation and could show larger effects. No interaction analysis between model training domain and diacritization robustness is conducted—models trained on more diacritized corpora (e.g., ALLaM) might show different sensitivity patterns for principled reasons worth investigating. Finally, models above 13B are absent; the Jais-2 (8B) vs. Jais (13B) comparison within the same family shows that 13B is more* fragile, suggesting scale alone does not fix the problem—but the question remains open for frontier-scale Arabic models. Bottom Line: Any practitioner building Arabic NLP pipelines or evaluating Arabic LLMs should read this paper: it provides the first rigorous multi-model evidence that diacritic stripping is often unnecessary and occasionally harmful, directly overturning a field-wide preprocessing default.
Recommended
#63 — Innovative (70)

ASGuard: Activation-Scaling Guard to Mitigate Targeted Jailbreaking Attack (17 ▲ )

Yein Park, Jungwoo Park, Jaewoo Kang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — attack-class-specific vulnerable heads + preventative fine-tuning combination is new. Rigor (70) — strong ablations (random-head controls, head ablation, Only-Scaling), linear-probe mechanistic validation, code released, but no multi-seed stats and GPT-4.1-only judge. Non-Obviousness (80) — mechanistic localization of tense-specific heads distinct from general safety heads is a genuine insight validated by linear probes. Completeness (70) — limitations honestly confronted. Resilience (65) — Pareto analysis is robust to reviewer pushback; JBB-only circuit construction is a weakness. Impact (65) — code released, cross-attack generalization demonstrated, but 7–9B only. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*70+0.15*80+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*65 ≈ 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ASGuard, a three-stage mechanistically-informed framework that uses transformer circuit analysis to identify tense-vulnerable attention heads, trains channel-wise activation scaling vectors to suppress them, and applies preventative fine-tuning to patch tense jailbreaking vulnerabilities in open-source LLMs. - Why it's important: Rephrasing a harmful request from present tense to past tense bypasses safety alignment in current instruction-tuned models with up to 51% attack success rate, yet existing defenses either fail to close the gap or achieve 0% ASR only by inducing catastrophic over-refusal (98.5% on Qwen2.5 with SFT 30/70) or world-knowledge loss (MMLU drop from 72.2 to 43.1 on Gemma-2). - How it does it: EAP-IG circuit analysis causally localizes the minimal set of attention heads exclusively active in tense-jailbreak-success circuits; learned channel-wise scaling vectors recalibrate those heads toward refusal; preventative fine-tuning with scaling vectors temporarily attached forces the model to discover alternative, non-vulnerable refusal pathways so the fix internalizes after the vectors are detached. - The good: On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, ASGuard reduces past-tense ASR from 42% to 8% while holding MMLU at 68.2 (baseline) and achieving the best Overall balance score (52.9 vs. RepBend's 48.4 and SFT 30/70's 45.6); cross-attack tests show GCG ASR falls to 1% and LogiBreak to 13% without retraining on those attacks. - The missing: The approach is evaluated only on 7–9B dense instruction-tuned models; there is no test at ≥13B scale, no MoE or distilled architecture, and the tense-circuit construction and ASR evaluation both depend on GPT-4.1 as judge with no human validation of judge accuracy. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on mechanistic interpretability applied to safety or designing surgical defenses against specific jailbreak classes; practitioners deploying frontier-scale or closed models will find limited direct applicability. Problem & Motivation Safety-aligned LLMs exhibit a systematic generalization failure: rephrasing a harmful request from present tense ("How do I make X?") to past tense ("How did people make X?") reliably elicits compliance, with ASRs of 28–51% across Llama-3.1, Qwen2.5, Gemma-2, and OLMo-2. Andriushchenko & Flammarion (ICLR 2025) characterized the phenomenon but did not explain the underlying mechanism. Standard alignment techniques (SFT, DPO) train on output distributions and fail to instill semantic generalization across tense, producing brittle refusal heuristics. Prior mechanistic safety work (Zhou et al., 2025) identifies general "safety attention heads" whose ablation degrades guardrails, but has not addressed attack-specific "vulnerable heads" that are exploited by a particular jailbreak class. Without localizing the specific internal mechanism, remediation remains blunt — sacrificing utility to suppress surface patterns. Key Idea Tense jailbreaking succeeds because a small, identifiable set of attention heads — "tense vulnerable heads" — act as upstream feature extractors that detect past tense and reclassify the query as a historical inquiry, short-circuiting downstream refusal logic. Once causally localized via circuit analysis, these heads can be recalibrated with a lightweight channel-wise scaling vector; then preventative fine-tuning, applied while the scaling intervention is active, forces the optimizer to discover alternative non-vulnerable refusal pathways, so the safety patch is internalized even after the scaling vectors are removed at inference time. Methodology Stage 1 — Circuit construction: 100 jailbreak prompts from JBB-Behaviors are reformulated 20× each into past and present tense by GPT-3.5-turbo; GPT-4.1 judges attack success. Two circuit categories are sampled: "False-to-True" (present tense refused, past tense complies) and "Always-False" (both refused). EAP-IG (edge attribution patching with integrated gradients) constructs DAG circuits over attention heads and MLP modules using KL divergence as the task-agnostic loss; top-n=5000 edges are retained and isolated nodes pruned (τ=0.1–0.03). Heads appearing exclusively in False-to-True circuits become the tense vulnerable head set H_vuln (4–12 heads per model). Stage 2 — Activation scaling: For each head j ∈ H_vuln, a learnable channel-wise scaling vector s_j ∈ ℝ^d_head is trained while all model weights θ are frozen. The objective minimizes cross-entropy on jailbreak prompts paired with predefined refusal responses (equation 8). The scaling fuses into W'_O,k = diag(s_k)W_O,k at zero inference overhead. Stage 3 — Preventative fine-tuning (PFT): Optimal scaling vectors s* are fixed (non-trainable) and attached to the model; standard fine-tuning on a refusal dataset D_refusal updates only base parameters θ (equation 10). After convergence, scaling vectors are detached. Training: 2×NVIDIA H100 80GB, FSDP. Critical design decisions: (a) channel-wise scaling over whole-head ablation — justified because ablation disrupts downstream refusal mechanism rather than recalibrating the input assessment, yielding only 4–13% ASR drops vs. larger drops from scaling; (b) PFT over scaling alone — justified on Gemma-2 where scaling alone collapses MMLU from 72.2 to 50.3 while PFT preserves it at 72.2; (c) EAP-IG over simpler attribution methods — justified by faithfulness validation (ablating non-circuit edges preserves task performance). Key Results On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: past-tense ASR 42%→8% (ASGuard) vs. 3% for SFT 30/70 (best ASR competitor); ASGuard OR-Bench-Hard=66.8 vs. SFT 30/70's 80.3 (lower is more permissive/less over-refusing); MMLU preserved at 68.2; Overall=52.9 vs. RepBend=48.4. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: ASR 51%→8%, Overall=58.8 (best), vs. SFT 30/70 achieving 0% ASR but 98.5% OR-Bench-Toxic (near-total over-refusal). On Gemma-2-9b-it: ASR 38%→19% (weakest result, only ~50% relative reduction), but uniquely preserves MMLU=72.2 while all SFT variants either catastrophically forget (MMLU→43.1) or over-refuse. On OLMo-2-7B-Instruct: ASR 28%→9%, Overall=46.3. Cross-attack generalization on Llama-3.1: GCG ASR 15%→1%, LogiBreak 30%→13% without attack-specific retraining. Linear probe analysis confirms mechanism: L10H25 distinguishes past/present tense at 73.44% accuracy, L13H25 at 76.56%, validating the causal role of identified heads. Random head controls (ablating/scaling 10 non-circuit heads) reduce ASR by only 1–2% and substantially increase over-refusal, confirming circuit specificity. Baselines are current: SFT (two data mixtures), DPO, RepE, Circuit Breaker (CB), RepBend. All re-evaluated under a unified pipeline; absolute ASR values may differ from original papers' reported numbers, but relative comparisons among methods are consistent. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel contribution is the three-stage "Identify-then-Scale + Preventative Fine-Tuning" pipeline applied to targeted jailbreak remediation. Individual components — EAP-IG circuit construction, channel-wise activation scaling, refusal fine-tuning — appear in prior work (Nanda 2023, Stoehr et al. 2024, Lee et al. 2025); what is genuinely novel is (1) applying circuit analysis to identify attack-class-specific vulnerable heads distinct from general safety heads, and (2) preventative fine-tuning that uses the scaling intervention as implicit regularization during training to force internalization. The empirical finding that tense jailbreaking is mediated by a localizable, consistent, small set of attention heads that differ from both temporal-knowledge heads (Park et al. 2025) and general safety heads (Zhou et al. 2025) is also a genuine new result. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are substantive: head ablation, random head scaling/PFT, and Only-Scaling vs. full ASGuard are all reported with full benchmark suites. No error bars or multi-seed statistics are reported for any metric; ASR measurement depends on GPT-4.1 as judge with a fixed scoring threshold (≥10), introducing unquantified variance. Code and datasets are publicly released at https://github.com/dmis-lab/ASGuard. Partial Goodhart's Law concern: JBB-Behaviors prompts are used to construct the circuit (§3.1) and to evaluate past-tense ASR, meaning the method is both developed and evaluated on the same prompt distribution. Cross-attack experiments (GCG, LogiBreak) provide partial mitigation. Baseline re-implementations under the authors' unified pipeline differ from original paper settings, which is disclosed but means baseline numbers may not represent each method's best possible performance. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Circuit construction requires running ~2000 prompt pairs through inference plus gradient computation; fine-tuning uses 2×H100 80GB with FSDP. Both stages are within reach of academic labs and smaller industry teams. - Efficient: Scaling vectors fuse into W_O with zero inference overhead; Stage 2 trains only d_head × |H_vuln| parameters (far fewer than LoRA); PFT is comparable to one epoch of LoRA fine-tuning, making the approach more parameter-efficient than DPO or full SFT. - Effective: ASGuard achieves the best Overall score on 3 of 4 models and is the only method to preserve MMLU on Gemma-2 while reducing ASR; however, Gemma-2's residual 19% ASR indicates incomplete effectiveness on at least one architecture. - Scalable: Tested only on 7–9B dense transformers. Authors explicitly flag MoE architectures, distilled models, and small models as known failures; no experiments above 9B parameters are reported. The work delivers a software artifact (code + training pipeline) but does not explicitly state that patched model weights are released. Strengths - The linear probe validation (L13H25 at 76.56% tense-classification accuracy, with clear bimodal dot-product distributions for past vs. present tense) provides direct mechanistic evidence beyond mere correlation, meaningfully distinguishing this from representation-engineering approaches that intervene without causal localization. - The Pareto-frontier analysis is methodologically sound and exposes that 0% ASR via brute-force SFT is a Pyrrhic outcome when OR-Bench-Hard or MMLU degrades catastrophically; ASGuard's non-dominated position on that frontier across all four models is the paper's strongest empirical claim. - Cross-attack generalization (GCG and LogiBreak reductions without retraining) supports the claim that PFT builds genuinely more robust refusal rather than patching a narrow surface, and the random-head ablation control cleanly rules out the explanation that any head suppression achieves the result. - The preventative fine-tuning idea — using an explicit activation intervention as implicit regularization to force discovery of alternative internal pathways — is conceptually clean and likely to generalize beyond tense jailbreaking. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and credibly acknowledge: (1) efficacy depends on the existence of localizable causal circuits, not guaranteed for all attack types; (2) MoE-routed, distilled, or differently pretrained architectures may have different internal computation limiting transfer; (3) small models like Phi-3-mini are too sensitive to attention head intervention; (4) application to compositional representations requires deeper investigation. These limitations are stated in §7 without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - GPT-4.1 serves as both the tense reformulation judge (indirectly, via GPT-3.5 + GPT-4.1 scoring) and the ASR evaluator; no human annotation study validates judge calibration, so systematic over- or under-counting of jailbreak successes propagates silently into circuit construction and evaluation. - The tense-circuit heads are discovered and evaluated on the same JBB-Behaviors prompt set (100 prompts); the paper does not test whether circuits discovered on one harmful topic domain transfer to a disjoint domain, leaving possible topic-level overfitting unexamined. - No sensitivity analysis over the number of vulnerable heads selected or the circuit-pruning threshold τ; it is unclear whether including more or fewer heads changes the safety-utility tradeoff substantially. - Gemma-2's residual 19% ASR is the weakest result among the four models, and the explanation ("more complex, less sparse attention head mechanism" due to distillation) is speculative and unsupported by specific mechanistic evidence. - Baselines are re-implemented under the authors' unified pipeline at settings that may not be each baseline's optimal; for instance, CB achieves 0% ASR in the authors' setup on Llama3.1, suggesting the CB implementation is functional, but whether RepBend's hyperparameters were tuned to the same degree as ASGuard's is not verified independently. Bottom Line A practitioner building safety patches for 7–9B open-source dense LLMs against tense-class jailbreaks gains a concrete, well-grounded method with demonstrated Pareto superiority over SFT, DPO, and representation-engineering baselines, but must accept unproven scalability beyond 9B parameters and a per-model circuit-discovery workflow that requires GPT-4.1 in the loop.
Recommended 2509.25843 PDF
#64 — Innovative (70)

Rethinking On-Policy Distillation of Large Language Models: Phenomenology, Mechanism, and Recipe (59 ▲ )

Yaxuan Li, Yuxin Zuo, Bingxiang He, Jinqian Zhang, Chaojun Xiao, Cheng Qian, Tianyu Yu, Huan-ang Gao, Wenkai Yang, Zhiyuan Liu, Ning Ding · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is a strength — the reverse-distillation control and overlap/non-overlap interventional ablation provide causal rather than correlational evidence; code released; but no error bars and preprint status. Non-Obviousness (80) is high — the 'stronger teacher fails identically to weaker teacher' result overturns a dominant intuition, and the overlap-token mechanism is a fresh formulation. Novelty (70) — diagnostic framework rather than new algorithm. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) suffer from math-only scope, ≤7B models, no RL baseline comparison, and 'thinking pattern' being post-hoc operationalized. Impact (55) — diagnostic metrics and recipes are directly usable but no released models. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 = 69.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper identifies two governing conditions for on-policy distillation (OPD) success — thinking-pattern consistency between student and teacher, and the teacher possessing genuinely new knowledge beyond what the student has seen — reveals the causal token-level mechanism (progressive alignment on shared high-probability overlap tokens), and proposes two practical recipes (off-policy cold start and teacher-aligned prompt selection) to recover failing OPD runs. - Why it's important: OPD is deployed in production post-training pipelines for Qwen3, MiMo, and GLM-5, yet practitioners use teacher benchmark scores as a proxy for distillation quality — a proxy this paper demonstrates is unreliable and can predict the wrong outcome. - How it does it: Controlled reverse-distillation experiments hold the student fixed while varying the teacher, and token-level metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are tracked throughout training to distinguish successful from failing runs causally, not just correlationally. - The good: The reverse distillation finding — that a weaker 1.5B teacher and a strictly stronger 7B teacher from the same model family drive the student to virtually identical (poor) outcomes despite the 7B outperforming on benchmarks — is a concrete, hard-to-dismiss counter-example to the dominant intuition that stronger teachers are always better. - The missing: All experiments use mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME 2024, AIME 2025, AMC 2023) with models in the 1.5B–7B range; generalization to code, open-ended tasks, or frontier-scale models is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers designing LLM post-training pipelines should read this; it provides precisely defined diagnostic metrics and empirically validated recipes, though its math-only scope and absence of RL baselines leave the most practically decisive comparison open. Problem & Motivation On-policy distillation uses the student's own sampled rollouts as training contexts and leverages the teacher's per-token log-probabilities as dense reward, avoiding the exposure-bias problem of supervised fine-tuning on fixed teacher sequences. Despite rapid adoption in industry post-training pipelines, OPD exhibits a striking failure mode: a stronger teacher can completely fail to improve the student while a weaker teacher succeeds. No prior work has systematically identified the conditions under which OPD succeeds or fails, nor explained the token-level mechanism through which teacher supervision steers the student. Practitioners therefore lack principled tools to select teachers, configure runs, or diagnose failures — and the implicit assumption that higher teacher benchmark scores guarantee better distillation is empirically wrong. Key Idea The central insight is that OPD's effective gradient signal is concentrated almost entirely on the small set of tokens that appear in both the student's and the teacher's high-probability top-k sets (the "overlap" region), which carries 97–99% of the combined probability mass. For OPD to succeed, (1) this overlap must be non-negligible from the outset (thinking-pattern consistency), and (2) the teacher must assign different probabilities to those shared tokens than the student already learned (new knowledge). When both conditions hold, the overlap set grows self-reinforcingly — each reverse-KL update pushes more student mass toward teacher-preferred tokens within the overlap, enlarging it further — creating a virtuous cycle. When either condition fails, the overlap stagnates and gradient updates point in incoherent directions despite the teacher's reward being globally informative. Methodology The paper is organized into three empirical components. In phenomenology (§3), the authors design controlled OPD experiments holding the student fixed and varying the teacher across two model families: (i) Qwen3-1.7B-Base with Qwen3-4B (non-thinking) vs. Qwen3-4B-Base-GRPO, and (ii) R1-Distill-1.5B with R1-Distill-7B vs. Skywork-OR1-Math-7B. A reverse-distillation experiment uses JustRL-1.5B (an RL-trained 1.5B model) as the student, distilling from its own pre-RL checkpoint (R1-Distill-1.5B) and from R1-Distill-7B, to disentangle thinking-pattern alignment from benchmark performance. In mechanism (§4), three dynamic metrics — overlap ratio M_overlap (fraction of shared top-k tokens), overlap-token advantage M_adv (average log-ratio within overlap), and entropy gap ΔH (|H(q_t)−H(p_t)|) — are tracked throughout training. An ablation partitions the top-k support into overlap and non-overlap subsets and trains on each in isolation to test causal sufficiency. In recipe (§5), off-policy cold start (SFT on teacher rollouts before OPD) and teacher-aligned prompts (matching the prompt template or content from the teacher's post-training data) are each tested as interventions on otherwise-failing configurations. In §6, reward reliability is probed by training across six maximum response lengths (0.5K–15K tokens) and measuring teacher continuation accuracy from student-generated prefixes at truncation depths of 1K, 4K, 8K, 16K tokens. The loss throughout is per-token reverse KL over student-generated trajectories. All math experiments use DAPO-Math-17K or subsets of DeepMath, evaluated at avg@16 (temperature 0.7, top-p 0.95) on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and AMC 2023. Key Results Thinking-pattern consistency: Skywork-OR1-Math-7B (post-RL teacher) achieves a 16.9% gap recovery rate vs. 5.3% for same-family R1-Distill-7B in the DeepSeek family; in the Qwen family, Qwen3-4B-Non-Thinking-RL-Math achieves 58.6% vs. 15.6% for Qwen3-4B (non-thinking), despite the RL teachers sharing the same base checkpoint as the student. Reverse distillation: both R1-Distill-1.5B and R1-Distill-7B as teachers drive JustRL-1.5B to the same regressed accuracy level (~0.28 on AIME 2025), with indistinguishable training curves despite the 7B scoring higher than 1.5B on benchmarks. Overlap sufficiency: Overlap Top-k recovers full Student Top-k performance on all three benchmarks, while Non-Overlap Top-k consistently underperforms; overlap ratio rises from ~72% to ~91% in successful runs. Off-policy cold start: SFT-initialized OPD outperforms pure OPD throughout training and maintains a higher overlap ratio and lower entropy gap from the start. Reward degradation: teacher continuation advantage over student prefixes decreases monotonically from +0.37 at 1K tokens to +0.02 at 16K tokens; moderate response lengths (3K–7K tokens) yield the strongest downstream accuracy, while 10K–15K exhibits late-stage collapse with entropy spikes and overlap ratio drops. Sampled-token OPD matches Top-4 through Top-64 on all three benchmarks; Top-1 consistently underperforms due to biased mode-concentrated selection. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The two governing conditions and the associated diagnostic metrics are new empirical contributions — prior OPD work focused on demonstrating promise across varied objectives without analyzing when or why it fails. The reverse distillation design is an original controlled experiment not seen in the literature. The overlap-token mechanism provides a new formulation of what OPD optimizes in practice. This is a mechanistic study of an existing technique rather than a new training algorithm; the genuinely novel contribution is the causal evidence (from ablations and controlled reversals) rather than a new method. Rigor & Reproducibility The overlap vs. non-overlap ablation directly tests the causal claim (not just observational correlation). The reverse distillation experiment is a well-designed internal control isolating thinking-pattern from benchmark performance. Multiple model families (DeepSeek, Qwen) and three benchmarks provide empirical breadth. avg@16 reduces sample variance at the problem level, though no formal significance tests, confidence intervals, or error bars appear anywhere in the paper. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/thunlp/OPD; trained model weights and full training datasets are not released. The paper is an arXiv preprint (April 2026), not peer-reviewed. One Goodhart's Law concern: all evaluation benchmarks (AIME, AMC) are in the same mathematical reasoning domain as the training data; the recipes may be optimizing for the evaluation domain rather than generalizable OPD behavior. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments use 1.5B–7B parameter models and standard GPU training; no specialized hardware is required, and the recommended sampled-token OPD is the cheapest computational variant. - Efficient: The finding that sampled-token OPD matches Top-4 through Top-64 directly simplifies implementation; the off-policy cold start adds a SFT phase but the paper does not report its compute cost relative to additional RL training. - Effective: The overlap-token ablation provides causal evidence rather than correlation, and the recovery recipes show measurable gains across all three benchmarks on previously failing configurations. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond 7B parameter models or 200 training steps; the reward degradation finding at long trajectories (>7K tokens) is a concrete limitation for long-horizon and agentic settings that the authors explicitly acknowledge but do not resolve. The work delivers code (scripts and training configs); no pre-trained model weights, no new dataset, and no standalone library. Strengths - The reverse distillation experiment is an elegant natural experiment that simultaneously validates both conditions — by showing a weaker and a stronger same-family teacher produce identical outcomes, the paper rules out benchmark performance as a confound in a single controlled design. - The overlap-token sufficiency ablation provides interventional (not just observational) evidence that the overlap set is the causal gradient locus, distinguishing this analysis from descriptive diagnostic papers. - The reward degradation analysis (§6) identifies a fundamental ceiling for OPD in long-horizon settings: teacher continuation advantage collapses from +0.37 at 1K to +0.02 at 16K prefix depth, with collapse propagating backward through the trajectory — an important finding for practitioners targeting agentic or extended chain-of-thought settings. - The three diagnostic metrics (overlap ratio, overlap-token advantage, entropy gap) are precisely defined and consistently discriminate successful from failing runs across model families and teacher-student pairs, providing monitoring tools that a practitioner can implement directly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) all experiments are on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and generalization to code and open-ended tasks remains open; (2) isolating the new-knowledge condition from pre-training corpus differences (vs. tokenizer or architecture differences) requires controlled pre-training ablations that are prohibitively expensive; (3) the anisotropy hypothesis for why globally informative rewards fail locally is suggestive but not directly verified; (4) teacher-aligned prompts substantially reduce student entropy, requiring mixing with out-of-distribution prompts to preserve exploration. These are confronted explicitly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper does not compare OPD-with-recipe against RL (GRPO/PPO) with the same compute budget; this is the most practically decisive comparison for post-training pipeline designers, and its absence leaves the central design question — when to choose OPD over RL — unanswered. - All students are base or lightly RL-trained models at 1.5B–7B scale; whether thinking-pattern alignment plays the same role at 70B+ is unknown and cannot be inferred from the current experiments. - The AIME/AMC evaluation domain matches the training data domain exactly; no out-of-domain generalization test is reported, which creates a risk that the recipes optimize for benchmark-specific patterns rather than general OPD behavior. - "Thinking pattern" is operationalized through the observable overlap ratio metric but is not defined a priori; a practitioner cannot determine compatibility before training begins, limiting the prescriptive value of the framework. - The off-policy cold start recipe's total compute cost (SFT + OPD) versus just running more RL from the base model is never reported, which is necessary for a cost-benefit assessment. Bottom Line Practitioners designing OPD post-training pipelines gain concrete diagnostic tools and empirically validated recipes from this work, anchored by a well-designed reverse-distillation experiment that overturns the "stronger teacher is always better" assumption — but the math-only evaluation scope, absence of RL baselines, and untested scaling behavior mean the work answers "why does OPD fail?" more definitively than "should you use OPD at all?"
Recommended 2604.13016 PDF
#65 — Innovative (70)

Tracing the Roots: A Multi-Agent Framework for Uncovering Data Lineage in Post-Training LLMs (14 ▲ )

Yu Li, Xiaoran Shang, Qizhi Pei, Yun Zhu, Xin Gao, Honglin Lin, Zhanping Zhong, Zhuoshi Pan, Zheng Liu, Xiaoyang Wang, Conghui He, Dahua Lin, Feng Zhao, Lijun Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) drives the score: released code, a concrete 430-node lineage graph, and immediately actionable contamination findings (96.80% Omni-Math in Skywork-OR1; 37.95% indirect inheritance by Caco-1.3M) that data curators can act on today. Novelty (70) from the graph-level reframing and automated multi-agent construction. Non-Obviousness (75) — the indirect contamination propagation pathways are genuinely surprising. Rigor (55): no graph precision/recall against a gold standard, no ablation on agent contributions, no downstream training validation of the 570K corpus. Completeness (60) and Resilience (55): diversity advantage measured only in embedding space, exact-triplet metric underestimates paraphrased contamination, preprint status. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*55+0.10*75+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.30*80 = 66.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a multi-agent automated framework that reconstructs the evolutionary dependency graph of post-training LLM datasets from unstructured documentation, producing a 430-node, 971-edge lineage graph covering math, code, general, and science domains, and uses it to audit structural redundancy, benchmark contamination, and guide diversity-optimized data curation. - Why it's important: LLM post-training datasets are rarely constructed from scratch — they recursively derive from upstream sources — yet these dependencies are undocumented, causing hidden redundancy that erodes effective diversity and benchmark contamination that silently inflates evaluation scores across the community. - How it does it: A four-stage multi-agent pipeline (candidate validation → multi-source retrieval → parallel semantic source inference → aggregation + canonical resolution) performs depth-first traversal from 83 seed datasets, extracting structured ⟨source, relation, confidence, evidence⟩ tuples from HuggingFace READMEs, GitHub repos, blogs, and arXiv papers, then canonicalizes aliases and filters hallucinated edges before adding upstream nodes back to the processing queue. - The good: The contamination propagation finding is concrete and alarming — Caco-1.3M inherits 37.95% Omni-Math leakage without directly including the benchmark itself, a latent pathway invisible to sample-level scanning, demonstrated via traceable inheritance paths. - The missing: The paper never trains a model on the provenance-sampled 570K dataset and evaluates it against baselines; the diversity advantage (Vendi Score 452.44 vs 437.76 for OpenHermes-2.5) is measured purely on embedding geometry, not on downstream task performance, so the practical payoff of the method is unconfirmed. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or audit post-training datasets — the contamination propagation findings and lineage-centric auditing methodology are directly actionable; skip it if you need proof that diversity metrics translate to model quality improvements. Problem & Motivation: Post-training data construction has shifted from ground-up human annotation to a recursive repurposing paradigm — datasets are derived from upstream corpora via semantic evolution, knowledge distillation, structured fusion, and subset inclusion. The community lacks any systematic mechanism to track these dependencies. Two concrete risks follow. First, datasets that appear distinct may silently share large portions of the same upstream material, causing structural redundancy that reduces effective training diversity while inflating apparent data volume. Second, benchmark test samples embedded in upstream datasets are unknowingly inherited by derivatives, creating contamination pathways that inflate evaluation scores without the downstream data curator being aware. Neither risk is addressable by sample-level scanning alone when the contamination or overlap is inherited indirectly through intermediary datasets. Key Idea: The core insight is that dataset provenance is a graph problem, not a sample-matching problem. By representing datasets as nodes and inheritance relationships as directed edges in an evolutionary graph, one can trace contamination and redundancy along topological paths rather than scanning millions of examples pairwise. This reframing allows efficient, scalable auditing — detecting that Caco-1.3M inherits Omni-Math contamination without ever directly indexing that benchmark — and enables provenance-anchored diversity sampling by selecting from graph leaf nodes (original upstream sources with no known ancestors) rather than from derivative mixtures. Methodology: The framework formalizes data lineage as a directed graph G = (V, E), where nodes are post-training datasets and directed edges indicate upstream inheritance (v_i → v_j means v_i contributes to v_j). The pipeline executes four sequential stages per candidate dataset. (1) Candidate Validation: deduplication filter + HuggingFace API availability check + effective release date assignment (min of HF timestamp and associated paper date) + post-2020 filter to align with the modern LLM era. (2) Multi-Source Information Retrieval: a sourcing agent (GPT-5.1) parses HuggingFace READMEs to discover GitHub repos, technical blogs, and arXiv papers; specialized extracting agents (Gemini-2.5-Flash) fetch and noise-prune this content into a unified resource context. (3) Semantic Source Inference: a pool of parallel tracing agents (GPT-5.1) analyzes the unified context to extract structured ⟨S, R, C, E⟩ tuples — source identity, relation type (semantic evolution / CoT distillation / synthetic generation / structured fusion / direct inclusion), confidence score, and supporting evidence — explicitly instructed to exclude evaluation baselines and non-integrated references. (4) Aggregation, Resolution, and Recursive Expansion: an aggregation agent (Gemini-2.5-Pro) deduplicates and canonicalizes dataset names to HuggingFace org/name identifiers via RAG-style similarity reasoning; anachronistic edges (source release date after target) and low-confidence hallucinated edges are pruned; the semantic profile of the target is synthesized; and newly discovered upstream sources are submitted to the centralized queue for recursive processing. Graph traversal terminates at leaf nodes (no known upstream) or pre-2020 datasets. A confidence-aware human verification protocol routes low-confidence extractions for manual review. Provenance-based sampling anchors instruction selection at leaf nodes (d_in = 0), ranks them by out-degree and domain coverage, applies MinHash deduplication (128 permutations, threshold 0.7, N-gram 8), and format-unifies to Alpaca format, yielding 570K samples. Diversity is measured via Vendi Score (Shannon entropy of eigenvalues of the cosine similarity kernel matrix) and Centroid Distance (complement of average cosine similarity to global centroid), both computed using Qwen3-Embedding-8B embeddings at 4096 dimensions. Key Results: Starting from 83 high-impact seed datasets, the framework reconstructed a graph of 430 nodes and 971 edges. Domain-level topology is highly differentiated: math (99 nodes, average depth 2.92, out-degree 1.54) shows deep vertical refinement; general (285 nodes, average depth 1.05, leaf ratio 68.42%) shows broad shallow accumulation. Cross-domain analysis shows general domain is near saturation (new leaf injection dropped from 41 in 2024 to 5 in 2025), while math intermediate nodes surged from 18 to 33 in the same period. Structural redundancy: 17/83 examined datasets exceed 1% exact-match redundancy rate; open-instruct-v1 reaches 46.48% redundancy by incorporating its own superset. Benchmark contamination (exact (instruction, input, output) triplet matching): Skywork-OR1-RL-Data contains 96.80% of Omni-Math (4265/4406 samples); openbmb/UltraFeedback contains 99.27% of TruthfulQA (811/817); agentica-org/DeepCoder-Preview-Dataset contains 88.12% of LiveCodeBench v5 (89/101); garage-bAInd/Open-Platypus contains 70.59% of TheoremQA and 76.23% of SciBench. Caco-1.3M inherits 37.95% Omni-Math contamination without directly ingesting the benchmark, through upstream propagation. Provenance-based sampling achieves Vendi Score 452.44 and Centroid Distance 0.6385 at 570K samples, outperforming OpenHermes-2.5 (437.76 / 0.6271 at 615K), TextbookReasoning (283.75 / 0.5598 at 651K), MegaScience (373.78 / 0.6150 at 1.2M), and OpenThoughts3 (133.26 / 0.4970 at 1.2M). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.10480, April 2026); results are self-reported and not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The data lineage graph formalism applied to the LLM post-training ecosystem is new; the multi-agent automated construction pipeline is a novel system artifact. The contamination propagation findings and quantitative redundancy measurements constitute new empirical findings about the ecosystem. The provenance-based sampling strategy is a new application of the lineage graph to data curation. The individual components — LLM agents, graph traversal, MinHash deduplication, Vendi Score — are standard techniques; the contribution is their orchestration for this previously unaddressed problem. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is released at https://github.com/Leey21/data-lineage. Contamination measurement uses strict exact triplet matching, which is well-defined but conservative — paraphrased or reformatted benchmark samples are not detected. No ablation isolates which agent (sourcing vs. tracing vs. aggregation) contributes the most to graph accuracy, or how sensitive the graph topology is to the choice of underlying LLMs (GPT-5.1 vs. Gemini). The lineage graph's precision and recall are not measured against a human-constructed gold standard; the paper reports a confidence-aware verification protocol but does not quantify the fraction of extractions requiring manual review or the precision of the automated extractions. Diversity metrics are computed on a single embedding model (Qwen3-Embedding-8B); sensitivity to embedding choice is not tested. No error bars or significance tests accompany the diversity comparison in Table 4. No downstream model is trained on the 570K dataset to validate that embedding-space diversity gains translate to task performance improvements. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (built tool with lineage graph as deployed artifact, evaluated on real HuggingFace datasets). - Practical: The framework runs via LangChain with GPT-5.1 and Gemini-2.5-Pro/Flash API calls, requiring no specialized hardware, but does require access to proprietary models, making reproducibility contingent on API availability and cost. - Efficient: Computational cost is not stated; each dataset requires multi-source retrieval (web crawl + arXiv API) plus multiple LLM calls across four agent types, applied recursively to 430 nodes — the total API cost is not reported, making it unclear whether this is tractable for individual researchers. - Effective: The graph successfully surfaces latent contamination pathways (e.g., Caco-1.3M → Omni-Math) and redundancy rates that exact-match sample scanning would not detect without traversing the full upstream chain; the lineage mechanism demonstrably solves the stated problem for the sampled ecosystem. - Scalable: The framework is designed for recursive expansion and can trace newly encountered datasets in real time, but it is bounded by documentary transparency and tested on only 430 nodes; behavior at 10×–100× scale (thousands of datasets, including undocumented or closed ones) is claimed but not demonstrated. The paper delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) and a released lineage graph/dataset. Strengths: - The reframing from sample-level to graph-level auditing is the right conceptual move: it catches indirect contamination paths (Caco-1.3M inheriting Omni-Math via two hops) that no amount of pairwise sample scanning would reveal, and it does so without reading millions of samples. - The contamination propagation measurements are concrete and consequential: 96.80% of Omni-Math appearing in Skywork-OR1-RL-Data is not a marginal finding — it directly invalidates evaluations of models trained on that corpus, and tracing the exact propagation paths (Table 11) gives data curators actionable guidance. - The domain-level topological analysis (math deep/vertical, general shallow/horizontal, science underdeveloped) reveals structural patterns in how the community has invested in data construction that are not visible from any individual dataset's README. - Provenance-based sampling demonstrating that a 570K root-anchored dataset exceeds 1.2M-sample collections on both diversity metrics provides strong evidence that structural diversity (avoiding derivative redundancy) matters more than raw volume. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state two limitations directly: (1) LLM hallucination risk requiring human verification for low-confidence extractions; (2) the framework is bounded by documentary transparency and cannot recover dependencies when creators do not disclose or intentionally conceal upstream sources. These are confronted plainly and are genuine scope boundaries, not minimized.
