Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most critical unacknowledged gap is the absence of any downstream model training evaluation: the 570K provenance-sampled corpus is only validated on embedding-based diversity metrics, never on actual model performance. It is entirely possible that embedding geometry diversity does not translate to task diversity that benefits training. The framework uses GPT-5.1 and Gemini-2.5-Pro as backbone agents — these are frontier proprietary models with specific knowledge of the datasets being traced (they were likely trained on READMEs describing these datasets), introducing a potential circularity or advantage that an open-source reproduction would not have. The exact-triplet contamination metric will systematically underestimate contamination for datasets that paraphrase or reformat upstream content (e.g., CoT distillation preserves the question but rewrites the answer); the paper does not quantify how much contamination is missed by this choice. Finally, the 83 seed datasets were selected based on download volume and community engagement — the lineage graph covers only high-impact public HuggingFace datasets, so contamination and redundancy findings for proprietary or lower-profile training corpora used by commercial LLM providers are entirely outside scope, yet those are the datasets where contamination is most consequential for leaderboard integrity. Bottom Line: Any practitioner building, auditing, or selecting post-training datasets should read this paper — the contamination propagation findings alone justify attention, and the lineage-centric auditing methodology offers a qualitatively different and more efficient alternative to sample-level scanning, though the missing downstream evaluation means the data curation recommendation rests on geometry rather than proven training benefit.
Recommended 2604.10480 PDF
#66 — Innovative (70)

WebForge: Breaking the Realism-Reproducibility-Scalability Trilemma in Browser Agent Benchmark

Peng Yuan, Yuyang Yin, Yuxuan Cai, Zheng Wei · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) — first end-to-end automated pipeline producing 934 verified interactive web tasks with anti-cheating and multi-dim difficulty; clear adoption potential pending code release. Novelty (70) — pipeline integration and a-priori difficulty control are genuinely new. Rigor (60) — pipeline ablation strong, but no human baseline, no error bars, no released URL yet, and validator/agent loop creates a circularity (LLM-solvable ≠ hard). Non-Obv (60), Completeness (60) and Resilience (55) — moderate inter-dimension correlations (|ρ|=0.495) directly undercut orthogonality claim, simulated noise unvalidated against real noise. Weighted ≈ 0.15*70+0.15*60+0.10*60+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.30*80 = 64.75. Bumped to 70 reflecting scale of the engineering contribution and concrete capability-profiling findings.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WebForge, a fully automated four-agent pipeline (Plan → Generate → Refine → Validate) that generates realistic, reproducible, and scalable browser agent benchmarks with seven-dimensional difficulty control, producing WebForge-Bench: 934 verified tasks across 7 domains and 3 difficulty levels. - Why it's important: Existing browser agent benchmarks are stuck in a trilemma — real-website benchmarks rot (nearly half of Mind2Web's tasks expired within two years), controlled environments lack real-web noise, and both require expensive human curation — making it impossible to reliably track agent progress. - How it does it: Four specialized LLM agents collaboratively design tasks with 7-dimensional difficulty vectors, build static self-contained websites with injected real-web noise and anti-cheating mechanisms, then verify solvability end-to-end in an actual Chromium browser before a task enters the benchmark. - The good: Removing screenshot input from multimodal models drops accuracy by 14–16 percentage points overall (Gemini-3-Pro: 75.9%→59.2%), confirming the benchmark genuinely requires visual understanding rather than just DOM parsing. - The missing: The seven difficulty dimensions share average off-diagonal Spearman correlation |ρ|=0.495, meaning they are meaningfully correlated — the claim of orthogonal capability profiling is partially undermined, and no analysis tests whether simulated noise (injected pop-ups, cookie dialogs) produces the same agent failure modes as genuine real-web noise. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating browser agents should read this — the multi-dimensional profiling reveals capability gaps (e.g., a 52-point L3 Reasoning/Calc spread between Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5-Nano) that single aggregate scores obscure, and the pipeline is the first to automate full interactive environment construction end-to-end. Problem & Motivation: Browser agent benchmarks suffer from a structural trilemma. Real-website benchmarks (Mind2Web, WebVoyager, MMInA) maximize realism but suffer from content drift — Mind2Web lost ~50% of tasks within two years, and WebVoyager reports 22.3% non-deterministic answers. Controlled environments (WebArena, VisualWebArena, EntWorld) guarantee reproducibility but omit real-web noise (pop-ups, cookie dialogs, network latency) and require thousands of person-hours of manual curation — TheAgentCompany needed ~3,000 person-hours for 175 tasks. Additionally, no existing benchmark supports a priori, multi-dimensional difficulty control: difficulty is either absent (WebArena), limited to 1–2 post-hoc dimensions (VisualWebArena, WorkArena++), or computed from SQL structural features post-hoc (EntWorld). This makes it impossible to attribute model failures to specific capability axes, and aggregate scores mask domain-specific biases invisible when evaluating only one domain. Key Idea: The core insight is that a multi-agent LLM pipeline can bootstrap complete, self-contained interactive web environments from scratch — designing tasks, scraping real visual and content assets from the web, building static HTML/JS/CSS sites with localStorage-based stateful interactions, injecting real-web noise, and verifying solvability in a live browser — without any human annotation, while using a seven-dimensional difficulty vector as a structured generative constraint rather than a post-hoc label. Methodology: WebForge instantiates the pipeline as four specialized agents forming a closed loop: (d, l) →[Plan]→ P →[Generate]→ W →[Refine]→ W →[Validate]→ {0,1}. The Plan Agent uses dual-LLM generation: a creativity-oriented model at temperature T=2.0 drafts diverse task objectives with seven-dimensional difficulty configurations, then a precision-oriented model at T=1.0 performs logic verification and enforces compositional difficulty constraints (e.g., Level 3 requires ≥2 dimensions at L3 and ≥2 at L2). The Generation Agent produces a runnable web environment W = (S, A, M) — website files, answer configuration, and metadata — by searching real websites for visual assets, embedding real-world data (product listings, news text), implementing all navigation links as functional pages, and using localStorage for stateful interactions within purely static pages. Critical design decision: a final-state evaluation paradigm (not step-wise), with three anti-cheating mechanisms: encrypted data storage (Base64+XOR), deceptive error codes (wrong order IDs rendered as plausible-looking wrong values), and code obfuscation. The Refinement Agent follows an Assess→Plan→Execute→Verify loop against a quality rules checklist, with real-web noise injection (pop-up advertisements, cookie consent prompts, network latency simulation) as a key realism step. The Validation Agent operates in a Chromium browser in GUI non-headless mode, replaying the solution path in an Observe–Reason–Act loop (≤50 steps); f_val(W) = 1 iff s_T = s* and T ≤ 50, with a 3-retry mechanism for transient failures. Three answer types are supported: Direct Answer (exact value matching), Operation Code (unique code computed from accumulated state via the anti-cheating mechanism), and Mixed. The seven difficulty dimensions are: Jump Depth (page transitions: L1=1–2, L3=6+), Jump Breadth (links per page: L1=1–2, L3=6+), Page Interaction (actions per page: L1=1–2 simple clicks, L3=6+ complex forms), Visual Complexity (L1=text/DOM only, L3=correlating multiple charts/images), Info Complexity (L1=prominent key info, L3=high density/buried in noise), Reasoning/Calc (L1=direct lookup, L3=multi-step/optimization/complex math), and Risk Factor (L1=read-only, L3=subtle irreversible actions with no obvious warnings). Key Results: The pipeline generates 1,260 candidate tasks (60 per domain–level pair across 7×3); 934 pass Validation Agent solvability verification (74.1% pipeline pass rate). Fourteen model configurations are evaluated. Gemini-3-Pro achieves the highest overall accuracy at 75.9%, followed by Claude-4.5-Sonnet (69.9%), Gemini-3-Flash (67.1%), and Kimi-K2.5 (66.4%, best open-source, surpassing GPT-5.2 at 59.5%). At Level 3, the strongest-to-weakest gap is 56 points (Gemini-3-Pro 58.0% vs. Qwen3-Omni-30B 2.4%). Cross-domain analysis reveals D4 (Info Retrieval) is the easiest (56.9% average) while D1 (Consumer Transaction, 48.3%) and D2 (Content Moderation, 48.3%) are hardest — a 9-point gap invisible in single-domain benchmarks. GPT-5-Mini scores 73.8% on D4 but drops to 50.4% on D3 (Enterprise Process), a 23-point domain gap. Removing screenshots from multimodal models reduces accuracy by 16–17 points overall (Gemini-3-Pro: 75.9%→59.2%; Gemini-3-Flash: 67.1%→51.2%), with the gap widening at higher levels (6 points at L1, 16–23 at L2, 20+ at L3). Pipeline ablation on a 210-task subset confirms each stage matters: no refinement at all achieves 51.4%, adding Refinement Agent alone (no Plan refinement) reaches 59.5%, and the full pipeline reaches 74.1%. Prompt tokens scale super-linearly with difficulty (5–8× from L1 to L3 across comparable models). Per-dimension analysis shows Reasoning/Calc most separates strong from weak models at L3: Gemini-3-Pro 58.3% vs. GPT-5-Nano 6.4% (52-point spread). Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the end-to-end automated pipeline that produces fully interactive browser environments with anti-cheating mechanisms, real-web noise, and a priori seven-dimensional difficulty control — no prior work automates complete interactive web environment generation. The individual components (LLM agents, dual-temperature generation, Chromium-based validation) are combinations of existing techniques applied to a new construction problem. Rigor & Reproducibility: Pipeline ablations isolate contribution of each stage. Statistical validation of the seven-dimensional framework includes Spearman rank correlation analysis (average |ρ|=0.495 off-diagonal), dimension-level distribution tables, and domain×level pass rate breakdown. No error bars or significance tests are reported for model accuracy scores (single-run evaluation across 934 tasks). The paper states code and benchmark will be publicly released, but no repository URL is provided in the manuscript. A potential methodological concern: the Validation Agent uses the same LLM-based browsing approach that tested agents will use, so difficulty calibration reflects LLM-solvable tasks rather than an objective difficulty ground truth — the benchmark may be biased toward problem types that LLM agents can solve rather than those that humans find hard. No human baseline is reported. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built pipeline and benchmark, evaluated by deployment and multi-model experiments. - Practical: The pipeline runs LLM API calls and a Chromium browser, requiring no specialized hardware; evaluation agents run in standard GPU-free Chromium instances. - Efficient: Prompt tokens per task at Level 3 reach up to 1608K for some models; the pipeline's generation cost per task is not reported, making total benchmark construction cost opaque. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably discriminates models (56-point L3 gap) and exposes domain and visual capability biases that aggregate scores hide; solvability is machine-verified in a live browser rather than claimed by construction. - Scalable: The pipeline is designed to be run repeatedly with zero human annotation and already generated 1,260 tasks; the paper claims it can scale to new domains and difficulty settings, but no stress test at 10×–100× scale or cost analysis of large-scale generation is presented. The paper delivers a software artifact (generation pipeline + WebForge-Bench) that will be publicly released. Strengths: - The four-agent pipeline achieves genuine end-to-end automation of interactive web environment construction — a step beyond all compared benchmarks, which require manual deployment, data population, or annotation. - The seven-dimensional difficulty framework with a priori control enables diagnostic evaluation that reveals capability biases (domain, visual, reasoning) completely invisible in aggregate scores — empirically confirmed by the cross-model analysis. - Anti-cheating mechanisms (encrypted data, deceptive codes, obfuscation) and the final-state evaluation paradigm meaningfully raise the bar against spurious task completion strategies. - The ablation showing each pipeline stage contributes substantially (51.4%→59.5%→74.1% solvability pass rate) provides real evidence that complexity in the design is earned. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that WebForge currently targets benchmark generation rather than training data generation, and identify large-scale training data production as a promising future direction. They do not confront other limitations in the main text, though the moderate inter-dimension correlations (|ρ|=0.495) are reported as a factual observation rather than a limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: First, the moderate inter-dimension correlations undercut the claim of independent capability profiling — dimensions that correlate at ρ=0.69–0.75 (Jump Depth with Jump Breadth, Reasoning/Calc, and Info Complexity) do not cleanly isolate distinct agent capabilities. Second, no human baseline performance is reported, making it impossible to assess whether the benchmark's difficulty gradient reflects human-meaningful task difficulty or LLM-specific failure modes. Third, the simulated noise injection (LLM-generated pop-ups, fake cookie dialogs) is a proxy for real-web noise — no validation that simulated disturbances produce the same agent failure modes as genuine advertising pop-ups or real cookie consent flows is provided. Fourth, the 14 evaluated models all appear to be frontier LLMs; no smaller/weaker models at the very bottom are included to characterize whether Level 1 tasks are trivially solvable baselines. Fifth, this is an arXiv preprint and has not undergone peer review. Bottom Line: Researchers evaluating browser agents have a concrete need for reproducible, scalable benchmarks with diagnostic resolution, and WebForge-Bench is the first that simultaneously delivers all three properties — practitioners building or comparing web agents should adopt it as a primary evaluation suite once the code is released.
Search Results 2604.10988 PDF
#67 — Innovative (69)

GlobalSplat: Efficient Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting via Global Scene Tokens (15 ▲ )

Roni Itkin, Noam Issachar, Yehonatan Keypur, Yehonatan Keypur, Anpei Chen, Sagie Benaim · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) drives the composite — careful capacity-controlled ablations, strong baseline coverage, reasonable design-choice isolation, though no seeds/error bars and evaluation limited to 256×256 forward-facing video. Novelty (70) — 'align first, decode later' via fixed global latents is a genuine architectural reorientation for feed-forward 3DGS, though concurrent C3G weakens first-mover status. Non-Obviousness (65) — the fixed-latent bottleneck principle is non-obvious. Completeness (65) — LPIPS gap and narrow dataset scope acknowledged but not closed. Resilience (65) — claims well-bounded, limitations stated honestly. Impact (65) — project page exists, compelling efficiency footprint, but explicit code/weights release not confirmed. Weighted: 0.20×70+0.25×75+0.15×65+0.15×65+0.15×65+0.10×65 ≈ 69.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GlobalSplat, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting framework that encodes any number of input views into a fixed set of 2,048 global latent scene tokens and then decodes them into a view-count-invariant budget of 16K 3D Gaussians in a single forward pass. - Why it's important: Existing feed-forward 3DGS pipelines form primitives from per-pixel or per-voxel view-aligned intermediates, so representation size grows with input-view count, inflating GPU memory and inference time and degrading global consistency at large context sizes. - How it does it: A dual-branch cross/self-attention encoder iteratively fuses multi-view patch features into fixed learnable latent tokens (geometry and appearance streams disentangled), followed by a coarse-to-fine training curriculum that progressively unlocks Gaussian capacity from 1 to 8 per token to prevent representation bloat. - The good: GlobalSplat achieves 28.53 dB PSNR at 24 views on RealEstate10K using only 16K Gaussians and 1.79 GB peak GPU memory — the best memory efficiency reported and 2.5× faster than the next-fastest Gaussian-based baseline (Zpressor at 194 ms vs. 78 ms), while staying within ~0.1 dB PSNR of Zpressor. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to two indoor/aerial video datasets at 256×256 resolution; there is no test on object-centric or 360° unbounded scenes (e.g., DTU, Tanks and Temples), and the quality gap versus Zpressor on perceptual metrics (LPIPS 0.140 vs. 0.097) is not closed. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building real-time or resource-constrained novel-view synthesis pipelines should read this; those primarily concerned with peak reconstruction fidelity will find Zpressor still dominant on perceptual quality. Problem & Motivation Feed-forward novel-view synthesis requires predicting a 3D scene representation from a sparse set of calibrated images in a single network pass — no per-scene optimization. Existing feed-forward 3DGS pipelines (PixelSplat, DepthSplat, MVSplat, etc.) decode scene primitives from dense pixel-aligned or voxel-aligned intermediates. As the number of context views grows (12→24→36), these pipelines bake massive redundancy into the 3D asset: DepthSplat uses 786K–2,359K Gaussians for 12–36 views, and memory scales accordingly (up to 29.84 GB peak). This makes large-context reconstruction slow, memory-prohibitive, and prone to inconsistency at overlapping regions. A globally coherent, view-count-invariant scene representation is missing. Key Idea Instead of allocating primitives per pixel or per voxel (local, view-anchored), GlobalSplat first fuses all input views into a fixed-cardinality set of learnable global scene tokens via cross-attention, resolving multi-view correspondences in latent space before any geometry is decoded. Because the latent bottleneck is fixed at M=2,048 tokens regardless of input-view count, the decoded 3D Gaussian count (16K) is also fixed, eliminating representation bloat. The coarse-to-fine curriculum — starting with G=1 merged Gaussian per token and progressively increasing to G=8 — prevents the optimizer from committing to fine geometry before global structure has converged. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Input preparation: Each view's 8×8 RGB patches are patchified and concatenated with Plücker-ray embeddings plus a per-view camera code (Fourier-encoded camera center + intrinsics MLP) to form input tokens u_{i,p} ∈ ℝ^{512+256}. (2) Dual-branch encoder: B=4 iterative blocks update M=2,048 learnable latent tokens via parallel geometry (Q_G) and appearance (Q_A) streams. Each stream applies cross-attention over all input patches followed by L=2 self-attention blocks; the two streams are fused through a 2-layer mixer MLP that produces updated latent tokens for the next block. (3) Coarse-to-fine decoder: Each latent token predicts 16 Gaussian candidates; during training, stages s∈{0,1,2,3} expose G=2^s Gaussians per token (1,2,4,8), merging candidates via a geometry-conditioned temperature-scaled softmax gate. Smooth stage transitions use linear interpolation between adjacent merged representations over 2k optimizer steps. The final model yields N=2,048×8=16,384 Gaussians total. Geometry attributes (positions ℝ^{16×3}, anisotropic log-scales ℝ^{16×3}, 6D rotations ℝ^{16×6}, opacity logits ℝ^{16×1}) and appearance attributes (SH degree-3 coefficients ℝ^{16×48}) use attribute-specific merge rules: log-space volume-preserving for scales, transmittance-space for opacity. (4) Training objective: Three-term loss — (i) rendering loss (MSE + perceptual), (ii) self-supervised consistency loss enforcing matching opacity and depth maps between reconstructions from two interleaved input subsets I_a, I_b (symmetric stop-gradient), and (iii) regularization (frustum constraint, opacity/scale/rotation/SH soft penalties). Training uses AdamW, 5×10^{-4} LR, 220k steps, global batch size 16 at 256×256 resolution. Key Results On RealEstate10K (primary benchmark), GlobalSplat16K achieves PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS of 26.83/0.838/0.198 (12 views), 28.53/0.885/0.140 (24 views), and 28.45/0.880/0.144 (36 views), all using exactly 16K Gaussians. The stronger 32K variant reaches 29.54/0.903/0.121. For context, Zpressor (the quality leader among Gaussian-based methods) achieves 28.46/0.910/0.097 at 24 views using 393K Gaussians — GlobalSplat matches PSNR but trails substantially on SSIM and LPIPS. C3G (concurrent compact work with 2 Gaussians) achieves only 22.24/0.598/0.331. DepthSplat with 1,572K Gaussians reaches only 19.66/0.743/0.239. Efficiency benchmark at 24 views on one NVIDIA A100: GlobalSplat requires 1.79 GB peak memory and 77.88 ms inference vs. Zpressor's 3.70 GB / 194.20 ms, C3G's 6.04 GB / 387.14 ms, and DepthSplat's 29.84 GB / 669.50 ms. Disk footprint is 3.8 MB vs. 134 MB (Zpressor) and 534 MB (DepthSplat). Cross-dataset zero-shot transfer to ACID (24 views) gives GlobalSplat16K PSNR 28.03/SSIM 0.813/LPIPS 0.208 vs. Zpressor 28.44/0.860/0.140 and C3G 22.24/0.598/0.332. Ablations (Table 5) show each component contributes: removing the coarse-to-fine curriculum drops PSNR 0.88 dB; removing the consistency loss drops 0.42 dB; replacing dual-stream with single-stream drops 0.55 dB; removing extra camera metadata (using Plücker only) drops 0.27 dB. Importantly, increasing latent count is substantially more effective than increasing Gaussians per token at fixed total Gaussian budget (26.83 vs. 25.25 PSNR for 2K-Gaussian configurations). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The "align first, decode later" principle applied to feed-forward 3DGS — using fixed-cardinality learnable global latents as the fusion bottleneck rather than per-pixel or per-voxel intermediates — is a genuine architectural reorientation. The dual-branch disentangled encoder, coarse-to-fine merging curriculum, and subset-consistency self-supervision are novel in combination, though individually each borrows from established repertoire (cross-attention for multi-view aggregation is standard; curriculum training is well-known; consistency losses appear in other self-supervised 3D works). LVSM [Jin et al., 2024] used global latents but did not produce explicit 3DGS; C3G [An et al., 2025] is acknowledged as concurrent and uses global Gaussians but with full self-attention and single-branch decoding. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Tables 4 and 5) cover the four main design choices — dual-stream vs. single-stream, coarse-to-fine vs. direct full-capacity, consistency loss presence, camera metadata — and are compared on the same benchmark and resolution. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple random seeds are reported. The ablation comparing single-stream uses a widened model (90M vs. 83.4M parameters) to control for capacity — a methodologically careful choice. Baselines are evaluated using official codebases and checkpoints, with specific caveats documented (e.g., GGN collapses at 36 views; EcoSplat is taken from published numbers due to code unavailability; AnySplat/NoPoSplat numbers taken from C3G because official checkpoints focus on sparse settings). The paper does not state code or model weight release explicitly, though a project page (https://r-itk.github.io/globalsplat/) is listed; it is a preprint and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (new method with experimental evaluation). - Practical: Inference runs on a single NVIDIA A100 with 1.79 GB peak memory; the compact 3.8 MB model footprint and 78 ms latency are compatible with real-time or near-real-time deployment on commodity hardware. - Efficient: GlobalSplat is the most memory-efficient and fastest-inference method among all Gaussian-based baselines tested, by a substantial margin (5–15× less memory, 2.5–23× faster than competitors), at comparable PSNR. - Effective: PSNR is competitive with the best Gaussian-based method (Zpressor), but perceptual quality (LPIPS 0.140 vs. 0.097 at 24 views) indicates GlobalSplat still produces less sharp, detail-accurate reconstructions than the best quality-focused baselines at larger Gaussian budgets. - Scalable: The fixed-size latent set is the model's core scalability claim — representation size does not grow with input-view count, tested at 12/24/36 views; no experiment tests beyond 36 views, and the authors acknowledge fixed-budget limitations for city-scale or unbounded scenes. The work delivers a trained model (weights implied by the project page) and presumably inference code; training code and dataset preprocessing are not explicitly confirmed as released. Strengths - The core inversion of the primitive-formation pipeline — fusing views globally before allocating any geometry — is a principled and practically validated design decision, not just an engineering variation. - The coarse-to-fine curriculum is well-motivated and the ablation (−0.88 dB PSNR without it) demonstrates it is load-bearing, not cosmetic; the smooth-transition interpolation between stages is a clean implementation that avoids discontinuities. - Efficiency gains are genuine and large: 1.79 GB vs. 3.70–29.84 GB peak memory, 78 ms vs. 194–1,801 ms inference — these are not obtained by sacrificing quality, since PSNR is matched or exceeded versus most baselines. - Cross-dataset zero-shot transfer to ACID without retraining, with competitive results, supports the claim that global scene tokens capture transferable structure rather than overfitting to RealEstate10K's visual distribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors forthrightly state three limitations in Section 4.5: (1) The fixed 16K Gaussian budget may be representationally insufficient for unbounded or city-scale environments. (2) GlobalSplat assumes static scenes; extending to dynamic/temporal reconstruction would require architectural additions (e.g., spatio-temporal cross-attention). (3) Extreme sparse-view settings (2–3 images) remain challenging because insufficient parallax prevents global latent resolution. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The LPIPS gap versus Zpressor (0.140 vs. 0.097 at 24 views, 0.121 vs. 0.097 at 32K Gaussians) is significant for any application where perceptual sharpness matters; the paper frames this as a quality-efficiency tradeoff but does not investigate whether further scaling the Gaussian budget (e.g., 64K–128K) could close the LPIPS gap while preserving memory/speed advantages. - Evaluation is restricted to forward-facing or localized aerial video sequences (RealEstate10K, ACID) at 256×256 resolution. No evaluation on object-centric scenes (DTU, CO3D), 360° unbounded scenes (NeRF Synthetic, Mip-NeRF 360), or higher resolutions (512×512, 1024×1024) is provided; the claim of generality is therefore only partially supported. - The consistency loss is applied symmetrically between two input subsets, but the degree to which this actually improves multi-view geometric consistency vs. acting as a regularizer is not tested with a direct geometric metric (e.g., depth error or point cloud accuracy against LiDAR ground truth). - No analysis of failure modes beyond qualitative figures: regions of very high frequency (reflections, transparent surfaces, fine text) are not specifically quantified, and no per-scene variance is reported. Bottom Line For practitioners who need fast, memory-efficient, deployable feed-forward 3D Gaussian reconstruction from multi-view video and can accept LPIPS competitive with — but not leading — Zpressor, GlobalSplat offers the best practical operating point in the current literature and is worth reading.
Recommended 2604.15284 PDF
#68 — Innovative (68)

PipeLive: Efficient Live In-place Pipeline Parallelism Reconfiguration for Dynamic LLM Serving

Xu Bai, Muhammed Tawfiqul Islam, Chen Wang, Adel N. Toosi · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (70) solid — first open-source live in-place PP reconfiguration with genuine algorithmic contributions (KV patching, layer stacking). Rigor (60) limited by two-GPU testbed, single-run 200-request benchmark, no error bars, no public repo URL stated. Non-Obviousness (70) — VM-live-migration-inspired KV patching is a non-obvious adaptation. Completeness (65) — honest about policy deferral but related systems (HydraServe, FlexPipe) not benchmarked. Resilience (60) — scaling claims beyond 2 GPUs are unsupported. Impact (75) — working vLLM-based implementation with concrete, measurable gains. Composite: 0.15*70+0.15*60+0.10*70+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*75 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PipeLive, an open-source LLM serving system built on vLLM that enables live, in-place pipeline parallelism (PP) reconfiguration — dynamically redistributing model layers and KV cache across GPUs without interrupting ongoing inference. - Why it's important: Static PP configurations are profiled offline and fixed for the serving lifetime, causing up to 20–30% throughput degradation when workload characteristics shift (e.g., from prefill-heavy to decode-heavy), a particularly acute problem in heterogeneous GPU deployments. - How it does it: PipeLive co-designs a five-phase reconfiguration coordinator with a redesigned non-contiguous KV cache layout (extending PagedAttention), layer stacking to align KV blocks with GPU memory allocation granularity, and incremental KV patching (inspired by VM live migration) to continuously synchronize KV states during migration, reducing final cutover to a brief atomic switch. - The good: KV patching reduces service interruption from seconds to ~10ms across all tested migration sizes, and PipeLive achieves 36% and 33% composite performance score gains over the best static baseline on LLaMA-70B and Qwen3-30B respectively. - The missing: All experiments use exactly two GPUs (A100 + L40S); behavior at 4, 8, or more GPUs — where coordination, deadlock avoidance, and migration bandwidth contention compound — is not evaluated. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and practitioners building heterogeneous or serverless LLM serving infrastructure should read this; it is the first complete, open-source treatment of in-place PP reconfiguration with a working implementation and honest ablations. Problem & Motivation: LLM serving over heterogeneous GPU clusters uses pipeline parallelism to partition model layers across devices. The optimal layer-to-GPU assignment depends on workload characteristics: prefill-heavy traffic (long inputs, short outputs) favors assigning more layers to compute-strong GPUs, while decode-heavy traffic (short inputs, long outputs) favors GPUs with high memory bandwidth. Existing serving systems (vLLM, SGLang, DistServe) profile a single PP configuration offline and hold it fixed. When workload mix shifts — as in serverless platforms or multi-tenant deployments — the static assignment can degrade TTFT or TPOT by 20–30% compared to the workload-optimal configuration. Reconfiguration via service restart discards in-flight requests and incurs minutes of downtime. The core gap is that no prior system supports live, in-place PP reconfiguration: redistributing layers and KV cache across existing GPUs while inference continues. Key Idea: The central insight is that PP reconfiguration's three hard sub-problems — (1) GPU memory is full of weights and KV cache leaving no room for new layers, (2) KV cache is physically contiguous in PagedAttention so resizing requires expensive copy, and (3) KV states must stay consistent during migration without blocking inference — can each be solved by a corresponding mechanism: KV cache shrinking via block-level allocation, layer stacking to align KV block sizes with GPU allocation granularity (eliminating fragmentation from dynamic sizing), and incremental KV patching (dirty-bitmap-driven continuous synchronization) that converges KV state divergence to a negligible residual before a brief atomic cutover. Methodology: PipeLive implements a five-phase reconfiguration protocol coordinated by a centralized Reconfiguration Coordinator. Phase 1 computes an intermediate configuration Cᵢₙₜ — the union of source and target layer sets per GPU — and checks feasibility under current KV block budget; if infeasible, reconfiguration aborts. Phase 2 performs KV cache shrinking by defragmenting live blocks (pointer-only operations, <1ms) and releasing freed blocks. Phase 3 launches asynchronous, non-blocking weight loading and KV state migration concurrently with ongoing inference. Phase 4 performs convergence monitoring: the coordinator tracks Tₛₒₕₑd (last scheduled token) and Tₐₚₚₗᵢₑd (last synchronized KV index) on each GPU; once the gap drops below threshold τ (set to 50 tokens on InfiniBand at 100 Gbps), it triggers Phase 5 — an atomic SyncAndCommit that switches to the target configuration, deletes obsolete layers, and reclaims KV memory. Critical design decisions: (a) Non-contiguous KV block access stores resolved GPU addresses directly in the block table rather than remapping virtual→physical at each inference step (avoiding PagedAttention's per-step remapping overhead); (b) Layer stacking packs k=4 layers' KV blocks into one 2MB GPU physical allocation unit, reducing effective KV block size by 4× and cutting fragmentation from ~44% to ~5%; (c) KV migration uses dedicated lower-priority NCCL communicator groups separate from the pipeline forwarding group, with a two-phase handshake protocol to prevent circular-wait deadlocks when migration and pipeline communication share overlapping GPU pairs. The KV patching dirty-bitmap drain-and-transmit loop runs continuously until the coordinator detects convergence and commits. Key Results: Evaluated on a two-GPU heterogeneous testbed (NVIDIA A100 80GB + NVIDIA L40S 48GB, InfiniBand 100 Gbps) with LLaMA-70B and Qwen3-30B. A pattern-shifting benchmark of 200 requests alternates between prefill-heavy (input=512, output=16) and decode-heavy (input=128, output=512) workloads at high request rates (3 req/s for LLaMA-70B, 5 req/s for Qwen3-30B). PipeLive achieves +36% composite performance score over the best static baseline (Prefill-Optimal) for LLaMA-70B and +33% for Qwen3-30B. For LLaMA-70B specifically, PipeLive reduces TTFT from 12,623ms (Prefill-Optimal) to 2,390ms and achieves TPOT of 346ms vs. 916ms (Decode-Optimal) — because Prefill-Optimal overloads L40S memory during decode phases. KV resizing enables sustaining 2.5× higher request rates without KV overflow compared to reconfiguration with KV resizing disabled. KV patching alone improves TTFT by up to 49.7% and TPOT by up to 29.5% compared to KV patch disabled; enabling asynchronous weight loading additionally improves TTFT by up to 72.4%. Stop time is held to ~10ms across all migration sizes, whereas both baselines (async-WL disabled, KV patch disabled) incur stop times that grow substantially with migrated layer count. Baselines are static configurations selected as workload-optimal by exhaustive profiling — the strongest achievable fixed-PP alternatives. No open-source dynamic PP reconfiguration system exists for direct comparison; closest prior work (HydraServe, FlexPipe, DynaPipe) is described but not benchmarked against with numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. PipeLive is the first complete, open-source system for live in-place PP reconfiguration in LLM serving — no prior system supports this capability. The KV patching mechanism (incremental dirty-bitmap-driven synchronization of KV states across GPUs during live migration) and the layer stacking technique (packing multiple layers' KV caches into a single GPU physical allocation unit) are novel algorithmic contributions. The non-contiguous PagedAttention extension and deadlock-avoiding NCCL two-phase handshake are engineering contributions applied in a genuinely new setting, not simple combinations of existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and thorough: layer stacking factor k is swept (1, 2, 4, 8, 16), KV patching is disabled/enabled independently, asynchronous weight loading is disabled/enabled, and synchronous vs. asynchronous migration modes are compared. Multiple request rates are swept for each configuration. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — the 200-request benchmark is a single run. Code is described as implemented in ~5,000 lines of Python and CUDA/C++ on top of vLLM and FlashAttention; the paper does not state whether the code is publicly released or provide a repository URL. The evaluation testbed has only two GPUs, which limits generalizability. No vendor self-reported numbers; all measurements are first-party. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.12171v1, April 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS paper. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (NVIDIA A100 and L40S) with standard interconnect (InfiniBand); built on the widely deployed vLLM framework. - Efficient: Service interruption reduced to ~10ms vs. seconds in naive approaches; KV resizing compaction involves pointer updates only (<1ms); layer stacking reduces fragmentation from 44% to ~5% with factor 4, recovering nearly half of previously wasted KV memory. - Effective: End-to-end results on LLaMA-70B and Qwen3-30B under realistic pattern-shifting workloads demonstrate PipeLive consistently outperforms all three static configurations across TTFT, TPOT, and throughput simultaneously, not just on one metric. - Scalable: Not tested beyond two GPUs; the paper explicitly defers multi-GPU scaling, and coordination complexity, NCCL group management, and migration bandwidth contention are all uncharacterized at larger pipeline depths. PipeLive delivers a software artifact (system implementation on vLLM + FlashAttention); repository release status is not stated. Strengths: - The KV patching mechanism is a genuinely novel adaptation of VM live migration semantics to GPU KV cache state, converting a blocking seconds-long synchronization barrier into a continuous background activity with a ~10ms terminal pause — a concrete, measurable advance over prior approaches. - The layer stacking technique elegantly resolves the fragmentation problem that arises when block-level KV allocation is used: aligning logical KV block size with the 2MB GPU physical allocation granularity by packing multiple layers eliminates ~88% of wasted memory (from 44% loss to ~5% loss) without changing the total KV capacity. - The five-phase reconfiguration protocol is formally specified with primitives (Collective and Synchronization), a well-defined intermediate configuration, and a published algorithm (Algorithm 1); this is more rigorous than typical systems papers. - Ablations cleanly isolate each component's contribution and reveal non-obvious interactions: disabling KV resizing causes sharp TTFT degradation even at moderate load (request rate > 1), demonstrating that KV resizing is load-bearing, not cosmetic. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly states it does not address when or why to trigger reconfiguration, nor how to determine optimal source/target PP configurations — these policy decisions are deferred to future work. The evaluation testbed is limited to two GPUs. The deadlock avoidance two-phase handshake adds protocol complexity. Layer stacking reduces reconfiguration granularity (migrations must occur in multiples of k), limiting fine-grained layer redistribution. The authors confront these limitations directly and proportionately. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Scaling behavior beyond two GPUs is entirely uncharacterized — with N GPUs, the number of GPU pairs requiring KV migration grows as O(N), NCCL group management complexity increases, and the two-phase deadlock avoidance handshake must coordinate more concurrent transfers; none of this is analyzed or measured. The 200-request benchmark with no repeated trials or error bars makes it difficult to assess result variance — a single pattern-shifting sequence of 200 requests may not be representative of production traffic distributions. The policy question (trigger timing, target config selection) is not just deferred but entirely absent: without a reconfiguration policy, the mechanism cannot be used autonomously, and the paper provides no guidance on how to build one or what its overhead would be. HydraServe, FlexPipe, and DynaPipe are described in related work but not benchmarked directly, leaving open the question of whether a less-invasive alternative (e.g., FlexPipe's inflight refactoring) might close the gap at lower implementation complexity. No repository URL is provided, raising reproducibility concerns for a systems paper. Bottom Line: PipeLive solves a real, well-defined problem — live PP reconfiguration — with technically sound mechanisms and honest ablations, and any team deploying LLMs across heterogeneous GPUs at variable workload intensity should read it; the policy question (when and how to trigger reconfiguration) is the most important remaining gap before the system can operate fully autonomously.
Search Results 2604.12171 PDF
#69 — Innovative (68)

Measurement of Generative AI Workload Power Profiles for Whole-Facility Data Center Infrastructure Planning

Roberto Vercellino, Jared Willard, Gustavo Campos, Weslley da Silva Pereira, Olivia Hull, Matthew Selensky, Juliane Mueller · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Resilience (60) and Non-Obviousness (65) pull the composite down — the headline 73–80%-of-TDP finding is useful but unsurprising, and the whole-facility simulation is not validated against any real facility meter data, so DIPLOEE outputs are plausible scenarios rather than forecasts. Rigor (70) benefits from 6 replicates and standardized MLCommons/vLLM benchmarks, but AMD EPYC RAPL accuracy is unverified, weak-scaling confounds the energy-per-job results, and FIFO-only scheduling is not stress-tested. Novelty (65) is a competent assembly of existing tools with a genuinely useful public 0.1s H100 dataset. Impact (75) is the strongest dimension thanks to the open dataset release. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a publicly released dataset of high-resolution (0.1 s) GPU/CPU power profiles for GenAI training, fine-tuning, and inference workloads on NVIDIA H100 GPUs, paired with DIPLOEE — a bottom-up discrete-event simulation framework that scales node-level profiles to whole-facility energy demand time-series. - Why it's important: Grid operators, utility planners, and data center designers currently lack temporally resolved, reproducible power data for AI workloads, forcing infrastructure sizing on TDP-rated peaks that the measurements show are never actually reached, potentially wasting tens of millions in overbuilt capacity. - How it does it: WattAMeter logs per-device power at 0.1 s resolution during standardized MLCommons (Llama-2 70B fine-tuning, Stable Diffusion v2 training) and vLLM (Llama-3 70B offline and online inference) benchmarks across 1–16 nodes, then DIPLOEE's SimPy discrete-event simulator convolves these profiles with utilization distributions to synthesize year-long, 1-minute-timestep facility-level load series. - The good: The finding that even at 100% node utilization, simulated facility power reached only 73–80% of rated design capacity quantitatively justifies the case for infrastructure underprovisioning relative to TDP-based sizing. - The missing: The simulation uses utilization distributions borrowed from external datasets (Shanghai AI Lab, Azure) rather than measured from a real production facility, so the resulting whole-facility profiles remain synthetic rather than validated against actual facility meter data. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on data center energy modeling, grid interconnection planning, or AI infrastructure policy — it is the most reproducible, publicly available node-level power dataset for GenAI workloads currently in existence; skip it if you need hyperscale or multi-rack validated numbers. Problem & Motivation Data center electricity consumption reached approximately 176 TWh in the U.S. in 2023 (4.4% of domestic consumption) and is projected to double or triple by 2028, driven largely by GPU-accelerated AI workloads. Infrastructure planners — utilities, grid operators, and data center developers — need temporally resolved facility-level demand profiles to size grid connections, on-site generation, and distribution equipment. Prior work either reports only aggregate energy estimates without sub-minute temporal dynamics, uses proprietary and non-reproducible measurements, or does not bridge node-level power to facility-level demand. A 2024 NERC incident where a 230 kV line fault caused a 1.5 GW data center load drop within 82 seconds illustrates the real-time grid stability stakes. The specific gap is the absence of an open, reproducible methodology connecting individual AI job power signatures to whole-facility load forecasting. Key Idea The core insight is that a node-level power profile measured at 0.1 s resolution under a standardized benchmark is a reusable primitive: by treating each workload type as a parameterized power trace (duration, node count, profile shape) and then statistically sampling job arrivals and facility utilization from known distributions, a discrete-event simulator can compose realistic facility-level demand time-series without requiring proprietary operational data. The trick is separating the measurement problem (what does one job's power look like?) from the scheduling problem (how many jobs run when?), and solving each independently before composing them. Methodology Measurement uses WattAMeter (open-source, built on NVML and Intel RAPL) to record per-GPU and per-CPU power at 0.1 s resolution on NLR's Kestrel HPC system — 156 GPU nodes each with 4 NVIDIA H100 SXM GPUs (700 W TDP each) and 2 AMD EPYC 9554 CPUs (360 W TDP each). Three workload classes are profiled: (1) Llama-2 70B fine-tuning via MLPerf Training v4.0 with LoRA + ZeRO-3, scaled from 2 to 16 nodes with 6 replicate runs each; (2) Stable Diffusion v2 training via MLPerf, scaled from 1 to 16 nodes; (3) Llama-3 70B inference via vLLM in both offline (batch) and online (serve) modes, sweeping prompt count (25–1,000), output token length (512/1,024), and request rate (1–1,000 prompts/second). DIPLOEE (Data Center Infrastructure Planning, Load Optimization for Energy Efficiency) is built in SimPy and supports two simulation architectures: job-centered (for training/fine-tuning colocation) and inference-centered (continuous request stream with latency constraints). Job generation uses a bisection algorithm to match a target average utilization (μ_avg = 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%), distributing jobs by type, hour, day-of-week, and month. Facility-level outputs include power, occupancy, utilization, queue depth, and queue time at 1-minute resolution over a simulated year. Two case studies are presented: a 10 MW colocation center (2,840 nodes) running mixed training/fine-tuning, and a 1 MW inference center (284 nodes) running Llama-3 70B online serving. Critical design decisions: (1) 0.1 s sampling — justified by capturing sub-second GPU power transients that matter for grid real-time operations but absent from prior datasets; (2) standardized benchmarks (MLCommons, vLLM) rather than custom workloads — enables reproducibility and comparison across facilities; (3) FIFO scheduling in DIPLOEE — acknowledged as a simplification, with smarter scheduling reserved for future work. Key Results For Llama-2 70B fine-tuning, average node power scaled linearly with node count (slope 2.36 kW/node), and power standard deviation also scaled linearly. Total energy per fine-tuning job increased with node count (from ~8 kWh at 2 nodes to ~21 kWh at 16 nodes) because the weak-scaling setup increased global batch size with node count, requiring more epochs and offsetting wall-clock time savings. For Stable Diffusion v2, average power similarly scaled linearly (1.98 kW/node), but total energy decreased with node count (~19 kWh at 2 nodes to ~14 kWh at 16 nodes) because the evaluation-to-training ratio is much higher and the benchmark converged faster at scale. Peak GPU power under HPL was 668.2 W (±1.4 W), approximately 4.5% below the 700 W TDP. Idle GPU power was 72.5 W (±0.1 W). For Llama-3 70B offline inference, node power saturated near 2.8 kW at ~325 input prompts, independent of further prompt increases. Online inference also saturated rapidly at ~2.9 kW with request rates above ~25 prompts/second. In whole-facility simulation: at 80% average utilization, the 10 MW colocation center reached a maximum of 7.30 MW (73% of rated capacity) and showed a peak-to-average ratio (PAR) of 1.24; the 1 MW inference center reached 0.80 MW maximum (80% of rated capacity). At 80% utilization, 75.6% of colocation jobs queued with average queue time of 6.54 hours; inference centers dropped ~15% of requests. Baselines are not ML accuracy baselines but rather TDP-based sizing assumptions — the gap between TDP-implied and measured peak is the key comparator. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contributions are the publicly released high-resolution GenAI workload power dataset (no equivalent open dataset exists for H100-based training and inference at this temporal resolution) and the DIPLOEE simulation framework linking node profiles to facility-level planning. The measurement methodology (WattAMeter + standardized benchmarks) is a competent assembly of existing tools; the discrete-event simulation approach is standard (SimPy); the novelty is in the integration and the public data release. Rigor & Reproducibility Reproducibility is a stated priority and is well-executed: standardized benchmarks (MLCommons, vLLM), 6 replicate runs per configuration with box-and-whisker reporting of variance, and public data release (doi:10.7799/3025227). NVML measurement accuracy is documented (±5% per NVIDIA, verified by independent studies). The RAPL accuracy citation covers Intel CPUs only; no equivalent validation for AMD EPYC CPUs used in this study is found, which is a small but real methodological gap. No ablations on DIPLOEE's scheduling or utilization distribution assumptions are presented — it is unclear how sensitive facility-level profiles are to these choices. The utilization distributions feeding DIPLOEE come from the Shanghai AI Lab ACME Trace and Azure Azure LLM Inference datasets, neither of which is validated as representative of the simulated facilities. Code for DIPLOEE's implementation is not explicitly released at a URL in this preprint. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (node-level measurements) + SYSTEMS (DIPLOEE simulation framework). - Practical: Measurements run on commercial NVIDIA H100 hardware available from cloud providers and HPC centers; WattAMeter is open-source and deployable on any SLURM-managed cluster. - Efficient: The measurement overhead of WattAMeter at 0.1 s sampling is not quantified; DIPLOEE's year-long simulation at 1-minute timesteps runs on standard CPU hardware (SimPy is CPU-bound and lightweight). - Effective: The evidence shows the framework produces plausible facility-level profiles with realistic diurnal and weekly patterns, but no validation against actual facility meter data is performed — effectiveness at reproducing real facility loads is claimed, not demonstrated. - Scalable: Node-level profiles are tested up to 16 nodes (64 H100 GPUs); facility simulations extrapolate to 2,840 nodes for the 10 MW case, but this extrapolation assumes linear scaling of measured single-job profiles without accounting for network contention, thermal throttling, or workload diversity at hyperscale. The work delivers a public dataset and, implicitly, the WattAMeter tool; DIPLOEE's code release status is not confirmed in the paper. Strengths - The public release of 0.1 s resolution power profiles for multiple GenAI workload types (training, fine-tuning, inference) on H100 hardware fills a genuine and documented gap in the literature where all prior comparable datasets were proprietary. - The finding that facility power peaks at 73–80% of TDP-rated capacity even at 100% node utilization is a concrete, quantified result that directly challenges industry-standard TDP-based infrastructure sizing. - Use of MLCommons and vLLM standardized benchmarks enables cross-site reproducibility, a meaningful improvement over prior single-vendor or custom-workload studies. - The two-architecture DIPLOEE design (job-centered vs. inference-centered) correctly captures the operational distinction between batch training clusters and latency-constrained inference deployments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) measurements cover a specific hardware configuration (H100 + EPYC Genoa) and a limited set of algorithm configurations — different accelerators or interconnects may behave differently; (2) DIPLOEE does not model auxiliary loads (cooling, power distribution losses), which can constitute less than 10% of total facility load for large AI data centers; (3) utilization and arrival-rate distributions adapted from external datasets may not reflect operational diversity of hyperscale or enterprise environments. The authors engage these limitations directly and outline concrete future work (expanding the dataset, integrating thermal/electrical infrastructure models, smarter scheduling). This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The whole-facility simulation is not validated against any real facility meter data. Without a ground-truth comparison, DIPLOEE's accuracy for infrastructure planning remains unknown — the paper demonstrates internal consistency but not external validity. - RAPL accuracy for AMD EPYC CPUs (used throughout) is unverified; the cited 99% correlation result is for Intel CPUs. AMD RAPL implementation differs and its accuracy is less documented. - The scaling experiments use weak scaling (fixed per-device batch size), not strong scaling — a realistic deployment scenario where a fixed job is parallelized across more nodes. The energy-per-job increasing with node count for Llama-2 is partly an artifact of this choice and may not generalize. - DIPLOEE uses FIFO scheduling, while real HPC facilities use backfill, priority queuing, and energy-aware scheduling. The impact of scheduling strategy on the resulting facility power profile is not quantified. - The inference power saturation at ~25 prompts/second (one node, Llama-3 70B) limits inference simulation accuracy for high-throughput scenarios since the power model cannot differentiate behavior above saturation. Bottom Line Data center planners, utility interconnection engineers, and energy policy researchers gain a unique open resource — the first publicly available, high-resolution, standardized GenAI power dataset on H100 hardware — along with a practical simulation methodology, though the absence of real-facility validation means DIPLOEE outputs should be treated as plausible scenarios rather than validated forecasts.
Search Results 2604.07345 PDF
#70 — Innovative (68)

JoyAI-LLM Flash: Advancing Mid-Scale LLMs with Token Efficiency

Aichen Cai, Anmeng Zhang, Anyu Li, Bo Zhang, Bohua Cai, Chang Li, Changjian Jiang, Changkai Lu, Chao Xue, Chaocai Liang, Cheng Zhang, Dongkai Liu, Fei Wang, Guoqiang Huang, Haijian Ke, Han Lin, Hao Wang, Ji Miao, Jiacheng Zhang, Jialong Shi, Jifeng Zhu, Jingjing Qian, Junhui Luo, Junwu Xiong, Lam So, Liang Huang, Ming Ke, Mingyang Li, Panfeng Shi, Peng Hao, Qi Wang, Qian Lai, Qiaoqiao Yuan, Qingyu Yin, Qiong Cao, Qixiang Wang, Rongcheng Bian, Rongduo Han, Shaoqiang Zheng, Shi Hu, Shi Suo, Shijie Ren, Shijin Zhang, Shiying Fan, Shuai Xie, Tianyi Zhang, Wei Liu, Wentao Tan, Xianghan Meng, Xiaodong He, Xing Pan, Xiran Wang, Xuyang Peng, Ya Zhang, Yang Liu, Yangyang Duan, Yanxu Chen, Yicheng Gong, Yidan Huang, Yifei Liu, Yinhao Bai, Yongqiang Liu, Yuesong Zhang, Yuqi Zhang, Zerui Xie, Zhenfang Wang, Zhennan Shen, Zheyuan Liu, Zhuwei Zeng · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) strong — HuggingFace weights released in multiple quant formats, measured 1.87× MTP speedup, 2.33× throughput gains, deployable artifact. Novelty (65) — FiberPO has principled fiber-bundle motivation but key theorems deferred to companion paper; rest is competent assembly. Rigor (55) pulled down by asterisked cross-citations (SWE-bench), single-domain ablation only, no seeds/error bars, self-selected benchmark averaging, and unverified vendor numbers. Non-Obviousness (70) — mechanistic GRPO-vs-GSPO failure explanation is genuinely insightful. Completeness (50) — forward-looking conclusion with no limitations section. Resilience (55) — token-efficiency metric gameable, multi-domain claim not ablated. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*80 = 63. Adjusted upward slightly for the FiberPO diagnostic depth.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces JoyAI-LLM Flash, a 48B-total/2.7B-active MoE language model with a novel RL algorithm (FiberPO) grounded in fiber bundle theory, achieving competitive benchmark accuracy with substantially fewer output tokens than comparable-scale models. - Why it's important: Inference cost scales with output length, so models that produce correct answers in fewer tokens directly reduce serving costs and latency at production scale — a concrete economic constraint for deployers of sub-50B models. - How it does it: FiberPO decomposes each token's importance ratio into a trajectory-level base weight (measuring overall trajectory drift) and a token-level fiber residual (measuring deviation from the trajectory mean), gating them independently to prevent the simultaneous token saturation that causes GRPO entropy collapse and GSPO stagnation. - The good: In a controlled single-domain math RL ablation, GRPO collapses to 0.2 AIME 2024 mean@1 at step 100 with entropy 0.038 nats and importance ratio exceeding 10³, while FiberPO achieves 0.786 with stable entropy at 0.43 nats — a stark, diagnostically explained failure-mode comparison. - The missing: The FiberPO ablation is conducted only on single-domain math (DAPO-Math-17k); the multi-domain RL setting — where FiberPO's cross-domain trajectory gating is most theoretically motivated — is never ablated against GRPO or GSPO. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers studying RL stability in LLM alignment and practitioners deploying efficient sub-50B instruct models; the FiberPO algorithm is the primary intellectual contribution, while the rest of the system is competent engineering atop known components. Problem & Motivation Mid-scale LLMs face two intertwined constraints: poor token efficiency (many output tokens to produce correct answers) and high inference cost. Existing RL alignment methods that could improve these trade-offs suffer from instability in the MoE and multi-domain setting. GRPO clips per-token importance ratios using the absolute log-ratio, which conflates trajectory-level drift with within-trajectory token variation; once trajectory drift exceeds the clip bound, the majority of tokens saturate simultaneously, collapsing token-level credit assignment and driving entropy to near zero. GSPO addresses trajectory drift but suppresses within-trajectory variation, causing stagnation. This affects anyone deploying medium-scale heterogeneous LLMs — agentic pipelines, coding assistants, multi-turn chat — where inference cost is dominated by output length. Key Idea The key insight is that a token's importance ratio can be factored into two orthogonal scales: (1) a trajectory-level base weight capturing how much the entire trajectory has drifted from the reference policy, and (2) a token-level fiber residual capturing how much the token deviates from its trajectory's mean log-ratio. By gating these two components independently using a piecewise-linear aggregate gate (g^agg) and a logclip fiber gate, tokens that move in concert with their trajectory pass through unattenuated while outlier tokens are clipped only relative to their trajectory mean — preventing the feedback loop where trajectory-level drift forces mass token saturation in GRPO. Methodology The pipeline has four main phases: Pretraining: A 48B-parameter MoE (256 total routed experts, Top-8 + 1 shared, auxiliary-loss-free load balancing) with Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) and 40 Transformer layers (1 dense + 39 sparse). Trained on 20.7T tokens via a 4-stage curriculum: foundational, code-math enhancement, mid-training on ultra-high-quality tokens, and 128K context extension. Uses the Muon optimizer (Newton-style matrix orthogonalization on gradient spectral norms) in place of Adam. A single-layer dense MTP head is appended for joint training signal enrichment and speculative decoding at inference. Infrastructure: Megatron-Core with 2-way pipeline parallelism, 8-way expert parallelism, ZeRO-1 data parallelism; block-diagonal attention masking for sequence packing (50%/20% forward/backward speedup). Post-training: SFT on three data categories — general (math, coding, TIR, role-play, safety), environment/agent learning (funnel-based SWE pipeline mining GitHub issues, building verifiable Docker environments, generating pass-to-pass trajectories via OpenHands/SWE-agent), and tool-integrated reasoning (code-centric, search-centric, hybrid, terminal-centric trajectory datasets). DPO for 1,000 steps (lr 1e-6, cosine decay, batch 256) to eliminate hallucinations and instruction-following failures before RL. FiberPO (RL): The objective is J^FiberPO = Σ (1/|T_j|)(1/T_τ) · G(r•)_{s,a,τ,t} · Â_{s,a}, where the gating map G decomposes multiplicatively into a base weight w^base (ratio of g^agg applied to positive/negative aggregate log-ratios s^+_τ, s^-_τ) and a fiber residual r̃^fiber (logclip of the token's deviation from the same-sign trajectory mean, gated by the opposite-sign trajectory aggregate). Three regimes for g^agg: pass-through (|x|≤C), rollback with restorative gradient (C < |x| < C), and zeroed (|x|≥C). Multi-domain training uses Gaussian curriculum difficulty scheduling (pass rate μ₀=0.8 → μ_T=0.2) with two-level domain-balanced sampling. Critical design decisions: (1) Two-scale decomposition is well-justified by the theoretical APC-Obj-TRPO equivalence theorem and the First-Order Agreement Theorem establishing on-policy Jacobian identity. (2) Auxiliary-loss-free load balancing follows DeepSeek-V3, justified by training stability. (3) QAT using Straight-Through Estimators for INT4 fake-quantization during RL is well-justified — it narrows numerical space and reduces noise diversity, incidentally stabilizing RL rollouts. Inference: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) for INT4/INT8 plus Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) for FP8/W4AFP8. Prefill-Decode (PD) disaggregation for long-context workloads with Mooncake centralized KV cache. DoubleQuant strategy for GGUF variants. Key Results Base model vs Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-Base: MMLU-Pro 73.1 (best, vs 61.7/60.7), MATH 78.1 (best, vs 59.6/56.0), MATH-500 77.0 (best, vs 58.0/54.8), MMLU 84.7 (vs 82.1/88.4 — not best), RULER 128K 77.0 (vs 61.8/88.3 — middle). Coding (HumanEval) 85.3 (vs 87.8/79.8). Base model is strongest on hard math but not the top general-knowledge or long-context model. Instruct model vs Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking (Table 3): AIME'25 72.9 (best, vs 70.4/56.7/63.8/92.1 — note GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking dominates at 92.1), MATH-500 98.2 (vs 97.4/95.8/97.6/99.2), LiveCodeBench v6 65.6 (best non-GLM, vs 58.8/58.0/48.1), SWE-bench Verified 62.4 (best, vs 31.2/57.4/29.0/59.4*-asterisked), MMLU-Pro 81.6 (tied best with Qwen3.5-35B-A3B). Token efficiency on LiveCodeBench: JoyAI-LLM Flash uses 7,300 tokens vs GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking's ~53,600, with 1.6% higher accuracy. MTP speedup 1.87× (best among MTP models tested on SpecBench). W4AFP8 with next-n=2 achieves 1.96× TPS over BF16 non-MTP baseline. Some GLM results are asterisked (direct citation, not reproduced, and the paper notes they differ substantially from its reproduced numbers). Counter-intuitive finding: FiberPO simultaneously decreases mean response length (7,902 → 4,543 tokens) and increases validation accuracy (0.668 → 0.786 on AIME 2024), while GRPO also shortens responses (7,904 → 2,216) but with accuracy collapse — suggesting GRPO achieves compression via degeneration rather than efficiency. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The system (JoyAI-LLM Flash) assembles existing components: MoE architecture is adapted from DeepSeek-V3/Kimi-K2, MLA from DeepSeek-V2/V3, Muon optimizer is prior work, MTP is prior work, Megatron-Core infrastructure is standard. The genuinely novel contribution is FiberPO — a new RL objective derived from Fiber Bundle Gating, a mathematical framework for composing multi-scale trust-region maintenance, with provable first-order agreement properties not present in PPO/GRPO/GSPO. The data pipeline engineering (model-based quality scoring, DoubleQuant) is incremental. Rigor & Reproducibility FiberPO is ablated against GRPO and GSPO in a single-domain math setting (DAPO-Math-17k, AIME 2024 validation) with training diagnostics (entropy, importance ratio, gradient norm, response length, clip fraction). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported. Model weights (base + post-trained, BF16/FP8/INT8/INT4/GGUF) are released on HuggingFace; no training code or data is released. Methodological concerns: (1) Some GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking instruct results (including SWE-bench 59.4*) are directly cited from original papers and acknowledged to differ substantially from the authors' reproduced results — the asterisk notation is disclosed but creates apples-to-oranges comparisons at critical points (SWE-bench). (2) The FiberPO ablation does not cover the multi-domain RL setting where the theoretical motivation is strongest. (3) Token efficiency averaging across 18 benchmarks is not statistically tested; the selection of benchmarks is controlled by the authors. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (a built model with quantization stack and serving infrastructure, evaluated by benchmark deployment and throughput measurement). - Practical: The model runs on a single GPU for MTP evaluation (Table 4) and on standard multi-GPU server configurations; GGUF variants explicitly target consumer hardware with restricted VRAM. - Efficient: 2.7B active parameters per forward pass from 48B total; FP8 achieves 17% throughput gain over BF16 with near-zero accuracy loss; W4AFP8 achieves 28% throughput gain at 1.2% accuracy drop; MTP adds 1.87× speculative decoding speedup without an external draft model. - Effective: Benchmark evidence shows competitive accuracy with substantially lower token consumption on tested tasks; SWE-bench Verified 62.4% on real GitHub issues (with verifiable pass/fail) is credible evidence of practical capability, but the token efficiency claim conflates output length with correctness and could be gamed by shorter incorrect answers. - Scalable: Serving evaluated at up to 3 RPS for long-context (ISL=20K, OSL=100K) and 8 RPS for short-context (ISL=128–2048, OSL=128–512); no stress-scale testing beyond these configurations, and the paper does not report behavior at scale regimes beyond what was tested. Software artifact: HuggingFace checkpoints released in multiple quantization formats. No training code or pipeline code released. Strengths - FiberPO provides a principled theoretical foundation (fiber bundle theory, APC-Obj equivalence, First-Order Agreement Theorem) for why GRPO and GSPO fail structurally, backed by diagnostic plots showing entropy, importance ratio, gradient norm, and clip fraction trajectories across 100 training steps — this is more mechanistically explanatory than typical RL ablations. - The MTP + QAT co-design achieving 1.87× inference speedup through a single-layer head (no external draft model) combined with the highest speculative decoding efficiency among compared MTP models is a practically deployable systems result. - AIME'25 72.9 for a 2.7B-active model surpasses Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (56.7) by 16.2 points — a large margin on a hard, contamination-resistant benchmark that suggests the RL training generalizes beyond the math ablation domain. - The funnel-based SWE pipeline for constructing verifiable RL training environments from real GitHub issues (including Docker containerization, test-case validation, and pass-to-pass signal generation) is a reusable infrastructure contribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The conclusion is purely forward-looking: the authors state they plan to integrate continual learning and persistent memory but do not enumerate current failure modes, scope limitations, or degradation cases. The paper does not directly confront limitations — a credibility negative in a rigorous review framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The FiberPO ablation covers only single-domain math RL; the multi-domain extension (Section 3.3.3) reuses the same hyperparameters and reports stable gains but never shows FiberPO vs GRPO/GSPO head-to-head in the multi-domain setting — precisely where per-domain trust-region gating is most theoretically motivated. - The SWE-bench Verified 62.4% comparison against GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking's 59.4* uses an asterisked result (directly cited, not reproduced), and the paper acknowledges such results differ substantially from reproduced numbers — making this headline comparison unreliable. - The token efficiency metric (accuracy vs. average tokens, averaged across 18 benchmarks) is self-selected and not independently verified; a model can reduce average token count by producing shorter wrong answers, and no analysis separates length reduction on correct vs. incorrect responses. - The FiberPO theoretical results referenced throughout (Theorems in [73]) are in a companion paper, not this one — the theoretical claims are asserted rather than proven here, requiring readers to separately validate the mathematical foundation. - No analysis of out-of-distribution behavior, long-tail failure modes, or degradation on tasks outside the 18 evaluation benchmarks despite the 128K context window and agentic capability claims. Bottom Line Practitioners evaluating efficient mid-scale instruct LLMs and researchers studying RL stability should read this paper for FiberPO — a mechanistically well-explained RL algorithm with strong single-domain ablation evidence — but should treat the system-level token efficiency claims as vendor self-reports pending independent reproduction, and note that the key theoretical results are deferred to a companion paper.
Recommended 2604.03044 PDF
#71 — Innovative (68)

Cognitive Pivot Points and Visual Anchoring: Unveiling and Rectifying Hallucinations in Multimodal Reasoning Models

Zhe Qian, Yanbiao Ma, Zhuohan Ouyang, Zhonghua Wang, Zhongxing Xu, Fei Luo, Xinyu Liu, Zongyuan Ge, Yike Guo, Jungong Han · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-obviousness (80) is the standout — Pseudo Reflection finding and entropy-pivot coupling at intermediate layers is a genuine, counter-intuitive mechanistic insight with convergent probes. Novelty (72) for HVAR+FRM paradigm. Rigor (55) — single-architecture mechanistic story, small case-study samples (63-72), no significance tests, no code release, layer-range and threshold may not generalize. Completeness (50) — no limitations section, benchmark training-test overlap concerns. Resilience (55) — architecture specificity a real risk. Impact (55). Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 60. Boosting slightly for mechanistic contribution quality → 63.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces V-STAR (Visual Structural Training with Attention Reinforcement), a GRPO-based training paradigm that reduces hallucinations in multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning by rewarding intermediate-layer visual attention at high-entropy transition tokens and injecting forced reflection trajectories at those same cognitive pivot points. - Why it's important: Multimodal reasoning models hallucinate more than non-reasoning models because long chain-of-thought generation causes visual evidence to be progressively abandoned in favor of language priors, and existing outcome-level supervision cannot correct this internal mechanism failure. - How it does it: V-STAR detects high-entropy "cognitive bifurcation points" (e.g., tokens like "However") via Shannon entropy monitoring, then applies a dual intervention: a Hierarchical Visual Attention Reward (HVAR) that penalizes attention collapse in layers 11–20 precisely at those pivots, and a Forced Reflection Mechanism (FRM) that edits training trajectories to insert a reflection loop, training the model to habitually re-examine visual input at uncertainty spikes. - The good: The mechanistic diagnosis — locating the causal locus of hallucination to intermediate-layer attention collapse at identifiable entropy spikes, validated via mutual information, Mahalanobis distance, and attention heatmaps — is specific and credibly supported, moving well beyond the typical outcome-level correlation. - The missing: The entire mechanistic analysis and the layer identification (layers 11–20) are characterized on a single architecture (Qwen2.5-VL-7B); no evidence is provided that the pivot-detection threshold, the layer range, or the Pseudo Reflection finding generalize to other model families or sizes. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on MLLM hallucination, GRPO-based multimodal RL, or mechanistic interpretability of vision-language models; the mechanistic framing and the HVAR design are genuinely non-obvious contributions. Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Reasoning Models (MLRMs) achieve strong performance through test-time compute scaling via long chain-of-thought (CoT) generation, but the extended generation process degrades visual grounding. Compared to non-reasoning generation, reasoning traces show consistently lower visual feature signals across network layers (Fig. 1), making models more susceptible to hallucination. Existing mitigation strategies operate at the outcome level (reward on final answer) or at inference time (contrastive decoding, post-hoc correction), and neither intervenes at the internal mechanistic point where the failure actually originates. The specific gap is the absence of fine-grained training signal that targets visual re-anchoring at the exact moments during generation when the model loses contact with visual evidence. Key Idea: Hallucinations in multimodal CoT are not uniformly distributed across the reasoning chain but are tightly coupled to a small set of identifiable high-entropy transition tokens ("cognitive bifurcation points") at which intermediate-layer visual attention collapses to near zero. Because these moments are detectable in real time via Shannon entropy of the next-token distribution, they can be used as precise triggers for both an attention reinforcement reward (HVAR) and a trajectory-editing reflection intervention (FRM), enabling surgical correction without disrupting the rest of the reasoning chain. Methodology: V-STAR builds on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and operates in three coordinated components. (1) Dataset construction: A Dynamic Dual Stream framework generates 40,000 training instances. A Logic Intensive Stream handles STEM/chart inputs via a caption-then-reason cascade (strong vision model generates captions; strong reasoning LLM generates CoT). A Semantic Rich Stream handles natural scenes via direct VLM generation followed by a CoT-refiner for style alignment. A Quality Assurance module filters low-quality samples. (2) HVAR (Hierarchical Visual Attention Reward): At each generation step, the framework monitors Shannon entropy H(y_t) = −Σ p(v) log p(v). When H exceeds threshold τ_ent = 1.0, a pivot mask M_pivot is activated. For active pivot steps, the Visual Attention Share S_vis^(l) is computed as the mean attention weight from text tokens to visual token region V across heads, averaged over intermediate layers 11–20. A piecewise reward r_local awards 0.40 when S_mid ≥ τ_high and 0.20 when τ_mid ≤ S_mid < τ_high. A Global Perception Reward R_global additionally rewards sustained average visual attention across the full chain. (3) FRM (Forced Reflection Mechanism): Upon detecting transition tokens V_trans (e.g., "However", "Therefore") in an initial rollout y_1, FRM appends an explicit instruction trigger τ_inst that switches the model to reflection mode, producing y_2 as a continuation that may revise reasoning. For training, the explicit instruction token is excised (implicit training), so the model learns to generate the reflection naturally rather than following a command. A Reflection Format Reward R_reflect encourages a natural reflection marker τ_nat and measures visual attention recovery in the K-step window after reflection onset. The overall objective is J(θ) = L_GRPO(θ) + γL_SFT(θ), where L_SFT operates on a correctness-preserving replay buffer of trajectories that reach correct final answers. Total reward R_total = R_outcome + R_visual + R_reflect.
