Up to 1.8× speedup over FlashAttention on long-sequence prefill. - Multi-core scalability: Aggregate GFlops scales from ~280 GFlops at 1 thread to >1.1 TFlops at 12 threads; per-core efficiency drops from ~peak to ~30% at 12 threads due to shared GSM bandwidth contention. - Ablation (Table 3): Pipelining alone achieves 0.928× relative speedup vs. full DeferAttention. Without pipelining (V2), speedup drops to 0.824×. Without deferred reduction alone (V3), 0.944×. V8 (FlashAttention equivalent) achieves 0.003×, confirming the catastrophic cost of all-or-nothing GPU-style fusion on MT-3000. - Cost model accuracy: <5% relative error for N≥512; error rises to 20–60% for N=64–128 due to uncaptured kernel launch and pipeline stall overhead. The baselines are partially strong: the FlashAttention baseline uses DeferAttention's optimized GEMM micro-kernels, making it a fair test of the attention-specific innovations in isolation. However, Vanilla Attention is a weak lower bound. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The deferred-reduction attention formulation is a genuinely novel algorithmic contribution: it restructures the attention dataflow to move normalization outside the inner tile loop, exploiting the specific cost asymmetry between matrix multiplication and scalar reduction on explicitly managed architectures. This is distinct from FlashAttention (which fuses softmax inside the tile loop for I/O efficiency on GPUs) and from GEMM tiling work (which does not address the synchronization-heavy normalization problem). The runtime-adaptive scheme selection via a calibrated analytical cost model is also original in this setting. Micro-kernel implementations are engineering contributions applied to a non-standard architecture. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and systematic (Table 3, 8 configurations). Cost model accuracy is empirically validated across sequence lengths 64–4096 for both schemes (Fig. 15). Multi-core scalability is measured across 1–12 threads. No error bars or multiple-run statistics are reported; all results appear to be single-run measurements, which is common in systems papers but limits statistical confidence. Code is not publicly released — the implementation depends on proprietary m3cc compiler and hthreads library for MT-3000, restricting reproducibility to those with physical MT-3000 access. No model weights or calibrated tile-size tables are released. The key concern is hardware access: MT-3000 is a national defense-affiliated processor deployed in Tianhe, making independent replication essentially impossible for researchers outside the authoring institution. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS paper — built and evaluated on real MT-3000 hardware. - Practical: Runs on MT-3000 hardware in the Tianhe supercomputer using standard m3cc compiler and hthreads API; hardware is not commercially available, limiting deployment to national HPC centers with MT-3000 access. - Efficient: Achieves 85% hardware peak at operator level; analytical cost model adds <0.1% latency overhead; triple buffering fully overlaps DMA and compute with measured idle time near zero in the pipelined path. - Effective: Solves the stated problem across all tested configurations (sequence lengths 512–8192, batch sizes 1–8, four LLM model families); functions as a drop-in attention backend compatible with TensorRT-LLM and FasterTransformer serving stacks. - Scalable: Aggregate throughput scales to >1.1 TFlops at 12 threads; per-core efficiency degrades to ~30% at 12 threads due to GSM bandwidth contention, which the paper treats as an inherent hardware constraint rather than a solvable problem. Multi-node scaling is untest and left for future work, precluding conclusions about frontier-scale (>8B parameter) deployments. The work delivers a functional software artifact (attention operator library for MT-3000 via hthreads API) but the code is not publicly available. Strengths - The deferred-reduction formulation is a genuinely novel algorithmic restructuring of attention that exploits the cost asymmetry between GEMM and scalar reductions on explicitly managed architectures; the idea is transferable to any architecture lacking hardware reduction primitives (RISC-V vector cores, FPGA scratchpads). - The ablation study (Table 3) is unusually thorough for a systems paper: it isolates all three optimization components and their pairwise interactions, revealing the non-obvious result that pipelining is dominant and buffer reuse contributes negligibly without the other two strategies. - Empirical cost model validation (Fig. 15) covers a wide range (N=64 to 4096) and honestly reports where the model fails (short sequences), enabling practitioners to calibrate trust in the adaptive selector. - End-to-end evaluation on four real LLM architectures (GPT-small through LLaMA3-8B) rather than isolated kernel benchmarks grounds the claims in realistic serving workloads. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) single-node evaluation limits model scale to ≤8B parameters on a single MT-3000 node with 128 GB; frontier models (Llama-3.1 70B/405B) require multi-node deployment, which is deferred to future work. (2) Per-core efficiency drops to ~30% at 12 threads due to shared GSM bandwidth contention, consistent with prior mtBLAS observations on MT-3000. (3) The cost model has elevated prediction error for short sequences (N<512) due to runtime kernel launch overhead not captured by the compute-bound assumption. Authors confront all three limitations directly without minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The FlashAttention baseline is not a vanilla port — it runs on DeferAttention's optimized GEMM micro-kernels. A fully naive FlashAttention port (using unoptimized or reference GEMM) would better reveal the total system advantage, but the current choice is methodologically defensible as it isolates attention-dataflow innovations from GEMM micro-kernel improvements. - Grouped-query attention (GQA), used by LLaMA3 (tested end-to-end), significantly changes K/V access patterns relative to MHA. The paper does not discuss how deferred-reduction interacts with GQA's reduced K/V head count, where the score tile accumulation buffer S_G would be proportionally smaller — potentially affecting the scheme selection threshold. - Efficiency degradation at high core count (to ~30% at 12 threads) is noted but not addressed; no bandwidth-optimization roadmap is proposed, leaving a significant performance cliff for multi-core deployment unresolved. - Numerical verification (10^-6 tolerance) does not include model-level accuracy validation (perplexity, downstream task scores), leaving open whether reduced-precision paths in the micro-kernels introduce any measurable quality degradation in practice. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying LLMs on Tianhe/MT-3000 hardware gains directly usable techniques and a concrete performance reference; systems researchers studying attention optimization for non-GPU explicitly managed architectures will find the deferred-reduction formulation and runtime-adaptive scheme selection to be transferable ideas that justify reading the paper in full.
Search Results
#84 — Innovative (67)

Habitat-GS: A High-Fidelity Navigation Simulator with Dynamic Gaussian Splatting (12 ▲ )

Ziyuan Xia, Jingyi Xu, Chong Cui, Yuanhong Yu, Jiazhao Zhang, Qingsong Yan, Tao Ni, Junbo Chen, Xiaowei Zhou, Hujun Bao, Ruizhen Hu, Sida Peng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (60) — integration-level contribution; no individual component is new, but the specific combination (3DGS + drivable gaussian avatars + proxy-capsule navigation + full Habitat compatibility) is genuinely unoccupied in open source. Rigor (55) pulled down by single RL seeds, no component-level ablation disentangling scene vs avatar fidelity, VLM quality judged by one proprietary model, only 6 avatar identities, RTX 4090-only benchmarks despite A100/H100 claims. Non-Obviousness (60) — mixed-domain training superiority is a modestly surprising empirical finding. Completeness (65) — honest about manipulation limitations. Resilience (60) — close-call results (Config D vs E) untestable without seeds. Impact (85) — fully open-source drop-in upgrade to a widely-used ecosystem is high practical value. Composite ≈ 67.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Habitat-GS, an open-source embodied AI navigation simulator that extends Habitat-Sim with 3D Gaussian Splatting scene rendering and real-time drivable photorealistic gaussian avatars fully compatible with the existing Habitat-Lab ecosystem. - Why it's important: Agents trained in low-fidelity mesh simulators fail to generalize to realistic human-populated environments because the visual domain gap degrades Sim-to-Real policy transfer, and no existing open-source simulator combines photorealistic rendering with dynamic high-fidelity human avatars. - How it does it: It decouples visual rendering (CUDA-based 3DGS rasterizer bridged to OpenGL via zero-copy intra-GPU interop) from navigation physics (traditional NavMesh augmented with pre-computed skeletal proxy capsules for avatar collision), enabling photorealistic observations without modifying the navigation stack. - The good: Mixed-domain training (20M mesh + 80G GS scenes) achieves 79.60% GS-test success rate versus 61.70% for mesh-only training at 5×10^7 steps, while maintaining competitive mesh-test SR (59.60% vs. 62.80%), demonstrating that 3DGS training concretely improves cross-domain generalization. - The missing: All avatar motion follows pre-scripted GAMMA-generated trajectories — avatars never react to the agent's presence — so agents are never trained against truly interactive social dynamics. - Read it or not: Worth reading for embodied AI simulation researchers currently using Habitat-Sim who need photorealistic training environments with dynamic human avatars; safely skipped by those outside that ecosystem. Problem & Motivation Training embodied navigation agents at scale requires simulated environments with sufficient visual fidelity to support Sim-to-Real policy transfer, and realistic dynamic human avatars to develop socially compliant navigation behaviors. Prevailing open-source simulators — Habitat-Sim, iGibson, AI2-THOR, ThreeDWorld, SAPIEN — universally rely on mesh-based rasterization, which lacks high-frequency surface details and view-dependent appearance effects (specular reflections, subsurface scattering). This Sim-to-Real visual domain gap demonstrably degrades transferred navigation policies. Support for dynamic human avatars is equally limited: Habitat 3.0 introduced deformable SMPL-X mesh avatars, but mesh rendering constrains visual quality. Industrial alternatives such as NVIDIA Isaac Sim incorporate 3DGS but use closed-source rendering backends and require RT Core hardware (RTX-series GPUs), ruling out datacenter-class accelerators. Researchers working on human-aware navigation — collision avoidance, personal space compliance, human tracking — lack a fully open-source simulator combining high-fidelity scene rendering with high-fidelity dynamic human presence. Key Idea The central technical insight is visual–navigation decoupling: use 3D Gaussian Splatting exclusively for photorealistic RGB-D sensor rendering while keeping traditional NavMesh-based path planning for all navigation physics. The two subsystems are bridged via zero-copy CUDA–OpenGL interoperability for scenes (GPU buffers never touch the CPU) and pre-computed skeletal proxy capsules for avatar collision detection (one capsule per SMPL-X bone segment, positions pre-baked for all trajectory frames and retrieved at runtime via temporal interpolation). This sidesteps 3DGS's fundamental lack of explicit surface geometry required for collision detection, at the cost of restricting physical interaction to navigation-level obstacle avoidance. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. Asset Preparation: 3DGS scene assets are stored in standard PLY format alongside NavMeshes. Per avatar identity, canonical gaussian attributes (positions μ, SH coefficients, opacities α, scales s, rotations q, LBS weights W) are exported from trained AnimatableGaussians or GaussianAvatar models. GAMMA-generated motion trajectories are converted offline to per-frame SMPL-X joint transformation matrices T_j. Proxy capsule positions (p_0, p_1, radius) are pre-computed for each frame and stored as [T, C, 7] tensors in .pkl files. Simulation Environment: At each frame, scene gaussians are rasterized by the CUDA tile-based rasterizer, and avatar gaussians are deformed via CUDA LBS (μ'_i = Σ_j w_{i,j} T_j μ_i, with rotation quaternions adjusted via dual-quaternion blending) before a single-pass concatenated rasterization. RGB and depth outputs write directly to pre-registered OpenGL textures via zero-copy CUDA–OpenGL interop; a full-screen depth compositing pass merges 3DGS and OpenGL depth buffers for correct occlusion. Navigation uses dynamic capsule obstacles: each simulation step, the avatar manager injects current-frame proxy capsules into the NavMesh PathFinder, and a step-clipping routine prevents the agent from passing through any avatar's body. Avatar-aware PointNav augments standard geodesic progress reward r^prog with a two-stage safety penalty P(c_t) based on signed clearance c_t: weak polynomial repulsion for d_crit ≤ c_t < d_int (personal space zone, d_int=1.0m), steep polynomial ramp steepening for c_t < d_crit (critical zone, d_crit=0.5m), capped at p_max=0.6. Five PointNav training configurations vary mesh-to-GS scene ratios (100M, 100G, 80M+20G, 50M+50G, 20M+80G) and are evaluated at 5×10^7 and 1×10^8 steps using DD-PPO with ResNet visual encoder and GRU policy head receiving 256×256 RGB-D observations. Three critical design decisions: (1) visual–navigation decoupling — architecturally sound given 3DGS's lack of surface geometry but limits manipulation tasks; (2) offline pre-baked LBS — eliminates runtime neural inference, enabling >50 FPS with 1–2 avatars; (3) mixed-domain training — empirically justified as complementary data modalities rather than assumed. Key Results VLM scene quality (Gemini 3.0 Pro, 48 blinded batches of 10 images each, 240 total per domain): 3DGS scenes score 6.99 vs. 3.65 (rendering quality), 5.88 vs. 3.96 (realism), 8.47 vs. 7.08 (scene diversity) on a 1–10 scale — all substantially higher than mesh. PointNav at 5×10^7 steps: Config E (20M+80G) achieves 79.60% GS-test SR / 68.38% GS-test SPL vs. 61.70% / 52.09% for mesh-only (Config A); mesh-test SR remains competitive (59.60% vs. 62.80%). At full convergence (1×10^8 steps): Configs D (50M+50G) and E (20M+80G) tie for best average SR at 71.15%; Config D achieves best average SPL (61.35%). GS-only (Config B) achieves higher GS-test performance than mesh-only (77.60% vs. 61.70% GS SR) but weaker mesh-test performance (59.80% vs. 62.80% SR), confirming asymmetric visual transfer. Avatar-aware PointNav: GS Scene+GS Avatar training reduces collision rate (CR) from 6.713% to 4.746% on GS test and from 2.521% to 2.342% on mesh test (cross-domain transfer confirmed). Human tracking (TrackNav, cross-validated): GS-trained agent achieves average CC=5.68 (collision count) vs. 11.93 for mesh-trained agent — a 2.1× reduction in collisions — with only moderate track rate reduction (24.13% vs. 28.09%). Performance benchmarks on NVIDIA RTX 4090 at 256×256: >50 FPS maintained up to 5M Gaussians with 2 avatars; 10 avatars drops to 24.67 FPS at 2M Gaussians; GPU memory grows approximately linearly from 3.9 GB (no avatars) to 7.5 GB (10 avatars). Baselines are the immediate prior work (Habitat-Sim mesh rendering) and within-configuration cross-domain evaluation — appropriate given the simulator contribution framing, though no comparison against Isaac Sim navigation performance is provided. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The specific integration of real-time 3DGS scene rendering with drivable gaussian avatars, proxy-capsule navigation physics, and full Habitat ecosystem compatibility in a single open-source platform is genuinely novel — no prior work combines all four properties simultaneously. The finding that mixed-domain (mesh+GS) training is strictly superior to either single-source strategy for cross-domain generalization is a non-obvious empirical contribution. Individual components (3DGS, LBS-based gaussian avatars, NavMesh path planning) are established techniques; the contribution lies in their integration and the downstream empirical characterization. Rigor & Reproducibility Five training configurations systematically vary scene-domain ratios, evaluated on disjoint test sets (GS and mesh scenes drawn from different scene collections, not two representations of the same physical spaces). No ablations isolate individual system components — for example, the contribution of scene rendering quality versus avatar quality to navigation improvement is not disentangled. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for RL training experiments; single training runs per configuration make it impossible to assess whether performance differences (e.g., the tie between Configs D and E at convergence) are statistically meaningful. VLM scene quality evaluation uses blinded batches (randomized image indices hide rendering source) across 48 batches — a sound protocol — but relies on a single proprietary evaluator (Gemini 3.0 Pro) with no human perceptual study or multi-evaluator validation. Avatar identity set is extremely small: 6 total (3 training, 3 test). Code and system are fully open-source at https://zju3dv.github.io/habitat-gs/. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: Runs on standard CUDA-capable GPUs without RT Core hardware; the paper claims compatibility with A100 and H100 but reports all benchmarks on a single RTX 4090 — A100/H100 performance is unverified. - Efficient: Achieves >50 FPS at typical training loads (1–2 avatars, up to 5M Gaussians) on RTX 4090 via zero-copy CUDA–OpenGL interop and pre-baked LBS; 10-avatar scenes drop to 24.67 FPS, which may be marginal for high-throughput DD-PPO. - Effective: Navigation experiments show direct improvement in cross-domain generalization (79.60% vs. 61.70% GS-test SR) and collision avoidance (4.746% vs. 6.713% CR), not proxy metrics. - Scalable: Benchmarked up to 7M Gaussians and 10 avatars on a single GPU; multi-GPU DD-PPO parallelism (as used in production-scale embodied AI training) is not benchmarked, and scene diversity is limited to 100 training scenes per domain. Strengths - The visual–navigation decoupling architecture achieves backward compatibility with the full Habitat-Lab task library (PointNav, ImageNav, ObjectNav) without any modification to the navigation stack — researchers can upgrade rendering quality as a drop-in replacement. - Zero-copy CUDA–OpenGL interoperability eliminates CPU-side data movement, enabling photorealistic real-time rendering at training-relevant frame rates — a non-trivial engineering contribution that makes the system practically deployable. - The empirical demonstration that GS-scene-trained agents learn human-avoidance behaviors that generalize to lower-fidelity mesh environments (reduced CR on mesh test) confirms that higher visual fidelity teaches transferable perceptual representations, not domain-specific shortcuts. - The mixed-domain training finding is practically actionable: a 20M+80G ratio delivers the best cross-domain navigation agents with no additional architectural complexity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront one fundamental limitation: visual–navigation decoupling confines physical interaction to navigation-level obstacle avoidance. Tasks requiring fine-grained physical interaction (grasping or pushing 3DGS-represented objects) are outside scope because 3DGS lacks rigid-body properties and topological connectivity. They identify physics engine integration for manipulation as future work. This is an honest characterization of a genuine architectural tradeoff, not a minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All avatar motion is pre-scripted via GAMMA trajectories — avatars follow fixed paths regardless of the robot's behavior. The absence of reactive social dynamics (humans yielding to robots, reacting to obstacles) limits the realism of human-aware navigation training and may not reflect real-world human-robot interaction. - The avatar identity set (6 total, 3 train, 3 test) is too small to draw conclusions about generalization to novel human appearances; no evaluation of avatar identity generalization is provided. - Performance benchmarks are reported exclusively on RTX 4090; the claim that A100/H100 are supported (a differentiator over Isaac Sim's RT Core requirement) is stated but not measured. - Single training run per RL configuration with no error bars makes statistical interpretation of the close-call results (e.g., Config D vs. E at convergence, where both achieve 71.15% average SR) impossible. - The VLM-based scene quality assessment relies solely on Gemini 3.0 Pro; a human perceptual study or multi-model evaluation would provide more robust support for the claimed rendering advantage. Bottom Line Practitioners building embodied navigation agents in the Habitat ecosystem gain a well-engineered, open-source drop-in upgrade to photorealistic training environments with dynamic human avatars — a combination unavailable from any competing open-source platform — backed by convincing cross-domain generalization evidence.
Recommended 2604.12626 PDF
#85 — Innovative (67)

S-Eval: Towards Automated Safety Evaluation with Enhancement for Large Language Models

dl.acm.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) drives — deployed at Alibaba serving millions, benchmark released, large taxonomy, constitutional defense lifts GPT-4o 54%→99.7%. Novelty (60) — synthesis rather than invention, but reasoning-model CoT safety finding is new. Rigor (55) — circular validation of M_c on same-pipeline test set, no independent capability benchmarks, no error bars. Non-Obviousness (55). Completeness (60). Resilience (55) — circular validation weakens claims. Weighted (systems): 0.15*60+0.15*55+0.10*55+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.30*80 ≈ 63.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces S-Eval, an end-to-end LLM-based automated safety evaluation framework comprising a fine-tuned expert testing LLM (M_t), a fine-tuned safety critique LLM (M_c), a four-level 102-risk taxonomy, a 220,000-prompt bilingual benchmark, and a constitutional defense mechanism for 29 LLMs. - Why it's important: Existing LLM safety benchmarks have coarse single-level risk taxonomies, weak jailbreak coverage (0–6 attacks), partial automation, and no targeted defense, leaving organizations without a rigorous and adaptive pre-deployment safety audit tool. - How it does it: Both M_t and M_c are fine-tuned from Qwen-14B-Chat—M_t on ~2,000 expert-curated seed prompts per risk to generate harmful test cases without alignment restrictions, M_c on 100K GPT-4-annotated QA pairs to output a calibrated risk score (softmax probability of the "safe" token), risk tag, and explanation; a two-stage constitutional defense then appends risk-specific constraint principles to flagged inputs. - The good: M_c achieves 88.23% balanced accuracy in English vs. 78.00% for GPT-4-Turbo and 76.23% for LLaMA-Guard-2; constitutional defense raises GPT-4o's safety score from 54.00% to 99.70% with only 5.1×10⁻⁴% negative utility impact on real Alibaba production traffic. - The missing: M_c's accuracy is measured on a test set drawn from the same risk taxonomy and annotation pipeline used for its training data—there is no fully independent external ground truth, making the accuracy claims partially circular. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building bilingual (Chinese/English) LLM safety pipelines or researchers studying jailbreak robustness and reasoning-model safety; less critical for those focused on alignment training methods. Problem & Motivation Organizations deploying LLMs face a systematic gap: no unified, comprehensive framework exists to assess safety across all relevant risk dimensions before deployment. Prior safety benchmarks suffer from at least one of four deficiencies: (1) coarse, single-level risk taxonomies that miss fine-grained risks; (2) static or weakly adversarial prompt sets (0–6 jailbreak methods, 520–100,000 prompts) that underestimate real-world attack surface; (3) reliance on manual annotation or general-purpose LLMs that refuse to generate harmful test content, limiting scalability; and (4) no defense mechanism to act on identified vulnerabilities. As LLMs are deployed in medical, financial, and security-sensitive domains serving millions of users, the cost of incomplete safety audits is high. Key Idea The central insight is that a dedicated testing LLM trained specifically to bypass alignment (via "unalignment" fine-tuning on harmful content pairs) can continuously generate diverse, risky test prompts, while a separate safety critique LLM—whose score is the softmax probability of the first decoded token being "safe" rather than a generated text label—provides calibrated, threshold-tunable risk assessment without requiring explicit numerical supervision during fine-tuning. Methodology S-Eval operates as a four-component pipeline. (1) Risk management: a four-level taxonomy (8 first-level dimensions → 102 leaf risks) synthesized from NIST AI RMF 1.0, EU AI Act, Singapore MGF for GenAI, and China's Interim Measures, supplemented by crawled web risk knowledge. (2) Automatic test generation: M_t (Qwen-14B-Chat fine-tuned on ~2,000 expert-curated seed prompts per risk) generates base risk prompts via three strategies—definition-based (risk definitions as generation context), knowledge-based (crawled web knowledge), and rewriting-based (semantic fuzzification using synonym substitution and indirect framing). Attack prompts are then generated for 10 jailbreak types: Positive Induction, Reverse Induction, Code Injection, Instruction Jailbreak, Goal Hijacking, Instruction Encryption (Caesar/Base64/URL), DeepInception, In-Context Attack, Chain of Utterances, and Compositional Instruction Attack. Test selection uses combined semantic + Levenshtein similarity (α=0.2, θ_sim=0.55) to remove duplicates and a victim LLM ensemble with safety threshold θ_safe=0.95 to remove insufficiently risky prompts. (3) Safety evaluation: M_c (Qwen-14B-Chat fine-tuned on 100K bilingual QA pairs GPT-4-annotated and expert-reviewed) outputs a risk tag (safe/unsafe), a continuous score (softmax probability of the safe token), and a natural-language explanation. (4) Constitutional defense: a two-stage approach where risk classifier F (trained at 90.50% precision for second-level risk categorization) identifies the risk type in unsafe inputs, and principle library P appends the corresponding constitutional principle—comprising general safety rules, high-risk scenario prevention, proactive guidance, and principle confidentiality—to the model's system prompt. Three critical design decisions: using M_t to overcome general-LLM refusal (M_t RR=0% vs. GPT-4 RR=80%); using softmax probability as score (enabling deployment-time threshold tuning); constitutional defense over re-alignment (non-intrusive, preserves utility). Key Results M_c achieves 92.23%/88.23% balanced accuracy in Chinese/English, outperforming GPT-4-Turbo (78.00%/72.36%), LLaMA-Guard-2 (76.23%/69.32%), and rule matching (74.12%/70.19%) on a 2,000-prompt human-annotated test set; Pearson correlation between M_c and LLaMA-Guard-2 on a 12-model corpus = 0.92. Safety scores on S-Eval's benchmark are consistently lower and more discriminating than on four baselines (AdvBench, HH-RLHF, Flames, SafetyPrompts, Astral): 95% confidence interval widths of 30.62%/50.36% vs. 5.74%–26.60% for baselines. Across 29 evaluated LLMs (>670K total queries), ErnieBot-4.0 achieves the highest safety score among closed-source models (79.70%/87.60%); Gemini-1.0-Pro the lowest (53.90%/41.90%); LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct is the most robust open-source model (adaptive jailbreak ASR=76.10%, lower than GPT-4-Turbo's 95.10%). Compositional Instruction Attack achieves the highest average ASR (84.63% in English); Instruction Encryption achieves the lowest. Non-monotonic safety–parameter scaling finding: within a model series, safety first increases then decreases beyond an ability threshold. Reasoning LLMs show degraded safety from intermediate CoT steps: average reasoning-to-response safety consistency = 79.08% (Chinese) and 86.13% (English); DeepSeek-R1-distilled models show significantly worse safety than their base student models. Language safety decreases as resource availability decreases: Chinese ≈ English > French > Korean > Bengali ≈ Swahili. Constitutional defense raises safety score for all 18 tested LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o: 54.00%→99.70%; DeepSeek-R1: 30.90%→99.10%; LLaMA-2-13B-Chat: 85.10%→98.80%); risk classifier adds 50ms latency on RTX 2080 Ti and 10ms on H20. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The end-to-end pipeline coupling an unaligned test-generator LLM with a calibrated safety critic and constitutional defense is a genuine architectural contribution; the 220,000-prompt bilingual benchmark with 102-risk taxonomy and 10 jailbreak attacks exceeds prior work in all coverage dimensions. The empirical finding that reasoning LLMs' intermediate CoT steps degrade safety (with safety consistency rates quantified across 8 models) is not previously documented at this scale; the non-monotonic parameter–safety relationship is a non-obvious finding. The taxonomy itself is a principled synthesis rather than original theory. Rigor & Reproducibility Benchmark is publicly released at https://github.com/IS2Lab/S-Eval. Ablation compares M_t against GPT-4 and Qwen-14B-Chat (Table 8/9) for both established and new risk types. Sampling representativeness is validated by comparing sampled (2,000) against full (20,000) prompt results across 5 models. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported for safety scores across models. Key methodological concern: M_c's accuracy test set (2,000 prompts) is drawn from the same annotation pipeline (GPT-4 annotation + specialist review) as its 100K training set; the ground truth is not independently sourced, creating circular validation. The utility evaluation at Alibaba uses internal expert annotation, not independent capability benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, etc.). Trained model weights for M_t and M_c are not confirmed as publicly released—only benchmark prompt data is mentioned. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS - Practical: Both M_t and M_c are Qwen-14B-Chat fine-tuned on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with standard FP16 precision; inference runs on commodity hardware (RTX 2080 Ti confirmed). - Efficient: Risk classifier overhead is ~50ms on RTX 2080 Ti and ~10ms on H20, negligible relative to LLM inference time; full pipeline is automated via vLLM with no human involvement per evaluation. - Effective: Constitutional defense raises safety for all 18 tested models with only 5.1×10⁻⁴% utility degradation on 10 million real Alibaba user queries; the safety critique M_c provides explainable outputs (risk tag + score + explanation) that engineers use for model refinement. - Scalable: Deployed at Alibaba Group serving millions of users; adaptive update for a new risk requires only ~1,000 samples for M_t fine-tuning and ~1,000 samples for M_c; tested at 670K+ evaluations; large-scale (20,000-prompt full set) results are validated. Software artifact: benchmark data released on GitHub; framework deployed as a leaderboard at https://s.alibaba.com/aisec/seval; trained model weights not confirmed as publicly released. Strengths - The four-level 102-risk taxonomy is the first to be systematically cross-referenced against four major regulatory frameworks (NIST, EU AI Act, Singapore MGF, China interim measures) and includes novel risk categories (AI anthropomorphism, technological autonomy, resource allocation discrimination) absent from prior benchmarks. - M_c's softmax-probability scoring design provides a continuous, threshold-tunable risk signal deployable across variable organizational risk tolerances without model retraining—a practically significant engineering decision. - The finding that reasoning LLMs' intermediate CoT steps introduce harmful content at measurable rates (79–86% reasoning-response safety consistency) is the first systematic empirical characterization of this safety failure mode across multiple model families (Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1, distilled variants). - Industrial-scale deployment validation with quantified utility/safety tradeoff on real production traffic at Alibaba Group is rare in academic safety papers and substantially raises confidence in deployment claims beyond typical lab results. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge four limitations: (1) constitutional defense effectiveness depends on the underlying model's instruction-following capability, with low-capability models (Qwen3-0.6B, Gemma-7B-it, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B) showing smaller improvements; (2) using LLMs for safety evaluation introduces inherent bias from pre-training, though they argue this is controllable via their bias analysis (PCC=0.92, 88.50%/83.70% multi-method consistency); (3) evaluation uses a 2,000-prompt stratified sample rather than the full 20,000 due to compute constraints, though sample representativeness is validated; (4) adaptability to new threats requires some limited retraining effort. These are acknowledged forthrightly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The circular validation concern for M_c is more serious than acknowledged: the test set's ground truth comes from GPT-4 annotation of data drawn from the same S-Eval taxonomy as M_c's training distribution; M_c's 88.23% accuracy claim is not validated against any external, independently annotated safety dataset (e.g., OpenAI moderation test sets, ToxiGen human labels). - The adaptive attack evaluation defines success as "any one of 10 attacks succeeds," which gives a lower bound on model robustness but inflates the impression of attacker capability—a targeted attacker who already knows the most effective attack per model would achieve different ASR distributions. - Constitutional principles are manually engineered by domain experts for the 102 current risks; there is no evidence these principles generalize to out-of-taxonomy harmful requests, and the paper does not test this boundary condition. - The utility evaluation is entirely self-reported via Alibaba's internal expert panel on production traffic; no standard capability benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, MT-Bench) are used to measure whether constitutional defense causes capability degradation on general tasks. - The benchmark's riskiness threshold (θ_safe=0.95) is calibrated against a set of victim LLMs whose safety will improve over time, meaning the benchmark's effective difficulty will shrink as alignment improves—the paper does not address this benchmark aging problem. Bottom Line A practitioner auditing LLM safety for bilingual (Chinese/English) or adversarially robust deployment will gain directly usable benchmark data, a replicable evaluation pipeline design, quantitative baselines for 29 models, and the important empirical finding that reasoning model CoT degrades safety—but should treat M_c's accuracy numbers with appropriate skepticism given the circular validation, and should supplement with independent capability benchmarks before claiming negligible utility impact.
Recommended
#86 — Innovative (66)

" Here, Let Me Help": An Empirical Study of User Interventions in Human–Web Agent Collaboration

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — first process-level taxonomy of user interventions with functional categories. Rigor (75) strong κ=0.85 inter-rater, effect sizes, Latin square, peer-reviewed CHI. Completeness (70) and Resilience (55) limited by single-agent, single-user-type, no prototype validation of design implications. Impact (55) is a taxonomy + guidelines with no released artifact. Non-Obviousness (60) — findings are sensible but not deeply surprising. Weighted ≈66.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a functional taxonomy of user interventions in human–web agent collaboration, derived from a controlled in-lab study of 30 participants completing 360 web tasks across shopping, information, and travel domains. - Why it's important: Current web agents complete fewer than half of real-world tasks without human help, yet evaluation frameworks measure only success/failure outcomes, providing no process-level evidence for designing systems that anticipate and support user involvement. - How it does it: Participants completed 12 tasks on 6 live websites using a GPT-4o-powered browser agent, while interaction logs, stated intervention reasons, 7-point Likert UX ratings, and screen recordings were collected and analyzed via axial coding (κ=0.85 inter-rater reliability). - The good: Intervention frequency strongly and consistently predicts UX degradation (r=−0.37 to −0.57, all p<.001 for satisfaction, trust, ease, effectiveness), and the taxonomy achieves strong inter-rater reliability (κ=0.85), giving the framework empirical grounding rather than purely conceptual status. - The missing: The study uses a single GPT-4o agent with novice-only participants and no baseline manipulation of agent capability, so it is unknown how intervention patterns shift with weaker or stronger agents or with experienced users who have calibrated mental models of agent behavior. - Read it or not: Essential reading for CHI/CSCW researchers and practitioners designing mixed-initiative web agent interfaces; less relevant for those focused on agent performance benchmarking rather than human–agent interaction dynamics. Problem & Motivation LLM-powered web agents can execute browser-based tasks from natural language instructions, but they remain error-prone in practice. Benchmark evaluation has converged on outcome-centric metrics—success/failure binary signals (Outcome Reward Models)—that capture whether an agent ultimately completes a task but not the moment-to-moment dynamics by which collaboration fails and recovers. Designers of mixed-initiative systems lack empirical grounding for when users will step in, why they do so, what forms those interventions take, and how task structure and website domain modulate these patterns. Existing related work either proposes heuristic interfaces (Prompt2Task, CowPilot, MIWA) without studying real user behavior, or analyzes unintended consequences post hoc from a system perspective rather than from a user-agency perspective. Key Idea User interventions during agent execution are not random noise but systematic, predictable responses to identifiable agent failure modes; categorizing both the reasons for intervention (instruction–action misalignment, incomplete task termination, perceived delay, exceptions) and the forms they take (explicit agent-directed, explicit interface-directed, implicit monitoring) allows designers to anticipate bottlenecks and architect collaboration workflows that engage users at the right moment and in the right way. Methodology The agent is modeled as a Markov Decision Process (state = DOM + visual screenshot, action = click/type/scroll/submit via Playwright, policy = GPT-4o via the browser-use Python library, trajectory capped at T=100 steps). The study enrolled 30 university-affiliated participants (10F, 20M; ages 18–35; M=24.5, SD=3.7), all experienced with ChatGPT/Gemini but with no prior web agent experience. Each participant completed 12 tasks derived from Mind2Web benchmark templates, spanning 3 domains (shopping, information, travel) on 6 live websites (Amazon, Coupang, Reddit, X, Korean Air, Booking.com). A within-subjects Latin square design counterbalanced task order across 6 participant groups. Data collected: fine-grained interaction logs (mouse movements, hovers, clicks, keystrokes, tab focus), user-submitted text inputs with stated intervention reasons, 7-point Likert task-level ratings (effectiveness, ease, satisfaction, trust), and full-session screen recordings. Qualitative analysis used axial coding by 2 primary coders with a 3rd verifier; κ=0.85 across intervention-type coding. Implicit interventions were operationalized from mouse-movement events during agent execution (tracking, cross-checking, forecasting), with interaction logs as the primary evidence. Two-way ANOVA with partial η² and Pearson correlations with significance thresholds were used for quantitative analysis. Three critical design decisions: (1) controlled in-lab rather than in-the-wild to enable cross-participant comparison while using live websites—well-justified; (2) fixed pre-specified websites rather than user-chosen sites to prevent layout confounds—well-justified; (3) mouse-movement proxy for implicit interventions—acknowledged as coarse and a limitation. Key Results Across 360 task executions: 159 (44.2%) completed without intervention; 41 (11.4%) failed without intervention; 107 (29.7%) completed with intervention (representing 40.2% of the 266 completed tasks); 53 (14.7%) incomplete or failed even with intervention. From 309 explicit intervention instances: UI misinterpretation was the most frequent reason (127/309, 41%), predominantly in the travel domain (104/127). Instruction misunderstanding generated 43 instances, primarily in shopping. Task design dominated subjective UX variance: ease F(5,348)=23.11, p<.001, η²p=.25; satisfaction F(5,348)=17.07, p<.001, η²p=.20; effectiveness F(5,348)=26.67, p<.001, η²p=.28; trust F(5,348)=20.62, p<.001, η²p=.23. Website effects on satisfaction (η²p=.14) and effectiveness (η²p=.22) were smaller but significant. Intervention count was negatively correlated with satisfaction (r=−0.50), trust (r=−0.47), ease (r=−0.53), effectiveness (r=−0.41), all p<.001. A chi-square test confirmed that intervention type was not independent of intervention reason (χ²(72)=169.96, p<.001, V=.26), meaning users systematically match strategy to failure mode: UI misinterpretation predominantly triggered direct substitution (n=33) and error correction (n=28); instruction misunderstanding predominantly triggered prompt restructuring (n=15) and paraphrasing (n=7). Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The functional taxonomy—distinguishing explicit (agent-directed: prompt repetition, paraphrasing, restructuring, adaptive modification, contextual clarification; interface-directed: direct substitution, error correction, guidance) from implicit (tracking, cross-checking, forecasting) interventions and their reasons—is the first systematic, empirically-grounded framework characterizing human interventions during live web agent execution. The MDP agent formulation and GPT-4o backbone are standard; the empirical characterization of when and how novice users intervene, and the demonstration that task design (not website) is the dominant driver of UX, are the genuine contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Inter-rater reliability is strong (κ=0.85), and a third coder resolved discrepancies. Effect sizes (partial η²) are reported alongside ANOVA p-values. Latin square counterbalancing addresses order effects. The participant sample (N=30) is standard for controlled HCI qualitative studies but small for the quantitative claims about distribution of intervention reasons across domains (Table 2 Cramér's V=.26–.39, moderate not large). No code, dataset, or interaction logs are mentioned as publicly released. The implicit intervention operationalization via mouse movement is coarse—the authors acknowledge it and note that eye-tracking or think-aloud would improve granularity, but no validation against richer signals is provided. The study is peer-reviewed in CHI 2026 proceedings. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (user study with a deployed agent system). - Practical: The agent runs on a standard PC using commercially available websites and an API-accessible GPT-4o model; the study setup is straightforward to replicate. - Efficient: No computational cost analysis is reported; GPT-4o latency is noted qualitatively as a driver of user interventions (perceived delay), but no timing benchmarks or cost comparisons against alternative agents are provided. - Effective: The taxonomy is validated through κ=0.85 inter-rater reliability and strong statistical correspondence between intervention metrics and UX ratings; the design implications are qualitative and untested in any follow-up prototype. - Scalable: The taxonomy is conceptual and generalizes in principle, but empirical coverage is limited to 3 domains, 6 websites, novice users, and a single GPT-4o agent—scaling to expert users, higher-stakes domains (finance, healthcare), or different agent architectures is untested. The work delivers a taxonomy and design guidelines in paper form; no software artifact, dataset, or tool is released. Strengths - First process-level empirical account of when and why users intervene during live web agent execution, filling a gap left entirely open by outcome-centric benchmarking research. - The explicit/implicit intervention distinction is conceptually principled and backed by behavioral data: implicit interventions function as trust-calibration and readiness signals rather than corrections, a finding that directly informs interface design. - Strong quantitative validation: task design accounts for up to 28% of variance in effectiveness (η²p=.28) and 25% in ease (η²p=.25), demonstrating that prompt structure—not only agent capability—determines how much users must intervene. - The mapping of reasons to strategy types (χ²(72)=169.96, p<.001, V=.26) is a specific, actionable finding: UI misinterpretation reliably triggers interface takeover, while instruction misunderstanding triggers prompt revision cycles, enabling targeted design responses. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) young, university-recruited, agent-novice participants who may not represent expert or unfamiliar users; (2) coverage limited to 3 domains and 6 websites, excluding higher-stakes or unexplored domains; (3) a single GPT-4o agent, preventing cross-agent comparison; (4) coarse implicit intervention operationalization via mouse movement; (5) in-lab setting not capturing behavioral evolution with longitudinal exposure. These are confronted directly and credited as scope boundaries, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No baseline manipulation of agent capability: all results are from one agent, so the study cannot quantify how much of the intervention pattern is specific to GPT-4o versus general to imperfect agents. A weaker or stronger agent would almost certainly shift the distribution of reasons and types. - The implicit intervention corpus (87 tracking, 6 cross-checking, 37 forecasting instances) relies entirely on mouse movement, which is an unreliable proxy—participants routinely move mice without monitoring intent, and the threshold for what constitutes a "monitoring" movement vs. incidental movement is not validated. - Design implications (progress overlays, pre-listed interface candidates, prompt scaffolds) are practitioner-level suggestions derived from interview data; none are prototyped or evaluated, leaving open whether they actually reduce undesired interventions or introduce new usability problems. - The claim that task design is the dominant driver of UX (over website) is statistically supported within the 6 tested websites, but confounds remain: task design and website are not fully crossed (each task design maps to specific website pairs), making causal attribution imprecise. Bottom Line Practitioners and researchers designing human–web agent collaboration systems will find this the most empirically grounded, process-level account of user intervention dynamics currently available, making it the reference starting point for mixed-initiative interface design in this space.
Citations
#87 — Innovative (65)

Towards Long-horizon Agentic Multimodal Search (15 ▲ )

Yifan Du, Zikang Liu, Jinbiao Peng, Jie Wu, Junyi Li, Jinyang Li, Wayne Xin Zhao, Ji-Rong Wen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — UID-based lazy loading for visual context is an elegant mechanism; graph fuzzification for query synthesis is novel. Rigor (55) — no error bars, α=0.8 merging unablated (author-admitted), Seed-1.8 teacher closed-source. Non-Obviousness (70). Completeness (62) — limitations confronted but α gap significant. Resilience (60) — can't attribute credit between SFT and merging. Impact (75) — framework applies to multiple base models, code/model release promised. Weighted: ~65.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LMM-Searcher, a long-horizon multimodal deep search framework that offloads visual assets to an external file system referenced by lightweight UIDs, paired with a data synthesis pipeline and supervised fine-tuning to produce a 30B-parameter open-source multimodal search agent. - Why it's important: Multimodal search agents accumulate images in context across dozens of turns, causing token explosion that either crashes long-horizon tasks or forces lossy discarding of visual evidence; neither is acceptable for complex cross-modal reasoning chains. - How it does it: Rather than placing images directly in the LLM context, the framework serializes every retrieved image to disk and inserts only its UID into the reasoning trace, with a tailored `fetch-image` tool that rehydrates specific images on demand when fine-grained inspection is needed. - The good: On MM-BrowseComp and MMSearch-Plus, LMM-Searcher-30B (with 100-turn context management) achieves 30.1 and 34.8 respectively, establishing the best reported open-source numbers on those benchmarks, and the framework consistently improves performance when applied to stronger closed-source bases like Seed-1.8 (+13.7% on MMBC, +35.7% on MMSearch-Plus). - The missing: The model merging interpolation coefficient α=0.8 is chosen without ablation — a critical hyperparameter for which "a rigorous study is left for future work" — undermining confidence in the final model's results as a clean reflection of the framework's contribution. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building multimodal agentic systems who need a concrete, reproducible approach to visual context management at scale; practitioners wanting a drop-in framework enhancement applicable to any base MLLM will find the framework-comparison results (Table 3) particularly actionable. Problem & Motivation: Multimodal deep search agents must iteratively browse webpages, retrieve images, and reason across accumulated visual and textual evidence over many turns. Unlike text, images carry high token costs — a single webpage can introduce dozens of images — and this context explosion becomes catastrophic over 50–100-turn search horizons. Prior context-compression approaches (summarization, truncation) treat the problem as a text-compression task, which fails because image data cannot be losslessly represented as text summaries; heuristic discarding permanently destroys visual evidence. The domain is multimodal web search for complex, visually grounded questions requiring cross-modal multi-hop reasoning — tasks where knowing what a logo looks like or reading a photograph of a document is essential to answer the question. Key Idea: Decouple visual perception from reasoning by treating images as external file-system artifacts rather than context payload. Every image encountered during search is persistently stored with a unique identifier (UID); the agent's context contains only the UID as a lightweight proxy. When the agent needs to inspect an image visually, it calls `fetch-image(UID)` to load it on demand. This "lazy loading" paradigm eliminates context explosion while preserving lossless access to all visual evidence — the UID acts as a permanent, zero-cost pointer to the full image. Methodology: The system has two main components: a context management framework and an agentic training pipeline. The framework operates as intercepting middleware: when any search or browse tool returns a document containing images, the framework automatically indexes all images to a file system, assigns UIDs, and replaces raw image tokens with UID references before the serialized document enters the model context. The tool suite is divided into three categories — search tools (`google_search`, `image_search`, `visual_search`), browse tools (`scrape_website`, `fetch-image`), and visual processing tools (`zoom_in`) — forming a coarse-to-fine perception funnel. The key design decision is that `fetch-image` is the only tool that introduces actual image tokens into the context, making perceptual cost explicit and controllable. This is well-justified: it mirrors human browsing behavior (skim text, zoom in on relevant images) and provides a natural lossless guarantee. The training pipeline synthesizes 12,736 high-quality trajectories. Query synthesis starts by extracting core entities and related images from multimedia webpages, then generates single-hop visual questions constrained so they cannot be answered from text alone. A multi-hop knowledge graph is then constructed iteratively from the core entity, with "graph fuzzification" applied to leaf nodes to prevent shortcut reasoning. This yields multi-hop visual questions requiring sustained cross-modal search. Trajectories are collected by rolling out Seed-1.8 with rejection sampling (retaining only success within 40 turns, 64K context). Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is performed on Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking for 3 epochs (batch size 64, lr 1e-5, masking tool responses from the loss). The final model is then merged with MiroThinker-1.7-mini via parameter interpolation: Θ_final = 0.8·Θ_V + 0.2·Θ_T. This merging step is a critical design decision that is explicitly not ablated — the authors acknowledge it warrants future study, which is a notable gap. Key Results: On four benchmarks (MM-BrowseComp [MMBC], MMSearch-Plus, VisBrowse, MMSearch), LMM-Searcher-30B achieves 22.3/32.9/42.0/71.0 at 30-turn evaluation and 30.1/34.8/48.3/72.3 at 100-turn evaluation with context management. Against open-source multimodal search agents, this matches or exceeds WebWatcher-32B (55.3 on MMSearch), DeepEyesV2-7B (63.7 on MMSearch), Vision-DeepResearch-30B (69.6 on MMSearch), and REDSearcher-MM (72.9 on MMSearch). The framework, applied identically to other base models, improves Seed-1.8 from 21.4→35.1 on MMBC and 11.0→46.7 on MMSearch-Plus, and GPT-5 from 17.2→34.8 on MMSearch-Plus — indicating the framework contributes independently of base model identity. The synthesized training data averages 17.26 tool-use turns per query versus 5.63 (FVQA), 7.16 (LiveVQA), and 13.21 (REDSearcher-MM), confirming qualitatively harder long-horizon characteristics. The ablation in Table 5 shows removing `fetch-image` drops VisBrowse from 58.0 to 48.5 (−9.5 points), while MMSearch drops only 73.2→71.0 (−2.2), revealing benchmark-specific dependence on active visual inspection. All baselines are current (2025–2026 preprints and models). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The file-based UID context management mechanism and the `fetch-image` active-perception tool are genuinely novel contributions to multimodal agent design; the multi-hop knowledge-graph query synthesis with graph fuzzification is a new data generation method. The SFT training loop and model merging are standard techniques applied in a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for both the `fetch-image` tool (Table 5) and data sources (Table 4), and the framework generalization is tested across multiple base models (Table 3) — these are substantive rather than cosmetic ablations. However, no error bars or multiple-seed results are reported for any experiment, so variance is unknown. The model merging coefficient α=0.8 receives no ablation. The training data synthesis pipeline uses Seed-1.8 as teacher — a closed-source commercial model — which may limit reproducibility. Code is promised at https://github.com/RUCAIBox/LMM-Searcher but was not yet released at submission. The evaluation benchmarks (MMBC, MMSearch-Plus, MMSearch) are themselves arXiv preprints, adding uncertainty about benchmark stability and quality. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.12890v1, April 2026). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems + experiments). - Practical: The agent framework runs on commercially available hardware; the trained model (Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking) requires significant GPU memory (~80GB+ for 30B MoE with 3B active parameters) but is within reach of A100/H100 clusters. - Efficient: Context overhead is reduced by replacing image tokens with UIDs, but `fetch-image` calls re-inject full image tokens on demand; at 100-turn horizons with frequent fetches, the total token budget could still be substantial. No wall-clock or FLOPs comparison against baseline frameworks is provided. - Effective: Evidence across four benchmarks and three base models shows consistent improvement from both the framework and the trained model; the `fetch-image` ablation confirms the mechanism causally contributes, not just training data. - Scalable: Interactive scaling curves (Figure 5) show continuing improvement through 100 turns on MMBC and VisBrowse, suggesting the design has not saturated; however, 100-turn evaluation is the maximum tested and no stress test beyond this scale is performed. The paper delivers a software framework (code promised), a trained 30B model checkpoint (release announced), and a training dataset of 12,736 trajectories. Strengths: - The UID-based lazy-loading mechanism is an elegant solution to context explosion that, unlike summarization or truncation, provides a formal lossless guarantee: every visual asset remains retrievable as long as its UID is in the trace. - The data synthesis pipeline produces qualitatively harder queries than existing open-source datasets (17.26 avg turns vs ≤13 for alternatives), and the knowledge-graph fuzzification technique specifically prevents shortcut-answer leakage — a non-obvious design choice that is well-motivated. - Framework generalization across base models (GPT-5, Seed-1.8, Qwen3) demonstrates the approach is not overfit to a specific architecture or capability level, which substantially increases practical value. - Interactive scaling curves provide concrete evidence that the system benefits from increased interaction budget at test time — a property that text-only scaling research already confirmed but has not been demonstrated cleanly in multimodal search. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that the model merging coefficient α=0.8 is a design choice and that "a rigorous study of model merging is left for future work." They note that weaker base models (Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking) exhibit only marginal gains from the framework, suggesting a fixed search-and-look workflow suffices for simpler agents. These are stated forthrightly and represent credible scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The α=0.8 merging coefficient is the most significant gap: the final LMM-Searcher-30B results depend on this merged model, yet no ablation quantifies how much performance comes from SFT versus merging — a reviewer cannot attribute credit. There is no text-only search agent control at comparable scale, so the incremental contribution of visual modality cannot be isolated. The synthesized training queries are sourced from multimedia news and movie websites, which may introduce topic bias not present in the evaluation benchmarks. The trajectory teacher (Seed-1.8) is a closed-source model, creating a reproducibility gap for the full training pipeline. The paper does not characterize failure modes: what types of multi-hop visual queries does LMM-Searcher systematically fail on, and why? The VisBrowse benchmark appears in Table 2 but is not described in the experimental setup, leaving its provenance and difficulty unclear to the reader. Bottom Line: A practitioner building multimodal search agents will gain a concrete, principled context management technique (UID-based lazy loading) with credible empirical support across multiple benchmarks and base models — the framework contribution is more durable than the specific trained model, which is compromised by an unablated merging step.
Recommended 2604.12890 PDF
#88 — Innovative (65)

SPPO: Sequence-Level PPO for Long-Horizon Reasoning Tasks (25 ▲ )

Tianyi Wang, Yixia Li, Long Li, Yibiao Chen, Shaohan Huang, Yun Chen, Peng Li, Yang Liu, Guanhua Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) explicit bandit reformulation with scalar baseline is a clean contribution. Non-Obv (70) clarifying lens on GRPO. Rigor (55) no error bars, asymmetric training steps (1000 vs 200), β_KL=0 without stability tests. Completeness (60) limitations partially acknowledged. Resilience (55) 1-point margins over GRPO without significance, no DAPO/Dr.GRPO comparison. Impact (75) code released, claimed 5.9× speedup. Weighted ≈ 63.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SPPO (Sequence-Level PPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm that reformulates LLM reasoning as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit problem, using a learned scalar value function for advantage estimation without multi-sample rollouts. - Why it's important: Standard PPO collapses in long Chain-of-Thought reasoning due to the "tail effect" — the token-level critic cannot distinguish correct from incorrect reasoning chains until the final tokens, causing credit assignment to degrade; GRPO fixes stability at the cost of 8× sampling overhead that bottlenecks throughput. - How it does it: By collapsing the time horizon to H=1 and broadcasting a single sequence-level advantage signal A = R − V_φ(s_p) uniformly to all tokens in the response, where V_φ estimates prompt solvability via binary cross-entropy, SPPO eliminates both token-level credit assignment bias and the need for group sampling. - The good: An ablation (PPO+BCE) that adds only the BCE loss to standard token-level PPO fails to replicate SPPO's gains and collapses at 500 steps — directly isolating the bandit formulation, not the loss function, as the causal factor. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to mathematical reasoning with binary verifiable rewards; no demonstration that the approach generalizes to tasks with denser or non-binary reward signals, and no ablation on the number of training steps (GRPO runs 1000 steps on 1.5B but only 200 on 7B). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on RLVR training of LLMs who want a PPO-compatible, compute-efficient alternative to GRPO — the bandit framing is a clean conceptual lens, and the 5.9× speedup claim is backed by wall-clock comparisons. Problem & Motivation: Training LLMs to reason via Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) requires assigning credit across thousands of tokens in a Chain-of-Thought response, given only a sparse terminal reward. Standard PPO employs a token-level Critic with Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE), but in long-horizon reasoning the Critic systematically fails: it assigns near-identical value estimates to correct and incorrect trajectories throughout most of the response, only diverging in the final tokens (the "tail effect" visualized in Figures 1 and 9). This causes the advantage signal to vanish for early tokens on correct paths and to underestimate penalties on incorrect ones. GRPO eliminates the critic by using group-normalized rewards across N=8 samples per prompt, trading bias for variance and incurring a compute cost proportional to N, severely limiting training throughput. The gap this paper targets is a method that achieves GRPO's stability without its multi-sampling overhead. Key Idea: The core insight is that GRPO's empirical success stems from it implicitly modeling reasoning as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit (single-step, single-action) rather than a multi-step MDP — and that this framing can be made explicit and augmented with a learned scalar baseline. By collapsing H to 1, the entire response becomes one atomic action, removing all need for temporal credit assignment. A prompt-conditioned scalar value model V_φ(s_p) trained with BCE to estimate the probability of success for that prompt serves as a stable, generalizable baseline, enabling single-sample (N=1) policy updates while variance is controlled by the learned baseline rather than by group statistics. Methodology: SPPO has two learned components: a Policy π_θ and a scalar Value Model V_φ(s_p). The value model is trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss L_V(φ) = −E[R log V_φ(s_p) + (1−R) log(1−V_φ(s_p))], predicting the solvability of a prompt independent of any specific response. The advantage is computed as A(s_p, a) = R − V_φ(s_p) and broadcast uniformly to every token in the sequence. Policy optimization uses the standard PPO clipped surrogate objective J_SPPO(θ) = E[min(r_t(θ)A(s_p,a), clip(r_t(θ), 1−ε, 1+ε)A(s_p,a))], where the same A applies at every token position t. Critical design decisions: (1) Broadcast advantage is well-justified by the bandit formulation and confirmed by the PPO+BCE ablation; (2) BCE loss for V_φ is appropriate for the binary-reward setting and avoids value overestimation seen in GAE; (3) Decoupled Critic uses a smaller 1.5B model to train a 7B policy, which is justified empirically (best average score of 58.56) and reduces memory by 12.8%. The implementation uses the verl library with β_KL=0 to encourage exploration. Experiments run on 4×A100 (1.5B) and 4×H100 (7B). Key Results: On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B fine-tuned on DeepScaleR: SPPO achieves average 48.06 across five benchmarks (AIME24: 34.17, AIME25: 25.83, AMC23: 74.38, MATH500: 83.78, Minerva: 22.15), versus GRPO(N=8) at 47.08, ReMax at 46.74, RLOO at 46.15, and standard PPO at 44.06. On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B fine-tuned on DAPO-17K: SPPO achieves 58.11 vs GRPO(N=8) at 57.44; the decoupled 1.5B-critic variant reaches 58.56. SPPO reaches peak 7B performance (mean score ≈58) in approximately 22 hours of wall-clock time, while GRPO requires significantly longer. SPPO achieves a claimed 5.9× training speedup over GRPO due to single-sample efficiency. The value model achieves Pearson r=0.642 and Spearman 0.664 correlation with empirical AVG@64 pass rates on 200 held-out prompts. RLVR benchmark on 5 control tasks (CartPole H=200, MountainCar H=1000, Hopper H=1000, LunarLander H=1000, Pendulum H=1000) confirms SPPO converges where standard PPO flatlines, particularly in tasks with weak SFT priors. Baselines (GRPO, ReMax, RLOO, standard PPO) are implemented uniformly via verl and represent current strong comparators for this setting. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The explicit reformulation of RLVR as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit, combined with a prompt-conditioned scalar solvability baseline, is the genuine novelty — prior work either used token-level MDPs (PPO) or group-sampled empirical baselines (GRPO/RLOO/ReMax). The PPO clipping mechanism and BCE value training are standard; the novelty lies in their structural combination under the bandit horizon collapse and in the analysis explaining why GRPO works via this lens. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: the PPO+BCE control directly tests whether the loss function or the formulation drives improvement, and it cleanly fails. The decoupled critic ablation validates the small-critic hypothesis. The value model calibration analysis (N=200 prompts, AVG@64) provides quantitative evidence that V_φ captures prompt difficulty. However, error bars or standard deviations are absent from the main results table — results are reported as point estimates across single training runs, making it impossible to assess whether the 1-point margins over GRPO are statistically meaningful. The RLVR benchmark on control tasks uses 5 environments, which is reasonable for structural validation. Code is available at https://github.com/sustech-nlp/SPPO; training commands and hyperparameters are fully specified in Appendix E, enabling reproduction. This is an arXiv preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Yes — experiments run on 4×A100 (1.5B) and 4×H100 (7B); the decoupled critic variant reduces memory to a peak of ~71.5% normalized VRAM compared to GRPO's ~85.7%, making it accessible on consumer multi-GPU setups. - Efficient: Single-sample (N=1) updates yield a claimed 5.9× wall-clock speedup over GRPO(N=8), confirmed by Figure 5 showing SPPO reaching peak performance in ~22 hours vs. GRPO requiring substantially longer; the 1.5B decoupled critic cuts memory 12.8% relative to symmetric 7B+7B. - Effective: Evidence shows improvement over standard PPO is robust and consistent; margins over GRPO are 1 point average on both 1.5B and 7B, without confidence intervals — the method demonstrably solves tasks where PPO collapses (Hopper, MountainCar), which is the primary claim. - Scalable: Tested only up to 7B parameters; no evidence at 13B, 70B, or larger scales; no discussion of how value model calibration quality evolves with scale. The claim of scalability is asserted, not tested. The paper delivers a software artifact (code at https://github.com/sustech-nlp/SPPO) with complete training scripts. Strengths: - The bandit-formulation lens on GRPO is a genuine analytical contribution that clarifies why a class of existing methods works — this reframing has implications beyond SPPO itself. - The PPO+BCE ablation is clean and decisive: it rules out the simpler explanation that the BCE loss alone explains SPPO's gains, directly validating the structural hypothesis. - The decoupled critic result (1.5B critic aligning 7B policy achieves the best benchmark score) is a practically surprising and useful finding that challenges the assumption that critic and policy must be matched in size. - Full reproducibility: complete training commands, hyperparameters, and dataset/model links are provided across all five baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that SPPO is tailored for tasks with verifiable outcome rewards, and that extending to open-ended generation tasks lacking objective ground-truth verifiers is future work. They also acknowledge ethical risks from lowering the barrier to training capable reasoning models (bias amplification, misuse for automated harmful task completion). These acknowledgments are appropriately direct and not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No error bars or significance testing on the main results table — 1-point margins over GRPO across single runs are not statistically distinguishable without variance estimates. (2) The 7B GRPO baseline runs only 200 training steps while the 1.5B GRPO runs 1000 steps; this asymmetry could disadvantage GRPO at 7B scale and inflate SPPO's apparent advantage. (3) No evaluation of reward hacking or training stability across multiple seeds — particularly relevant since β_KL=0 removes the KL regularization safety net. (4) The value model's bimodal empirical distribution vs. unimodal predicted distribution is flagged as a calibration concern by the authors but the conservative prediction behavior is unexplored — it is unclear how miscalibration affects optimization dynamics for prompts near the decision boundary. (5) No comparison to DAPO (Luo et al., 2025) or Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025) which are current strong baselines for the 7B setting. Bottom Line: SPPO offers a principled, computationally efficient alternative to GRPO for RLVR training of reasoning LLMs, with a credible mechanistic explanation and solid reproducibility, but the narrow performance margins over GRPO — reported without statistical significance — and the absence of scale tests beyond 7B leave the magnitude of the practical gain uncertain.
Recommended 2604.08865 PDF
#89 — Innovative (64)

Free Geometry: Refining 3D Reconstruction from Longer Versions of Itself (14 ▲ )

Yuhang Dai, Xingyi Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (65) — the architecture-guaranteed more-views-is-better ordering as a free self-supervision signal is clean and original in combination. Rigor (65) pulled down by per-dataset hyperparameter tuning on the same benchmarks used for evaluation (Goodhart risk), only 3 seeds, and no head-to-head Test3R table. Non-Obviousness (70) — cross-frame relational triplet loss and the transfer-across-view-counts finding are non-obvious. Completeness (60) — several design choices (masking pattern, per-scene adaptation) not ablated. Impact (65) — public code on GitHub, plug-and-play across two backbones, modest absolute gains. Composite pulled down by modest gain magnitude and dataset-specific tuning confound.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Free Geometry, a test-time adaptation framework that self-supervises feed-forward multi-view 3D reconstruction models by distilling knowledge from full-view predictions into partial-view predictions, using lightweight LoRA updates without any 3D ground truth. - Why it's important: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models are frozen after training and degrade on novel scenes, particularly under occlusions, specularities, and sparse views — the exact conditions common in real-world deployment — with no practical mechanism to adapt without labeled 3D data. - How it does it: It exploits the architecture-guaranteed quality ordering that more views → more reliable internal representations, treating the full-observation backbone features as a teacher signal and enforcing intra-frame feature consistency plus cross-frame relational geometry preservation on a LoRA-augmented partial-view student. - The good: The framework is genuinely plug-and-play across two strong, independently developed foundation models (Depth Anything 3 and VGGT) with consistent gains across four diverse benchmarks and view counts, all within a 2-minute adaptation budget on a single GPU. - The missing: Dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning (different learning rates, sample counts, and epoch counts per dataset) is required and not automated, undermining the claim of fully deployment-ready plug-and-play adaptation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on 3D scene reconstruction, test-time adaptation, or multi-view representation learning — the core insight is clean and the integration with existing foundation models is practical. Problem & Motivation: Feed-forward multi-view 3D reconstruction models such as Depth Anything 3 and VGGT adopt a train-then-freeze paradigm: trained once on large datasets, they are deployed in zero-shot fashion without adaptation to the test distribution. This rigidity causes geometric errors on novel scenes — particularly under occlusions, reflective surfaces, repetitive textures, and other distribution-shift conditions. Collecting 3D ground truth (laser scans or dense SLAM outputs) across diverse real-world environments is prohibitively expensive, ruling out supervised fine-tuning. Existing test-time adaptation methods (TENT, TTT, MEMO) rely on weak self-supervised signals (entropy, rotation prediction) loosely coupled to geometry, while Test3R enforces symmetric output-level consistency between view pairs without any quality hierarchy, risking regression to the mean. Key Idea: The core insight is that the "more-views-is-better" property in multi-view transformer architectures is an architecturally guaranteed quality ordering: because cross-view global attention aggregates more geometric constraints with more input frames, the encoder features computed from a full view set are reliably more accurate than those from a masked subset. This quality gap is free — it requires no labels — and can be exploited as an asymmetric teacher-student signal at test time, with the full-view backbone serving as a scene-specific teacher for the partial-view LoRA-adapted student. Methodology: Free Geometry processes each test sequence in two parallel branches. The teacher branch feeds all N frames through the frozen backbone (e.g., DINOv2-based image patch encoder + multi-view transformer), extracting intermediate token features F_full without gradients. The student branch feeds the M unmasked frames (even-indexed subset, M = N/2 by default) through the same frozen backbone augmented with LoRA modules (rank 32, α = 32) inserted into the multi-view transformer, plus trainable camera tokens, producing F_partial. Two losses jointly optimize the LoRA weights and camera tokens: (1) Intra-frame consistency loss (L_intra) aligns student features to teacher features at matched spatial positions on unmasked frames using a Huber loss plus cosine similarity term, enforcing both magnitude and directional alignment. (2) Cross-frame relational loss (L_cross) transfers geometric structure implied by the held-out masked frames by constructing triplets (p, k_j, q) in feature space — reference tokens, K=4 extreme-similarity anchor tokens from masked frames, and additional unmasked tokens — and penalizing deviations in pairwise similarity distributions (via KL divergence) and triangle angles in feature space (via L1 on three cosine angles). The final objective is L_geo = L_intra + L_cross. Adaptation runs for 3–5 epochs on 5–20 samples per scene using AdamW with cosine LR schedule, taking under 2 minutes per dataset on an RTX Pro 6000. Critical design decisions: (a) feature-level rather than output-level distillation avoids per-view decoder interference and reduces compute; (b) asymmetric teacher-student formulation avoids the symmetric consistency averaging problem of Test3R; (c) anchor token selection by extreme cosine similarity (top-2 and bottom-2) rather than random sampling captures maximal geometric contrast. Key Results: Evaluated on ETH3D, ScanNet++, 7Scenes, and HiRoom across N ∈ {4, 8, 16, 32} views. Primary baselines are frozen DA3 Giant and frozen VGGT — strong, current foundation models. Results averaged over 3 random seeds. At 4 views on ETH3D: VGGT AUC@3 improves 0.157 → 0.178 (+13.4% relative), F1 0.102 → 0.110; DA3 AUC@3 0.286 → 0.305, F1 0.207 → 0.209. At 8 views on ETH3D: DA3 AUC@3 0.408 → 0.439, F1 0.495 → 0.500. Averaged across all 4 datasets and view counts: VGGT gains +3.73% AUC@3 and +4.51% F1 at 4 views; DA3 gains +2.74% AUC@3 and +2.85% F1.
