Problem & Motivation Cloud-hosted LLM services (MLaaS) require users to submit prompts as plain text, exposing sensitive medical, legal, and financial content to server-side interception, insider misuse, and storage in training pipelines. Prior defenses fail in distinct ways: prompt sanitization misses contextual and implicit disclosures; cryptographic inference (homomorphic encryption, secure multiparty computation) incurs prohibitive overhead for Transformer-scale models; and naive embedding transmission is vulnerable to generative inversion attacks that reconstruct semantically faithful text from embeddings even after heuristic noise addition. A further gap is that prior systems protecting inference-time privacy still require plain-text prompts during domain-specific fine-tuning, leaving sensitive training data fully visible to the server. No unified framework existed that enforces text-free operation at both inference and fine-tuning time while maintaining competitive task performance. Key Idea If a server-side LLM is trained from the outset to condition on compressed, noise-corrupted continuous embeddings rather than discrete tokens, plain text is never required at any point in the service lifecycle. k-Pooling before noise injection reduces token-level resolution geometrically — not just statistically — making exact inversion intrinsically harder because the mapping from obfuscated slots back to individual tokens is many-to-one even without noise. Fine-tuning on sensitive domain data proceeds through the same obfuscated interface, so the server trains on Laplace-perturbed embeddings and never observes the raw prompts. Methodology PPFT is a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (Alignment): ModernBERT-large encodes the input prompt to token-level embeddings H ∈ ℝ^{n×d_e}. Non-overlapping k-Pooling compresses H to m = ⌈n/k⌉ slot vectors U by block-wise mean-pooling (k=4 in main experiments, sequences capped at 512 tokens). A trainable projection layer P_ψ maps U to the decoder's input space Z ∈ ℝ^{m×d_d}. The LLaMA-family decoder (1B or 8B, LoRA rank=16, α=32) is trained with L_align = -Σ log p_θ(y_t | y_{<t}, Z) on seven general-domain instruction and QA corpora. Encoder, projection, and decoder LoRA are jointly optimized with AdamW (lr=2×10^{-5}, cosine schedule). Stage 2 (Domain Adaptation): The client-side encoder is frozen. For each example, the client applies k-Pooling and then injects isotropic ℓ₂-Laplace noise (direction sampled uniformly from the unit sphere, magnitude from Gamma(d_e, ε)), followed by ℓ₂ re-normalization to obtain obfuscated embeddings Ũ. Only Ũ reaches the server; the server optimizes L_priv = -Σ log p_θ(y_t | y_{<t}, Z̃) where Z̃ = P_ψ(Ũ). The noise mechanism satisfies metric-DP (dχ-privacy) per slot, with the global budget calibrated to B=150 across all methods via unified accounting rules. Three design decisions are well-justified by ablations: (1) k-Pooling is validated by a pooling-size sweep (k ∈ {4,8,16}) showing monotonically increasing reconstruction resistance with k at the cost of monotonically decreasing task accuracy; (2) Laplace noise is chosen over Gaussian by direct comparison showing Laplace maintains lower ROUGE-L across all ε values; (3) applying noise during Stage 2 fine-tuning (not just inference) is validated by a no-noise ablation showing that noise-aware fine-tuning produces a decoder that is more robust to obfuscated inputs without performance regression on clean inputs. Key Results Downstream task performance (Table 1): With Llama-3.1-8B, PPFT achieves average accuracy 0.7314 across Pri-DDX, Pri-NLICE, and Pri-SLJA, degrading 0.1977 from the noise-free upper bound (0.9291) and outperforming the best baseline PrivacyRestore (0.6343) and dχ-privacy (0.2750). On Pri-SLJA, PPFT reaches 0.9049 vs. upper bound 0.9466, a gap of 4.2 percentage points. With Llama-3.2-1B, PPFT average is 0.5699 vs. upper bound 0.7573. General-domain retention (Table 4, 8B): PPFT scores CSQA 0.5278 and SQuAD 0.7085 vs. upper bound 0.6086/0.8930, drops of 0.0808 and 0.1845. Reconstruction resistance (Figure 3, Table 8): PPFT maintains ROUGE-L < 0.25 across all ε values under a standard GPT-2-medium inversion attacker. Under a noise-aware adversary at ε=75, PPFT pooling-4 reaches ROUGE-L 0.4397, pooling-16 reaches 0.1799, while PrivacyRestore reaches 0.72 under the same attacker. Attribute-level reconstruction (Table 3): PPFT achieves age recall 0.0071 and antecedent recall 0.0115, near-zero sensitive attribute recovery. The zero-shot pooled-embedding inversion experiment (Appendix K) shows ROUGE-L stays below 0.06 across 10 iterative decoding rounds, demonstrating that k-Pooling breaks the iterative inversion paradigm effective against single-vector encoders. Baselines (dχ-privacy, Paraphrase via FLAN-T5-base, PrivacyRestore) are current and representative of the embedding-protection literature; no cryptographic baseline is included for compute-cost comparison. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The unified text-free interface enforced consistently across both inference and domain adaptation is genuinely novel — prior work addressed one or the other but not both under a single obfuscated embedding protocol. The combination of k-Pooling, metric-DP Laplace noise, and two-stage continuous-prefix fine-tuning is novel as an integrated system. Each individual component (k-Pooling, metric-DP, continuous prefix tuning, LoRA) is drawn from prior work; the contribution is their integration under a coherent privacy model with unified privacy budget accounting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover pooling size (k ∈ {4,8,16}), noise type (Laplace vs. Gaussian), and noise presence/absence — sufficient to validate key design choices. However, all results are from a single evaluation run per configuration with no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed averaging; for accuracy differences in the range of 0.03–0.08, statistical significance is not established. Privacy budget alignment via unified global budget B=150 (Appendix E) is a genuine methodological contribution that prevents unfair comparisons across methods operating at different granularities. The inversion attacker is GPT-2-medium (117M parameters) fine-tuned for 20 epochs — capable but not a modern large LLM. Domain datasets (Pri-DDX, Pri-NLICE, Pri-SLJA) are synthetically generated fictional cases from prior work; no real clinical or legal corpora are used. No code, weights, or data artifacts are mentioned as publicly released. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.06831v1, April 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built client-server pipeline evaluated through inference and fine-tuning runs. - Practical: The client-side encoder (ModernBERT-large) is explicitly validated to run on CPU-only commodity hardware; server-side LLaMA inference requires standard GPU infrastructure already assumed in MLaaS settings. - Efficient: Client overhead is a ModernBERT-large forward pass plus k-Pooling plus noise injection — lightweight relative to LLM inference — but Stage 1 alignment requires server-side training from scratch on general-domain data, a one-time cost the paper does not quantify. - Effective: PPFT recovers ≥95% of the noise-free upper bound on Pri-SLJA and demonstrates near-zero sensitive attribute reconstruction, providing credible evidence of practical utility for MCQA-format sensitive-domain tasks; open-ended generation is not evaluated. - Scalable: Tested up to 8B parameters and 512-token sequences; applicability to larger decoders, closed-source API-based LLMs, and longer contexts is explicitly flagged as unresolved, with no experiments beyond these bounds. No software artifact (library, CLI, or model weights) is released; the work stops at paper form. Strengths - The unified text-free interface for both inference and fine-tuning is the paper's most significant intellectual contribution — prior systems left fine-tuning as a blind spot, and closing this gap is non-trivial because it requires training the decoder to accept obfuscated inputs as the supervisory signal. - The unified global privacy budget alignment across heterogeneous methods (Appendix E) is methodologically careful and prevents the apples-to-oranges comparison that plagues much of the differential-privacy-for-NLP literature. - The multi-layer inversion evaluation — ROUGE-L across ε, attribute-level recall, noise-aware adversary, Stage-1-aligned adversary, and zero-shot pooled-embedding inversion — anticipates and closes the most obvious loopholes a reviewer would raise. - The pooled-embedding inversion analysis (Appendix K) contributes an empirically non-obvious finding: k-Pooling breaks the iterative cosine-similarity-guided inversion paradigm that successfully attacks single-vector encoders, qualifying as a standalone result for the privacy literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations - Output-side exposure: PPFT protects prompt confidentiality but does not prevent leakage through model outputs; the authors explicitly recommend complementing PPFT with output-side content filtering and sensitive-information detection. - Generality across model pairs: Validated only with ModernBERT-large encoder and LLaMA-family decoders; applicability to smaller encoders, alternative architectures, or closed-source API-based LLMs is flagged as requiring further work. - Multilingual and multimodal extension: Explicitly noted as an open question with no preliminary results. The authors address limitations directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single evaluation run with no error bars: For accuracy differences of 0.03–0.08 between PPFT and baselines on small test sets, statistical significance is unconfirmed — this is the most serious methodological gap. - Synthetic dataset ecological validity: Pri-DDX, Pri-NLICE, and Pri-SLJA are synthetically generated fictional cases designed for privacy evaluation; their templated structure (acknowledged to inflate ROUGE-L for Pri-SLJA) means inversion difficulty may be artificially elevated compared to real clinical text with higher lexical diversity. - Attacker strength ceiling: The strongest tested attacker is GPT-2-medium; a modern LLaMA-3-8B inversion model could plausibly achieve higher ROUGE-L, particularly if fine-tuned on large amounts of domain-specific text that matches the synthetic dataset's templates. - No open-ended generation evaluation: All downstream tasks are MCQA where multiple-choice options anchor the decoder's output; performance on summarization, free-form QA, or clinical note generation through the compressed prefix interface is entirely unknown. - Stage 1 alignment cost not reported: The computational requirements and general-domain data volume for Stage 1 alignment are not analyzed, leaving a practical deployment barrier unquantified for service providers. Bottom Line PPFT is the first credible system to enforce a consistent text-free interface across both LLM inference and domain fine-tuning, and practitioners building privacy-sensitive MLaaS pipelines should read it — but the evaluation's reliance on synthetic MCQA datasets and single-run reporting means its performance claims require independent replication before production deployment.
Search Results 2604.06831 PDF
#109 — Innovative (62)

Memory Transfer Learning: How Memories are Transferred Across Domains in Coding Agents (24 ▲ )

Kangsan Kim, Minki Kang, Taeil Kim, Yanlai Yang, Mengye Ren, Sung Ju Hwang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) highest: 431 abstract memories beating 5,899 same-domain memories inverts the scale-by-accumulation intuition. Novelty (65) is a clean cross-domain reframing with leave-one-domain-out evaluation. Rigor (55): 3 models and targeted ablations, but no error bars on 100-task benchmarks where deltas are 1–2%, post-hoc formal proposition with unvalidated assumptions, primary tables self-referential (GPT-5-mini generates and tests). Completeness (60), Resilience (55), Impact (55) — no released library or memory dump, modest absolute gains.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Memory Transfer Learning (MTL), a framework that builds a shared memory pool from heterogeneous coding task domains and retrieves cross-domain memories at inference time to improve coding agent performance. - Why it's important: Self-evolving coding agents that silo memory to a single benchmark miss the shared procedural knowledge (shell workflows, validation routines, anti-patterns) applicable across all coding tasks, leaving substantial performance on the table. - How it does it: LLM-generated memories at four abstraction levels (Trajectory, Workflow, Summary, Insight) are extracted offline from agent trajectories across source benchmarks, indexed by embedding, and retrieved via cosine similarity with source-benchmark memories excluded from the test pool. - The good: MTL with Insight memory achieves +3.7% average Pass@3 over zero-shot across 6 benchmarks using only 431 memories, outperforming AgentKB (+1.7%) which uses 5,899 memories — counter-intuitive and practically significant. - The missing: The formal abstraction-transfer proposition in Appendix C rests on unvalidated assumptions about embedding geometry, and the primary results have a self-referential structure (GPT-5-mini generates memories for and is tested as GPT-5-mini), which cross-model experiments only partially resolve. - Read it or not: Practitioners building memory-augmented coding agents should read it — the three design principles (prefer abstract memory, pool across domains, use simple retrieval) are immediately actionable and empirically grounded. Problem & Motivation Self-evolving coding agents accumulate memories of past task outcomes to guide future behavior, but existing systems (ReasoningBank, AgentKB, Dynamic Cheatsheet) restrict memory generation and retrieval to the same benchmark or task domain. In practice, coding agents must handle heterogeneous work — competitive programming, repository-level bug fixes, ML research pipelines, scientific replication — all of which share a common substrate: Linux shells, file navigation patterns, test-validation idioms, and API contracts. Siloing memory by domain prevents agents from exploiting this shared infrastructure knowledge. The question of which forms of memory actually transfer, and why, has not been systematically studied. Key Idea The central insight is that what transfers across coding task domains is not task-specific code, algorithms, or language idioms, but meta-knowledge — procedural and behavioral guidance such as iterative inspect-edit-verify cycles, inline test creation before submission, and guardrails against blind overwrites. High-level "Insight" memories that strip domain-specific details and encode general principles transfer well; low-level "Trajectory" memories that preserve exact command sequences act as brittle anchors causing negative transfer when the language or file structure changes. Methodology The pipeline has two offline stages and one online stage. Offline: (1) run the coding agent (mini-swe-agent + GPT-5-mini) across all 6 benchmarks and record full inference trajectories H = (t, [(r₁,a₁,o₁),...,(rₙ,aₙ,oₙ)]); (2) classify each trajectory success/failure via LLM judge, then generate four memory types — Trajectory (raw action-observation concatenation), Workflow (LLM-extracted goal + reusable action sequence), Summary (task_summary + experience_summary explaining why the run succeeded or failed), and Insight (title + one-sentence description + generalizable content, explicitly prompted to omit file-specific details). Memories are embedded with text-embedding-3-small and indexed. Online: retrieve N=3 memories from pool P_τ(B_i) = {M_τ^(k) | t^(k) ∉ B_i} — the leave-one-domain-out constraint excludes source memories from the test benchmark — selecting by top-N cosine similarity and prepending to the agent's system prompt. Three critical design decisions: (1) Leave-one-benchmark-out pool construction enforces genuine cross-domain transfer; methodologically sound. (2) Offline memory generation decouples memory cost from inference but risks distributional mismatch with target tasks. (3) Simple embedding retrieval over LLM reranking or adaptive rewriting — empirically validated: Table 7 shows embedding similarity achieves avg Pass@3 of 0.630 vs. 0.608 for adaptive rewriting and 0.598 for LLM reranking, with the explanation that reranking heuristics designed for static settings don't generalize to dynamic multi-step agentic contexts. Key Results GPT-5-mini MTL(Insight) achieves Pass@3 of 0.560 average vs. 0.523 zero-shot (+3.7%); individual benchmark gains range from +2.0% (LiveCodeBench6) to +7.8% (ReplicationBench) and +8.3% (MLGym-Bench). Against self-evolving baselines on 3 benchmarks: MTL achieves 0.630 avg vs. ReasoningBank 0.601 (+2.9%) and AgentKB 0.613 (+1.7%), with 431 memories vs. 97 and 5,899. Pass@1 results (Table 8): GPT-5-mini MTL(I) averages 0.454 vs. 0.435 zero-shot (+1.9%); DeepSeek V3.2 +2.0%; Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct +1.2%. Cross-model transfer: GPT-5-mini-generated memories improve DeepSeek V3.2 to 0.501 avg (vs. 0.486 zero-shot) and Qwen3-Coder to 0.413 (vs. 0.402), confirming model-agnostic meta-knowledge. Abstraction ordering is monotonic: Insight > Summary > Workflow > Trajectory. Within Insight format, task-agnostic memories outperform task-specific memories by +1.1% avg (Table 4). Embedding distribution analysis (DBI/LISI): Trajectory DBI=3.09/LISI=1.70; Insight DBI=6.50/LISI=4.00, quantitatively confirming Insight embeddings mix across domains. Negative transfer observed on Aider-Polyglot for Trajectory (-1.0% vs. zero-shot). Baselines are current (2024–2026); all benchmarks are external and not constructed by the authors. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The systematic investigation of cross-domain memory transferability in coding agents is novel; the abstraction-transfer correlation, the counter-intuitive finding that simple retrieval beats complex retrieval for agentic memory, and the pool-size/domain-count scaling results are genuinely new empirical contributions. The memory generation machinery (trajectory extraction, LLM-based summarization) draws on prior work (ReasoningBank, AWM, Dynamic Cheatsheet); what is new is the multi-domain pooling design, leave-one-benchmark-out evaluation, and the abstraction-level analysis. The formal model in Appendix C is a new formalization but is post-hoc rather than predictive. Rigor & Reproducibility Three models tested (GPT-5-mini, DeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3-Coder-480B); Pass@3 over 3 independent runs is used for primary comparisons. Ablations are present and targeted: memory format (Table 1), self-evolving baselines (Table 2), abstraction isolation within format (Table 4), retrieval methods (Table 7), cross-model transfer (Table 6), pool size and domain count scaling (Figure 6). Error bars and significance tests are not reported — 100-task samples per benchmark create fragile estimates, especially on ReplicationBench (zero-shot Pass@3 = 0.111). A project page is listed (https://memorytransfer.github.io/) but no explicit code or memory dump release is stated in the paper. Methodological concern: GPT-5-mini generates all memories and serves as the primary test agent, creating a self-referential structure; cross-model experiments partially mitigate this but the primary table is still circular. The formal proposition (Appendix C) assumes embeddings decompose cleanly into domain-invariant and domain-specific components with bounded norms — neither assumption is validated on the actual embedding data. Deployability EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Memory generation and retrieval rely on GPT-5-mini API and text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI) — no special hardware required. - Efficient: Retrieval of top-3 from 431 memories via cosine similarity adds negligible inference overhead; the offline trajectory generation cost (running the agent on 5 source benchmarks) is not quantified, which matters when comparing against AgentKB's claimed inefficiency. - Effective: Average +3.7% Pass@3 is modest but consistent across 3 model families and most benchmarks; qualitative analysis (Figure 3, Tables 3 and 5) supports the meta-knowledge mechanism. - Scalable: Performance improves monotonically with pool size up to full (431 memories) and number of domains up to 9 (Figure 6), but these tests stay within the 6-benchmark experimental setup — 10×–100× scale is not tested. The paper delivers experimental findings and analysis; no software library, CLI tool, trained model, or memory dataset is released. Strengths - A small abstract memory pool (431 Insight memories) outperforms a large same-domain pool (5,899 AgentKB memories) — this is a non-obvious and practically important result that challenges the scaling-by-accumulation intuition in self-evolving agent design. - The leave-one-domain-out evaluation design cleanly measures genuine cross-domain transfer, avoiding benchmark contamination that plagues same-domain self-evolving baselines. - The controlled within-format ablation (task-specific vs. task-agnostic Insights, Table 4) isolates the effect of abstraction independently of memory format, providing clean evidence for the core claim. - Cross-model transfer experiments confirm meta-knowledge is not model-specific, broadening the framework's practical applicability. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly address negative transfer risk (Section 4.4): domain-mismatched anchoring, false validation confidence, and misapplied best-practice transfer are identified with case studies. They acknowledge that cross-model memories underperform self-generated memories (Finding 6) and that static retrieval methods don't generalize to dynamic agentic settings (Finding 7), calling for step-wise retrieval and domain-routing research. This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The self-referential loop (GPT-5-mini generates memories for GPT-5-mini) is not fully broken by cross-model experiments because the cross-model results are secondary, and the primary tables drive the main claims. - With 100 tasks per benchmark and no variance estimates, a +1.0% to +2.0% delta is statistically fragile — the differences could plausibly fall within noise on some benchmarks. - The offline trajectory generation cost (running the agent on 5 source benchmarks to populate the memory pool) is unreported, making the efficiency comparison with AgentKB incomplete. - All benchmarks are coding tasks; no negative control from a truly unrelated domain (e.g., pure mathematical reasoning) tests the limits of coding-specific transferability. - The formal abstraction-transfer theorem in Appendix C makes two strong, unverified assumptions about embedding geometry; it provides rhetorical rather than predictive support. Bottom Line A practitioner building self-evolving coding agents gains three immediately actionable, empirically grounded design rules from this work — use abstract Insight-level memory, pool across multiple coding domains, and prefer simple embedding retrieval over complex reranking — making it worth reading despite modest gain magnitudes and the self-referential experimental design.
Recommended 2604.14004 PDF
#110 — Innovative (62)

CocoaBench: Evaluating Unified Digital Agents in the Wild (29 ▲ )

CocoaBench Team, Shibo Hao, Zhining Zhang, Zhiqi Liang, Tianyang Liu, Yuheng Zha, Qiyue Gao, Jixuan Chen, Zilong Wang, Zhoujun Cheng, Haoxiang Zhang, Junli Wang, Hexi Jin, Boyuan Zheng, Kun Zhou, Yu Wang, Feng Yao, Licheng Liu, Yijiang Li, Zhifei Li, Zhengtao Han, Pracha Promthaw, Tommaso Cerruti, Xiaohan Fu, Ziqiao Ma, Jingbo Shang, Lianhui Qin, Julian McAuley, Eric P. Xing, Zhengzhong Liu, Rupesh Kumar Srivastava, Zhiting Hu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (70) — open-sourced benchmark, scaffold, taxonomy at modest cost. Novelty (65) — composition-required, infrastructure-agnostic spec is a meaningful design contribution though components are familiar. Rigor (55) weakened by 153-task scale, single-run results, LLM-judge that is also an evaluated model, and no validation of proxy verifiers. Completeness (55) and Resilience (55) — domain breakdowns lack power and verifier validity is assumed. Non-Obv (60) — coding-scaffold dominance is an interesting empirical signal. Weighted ≈ 0.15*65+0.15*55+0.10*60+0.15*55+0.15*55+0.30*70 = 60.5 ≈ 61.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CocoaBench, a 153-task benchmark for evaluating unified digital agents that must flexibly compose vision, search, and coding capabilities, paired with Cocoa-Agent, a lightweight ReAct scaffold for controlled backbone comparisons. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks test capabilities in isolation (SWE-bench = coding only, OSWorld = GUI only, BrowseComp = search only), leaving no standard way to measure whether agents can compose all three in open-ended real-world tasks — the gap that actually matters for general-purpose digital assistants. - How it does it: Each task is specified only by a natural-language instruction and an automatic evaluation function over final outputs (no fixed runtime, no predefined toolset), so agents must reason about which tools to use and in what order, while evaluation remains scalable and reproducible without LLM judges. - The good: The finding that coding-oriented scaffolds (Codex, Claude Code) generalize far beyond software engineering to achieve top performance on CocoaBench is concrete and non-obvious, directly supported by tool-call distribution data showing GPT-5.4 directing 64.0% of calls to coding tools. - The missing: With only 153 tasks across 9 domains, the benchmark is too small to detect domain-level performance differences reliably, and no inter-annotator agreement or adversarial robustness check is reported for the evaluation functions themselves. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating general-purpose agents — the structured failure taxonomy (9 subcategories across 712 trajectories) and the scaffold-vs-backbone decomposition provide rare diagnostic depth absent from most leaderboard papers. Problem & Motivation: Current LLM-based agent benchmarks each test a narrow slice of capability: SWE-bench Pro focuses on repository-level code repair, OSWorld on VM-based GUI control, BrowseComp on web search. None requires an agent to plan across all three modalities within the same task. Practitioners deploying digital assistants for everyday professional tasks (shopping, research, scheduling, analysis) face exactly this composition requirement, yet no benchmark measures it. The additional gap is evaluation scalability: benchmarks like GDPval rely on 70.8% inter-rater human agreement, which does not scale to hundreds of agents. Key Idea: Decompose task specification from infrastructure: define each task only by (instruction, evaluation function over outputs), with no fixed runtime, tool ecosystem, or application scaffold. This makes the benchmark agent-agnostic while keeping evaluation automatic and reproducible. The implicit bet is that proxy outcome verifiers — checking final outputs rather than action sequences — are sufficient to distinguish correct from incorrect execution on tasks complex enough that correct outputs are unlikely without correct execution. Methodology: Task construction followed three criteria: require multiple capability types, pose non-trivial human challenge in realistic settings, and depend only on externally stable resources. 153 tasks were authored across 9 domains (Business, Culture, Education, Life, Logic & Puzzles, Science, Sports, Technology, Travel), peer-reviewed for ambiguity, and piloted on agent systems before finalization. Resource types span websites (32%), images (37.3%), documents (13.1%), and video (5.2%). Capability labels (Vision, Search, Coding) were annotated by human raters; 98% of tasks carry at least two labels. Cocoa-Agent is a ReAct scaffold built on AIO Sandbox (Docker container with browser, shell, filesystem), exposing 39 tools across five categories: browser interaction (17 tools), DOM access (11 tools), file operations (9 tools), shell/code execution (2 tools), and task completion signal. Evaluation uses per-task Python functions that check structured outputs (str, list, dict); for action-dependent tasks, proxy outcome verifiers are designed so that a correct final value strongly implies correct execution. Error classification uses Claude Sonnet 4.6 as an LLM judge, receiving task description, expected answer, run summary, and a truncated action trace (capped at 10^6 characters with per-action-type character budgets). The judge assigns one or more codes from a 9-node taxonomy (E1.1–E3.3). Critical design decisions: (1) outcome-based evaluation without action-sequence verification — justified by proxy-outcome argument but not formally validated; (2) 30-minute wall-clock budget with 50-turn cap — constrains long-horizon task styles; (3) LLM-as-judge for error classification — introduces potential bias since the judge model (Claude Sonnet 4.6) is also one of the evaluated backbones. Key Results: Under existing agent systems, GPT-5.4 via Codex achieves 45.1% success rate on CocoaBench; Claude Sonnet 4.6 via Claude Code also reaches 45.1% (different scaffold). OpenAI Deep Research reaches only 3.3%, suggesting research-oriented agents do not generalize to multi-modal composition tasks. Under the shared Cocoa-Agent scaffold, GPT-5.4 achieves 36.6%, Gemini-3.1-pro 30.7%, Claude Sonnet 4.6 19.6%, Gemini-Flash-3.0 15.7%, Kimi-k2.5 11.8%, and Qwen3.5-397B-A13B 9.8%. Cost per task ranges from $0.50 (Codex/GPT-5.4) to $2.31 (Cocoa-Agent/GPT-5.4); completion time from 380s to 3400s. Codex lies on the Pareto frontier of cost vs. accuracy. Tool analysis under Cocoa-Agent shows code_execute and shell_execute together dominate call volume (code_execute: ~7799 calls, shell_execute: ~4050), with browser_navigate third (~3409). GPT-5.4 allocates 64.0% of calls to coding tools and achieves 36.6% success; Kimi-k2.5 allocates 26.4% to coding and achieves 11.8%. Error analysis over 712 failure trajectories (918 total attempts) shows Reasoning & Planning failures (E1) account for 54% of all failure mentions, Visual Grounding (E3) 29%, and Tool & Execution (E2) 17%. Dominant subcategories: E1.1 Incorrect Reasoning (25%), E1.2 Imprecision (15.5%), E1.3 Format Error (13.6%), E3.1 Visual Detail (15.8%). Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The novel contribution is the benchmark design principle (outcome-based, infrastructure-agnostic, multi-capability composition required) and the accompanying Cocoa-Agent scaffold plus failure taxonomy. The individual components — ReAct scaffolding, Docker sandbox, LLM-as-judge error classification — are all known; the contribution is their integration in service of a new evaluation regime and the empirical findings the regime enables. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper open-sources all 153 task instructions, evaluation scripts, and the full Cocoa-Agent implementation at cocoabench.github.io. Peer review of tasks before inclusion and pilot testing to remove persistently ambiguous tasks are reported. No ablations of the evaluation function design are provided — the proxy-outcome verifier assumption is stated but not validated against human ground truth on a held-out sample. No inter-annotator agreement is reported for the capability labels (Vision/Search/Coding) or the LLM-judge error classifications. The LLM judge (Claude Sonnet 4.6) is also an evaluated backbone, creating a potential conflict: the judge's error classifications of its own failures may be systematically different from its classifications of GPT failures. No multiple-run variance or confidence intervals are reported for the success rates; with 153 tasks and pass/fail scoring, a 2-3 task difference corresponds to ~1.3–2.0 percentage points, making fine-grained comparisons unreliable. Baselines (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro) are current frontier models as of early 2026. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — Cocoa-Agent is a built tool evaluated by deployment on benchmark tasks. - Practical: Cocoa-Agent runs inside a single Docker container with standard browser, shell, and code execution tooling; any cloud VM with Docker support suffices. - Efficient: Average cost per task is $0.50–$2.31 depending on backbone/scaffold, and average wall-clock time is 380–3400 seconds per task; at $2.31/task × 153 tasks, a full benchmark run costs under $354, which is accessible for research use. - Effective: The benchmark reveals genuine capability gaps — best system at 45.1% vs. near-random for weak open-source models — but whether the proxy outcome verifiers correctly distinguish agent skill from lucky guessing on specific tasks is not validated. - Scalable: Task count (153) is small; scaling to thousands of tasks with the same human-authoring and peer-review pipeline would be expensive, and the paper does not address automated task generation. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark tasks + evaluation scripts + Cocoa-Agent scaffold) at the project page. Strengths: - The infrastructure-agnostic task specification (instruction + evaluation function, no fixed runtime) is the right design choice and distinguishes CocoaBench from benchmarks that measure scaffold quality as much as model quality; the controlled Cocoa-Agent experiments directly exploit this. - The finding that coding-oriented scaffolds outperform GUI-first and research-first agents on a benchmark explicitly requiring all three capabilities is a substantive empirical result, not a foregone conclusion. - The 9-node failure taxonomy, applied to 712 trajectories with concrete annotated examples, provides more actionable diagnostic information than a simple leaderboard; the distinction between E1.1 goal displacement vs. E1.2 imprecision, and between E2.1 infinite loops vs. E2.3 context truncation, is practically useful for targeted improvement. - Open-sourcing the full task set, evaluation scripts, and scaffold enables reproducible follow-up research, including RL-based training, which the authors explicitly anticipate. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that benchmark task design requires dependencies on externally stable resources and note efforts to maximize stability, but do not quantify link rot risk. They note that Cocoa-Agent is intentionally lightweight and not the strongest-performing scaffold. They do not explicitly acknowledge the LLM-judge conflict-of-interest, the small task count, or the absence of validation for proxy outcome verifiers. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Proxy outcome verifier validity is assumed, not measured — a 10% sample with human ground truth labels would strengthen the evaluation framework's credibility. (2) LLM-as-judge bias: Claude Sonnet 4.6 classifying its own failure modes introduces circular evaluation; an independent judge or inter-annotator check between two different LLMs would be more credible. (3) 153 tasks is too small to support domain-level breakdowns with statistical confidence; the paper presents domain distributions but does not compare per-domain success rates. (4) No multi-run variance is reported — a single run per agent/task means reported percentages carry unknown standard error (rough estimate: ±4% at 153 tasks for a 50% base rate). (5) The evaluation budget (30 min, 50 turns) is fixed across all tasks regardless of complexity; tasks that require more interaction are systematically penalized, but this design choice is not analyzed. Bottom Line: CocoaBench is a timely and well-executed benchmark that fills a real gap — no prior benchmark requires composing vision, search, and coding in an infrastructure-agnostic setting — and the failure taxonomy with trajectory evidence makes it more actionable than a raw leaderboard, so researchers building or diagnosing general-purpose agents should read it, though the small task count and unvalidated proxy evaluators warrant caution before treating individual rankings as definitive.
Recommended 2604.11201 PDF
#111 — Innovative (60)

FlexVector: A SpMM Vector Processor with Flexible VRF for GCNs on Varying-Sparsity Graphs

Bohan Li, Shengmin Li, Xinyu Shi, Enyi Yao, Francky Catthoor, Simei Yang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (70) — software-managed flexible VRF with fixed/dynamic regions and double-VRF prefetching is a genuine architectural contribution. Rigor (60) — clean incremental ablation and re-implemented GROW-like baseline, but no RTL/simulator release, single-layer eval only, preprint without peer review, and in-house Python simulator not independently validated against RTL. Non-Obviousness (65). Completeness (55) — no comparison against heterogeneous-engine baselines (HyGCN, GraphACT, SGCN); no end-to-end multi-layer GCN timing; preprocessing overhead unquantified. Resilience (55) — unified-engine constraint not justified against heterogeneous baselines. Impact (55) — no artifact released; inference-only; largest graph is 716K nodes. Weighted (systems): 0.15*70+0.15*60+0.10*65+0.15*55+0.15*55+0.30*55 ≈ 59.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FlexVector, a hardware-software co-designed vector processor for GCN inference that uses software-managed flexible vector register files (VRFs) and a hierarchical dataflow to accelerate sparse-dense matrix multiplication (SpMM) under power-law graph sparsity. - Why it's important: Power-law degree distributions in real graphs cause irregular memory access patterns that force existing GCN accelerators to use large on-chip caches (e.g., 512 KB in GROW), incurring prohibitive area and energy costs that limit deployment in resource-constrained settings. - How it does it: Irregular dense-row accesses are pushed from the DRAM–cache boundary down to the buffer–VRF boundary by partitioning each VRF into a software-managed fixed region (high-reuse rows) and a dynamic region (low-reuse rows with double-VRF prefetching), coordinated by a coarse-grained ISA operating at sparse-row × dense-submatrix granularity. - The good: On five real GCN datasets synthesized at 28 nm, FlexVector achieves 3.78× speedup and 40.5% energy reduction over a carefully matched GROW-equivalent baseline using only 2 KB Dense Buffer (vs. 512 KB in original GROW), with comparable area (+4.7%). - The missing: The evaluation compares exclusively against one family of prior work (GROW-based unified engines); heterogeneous-engine baselines (HyGCN, GraphACT, SGCN) are not benchmarked, and no multi-layer or end-to-end GCN timing is reported. - Read it or not: Computer architecture researchers working on GNN/sparse-compute accelerators should read this for the flexible VRF mechanism and hierarchical dataflow analysis; those focused on algorithmic or software-level GNN optimization will find little applicable content. Problem & Motivation GCN inference alternates between a sparse aggregation phase (SpMM with the adjacency matrix) and a dense combination phase (matrix multiply with a weight matrix). Real-world graphs follow power-law degree distributions: a few super-nodes have very high connectivity while most nodes have very few neighbors. This creates two competing pressures on on-chip memory. First, super-nodes require large buffers to avoid costly spill-and-fill DRAM traffic; second, sparse nodes underutilize those same buffers, wasting energy and area. Existing unified-engine accelerators (GCNAX, GROW) address this with large caches or scratchpads (up to 512 KB) that cache high-degree node (HDN) feature rows. This approach achieves good performance at large buffer sizes but incurs high energy costs dominated by SRAM access, and its efficiency degrades sharply as buffer size shrinks. The gap is: no prior design handles irregular access efficiently at the register-file level, eliminating the need for large caches while maintaining high lane utilization. Key Idea The central insight is to shift irregular memory access management from the DRAM–buffer boundary (where prior work handles it via software-controlled caches) to the buffer–VRF boundary, using software-managed flexible VRFs that assign frequently accessed dense rows to a fixed register region and infrequently accessed rows to a dynamic region that overlaps data loading with computation via double-VRF prefetching — enabling full-row, lane-parallel SpMM access without multi-banked VRFs or large caches. Methodology FlexVector integrates three tightly coupled components. Hardware: The Vector Execution Unit (VEX) contains multiple parallel compute lanes, each with a dedicated MAC unit, connected to flexible VRFs via configurable pack/unpack networks supporting 8-bit and 32-bit integer execution. The VRF is logically split into a fixed region (top-k high-reuse rows per tile, determined at compile time by Algorithm 2) and a dynamic region (low-reuse rows, operating in double-VRF mode to pipeline data fetch with computation). A CSR Decoder converts sparse adjacency data into broadcast scalars and one-hot row-index bitmaps for high-throughput address generation. On-chip memory uses 2 KB Dense Buffer (with three logical regions: Rows-to-Compute, Result Matrix, Temp Matrix), 256 B Sparse Buffer, and m-way multi-buffering (default m=6) to hide DRAM latency. Total area: 39.43 K μm² at 28 nm, 1 GHz. Off-chip uses HBM1.0 at 128 GB/s. Coarse-grained ISA: Seven instructions (Config, LD_S, LD_D, CAL_IDX, MV_Fixed, MV_Dyn, CMP, ST_D) operate at the granularity of a sparse row × dense submatrix (e.g., 16×16 elements), reducing instruction count by 3–20× relative to per-nonzero fine-grained ISA. Hybrid Graph Preprocessing: Inter-tile edge-cut (via METIS) partitions the adjacency matrix into tiles sized to fit VRF capacity, preserving data locality. Intra-tile vertex-cut (Algorithm 1) splits high-degree vertices whose per-row nonzero count exceeds threshold τ, redistributing nonzeros across synthetic sub-rows to balance workload within tiles. Algorithm 2 computes the optimal fixed-region size k per tile by iteratively adjusting from an initial fraction of the per-row nonzero bound, checking VRF depth constraints, and selecting the k that maximizes cache hit coverage. Hierarchical Dataflow: At the buffer–VRF level, row-wise product dataflow broadcasts sparse scalars to all lanes operating on the corresponding dense row, enabling full VRF row access and high lane utilization. At the DRAM–buffer level, inner-product dataflow makes the output tile stationary across m input tiles, enabling multi-buffer overlap of DRAM transfers with SpMM computation. Critical design decisions: (1) Coarse-grained ISA is well-justified by substantial instruction count reduction and scheduling simplification, at the cost of some per-row control flexibility. (2) Double-VRF partitioning (splitting depth D into two regions of D/2 rather than increasing total VRF capacity) is well-justified as it delivers pipelining benefit with negligible area overhead. (3) The choice of τ as a compile-time preprocessing parameter rather than a runtime bound is partially justified by ablation but introduces sensitivity to dataset-specific sparsity not fully characterized. Key Results Evaluation uses five GCN datasets: Cora (2,708 nodes, 5,429 edges), CiteSeer (3,327 nodes, 4,732 edges), Pubmed (19,717 nodes, 44,338 edges), Reddit (232,965 nodes, 11.6M edges), Yelp (716,847 nodes, 13.9M edges). Default tile size 16×16, VRF 128-bit rows, depth 12 (6×2 double-VRF), m=6 multi-buffering. Vs. GROW-like (2 KB Dense Buffer, 256 B Sparse Buffer, m=6): 3.78× geometric mean speedup across five datasets, 40.5% lower energy, 4.7% higher area. The speedup decomposes as: multi-buffering alone 3.34×, double-VRF adds 5.1%, vertex-cut adds 0.3%, flexible-k fixed region adds 7.4%. Vs. GROW-like† (512 KB Dense Buffer, 12 KB Sparse Buffer, m=2273 — original GROW configuration): FlexVector is 1.54× slower in latency but uses >50× less area and achieves 7.2× lower energy, since the large-buffer baseline becomes dominated by SRAM leakage and access energy. VRF sensitivity: Optimal VLEN=512 bits for balanced PPA; performance degrades beyond 512 bits due to memory-bandwidth saturation. VRF depth D=8×2 or D=16×2 at VLEN=512 achieves 6.77–7.26× speedup and 49.7–50.0% energy reduction over the 64-bit D=6×2 baseline. Algorithm 2 achieves within 2% of the best static k across all tested VRF configurations and datasets, validating the adaptive selection strategy. DRAM access counts are reduced 3.0–8.6× versus GROW-like at the same buffer factor m; miss counts are 3.79–27.53× higher in FlexVector without fixed VRF region (k=0) than with it, quantifying the fixed-region benefit. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The software-managed flexible VRF mechanism — partitioning register files into fixed and dynamic regions with double-VRF prefetching and a compile-time adaptive selection algorithm — is a genuinely novel architectural contribution distinct from prior cache-centric or scratchpad-centric approaches. The hybrid inter-tile edge-cut + intra-tile vertex-cut preprocessing tailored to VRF capacity constraints is new. The coarse-grained ISA operating at sparse-row granularity is an incremental refinement of row-wise dataflow ideas established in GROW. The overall contribution is not a straightforward combination of prior ideas applied to a new setting; the VRF-centric handling of SpMM irregularity at the register level is the substantive architectural novelty. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough (Fig. 10): each component is incrementally added and both speedup and energy are measured, which correctly separates contributions. Sensitivity analyses cover buffer size (m=1,6,8,2273), VRF length (64–2048 bits), and VRF depth (D∈{6×2, 8×2, 16×2, 32×2}). RTL is implemented in SystemVerilog and synthesized with Synopsys Design Compiler at a 28 nm standard cell library; this is a credible methodology for academic hardware papers. The baseline is re-implemented (GROW-like) rather than taken from prior publications, enabling fair process-node and methodology control — a positive methodological choice. No error bars or multi-seed results are reported (not applicable for deterministic RTL simulation). No code, RTL, or simulator is publicly released; no repository URL is mentioned. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.10113v1) without peer review. One concern: the in-house Python instruction-driven simulator used for PPA evaluation is not independently validated against the RTL for all configurations; only that the RTL provides PPA numbers for area/power is stated. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: The design targets a 28 nm ASIC with HBM1.0; both are commercially available process/memory technologies, making fabrication feasible. - Efficient: Total chip area is 39.43 K μm² with only 2 KB Dense Buffer; energy at matched buffer size is 40.5% below GROW-like, primarily by eliminating repeated irregular DRAM accesses at the buffer–VRF interface. - Effective: The system demonstrably reduces SpMM latency and energy on five real GCN datasets under inference; the VRF miss and DRAM access counts confirm the mechanism operates as claimed, not just as a proxy metric. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 716,847 nodes and 13.9M edges (Yelp). No evaluation on billion-edge graphs; the authors acknowledge multi-engine scaling as future work only, and extrapolation beyond tested scale is unverified. The work delivers an RTL implementation (SystemVerilog) and an in-house Python simulator, but neither is released publicly; the artifact stops at paper form for external researchers. Strengths - The flexible VRF mechanism is a genuine architectural insight: by managing cache-like irregular access at the register-file level rather than requiring large SRAM caches, FlexVector achieves energy efficiency gains independent of buffer capacity increases. - The hierarchical dataflow analysis correctly identifies that a single-level row-wise product is insufficient for the DRAM–buffer interface, and the inner-product combination is well-motivated and precisely implemented. - Ablation study is structured to isolate each component's contribution incrementally (multi-buffering → double-VRF → vertex-cut → flexible-k), giving a clear causal account of the 3.78× total speedup. - The re-implemented GROW-like baseline controlled for process node and buffer configuration is methodologically superior to citing vendor or paper-reported numbers and enables honest PPA comparison. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) FlexVector with large buffers (512 KB, m=2273) is 1.54× slower in latency than GROW-like† because the VRF bottleneck at the buffer–VRF interface dominates over DRAM savings; (2) outer-product inter-tile dataflow exploration is left for future work; (3) only 8-bit and 32-bit integer execution is currently supported; (4) scaling to multi-engine configurations is deferred. These are substantive, honestly stated limitations, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison against heterogeneous-engine baselines (HyGCN, GraphACT, SGCN) — the paper argues these are a different architectural paradigm, but omitting them leaves the reader unable to judge whether the unified-engine constraint itself is worth the performance cost on certain workloads. - Single-layer evaluation only: GCN inference stacks multiple SpMM+dense-multiply layers; the interaction of preprocessed tiles across layers (where layer outputs become inputs) and end-to-end latency are not evaluated. - Preprocessing overhead (METIS partitioning + vertex-cut) is not quantified in terms of wall-clock cost or energy; for frequently changing graph topologies, this could be significant. - The GROW-like baseline re-implementation, while methodologically sound, may not exactly reproduce GROW's run-ahead and HDN-prefetch mechanisms, so the comparison may slightly favor FlexVector if any GROW optimization is incompletely modeled. - No training support is discussed; the system is inference-only, which limits deployment to static model serving. Bottom Line FlexVector delivers a credible, RTL-validated architectural advance for GCN SpMM acceleration — the flexible VRF mechanism is a concrete and non-obvious contribution that practitioners designing energy-efficient inference hardware for graph neural networks will find directly actionable.
Search Results 2604.10113 PDF
#112 — Innovative (60)

Visual Enhanced Depth Scaling for Multimodal Latent Reasoning

Yudong Han, Yong Wang, Zaiquan Yang, Zhen Qu, Liyuan Pan, Xiangxiang Chu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (65) — gradient dynamics diagnosis is a genuine empirical finding, SCF-VR+RDS combination is new. Rigor (55) — ablations across five backbones but no error bars, single-run, data-budget confound unaddressed, router cost unreported. Non-obviousness (70) — gradient dynamics diagnosis and spatial-coherence fix are principled. Completeness (55) — acknowledges some limits but silent on data-budget comparison. Resilience (55). Impact (65) — code released on GitHub. Weighted ≈ 60.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a unified framework combining a Spatially-Coherent Finer Visual Replay (SCF-VR) module and Routing Depth Scaling (RDS) mechanism to improve multimodal latent reasoning in MLLMs by addressing visual under-optimization and fixed-depth bottlenecks. - Why it's important: Latent reasoning reduces inference latency by ~10× versus explicit Chain-of-Thought, but existing latent approaches suffer from visual feature degradation and inability to allocate more compute to harder tokens, capping their accuracy on vision-intensive tasks. - How it does it: SCF-VR identifies spatially coherent high-attention visual regions at each latent step and uses self-distillation to reinforce fine-grained visual grounding; RDS routes top-scoring tokens through additional transformer block iterations while non-critical tokens skip, enabling per-token adaptive depth. - The good: On 12 benchmarks spanning vision-centric, compositional, and mathematical reasoning, the method achieves best-in-class latent reasoning accuracy (e.g., 76.67% on MMVP, +4.67% over prior best) while maintaining ~0.9s inference latency with only 30K training samples. - The missing: The training set (30K) is 5–15× smaller than competing latent methods (LVR: 470K, Laser: 267K), and no analysis equates performance under matched data budgets — making accuracy comparisons partially confounded by data volume. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on efficient multimodal inference or latent reasoning architectures; the gradient dynamics diagnosis is a concrete, actionable finding that motivates two well-ablated design choices. Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) performing explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning suffer from high inference latency because each reasoning step generates discrete tokens. Latent reasoning addresses this by computing reasoning steps as continuous hidden vectors without emitting tokens, but current latent approaches have two diagnosed failure modes: (1) visual tokens are systematically under-optimized during joint training because language tokens dominate the gradient signal — visual token Frobenius gradient norms are consistently higher and more volatile, indicating they struggle to converge; (2) all tokens receive equal computational depth regardless of difficulty, trapping hard samples in oscillatory optimization while wasting compute on easy ones. These problems specifically hurt performance on vision-intensive tasks requiring fine-grained spatial reasoning. Key Idea: The core insight is that latent reasoning quality is bottlenecked by two orthogonal deficits — visual under-grounding and uniform depth allocation — each fixable independently: re-inject spatially coherent high-attention visual patches as auxiliary visual latents at every reasoning step (SCF-VR), and selectively route high-importance tokens through additional transformer iterations via a lightweight learned router (RDS), while passing low-importance tokens through without extra compute. Methodology: The framework builds on a standard autoregressive MLLM in latent mode with T_r implicit reasoning steps followed by T_a explicit answer tokens. SCF-VR operates at each reasoning step t: it computes mean attention maps across all layers and heads, extracts top-K attended visual token indices (avoiding re-selection via a visited set), finds the densest W×W sub-grid patch in a G×G spatial grid, crops and re-encodes that region through the visual encoder, and aligns a coarse global pooled token b^(t) with the high-fidelity reference u^ref via a token super-resolution network F_SR minimizing reconstruction MSE (L_recon). This self-distillation signal enforces spatially coherent grounding without external annotations. RDS operates within each transformer layer l: a per-layer router F_router computes scalar importance scores s^(l,t) for each token in the sequence; the top-α tokens (with α either fixed or layer-dependent via cosine annealing) undergo d additional depth-scaling steps — repeated attention forward passes restricted to the selected token subset — while remaining tokens retain prior representations. The final hidden state aggregates depth-wise representations with step-aware positional encoding (equation 12). Training combines standard cross-entropy L_CE with λ·(1/T_r)·ΣL_recon, with λ=1.0 for 2B/3B models and λ=0.2 for 7B models. A curriculum schedule first trains with explicit CoT supervision, then progressively replaces explicit steps with latent tokens, one step at a time, so latents gradually internalize chain-of-thought structure. Critical design decisions: (1) keeping original positional encodings during depth scaling rather than re-indexing — justified by ablation showing −0.7% if re-indexed; (2) using 32-patch discrete selection over soft-mix aggregation — justified by ablation (+1.2% on M³CoT); (3) fixed α=32 rather than cosine-annealed retention — justified by ablation showing CTA degrades by −0.3%. Key Results: On Table 1 (M³CoT, ScienceQA, GQA with fine-grained CoT training), using Qwen2-VL-7B: 43.4% accuracy on M³CoT vs. 40.8% for the best baseline (IVT-LR), 65.7% on ScienceQA vs. 63.2%, 39.4% on GQA vs. 38.1%, with only 7 average autoregressive steps vs. 10 for IVT-LR and >100 for explicit CoT baselines, at 1.24s average generation time vs. 1.56s. On Table 2 (12 diverse benchmarks, Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbone): 76.67% MMVP (vs. Laser 72.00%, best prior latent), 66.86% SeedBench-2-Plus, 68.63% HallusionBench, 74.21% HR-Bench, 57.96% BLINK, 60.82% MMStar, 69.80% MathVista, 25.89% MathVision, 39.82% MM-Math. The method uses only 30K training samples versus 470K (LVR), 267K (Laser), 200K (Vision-R1). Wall-clock inference: ~0.9s, 3–6× faster than explicit CoT. Ablation (Table 3, Qwen2.5-VL-7B): RDS alone yields +1.8/+1.5/+1.3 on M³CoT/ScienceQA/GQA; adding SCF-VR gives +2.8/+2.5/+2.0 cumulatively. Most substantial gains on MMVP (+4.67%), which requires CLIP-blind fine-grained visual discrimination. Weakest gains on SeedBench-2-Plus, attributed to domain-specific data scarcity. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The gradient dynamics analysis revealing systematic visual token under-optimization during latent training is a novel empirical observation. The SCF-VR mechanism (spatial coherence via self-distillation) and RDS (token-level adaptive depth routing within a fixed MLLM backbone) are technically novel combinations within the latent reasoning paradigm, though each component individually draws on established ideas (attention-guided visual grounding, mixture-of-depths routing). The combination applied to diagnose and fix two specific failure modes in multimodal latent reasoning is the genuine novel contribution, rather than a straightforward application of existing techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present across four backbone architectures (Qwen2-VL-2B/7B, Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, Chameleon-7B) and three benchmarks, with component contributions clearly separated (Table 3). Key hyperparameters (α, W, λ) are individually ablated in Table 5 and Table 6. No error bars or multiple-seed results are reported in any table — all results are single-run. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Simon98-AI/Vedas. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v2, April 16 2026), not peer-reviewed. Training data (OneThinker) is described but not released by this work. A methodological concern: the model is evaluated on benchmarks (M³CoT, ScienceQA, GQA) using training data derived from the same domains, which may inflate relative improvements over baselines trained on different distributions. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on 16 NVIDIA H20 GPUs (96GB VRAM each) for training; inference requires only the LLM backbone since SCF-VR is disabled at test time, making deployment equivalent to the base MLLM. - Efficient: Training overhead relative to base latent MLLM is the self-distillation reconstruction loss during training only; inference adds zero overhead beyond the base latent model's cost (~0.9s wall-clock on Qwen backbone). - Effective: Evidence on 12 benchmarks across three task categories shows consistent accuracy improvements with near-parity inference latency to the fastest latent baseline (IVT-LR), supporting the central claim of accuracy-efficiency improvement. - Scalable: Tested up to 7B-parameter backbones; no experiments at 13B+ or on longer reasoning chains beyond those in OneThinker; the paper claims future scaling to larger architectures but presents no evidence at 10× scale. The work delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) and trained model weights are implied but no explicit model card or HuggingFace release is mentioned. Strengths: - The empirical diagnosis of gradient dynamics (token-wise Frobenius norm tracking across training epochs) is a concrete, data-driven motivation rather than the usual intuitive justification; easy/hard sample divergence in nuclear norm evolution is a genuine insight. - SCF-VR's spatial coherence constraint (finding densest attended patch rather than scattered tokens) is a principled fix to a real failure mode (attention sink noise [62]), and self-distillation avoids the annotation dependency of prior supervised approaches. - Evaluating on five backbone architectures in ablation — including Chameleon-7B (a fundamentally different mixed-modal architecture) — demonstrates genuine generalizability beyond a single model family. - Curriculum latent training that progressively converts explicit CoT steps to latent tokens is a clean training protocol that removes the need for latent-supervision annotations, avoiding human-prior constraints. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Modest gains on SeedBench-2-Plus attributed to domain-specific training data scarcity — the authors directly acknowledge this. - Smaller improvements on GQA because the task is relatively simple and doesn't require complex visual reasoning. - Future work explicitly notes the need to extend to long-term video understanding and larger model architectures, implicitly acknowledging the current scope is limited to static images and ≤7B models. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Data budget confound: comparing to LVR (470K), Laser (267K), Vision-R1 (200K) while using only 30K samples is presented as an advantage, but without matching-data ablations it is unclear how much of the gain is method-driven versus the result of OneThinker's curated data quality. - No error bars or significance tests anywhere in the main results — given the small absolute differences on some benchmarks (e.g., +0.3% on BLINK), single-run numbers are insufficient to establish statistical significance. - The router network F_router is described as "lightweight" but its parameter count, architecture, and training cost are not reported, making it impossible to assess the actual overhead of RDS. - Visual replay is disabled at inference, meaning the SCF-VR module's benefit is entirely a training-time regularization effect; it is not demonstrated that the latent representations it shapes at training time generalize robustly out-of-distribution. - MathVision accuracy of 25.89% is modest, and no analysis explains why mathematical reasoning lags well behind vision-centric tasks despite the method's focus on visual grounding. Bottom Line: A practitioner building efficient multimodal reasoning systems will find the gradient dynamics diagnosis and the two modular fixes (SCF-VR, RDS) immediately applicable to existing latent MLLM pipelines, making this paper worth reading for that audience — though the missing data-budget controls and absence of error bars temper confidence in the magnitude of the reported gains.
Search Results 2604.10500 PDF
#113 — Innovative (60)

Generate, Analyze, and Refine: Training-Free Sound Source Localization via MLLM Meta-Reasoning

Subin Park, Jung Uk Kim · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (55) pulled down by no error bars despite stochastic N=5 voting, single-run numbers, single center frame, and unvalidated threshold choices. Resilience (50) — single-frame design contradicts motivating surveillance/navigation claims and multi-source limited to duets. Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (60) moderate — meta-reasoning framing is a sensible but not surprising recombination of known MLLM prompting patterns. Impact (75) helped by released code and large margin gains on standard benchmarks. Weighted ≈ 60.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 50 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GAR-SSL, a training-free three-stage pipeline (Generation–Analysis–Refinement) that uses Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) via prompt engineering alone to localize sound-emitting objects in images given paired audio. - Why it's important: Existing SSL methods require task-specific contrastive training and fail in complex multi-source acoustic scenes because they treat localization as feature matching rather than semantic reasoning, limiting their generalizability and interpretability. - How it does it: The pipeline chains three prompted MLLM calls: coarse bounding box generation, audio-visual consistency scoring via open-set role tagging and anchor voting (repeated N=5 times for consensus), and adaptive geometric refinement that fires only when consistency falls below threshold. - The good: On MUSIC-Duet, GAR-SSL (N=5) achieves 82.7% CIoU@0.3 and 53.2% AUC, outperforming the prior best (OA-SSL CVPR'25) by 34.9% in CIoU@0.3 and 15.3% in AUC — a large margin without any training. - The missing: All experiments use a single center frame from 3-second clips; temporal audio-visual dynamics are entirely ignored, and there is no evaluation on out-of-distribution or real-world surveillance/navigation scenarios despite these being listed as motivating applications. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in audio-visual learning or prompt-engineering for MLLMs; practitioners needing deployment-grade latency (4 seconds per sample with N=5) or temporal reasoning should temper expectations. Problem & Motivation: Sound Source Localization (SSL) requires identifying which region of an image corresponds to a sound heard in a paired audio signal. This is useful in autonomous navigation, human-robot interaction, and surveillance. Existing approaches — predominantly contrastive dual-stream networks trained end-to-end — align audio and visual embeddings but perform no explicit semantic verification of the match. This breaks down when scenes contain visually plausible but acoustically irrelevant objects (e.g., a drum kit visible but silent while a piano plays), or when multiple sources are active simultaneously. Recent MLLM-assisted SSL works use language models only as auxiliary feature encoders, not as reasoning agents, leaving their cross-modal inference capabilities unexploited. Key Idea: The core insight is to reframe SSL as a multi-step cognitive reasoning task rather than a metric-learning problem: first hypothesize all candidate emitters broadly (Generation), then verify each against both physical plausibility and audio-visual semantic consistency using structured MLLM introspection (Analysis), and finally apply geometry corrections only when verification signals indicate the initial hypothesis was wrong (Refinement). The "meta-reasoning" label refers to using MLLMs to monitor and correct their own spatial predictions — analogous to human metacognition. Methodology: The framework is entirely prompt-engineered; no gradient updates occur. Stage 1 (Generation) issues two independent MLLM calls to Qwen2.5-Omni-7B: one produces an initial bounding box b^init and a natural-language description d from the image-audio pair; the second independently classifies the audio to an open-vocabulary label c_aud with confidence s_aud ∈ [0,1]. Stage 2 (Analysis) receives the Stage 1 outputs and performs two checks: (i) open-set role tagging — the MLLM identifies up to 4 functionally relevant visual sub-parts (e.g., "violin_body", "bow_on_strings") constrained to be visually observable; (ii) anchor voting — up to 5 semantic anchors are scored for how directly they evidence the sound. These produce an Audio-Visual Consistency score S_av ∈ [0,1]. Analysis is repeated N times (N=5 in best configuration) and outputs are aggregated via averaging scores, frequency-based role selection, and majority voting on the binary keep flag k. Adaptive Gating (Stage 2→3 transition) skips refinement when k=1 AND S_av ≥ τ_av AND s_aud ≥ τ_aud (τ_av = τ_aud = 0.5 in best config), preventing unnecessary adjustments. Stage 3 (Refinement) applies one of four geometric operations (Delta/shift, Expand/Shrink, Recenter, or combinations) guided by anchor positions and role tags. Critical design decisions: (1) N=5 repeated Analysis passes — justified empirically but adds 5× MLLM inference cost; (2) adaptive gating — well-motivated to prevent over-refinement, shown in ablation; (3) fixed thresholds τ=0.5 — shown robust in Table S.7 but not theoretically grounded. Key Results: On VGGSound-Single: AP 60.5%, IoU@0.5 60.2%, AUC 55.2% (N=5), compared to OA-SSL CVPR'25 at AP 51.7%, IoU@0.5 47.3%, AUC 44.9% — improvements of 8.5%, 12.8 pp, and 10.1 pp respectively. On MUSIC-Solo: AP 80.6%, IoU@0.5 98.5%, AUC 78.2% vs. OA-SSL's 79.8%, 71.1%, 60.9% — most notably +27.3 pp IoU and +17.3 pp AUC. On VGGSound-Duet (multi-source): CAP 47.2%, CIoU@0.3 77.6%, AUC 45.8% vs. OA-SSL's 45.9%, 55.2%, 44.8% — with the CIoU@0.3 gap of +22.4 pp being the headline multi-source result. On MUSIC-Duet: CAP 56.7%, CIoU@0.3 82.7%, AUC 53.2% vs. OA-SSL's 61.4%, 45.9%, 36.1% — the 34.9 pp CIoU gain is the largest reported. Baselines include strong recent vision-based methods (NoPrior CVPR'24, OA-SSL CVPR'25) and three off-the-shelf MLLM baselines (Qwen2.5-Omni, MiniCPM-o, InteractiveOmni) without structured reasoning. The MLLM baselines are notably weak (e.g., MiniCPM-o CIoU@0.3 = 38.6% on VGGSound-Duet vs. ours 77.6%), demonstrating that the pipeline structure — not raw MLLM capability — drives gains. Scale: standard benchmarks (VGGSound ≈200k clips, MUSIC 448 videos), not stress-scale. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is applying meta-cognitive MLLM reasoning (iterative self-verification and targeted geometric correction) to SSL in a fully training-free way. The individual components — contrastive audio-visual embedding, open-vocabulary classification, prompt chaining — are known; the contribution is their integration into a structured reasoning pipeline that replaces learned similarity with prompted inference. Adaptive gating is a practical mechanism not seen in prior SSL work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: Table 3/4 isolate N iterations (N∈{1,3,5}), Table 5 isolates stage contributions (Stage 1 alone vs. all stages, showing CIoU@0.3 lift of +16.9 pp on VGGSound-Duet from Stages 2+3), Table 6 compares two backbone sizes (3B vs. 7B, with 7B clearly better). Table S.7 tests threshold sensitivity (τ_ac ∈ {0.5, 0.75}, τ_av ∈ {0.5, 0.75}) and shows only ~0.3 pp variation in best metric. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported for any metric, and there is only one experimental run reported per configuration (no multiple seeds). Source code is released at https://github.com/VisualAIKHU/GAR-SSL. This is a preprint (arXiv); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system paper with prompt-engineered pipeline, evaluated on standard benchmarks). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU using the publicly available Qwen2.5-Omni-7B model — commercially feasible hardware. - Efficient: Approximately 4 seconds per sample with N=5 on an RTX 4090; inference cost scales linearly with N and the number of MLLM stages (3 sequential calls plus N-1 repetitions of Stage 2), roughly 7 total MLLM calls at N=5, which is ~7× slower than a single-pass contrastive model. - Effective: Evidence on VGGSound and MUSIC benchmarks shows consistent improvements over trained baselines on localization metrics; the gap is largest on multi-source (duet) settings where semantic reasoning matters most. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 2-source duet scenarios; the paper acknowledges that temporal reasoning is absent and that generalization to complex real-world scenes is future work — scalability to N>2 simultaneous sources or long-form video is a claim without evidence. The paper delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub). Strengths: - Training-free competitive performance against fully supervised methods is a genuine result; the CIoU@0.3 improvements on MUSIC-Duet (+34.9 pp over OA-SSL) and VGGSound-Duet (+22.4 pp) are large and not easily dismissed. - The ablation comparing four prompt methods of increasing complexity (Table S.8/S.9) provides a clean ladder of evidence that each reasoning component (role tags, anchors, AV consistency) contributes incrementally. - Adaptive gating is a principled efficiency mechanism that avoids degrading well-localized predictions, backed by ablation (Stage 2+3 always active vs. gated — the gated version maintains gains while skipping unnecessary computation). - Open-set role tagging provides interpretable intermediate outputs (e.g., "bow_on_strings" for violin), enabling post-hoc debugging absent from black-box contrastive systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note three limitations in Section 4.6: (1) iterative analysis increases inference time; (2) performance depends on the underlying MLLM's capabilities; (3) future work will address temporal reasoning and broader real-world generalization. They confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single-frame assumption: All evaluations use one center frame from a 3-second clip. Sound source localization in practice requires temporal association; a sound may be off-screen or intermittent. This is a fundamental mismatch between the method's design and its claimed application domains (surveillance, navigation). - No error bars: With stochastic MLLM decoding at N=5 passes and majority voting, performance variance across runs is non-trivial and unreported. The claimed gains of 8–35 pp may be robust, but single-run numbers from a probabilistic system are insufficient evidence. - Audio representation: Log-mel spectrograms of the center 3 seconds are fed to the MLLM; no ablation tests whether audio quality or clip length affects performance, nor whether the MLLM actually "hears" the audio vs. relying primarily on visual cues (a known MLLM failure mode). - Inference cost underreported: 4 seconds/sample × test set size is glossed over; for VGGSound's 200k clips this implies ~222 GPU-hours on an RTX 4090 for a single pass, making pipeline-level deployment cost much higher than presented. - Multi-source beyond duets: The multi-source evaluation is strictly 2-source; claims about "complex acoustic scenes" are not validated beyond paired compositions. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on audio-visual grounding or investigating MLLM-based perception pipelines will find concrete, reproducible evidence that structured prompt chaining substantially outperforms both trained contrastive baselines and naive MLLM prompting on standard SSL benchmarks — the training-free framing is genuinely useful, but the single-frame, single-run evaluation and ~7× inference overhead relative to contrastive models are material caveats before deployment.
Search Results 2604.06824 PDF
#114 — Innovative (60)

From Perception to Planning: Evolving Ego-Centric Task-Oriented Spatiotemporal Reasoning via Curriculum Learning

Xiaoda Yang, Yuxiang Liu, Shenzhou Gao, Can Wang, Jingyang Xue, Lixin Yang, Yao Mu, Tao Jin, Shuicheng Yan, Zhimeng Zhang, Zhou Zhao · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (55) pulls the score down — no error bars, no code/weight release, and a real train-test leakage concern since test data comes from the same Agibot-World pipeline as training. Completeness (50) suffers because the evaluation never closes the loop to actual robot task success and there is only a perfunctory limitations section. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are solid: the bidirectional chronological-bias metric and the sequential explicit→implicit→planning curriculum are genuine contributions, and the InternVL 98.8%/1.5% forward/inverse result validates the metric's diagnostic power. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 60.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EgoTSR, a three-stage curriculum learning framework and a 46-million-sample dataset (EgoTSR-Data) that fine-tunes a 7B-parameter VLM to perform egocentric spatiotemporal reasoning — judging which of two frames is closer to task completion — while eliminating chronological ordering bias. - Why it's important: Existing VLMs exploit positional shortcuts (assuming the later frame represents completion) rather than reasoning from visual state, causing systematic failure in embodied robot tasks where state regressions and partial progress are common. - How it does it: A sequential curriculum progresses from explicit CoT supervision (stage 1), to weak-supervision intuitive judgment (stage 2), to long-horizon planning conditioned on a decomposed subtask skeleton (stage 3), enforced by bidirectional evaluation that forces equal accuracy on forward and reversed frame pairs. - The good: EgoTSR-LongTag achieves 92.4% long-horizon accuracy versus 58.2% for GPT-4o and 62.0% for Gemini-2.5-Pro, while compressing the chronological bias gap from 89.5 (stage 1 alone) to 0.1. - The missing: The paper never reports robot task-completion success rates — all evaluation is frame-pair judgment accuracy, leaving the link between better perception scores and actual manipulation performance unmeasured. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers studying temporal reasoning in embodied VLMs or building task-progress estimation components; practitioners seeking a full manipulation policy or end-to-end success metric will not find it here. Problem & Motivation Vision-language models trained on passively collected video data learn a "chronological bias": they implicitly assume the second frame in a presented pair corresponds to greater task completion, because training data is ordered temporally. In egocentric robotic manipulation — tasks like "tidy a cluttered desk" or "prepare a meal" — actions are non-monotonic, include failures, and can regress state. A VLM that exploits input order rather than visual state content will misjudge completion status and fail as a planning component. Existing spatiotemporal benchmarks (GQA, CLEVR, Assembly101) lack the granularity to test causal dependencies across multi-step manipulation sequences, and prior methods relying on simple data augmentation do not structurally decouple causal reasoning from sequential ordering shortcuts. Key Idea The central insight is that chronological bias can be eliminated by training on bidirectional frame pairs (both (Ia, Ib) and (Ib, Ia) for every sequence) under a curriculum that first teaches explicit spatial reasoning via chain-of-thought, then internalizes it into label-only visual intuition, then consolidates it across multi-subtask logical skeletons — so the model is forced to predict completion from visible state change rather than from input order, at increasing temporal complexity. Methodology EgoTSR-Data (46M samples) is derived from the Agibot-World egocentric robotic video dataset.
