They serve two roles: selecting the best partition among candidates from one algorithm and choosing the best algorithm altogether. Most established indices (Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz, Dunn, DBCV, VIASCKDE) compute compactness and separation at the cluster level using centroid distances, pairwise distances, or density estimates. These assume convex or Gaussian cluster shapes and degrade on clusters with non-convex topology (rings, spirals, interlocked chains), severe class imbalance, internal multi-modality, or noisy boundaries. A few recent indices (DBCV, VIASCKDE) address some of these cases but either require hyperparameters (bandwidth) or handle only one geometric failure mode at a time. The result is that practitioners running DBSCAN or HDBSCAN on spatial or manifold data have no reliable parameter-free CVI that correlates with ground-truth quality. Key Idea: The core insight is that measuring intra-cluster compactness on the full cluster conflates the dense cores with the boundary bridges linking them, making non-convex clusters look poorly compact. By first decomposing each cluster into sub-clusters via MST edge cutting — removing the long inter-modal bridge edges identified by the Three-Sigma rule — the index obtains a more faithful picture of true within-mode density and inter-mode separation structure. Inter-class distance is then measured from the cluster boundary (MST leaf nodes, called the contour) rather than the centroid, making it sensitive to actual boundary proximity rather than center displacement. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, Grouping Partition (Algorithm 1): construct the MST of cluster C (O(N_C^2 d) for dense computation, dominated by MST algorithm T_A). Compute mean μ and standard deviation σ of all edge lengths. For n = 3 down to 1, collect edges ≥ μ + nσ as cutting edges; if any are found, remove them to produce sub-clusters and stop. The adaptive decrement from 3 to 1 prevents over-splitting in compact clusters. Second, GPVI index construction: cluster-level intra-class distance S^C_intra = (d_sub-intra + d_sub-inter) / 2, where d_sub-intra is the size-weighted average of mean within-sub-cluster MST edge lengths and d_sub-inter is the size-weighted average of cutting edge lengths (weights = product of proportional sub-cluster sizes). Cluster-level inter-class distance S_inter(C_i, C_j) = mean of top-K shortest distances from each of C_i's contour samples to C_j, where K = floor(sqrt(n_cont)). Per-cluster gpvi(C_i) = (min_j S_inter(C_i,C_j) − S^Ci_intra) / max(min_j S_inter, S^Ci_intra), mirroring the Silhouette formula. Overall GPVI = size-weighted sum over clusters; range [−1, +1]. Third, GPVI-M replaces cluster-level inter-class distance with sample-level KNN inter-class distance (average of top-sqrt(|C_j|) shortest distances from each sample o to C_j), making it sensitive to local mixing. Critical design decisions: using MST rather than centroid representation is well-motivated by manifold-following properties; the adaptive Three-Sigma multiplier is heuristically motivated but not ablated; contour-based separation is justified by computational savings but the choice of K = sqrt(n_cont) is not thoroughly justified. Key Results: Experiments compare against 9 baselines (COP, SC, CHI, DB, Dunn, S_Dbw, DBCV, DSI, VIASCKDE) across 4 clustering algorithms (DBSCAN, HDBSCAN, KMeans, Agglomerative) on 10 datasets (5 synthetic: circle N=200, chainlink N=1000, moon N=500, 2d4cno4 N=863, donut N=1000; 5 real-world: iris N=150, glass N=214, wine N=178, seed N=210, haberman N=306). Evaluation metric: the internal CVI is used to select the best partition from each algorithm's parameter sweep; ARI and AMI of that selection against ground truth are reported. With DBSCAN/HDBSCAN (Table A1): GPVI-M ranks 1st, GPVI ranks 2nd overall. Both achieve ARI = 1.0 on circle, chainlink, moon; SC scores 0.578/0.146/0.396 on the same. With KMeans (Tables A3/A4): GPVI ranks 1st by AMI, GPVI-M ranks 1st by ARI. With Agglomerative (Tables A5/A6): GPVI-M ranks 1st, GPVI ranks 2nd. On 5 special-property datasets (Table 3): GPVI/GPVI-M achieve best ARI on 4 of 5 (ARI = 1.0 on 3spiral and zelnik4). High-dimensional stress test (Appendix B, Tables B7–B8): synthetic Gaussian/Student-t, d = 50/100/200; GPVI/GPVI-M achieve ARI = 1.000±0.000 (Gaussian) and 0.998–0.999 (Student-t); runtime 4–6 seconds, RSS ~162–168 MB. Real-world high-dimensional datasets (arrhythmia N=452 d=262; water-treatment N=496 d=533): ARI ≈ 0.131 and 0.004 — both low and comparable to all baselines, reflecting intrinsic difficulty. Baselines include DBCV and VIASCKDE (2022), which are current and strong for density-based settings. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The grouping partition strategy — using MST edge cutting to decompose clusters into sub-clusters for finer-grained validity estimation — is the genuine novel contribution. The constituent components (MST, Three-Sigma anomaly detection, Silhouette-style ratio, KNN inter-class distances) are individually well-known; the novelty lies in their specific integration into a unified parameter-free CVI framework that generalizes across cluster geometries. Rigor & Reproducibility: Source code is publicly available at https://github.com/JinzheLi/GPVI.git. High-dimensional experiments (Appendix B) report mean±std over 5 random seeds, which is a positive. Main experiments (Tables A1–A6) report point estimates with no confidence intervals, since the evaluation over algorithm parameter sweeps is deterministic given the algorithm. No ablation study is provided: the contribution of sub-cluster decomposition is never isolated (e.g., ablating to GPVI without the Three-Sigma split step), so it is unknown whether raw MST edge statistics without sub-clustering would achieve similar results. The adaptive multiplier (3→1) is not ablated. Several synthetic datasets (circle, moon) are author-generated and specifically represent the non-convex failure mode GPVI is designed for — not an independent benchmark. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on standard commodity hardware; ~9–12 seconds and ~170 MB RAM for N≤496, d≤533 in the high-dimensional experiments — feasible for small- to medium-scale use. - Efficient: O(N_C^2 d) per cluster for MST on dense graphs is quadratic in cluster size; the paper discusses sparse approximations but does not implement or benchmark them, so the runtime at N=10,000+ is uncharacterized. - Effective: On non-convex and multi-modal synthetic data, GPVI/GPVI-M consistently recover optimal partitions (ARI = 1.0) where centroid-based baselines fail; on heavily overlapping real-world data (haberman, glass) no index including GPVI succeeds meaningfully. - Scalable: Tested only up to N=2000 (main experiments) and N=496 d=533 (high-dimensional appendix); the paper claims scalability via approximate methods but provides no experiments at 10× or 100× scale — scalability is asserted, not demonstrated. The paper delivers a software artifact (Python code on GitHub) in addition to the index specification. Strengths: - Sub-cluster decomposition via MST cutting is a conceptually clean mechanism that genuinely extends internal CVI capability beyond centroid-based methods to handle multi-modal, non-convex, and imbalanced clusters. - Parameter-free design eliminates a practical barrier: VIASCKDE requires bandwidth selection, yet is the most comparable recent baseline. - Consistent top-2 ranking across four structurally different clustering algorithm families (density-based, hierarchical-density, prototype, agglomerative) indicates the index is not tuned to a specific algorithm's output structure. - The dual-variant design (GPVI for global boundary structure, GPVI-M for local mixing) provides a practical deployment heuristic backed by empirical characterization of when each variant is preferred. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state in the conclusion that systematic sensitivity analysis under controlled noise and outlier injection remains unfinished and is reserved for future work. They also note GPVI and GPVI-M perform poorly on 2d4cno4, wine, and seed under agglomerative clustering without fully explaining why. They flag that scalable approximations (sparse graphs, ANN) are needed but not yet evaluated. These acknowledgments are direct and credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most critical unaddressed gap is the missing ablation: no experiment tests whether the Three-Sigma MST sub-clustering step is actually responsible for the gains, or whether simpler MST-based compactness (without decomposition) would achieve similar results — making the core contribution hard to validate. The dataset selection risk is real: author-generated circle and moon datasets align precisely with the designed failure mode. The main result tables carry no error bars (only point estimates), so statistical significance of ranking differences is unclear. The newest baseline is from 2022; the field has continued advancing and more recent methods are not compared. The evaluation protocol (best partition selection within a single algorithm's sweep) does not test cross-algorithm selection, a more common and harder real-world scenario. Performance on haberman (heavily overlapping, ARI ~ −0.013 for GPVI) is worse than the simplest baselines in some algorithm configurations, which is not addressed. Bottom Line: Practitioners selecting clustering algorithms for non-convex or geometrically complex data will find GPVI/GPVI-M a useful, parameter-free improvement over Silhouette and DBCV, but should treat scalability beyond N=2000 and the absence of ablations as open questions before deploying in production.
Search Results
#139 — Moderately Innovative (55)

NovoExpert-2: State-of-the-Art ADMET Prediction via Gradient-Boosted Trees on MapLight Fingerprints and GIN Embeddings

chemrxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (65) is a relative strength — 5-seed variance reporting, fixed TDC splits, full code/models/predictions released under MIT, and proactive metric correction of prior work demonstrate above-average integrity, though the single-author COI and no within-paper GIN ablation pull it down. Novelty (35) and Non-Obviousness (35) are low — engineering combination of CatBoost, MapLight, GIN, Chemprop with no new algorithm; only 5/22 endpoints exceed SOTA, several by sub-noise margins. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) benefit from honest limitations and disclosed selection bias. Impact (70) is the highest dimension thanks to a usable artifact and reference implementation. Weighted ≈ 55.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NovoExpert-2, a dual-method ADMET prediction system combining a 5-seed CatBoost ensemble on 2,873-dim MapLight+GIN features with Chemprop v2 D-MPNN, achieving new top leaderboard scores on 5 of 22 TDC ADMET benchmark endpoints. - Why it's important: CYP2D6 and CYP3A4 together metabolize the majority of marketed drugs, and even modest AUPRC improvements reduce false-positive flagging in virtual screening, directly cutting downstream in vitro validation costs during lead optimization. - How it does it: Per-endpoint, the stronger of two model families is selected: CatBoost on concatenated fingerprints (Morgan ECFP, Avalon, ErG, RDKit 2D) augmented with 300-dim pretrained GIN embeddings, versus Chemprop v2 D-MPNN trained end-to-end on molecular graphs. - The good: All 5 individual random-seed runs for CYP2D6 Veith independently exceed the prior SOTA of 0.750 AUPRC (range 0.7710–0.7755, σ=0.0020), making the improvement robust to initialization noise. - The missing: No within-paper ablation isolates the GIN embedding contribution from the fingerprint-only baseline; the paper defers to ablations in the MapLight+GNN paper rather than rerunning them on its own feature configuration. - Read it or not: Computational chemists tracking the TDC ADMET leaderboard or using CatBoost+fingerprint pipelines should read it, primarily for the CYP metabolism wins and the honest NovoExpert-1 metric correction; readers seeking algorithmic novelty can skip it. Problem & Motivation Accurate in silico prediction of absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity (ADMET) properties is a persistent bottleneck in drug discovery, where late-stage attrition due to undetected ADMET liabilities is costly. The Therapeutics Data Commons (TDC) ADMET benchmark provides 22 standardized endpoints with fixed train/test splits and per-endpoint evaluation metrics, enabling rigorous cross-method comparison. The leaderboard has been dominated by gradient-boosted tree methods operating on concatenated molecular fingerprints, particularly the MapLight and MapLight+GNN families. A secondary problem addressed is the metric confusion in the author's own prior work (NovoExpert-1), which reported AUROC for CYP2D6 while the TDC leaderboard uses AUPRC — a critical distinction when positive-class prevalence is 16.9%, causing AUROC to be substantially more optimistic than AUPRC. This inflated the prior SOTA claim by a wide margin (0.864 AUROC ≈ 0.64 AUPRC, not above the 0.750 AUPRC leaderboard baseline). Key Idea The single core insight is that molecular phenotypes differ in whether local substructural features or global learned representations are more predictive: CYP metabolism is driven by local binding-pocket substructure patterns well-captured by fixed fingerprints fed to gradient-boosted trees, while drug-induced liver injury (DILI) arises from a heterogeneous multi-mechanism phenotype (reactive metabolites, mitochondrial dysfunction, BSEP inhibition) better captured by end-to-end D-MPNN representations. The dual-method design operationalizes this by evaluating both approaches on every endpoint and selecting the stronger, rather than committing to one universally. Methodology Feature engineering: for each molecule (SMILES input), four fingerprint/descriptor types are computed and concatenated — Morgan ECFP radius-2 (1024 bits), Avalon (1024 bits), ErG 2D pharmacophore (315 dims), RDKit 2D descriptors (~210 dims) — yielding 2,573-dim MapLight features. A 300-dim GIN supervised masking embedding is appended (pretrained GIN from DGL-LifeSci, mean-pooled node-level embeddings), giving a 2,873-dim feature vector. Primary method: one CatBoost model per endpoint using a unified hyperparameter configuration across all 22 tasks (max 2,000 iterations, depth 8, learning rate 0.03, L2=5, border count 254, early stopping 200 rounds, scale_pos_weight = n_neg/n_pos for imbalanced classification). Five seeds (0–4) each receive a separate train/validation split via the TDC API, producing five test predictions; final score is the mean of per-seed test scores (reported alongside σ), with an ensemble score from averaging the five prediction vectors. Secondary method: Chemprop v2 D-MPNN applied to SMILES directly, same 5-seed TDC protocol, evaluated on classification endpoints. Per-endpoint, the method with higher performance is reported. Critical design decisions: (1) unified hyperparameters across 22 endpoints — justified as demonstrating generalization but acknowledged to leave per-task performance on the table; (2) GIN embeddings used as-is from DGL-LifeSci pretrained weights without fine-tuning — this choice is not ablated or justified against a fine-tuned alternative; (3) per-endpoint best-of-two selection — acknowledged to introduce modest test-set selection bias. Key Results NovoExpert-2 exceeds published TDC SOTA on 5 of 22 endpoints (22.7%). Headline results: CYP2D6 Veith AUPRC 0.778 vs. 0.750 (+0.028, CB+MG); CYP3A4 Veith AUPRC 0.916 vs. 0.900 (+0.016, CB+MG; per-seed mean 0.9149±0.0007, ensemble 0.9163); CYP3A4 Substrate Carbonmangels AUROC 0.660 vs. 0.656 (+0.004, CB+MG; 534-example dataset, σ=0.0121, so ensemble averaging over per-seed mean of 0.6487 is critical); Clearance Hepatocyte AZ Spearman 0.511 vs. 0.487 (+0.024, CB+MG; per-seed mean 0.5068±0.0076); DILI AUROC 0.922 vs. 0.916 (+0.006, Chemprop v2; CatBoost ensemble scores 0.9057, Chemprop ensemble 0.9217 on 475 training/96 test examples). On 17 remaining endpoints, NovoExpert-2 is below SOTA; gaps under 0.02 on 8 endpoints, under 0.05 on 13. Largest shortfalls: VDss Lombardo (−0.157 Spearman), Half Life Obach (−0.135 Spearman), Bioavailability Ma (−0.097 AUROC) — all small-dataset (<1,200 examples) regression tasks. Baselines are the TDC public leaderboard values as of April 2026, which include the MapLight+GNN family and are current and strong. Novelty Classification ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method is a competent combination of established components (CatBoost, MapLight fingerprints, pretrained GIN from DGL-LifeSci, Chemprop v2) with no new algorithm. What is genuinely novel is: (a) the empirical demonstration that Chemprop v2 outperforms fingerprint-based methods specifically on DILI but not on CYP tasks, providing an actionable inductive bias for future endpoint-specific model selection; (b) the metric correction and re-evaluation of NovoExpert-1, which is a community service contribution; and (c) a complete 22-endpoint reference implementation with correct per-endpoint metrics. Rigor & Reproducibility Error bars are reported as standard deviation across 5 seeds for all headline results — appropriate given the small per-seed test sets. No significance tests are applied. No ablation within this paper isolates the GIN contribution; the paper cites MapLight+GNN ablations from Notwell and Wood (2023), which may not transfer exactly to this feature configuration. Code, trained CatBoost models (5 seeds × 22 endpoints as .cbm files), prediction files, and a reproduction script (run_benchmark_v2.py) are publicly released on GitHub under MIT license. This is strong by cheminformatics standards. One methodological concern: the author is the founder of NovoQuant Nexus, which commercially develops the NovoExpert suite, and results are self-reported with no independent reproduction. The paper explicitly acknowledges this. The per-endpoint selection between two model families introduces modest test-set selection bias; the DILI case (where the non-primary method wins) is disclosed fully with both methods' numbers. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: CatBoost inference runs on CPU; GIN embedding extraction requires a GPU or CPU forward pass through DGL-LifeSci; Chemprop v2 training uses GPU but is feasible on a single modern GPU — all commercially available. - Efficient: Exact runtime is not reported; CatBoost on 2,873-dim features with 2,000 tree depth-8 iterations is fast at inference; GIN embedding extraction is a one-time cost per molecule. - Effective: The method achieves above-prior-SOTA performance on 5 endpoints using the official fixed test splits with low seed variance on the top results, indicating real signal rather than chance. - Scalable: Tested only at TDC scale (~500–12,000 training compounds per endpoint); no evaluation on virtual library screening at 10⁵–10⁶ molecules is presented or claimed. The paper delivers software artifacts: trained models, prediction files, and a reproduction script. Strengths - Proactive metric correction for NovoExpert-1 (AUROC vs. AUPRC for imbalanced CYP classification), which invalidates the prior SOTA claim and is a rare act of scientific integrity that strengthens the credibility of NovoExpert-2's claims. - The CYP2D6 improvement (all 5 seeds individually above prior SOTA, σ=0.0020) is unusually stable for a benchmark with small test sets, providing strong evidence the result is not noise. - Full 22-endpoint coverage with correct per-endpoint metrics provides a clean, reproducible reference implementation of the MapLight+GIN family that the community can use for calibrated comparison. - The DILI result produces a falsifiable, mechanistically grounded heuristic — end-to-end learned representations outperform fixed fingerprints when the phenotype is driven by global molecular properties rather than local substructures — with empirical evidence to support it. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly enumerate four limitations: (1) per-endpoint model selection between two families introduces modest test-set selection bias, mitigated by reporting both methods' scores and noting the DILI margin exceeds typical noise; (2) unified hyperparameters across all 22 endpoints, which likely leaves performance on the table for small-dataset regression tasks; (3) TDC leaderboard values retrieved April 2026 and subject to change; (4) internal validation only, with independent reproduction needed to strengthen confidence. These are confronted directly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No within-paper ablation isolates the GIN embedding contribution from the fingerprint-only (2,573-dim) baseline; the claimed benefit of GIN features rests entirely on a different paper's ablation in a potentially different experimental setup. - The conflict of interest (author is founder and likely commercial beneficiary of NovoQuant Nexus) is disclosed but substantial; no co-author, external reviewer, or independent reproduction is present. - The CYP3A4 Substrate Carbonmangels win (+0.004 AUROC) is marginal relative to the within-endpoint seed standard deviation (σ=0.0121 per-seed); the ensemble compression reduces this, but the result is the weakest of the five wins. - Chemprop v2 is not evaluated on the CYP metabolism endpoints, leaving open whether per-task hyperparameter-tuned D-MPNN could match or exceed CatBoost there, which would undercut the fingerprint-superiority narrative for those tasks. - All evaluations use TDC's default splits; no scaffold-holdout or time-split evaluation is performed, leaving out-of-distribution generalization to genuinely novel chemical scaffolds untested. Bottom Line Computational chemists who track the TDC leaderboard or use CatBoost+fingerprint pipelines for ADMET prediction should read this for the CYP2D6/CYP3A4 improvements and the important metric correction, but should weight the results accordingly as a single-author preprint from the model's commercial developer with no independent reproduction and no new algorithmic ideas.
Citations
#140 — Moderately Innovative (55)

PRAGMA: Revolut Foundation Model

Maxim Ostroukhov, Ruslan Mikhailov, Vladimir Iashin, Artem Sokolov, Andrei Akshonov, Vitaly Protasov, Dmitrii Beloborodov, Vince Mullin, Roman Yokunda Enzmann, Georgios Kolovos, Jason Renders, Pavel Nesterov, Anton Repushko · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (60) moderate — hierarchical three-encoder composition for banking events is a meaningful adaptation but components are standard. Rigor (35) pulled down sharply by withheld absolute metrics, no error bars/seeds, unverifiable internal baselines, no public code/data/weights, and evaluation only on proprietary Revolut tasks — a vendor self-report. Non-Obviousness (55) reasonable — profile-branch ablation reveals genuine structural insight. Completeness (55) honest AML failure and non-monotonic scaling acknowledgment help, but missing flat-Transformer ablation and no public-benchmark comparison hurt. Resilience (45) claims collapse under reviewer scrutiny absent absolute numbers. Impact (70) a real deployed system at Revolut scale but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*60+0.25*35+0.15*55+0.15*55+0.15*45+0.10*70 = 51, rounded up slightly for scale achievement → 55.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PRAGMA, a family of encoder foundation models (10M–1B parameters) pre-trained with masked modelling on 207B tokens from 26M Revolut user records, designed as a general-purpose representation backbone for multi-source banking event sequences. - Why it's important: Banks and fintechs maintain costly, brittle fleets of task-specific ML pipelines that cannot share statistical strength across use cases; a single transferable backbone that matches or outperforms those pipelines cuts engineering overhead and enables rapid deployment to new tasks. - How it does it: A three-stage hierarchical Transformer — Profile State Encoder, Event Encoder, History Encoder — with a key–value–time tokenization scheme that preserves structured field identity across heterogeneous events, pre-trained with a three-level masking strategy (token, event, and semantic-type level). - The good: PRAGMA-L with LoRA fine-tuning achieves +130.2% relative PR-AUC on credit scoring and +79.4% relative PR-AUC on communication engagement versus task-specific baselines, while using only 2–4% of parameters for adaptation. - The missing: All absolute performance metrics are withheld for commercial reasons; relative improvements without denominator values or error bars make it impossible to assess statistical significance or practical magnitude, reducing this to a vendor self-report. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML engineers at fintechs building multi-task systems; researchers seeking reproducible benchmarks should be aware that no public data, code, weights, or absolute metrics are released. Problem & Motivation: Financial institutions accumulate heterogeneous event streams — card transactions, in-app navigation, communications — alongside static user profile state such as plan tier, balance quantile, and account region. Current practice builds isolated task-specific models per use case (credit scoring, fraud, LTV prediction), requiring hand-crafted feature engineering and preventing statistical strength sharing across tasks. Off-the-shelf LLM tokenization fails for this domain: text serialization of structured records inflates sequence length (every field name and delimiter becomes subword tokens) and destroys numerical magnitude and ordering. Sequential recommender models (SASRec, BERT4Rec) operate on item-like histories and omit profile state; tabular Transformers (TabTransformer, FT-Transformer) handle fixed-schema single rows. The gap: no multi-source encoder backbone that simultaneously handles variable-length heterogeneous events, explicit static profile state, and irregular temporal structure, while transferring across a broad range of discriminative banking tasks. Key Idea: The core insight is a hierarchical three-encoder architecture that decomposes the modelling problem to match the structure of banking data: an Event Encoder independently encodes each event's key–value field structure (enabling variable-length events without padding within a batch), a Profile State Encoder captures static user attributes in a parallel branch, and a History Encoder contextualizes the sequence of per-event summary tokens alongside the profile summary — all tied together with a type-aware key–value–time tokenization that encodes numerical values as percentile buckets, categoricals as single tokens, and text as BPE subwords, preserving field semantics rather than collapsing everything to raw text. Methodology: Tokenization decomposes each event field into three components: a semantic type (key) token from a ~60-token vocabulary, a value token (numerical → percentile bucket; categorical → single token; textual → BPE subword from a ~28k vocabulary), and a temporal coordinate (log-seconds since last event plus cyclical calendar features — hour, day-of-week, day-of-month — embedded via sinusoidal functions). The architecture consists of three stacked bidirectional Transformers. The Profile State Encoder (1/3/9 layers for S/M/L) processes profile key–value pairs with RoPE-encoded life-long event timestamps, producing a [USR] summary embedding. The Event Encoder (5/16/45 layers) processes each event independently, producing a [EVT] token per event. The History Encoder (2/6/18 layers) contextualizes the concatenation [USR]+[EVT]* with RoPE-encoded time-to-last-event, producing final user and event-level embeddings. Pre-training uses a BERT-style MLM objective combining three masking modes: 15% individual token masking, 10% whole-event masking, and 10% semantic-type (key-level) masking where all values for a selected key are masked. The MLM head concatenates the Event Encoder token output, the corresponding History Encoder [EVT] output (cross-event context), and the History Encoder [USR] output (user-level context) — a 3d-dimensional vector projected to d and matched against the embedding table with cross-entropy and label smoothing. Critical design decisions: (1) independent per-event encoding before history contextualization — justified by the variable-length heterogeneous event structure but not ablated against end-to-end flat encoding; (2) sequence packing with a varlen attention kernel to avoid padding overhead, yielding 2–5× throughput improvement; (3) dynamic batching by event count (shard-based) to keep history lengths uniform within a batch. Key Results: Pre-training corpus: 26M pseudonymized user records, 24B events, 207B tokens, 25-month window (2023–2025), 111 countries. PRAGMA-L (1B parameters, LoRA fine-tuning) versus task-specific baselines: Credit scoring +130.2% PR-AUC / +12.4% ROC-AUC; Communication engagement +79.4% PR-AUC / +20.4% ROC-AUC; External fraud +16.7% precision / +64.7% recall; Product recommendation +40.5% mAP; Recurrent transactions +5.8% F1; Lifetime value +1.8% PR-AUC / +2.6% ROC-AUC (Table 2). Scaling (M vs. S, L vs. S): gains are task-dependent; Credit scoring PR-AUC improves +16.3% and +35.2% respectively, but Communication engagement ROC-AUC is non-monotonic (M: −1.8%, L: +0.7%). LoRA vs. full-parameter training from scratch (PRAGMA-M): LoRA matches or exceeds scratch across all tasks — Communication engagement +18.6% PR-AUC, Credit scoring +13.0% PR-AUC (Table 4). Profile state ablation: removing the profile branch costs −31.8% PR-AUC on Credit scoring and −46.8%/−85.6% precision/recall on External fraud, validating the two-branch design (Table 6). Communication engagement uplift (AUUC): PRAGMA-L +163.7% versus internal baseline. Anti-money laundering: −47.1% F0.5 versus network-aware baseline — a clear failure case. Adding frozen Nemotron-1B-v2 text embeddings: +16.1% PR-AUC on Credit scoring, but −6.4% mAP on Product recommendation and +18% training latency. All absolute metric values withheld. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The hierarchical three-encoder architecture combining heterogeneous event encoding with explicit profile state for multi-source banking sequences is genuinely novel in the financial foundation model space. Each individual component — Transformer encoder, MLM pre-training, RoPE, BPE tokenization, LoRA fine-tuning — is standard; the novelty is their composition and adaptation to the structured, variable-length, multi-source event setting at this scale, and the empirical demonstration that a single backbone can replace multiple task-specific production pipelines at a major fintech. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and cover the key architectural choices: profile state contribution (Table 6), LoRA vs. embedding probe across scales (Table 5), scale ablation (Table 3), pre-training vs. from scratch (Table 4), text encoder integration (Table 8), and a negative result for AML (Table 9). However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results appear anywhere. Code, data, and model weights are not released — this is a fully proprietary internal system. The critical reproducibility concern is the withholding of all absolute metrics: relative improvements without denominator values make it impossible to assess whether a +130% improvement corresponds to a meaningful jump (0.40→0.92 PR-AUC) or a low-signal baseline (0.01→0.023). The task-specific baselines are internal Revolut models with no architectural or training detail provided, making it impossible to verify their competitiveness — this is a vendor self-report in the strictest sense. Evaluation is exclusively on internal Revolut tasks; no publicly available financial datasets (e.g., shared transaction benchmarks) are used, precluding third-party replication. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: LoRA fine-tuning runs on standard H100 GPU infrastructure and converges in 12 hours to a few days depending on dataset size; embedding probing runs in minutes. - Efficient: LoRA adds only 2–4% trainable parameters; sequence packing and dynamic batching yield 2–5× throughput improvement over padded baselines; pre-training PRAGMA-L requires 32×H100 for ~2 weeks, which is commercially feasible at Revolut scale but non-trivial for smaller institutions. - Effective: Evidence shows PRAGMA outperforms task-specific models on 5 of 6 evaluated tasks by substantial relative margins, and fails substantially on AML (−47.1%) where cross-record relational structure is required — the task boundary is identified and honestly reported. - Scalable: Tested up to 1B parameters on 26M users with 207B tokens; scaling is task-dependent rather than uniformly monotonic; no experiments beyond the 1B scale are reported or claimed. The work delivers a deployed internal system; no public software artifact is released. Strengths: - The hierarchical three-encoder decomposition is well-motivated by the structural properties of banking event data: independent event encoding naturally handles variable-length events without padding, and the profile branch cleanly separates time-invariant user attributes from behavioral sequences. - Pre-training scale (207B tokens, 26M users across 111 countries, 24B events) is among the largest reported for any structured/tabular event sequence model, lending credibility to the generalization claims. - The three-level masking strategy (token, event, key-level) is a principled adaptation of BERT masking to structured records, training the model to recover both individual values and entire event records from context. - Honest reporting of the AML failure case and non-monotonic scaling behavior adds credibility to the positive results and delineates the model's scope. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that PRAGMA fails on AML because isolated record-level representations miss cross-record relational signals, and identify this as future work. They note that the Nemotron text encoder extension adds ~18% training latency not justified for text-sparse tasks, making it opt-in only. Non-monotonic scaling for Communication Engagement is noted but not fully explained. The commercial sensitivity disclaimer around absolute metrics is stated upfront but not framed as a research limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The absence of absolute metrics is the most serious gap: relative-only reporting is consistent with cherry-picked or weak baselines, and the authors do not acknowledge this as a limitation. The task-specific baseline models are described only as "internal" — no architecture, feature set, or training detail is provided, making baseline strength unverifiable. No comparison against publicly available financial foundation models (nuFormer, TransactionGPT) on shared tasks appears, despite these being positioned as the nearest prior work. The necessity of the three-encoder hierarchy is not ablated against a flat Transformer over all tokens (which would be the natural competitive baseline). No error bars or statistical tests appear in any table despite multiple tasks spanning binary classification and ranking. The context truncation policy (6,500 events max) and its effect on high-activity users' task performance is not studied. The generalizability of the architecture to institutions without Revolut-scale pre-training data is entirely unstated. Bottom Line: A practitioner at a consumer fintech with sufficient scale will find PRAGMA's architecture and training recipe highly instructive, but the absence of absolute metrics, public benchmarks, external comparisons, statistical testing, and any public artifact makes this a vendor technical report rather than a peer-reviewed contribution, and its empirical claims cannot be independently assessed.
Search Results 2604.08649 PDF
#141 — Moderately Innovative (54)

A Differentiable Approach to Task Graph Partitioning: A Case Study in RTL Simulation

tsung-wei-huang.github.io · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (45) is the main drag — single baseline (Verilator default), no comparison against cited METIS/KL/FM, no ablations, no stddev, no accessible code, and the SHA256 55× result driven by a 138→1 mTask collapse suggests a pathological baseline rather than a general gain. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are genuine: GNN+Gumbel-Softmax relaxation with provably acyclic sampling optimizing real execution cost is a principled contribution. Impact (50) limited by 10–25× partitioning overhead, no public repo, and scale untested beyond 10K mTasks.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DiffPart, a differentiable task graph partitioning framework that uses a GNN + Gumbel-Softmax relaxation to optimize macro-task assignments for parallel RTL simulation by backpropagating through real threading execution cost. - Why it's important: Suboptimal task graph partitioning in RTL simulators like Verilator leaves 1.7–6.8× parallel performance unrealized, directly throttling chip verification throughput at industrial scale. - How it does it: Discrete node-to-partition assignments are relaxed into differentiable probability distributions via Gumbel-Softmax, enabling gradient-based optimization of a loss that jointly penalizes cluster cost imbalance and synchronization overhead under real operating conditions. - The good: DiffPart delivers 1.22–55.25× simulation speedup over Verilator's default partitioner across 9 diverse RISC-V benchmarks, and the speedups hold under an alternative dynamic scheduler (Taskflow), confirming scheduler independence. - The missing: Only Verilator's built-in heuristic is used as a baseline; standard graph partitioners (METIS, KL/FM) are cited in the related work but never compared against, leaving the relative merit of the differentiable approach over classical alternatives unevaluated. - Read it or not: EDA engineers working on parallel RTL simulation and researchers in differentiable combinatorial optimization should read it; the absent ablations and single baseline limit confidence in the specific claims, but the idea and results are directionally credible. Problem & Motivation: RTL simulation partitions a design's dataflow DAG into macro-tasks (mTasks) that execute in parallel across CPU threads. Verilator, the dominant open-source RTL simulator, partitions using a greedy critical-path heuristic with hardcoded per-instruction costs derived from Sarkar 1989, optimizing a proxy objective (cut size / path criticality) that ignores actual thread scheduling overhead. The heuristic also requires the user to manually specify thread count and can fail to produce any valid partition under its own constraints (e.g., the FMUL benchmark). Random sampling of alternative partitions across five real designs shows the default leaves 1.7–6.8× performance unrealized. The gap motivates learning partitioners that optimize directly against real execution cost rather than a proxy. Key Idea: The core trick is reformulating discrete graph partitioning as continuous optimization: each node's assignment to a partition (represented by an ancestor node) is expressed as a probability distribution over candidate parents, made differentiable via Gumbel-Softmax, so that backpropagation can directly minimize a loss function measuring real threading cost (cluster imbalance + synchronization count). A separate cycle-free sampling algorithm converts the learned probabilities into a valid acyclic partition at evaluation time. Methodology: DiffPart has three stages. (1) Preprocessing: each node receives a 6-dimensional feature vector (computational cost, forward/reverse critical path delay, fanout, fanin, normalized graph depth), which feeds a 2-layer GCN producing 8-dimensional node embeddings. Features are min-max normalized. (2) Core optimization loop (400 iterations): a 2-layer MLP Scorer takes concatenated embeddings of a node and each candidate parent and outputs a logit for that assignment. Gumbel-Softmax converts logits to differentiable probabilities. The total loss is L = λ_cluster · L̂_cluster + λ_sync · L̂_sync, where L̂_sync = Σ p_ii (expected number of self-representing nodes, i.e., cluster heads), and L̂_cluster = Σ_i (p_ii · A_i^(D))^2, penalizing variance in accumulated cluster costs propagated over graph depth D. λ_sync is scaled with thread count T via a tanh function, reflecting that synchronization cost grows with thread count. Temperature anneals from τ=50 to τ=0.8 at 0.985× per step; AdamW optimizer with lr=0.01. (3) Cycle-free sampling (every 10 backprop steps): nodes are processed in topological order; each is assigned to its highest-probability ancestor cluster that does not introduce a cycle in the evolving cluster dependency graph — guaranteed to terminate because the self-cluster is always a cycle-free fallback. Three critical design decisions: restricting candidates to ancestors enforces acyclicity structurally (well-justified); the adaptive λ_sync(T) schedule handles the parallelism–synchronization tradeoff (plausible but not ablated); fixing 400 iterations regardless of graph size is a pragmatic choice that is neither justified nor ablated. Key Results: Evaluated on 9 RISC-V benchmarks (RocketChip SoC at 81K LOC / 10K mTasks / 32K edges down to SHA256 at 1K LOC / 145 mTasks / 376 edges) on a 20-core Intel i5-13500 with 125 GB RAM. All simulations run for 1M cycles at 16 threads. DiffPart speedup over Verilator default: SHA256 55.25×, RISCVMini 24.49×, GEMM 5.54×, Conv2D 2.86×, RocketCore 2.58×, RocketChip 2.42×, SodorCore 1.87×, FPU 1.22×; FMUL: Verilator fails, DiffPart succeeds (3.34s runtime). The 55.25× SHA256 result coincides with a partition count collapse from 138 mTasks to 1, suggesting Verilator pathologically over-partitions small, shallow designs. For Conv2D at T=8, speedup grows from 4.76× (1M cycles) to 5.32× (100M cycles), consistent with amortized thread-management overhead. Robustness test substituting Taskflow dynamic scheduler yields 2.35× (RocketChip) and 1.6× (Conv2D). Partitioning overhead: DiffPart requires 7.9–78s vs. Verilator's 0.01–3.3s; 59–92% of DiffPart's partitioning budget is consumed by backpropagation. Results averaged over 10 runs; no variance or confidence intervals reported. Baseline is solely Verilator's built-in heuristic — METIS, KL/FM, and Scotch are cited but not evaluated. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the combination of GNN-based graph encoding, Gumbel-Softmax continuous relaxation of the node-to-partition assignment, and a provably acyclicity-preserving topological sampling algorithm, unified to optimize real execution cost end-to-end. Each component individually exists in prior work; their composition for task graph partitioning in EDA with the cycle-free guarantee constitutes a meaningful engineering contribution. The differentiable cost propagation algorithm (Algorithm 2) that computes expected cluster costs over graph depth is a non-trivial design. Rigor & Reproducibility: Results averaged over 10 runs, but no standard deviations are reported, making statistical significance indeterminate. No ablation studies are present: the paper does not isolate the contribution of the GNN vs. simpler features, the effect of λ weighting choices, or whether cycle-free sampling is critical vs. simpler post-processing. The authors state DiffPart will be released as open-source but provide no repository URL. Hyperparameters (k=27, λ_cluster=-1.0, λ_sync bounds, 400 iterations) are stated but not justified or swept. The evaluation uses only Verilator's default partitioner as a baseline despite citing METIS [24] and KL/FM [11, 25] — comparing against these classical methods would validate whether the differentiable approach adds value beyond simply doing more optimization work. The SHA256 extreme speedup warrants scrutiny: a 138→1 mTask collapse is not explained beyond the general claim that DiffPart minimizes threading overhead. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built partitioning tool, evaluated by deployment in a real simulation flow). - Practical: Runs on a commodity Intel i5 workstation with 125 GB RAM using JAX; no specialized hardware required, though the memory requirement for large designs is not characterized. - Efficient: Partitioning takes 7.9–78s vs. Verilator's 0.01–3.3s, a 10–25× overhead; the authors frame this as a one-time compilation cost, which is acceptable for designs simulated repeatedly but not for single-run verification flows. - Effective: Consistently improves simulation runtime across all 9 tested benchmarks and two scheduler backends; the evidence is directionally convincing, though the absence of standard baselines limits attribution. - Scalable: Largest tested design has 10K mTasks and 32K edges; industrial SoCs can have millions of nodes — no scalability evidence beyond the tested range, and the O(|V|²) probability matrix and D-step cost propagation suggest potential quadratic scaling concerns. The work delivers a software artifact (planned open-source release under JAX) but no repository is currently accessible. Strengths: - The cycle-free sampling algorithm (Algorithm 3) is provably correct — by construction, the self-cluster fallback guarantees a valid acyclic partition is always returned — and it accounts for under 10% of total runtime, making it practically efficient. - Directly optimizing against real execution cost (measured via actual simulation timing) rather than proxy objectives is a principled departure from all prior heuristics and is the paper's strongest conceptual contribution. - The adaptive λ_sync(T) formulation that couples the synchronization penalty to thread count is a thoughtful design decision that makes the partitioner intrinsically aware of the execution context. - Handling benchmark failures in Verilator (FMUL) — where the heuristic's hard-coded constraints produce no valid partition — demonstrates a practical robustness advantage over the baseline. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that partitioning time is higher than Verilator's, framing it as a one-time compilation overhead. They note GPU acceleration as future work. They acknowledge DiffPart is a case study in RTL simulation and claim (but do not demonstrate) generalization to other TGP-powered EDA applications. These limitations are stated fairly and not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The single baseline (Verilator default) is the most significant gap. METIS and KL/FM are directly cited and widely available; without comparing against them, the paper cannot claim the differentiable formulation outperforms classical graph partitioning, only that it outperforms one specific simulator's built-in heuristic. - No ablation isolates which design decisions contribute to the gains — GNN vs. flat features, Gumbel-Softmax vs. straight-through estimator, cycle-free sampling vs. rejection sampling, or number of backprop iterations. - The SHA256 55.25× speedup is driven by collapsing 138 mTasks to 1 (near-sequential execution), which suggests Verilator's heuristic is pathologically miscalibrated for small designs; presenting this as a general result is misleading without further analysis. - Tested at a maximum of 10K mTasks; industrial verification at full SoC scale (potentially 100K–1M nodes) is uncharacterized and may expose memory or runtime bottlenecks. - The paper does not compare against a prior differentiable or learning-based partitioner from the same group (RL-based initialization, [38]; GPU-accelerated stochastic partitioner, [6]), making it unclear how DiffPart compares even within the authors' own prior work. Bottom Line: Practitioners running repeated RTL simulations on mid-size Verilator designs will find the 2–6× typical speedup compelling, but the absence of classical graph partitioning baselines and ablation studies means the paper establishes DiffPart as better than one heuristic, not as the right framework for the problem.
Search Results
#142 — Moderately Innovative (54)

ECHO: Efficient Chest X-ray Report Generation with One-step Block Diffusion (14 ▲ )

Lifeng Chen, Tianqi You, Hao Liu, Zhimin Bao, Jile Jiao, Xiao Han, Zhicai Ou, Tao Sun, Xiaofeng Mou, Xiaojie Jin, Yi Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (45) and Resilience (40) are the dominant drags: the evaluation is circular — reference reports are normalized by the authors' own pipeline (which ECHO was trained on) and the RaTEScore metric is modified to zero out negative-finding weights, both of which advantage ECHO and penalize baselines on style rather than clinical content. No radiologist evaluation. AR baselines use greedy decoding while ECHO uses confidence remasking, conflating algorithmic and architectural speedup. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) genuinely good — non-factorized stitched distillation target directly addresses mean-field bias root cause. Completeness (45) — the circularity is not acknowledged.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ECHO, a discrete diffusion vision-language model for chest X-ray report generation that achieves one-step-per-block parallel decoding via a novel Direct Conditional Distillation (DCD) framework, outperforming autoregressive baselines in both clinical accuracy and throughput. - Why it's important: Radiology departments face a chronic bottleneck from sequential AR decoding in report generation systems; faster generation without clinical accuracy degradation directly reduces radiologist workload at scale. - How it does it: DCD constructs a non-factorized distillation target by stitching together high-confidence token distributions from the teacher's multi-step denoising trajectory, encoding joint inter-token dependencies into a single-step supervision signal that bypasses mean-field bias. - The good: ECHO achieves an 8× inference speedup over AR baselines while improving RaTEScore by 64.33% and SemScore by 60.58% over SOTA AR methods across three benchmarks, with the DCD ablation showing ROUGE-L gains of 36% and CIDEr gains of 44% over naive one-step decoding on CheXpert-Plus. - The missing: The evaluation test sets consist of the authors' own normalized reports (positive findings only), which raises a Goodhart's Law concern — the metric adaptation (zeroing out Non-Abnormality weights in RaTEScore) aligns the evaluation to ECHO's training objective rather than providing fully independent clinical validation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on diffusion language model acceleration or medical report generation who need a concrete, reproducible distillation framework that addresses mean-field bias; those primarily interested in clinical deployment should wait for independent clinical validation. Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) for chest X-ray report generation decode tokens sequentially, creating a latency bottleneck at clinical scale. Discrete diffusion language models offer parallel decoding, but their token-factorized (mean-field) parameterization forces multi-step denoising to progressively reduce inter-token incoherence. Compressing denoising to a single step exposes the full mean-field bias — all tokens are predicted simultaneously from a fully masked input, precisely the highest-bias scenario — producing repetitive, incoherent outputs. Prior distillation approaches align token-wise predictions with less-corrupted teacher states but preserve factorized targets, leaving the bias unchecked at one-step inference. Key Idea: The core insight is that one-step distillation fails not because distillation is wrong, but because the distillation target remains factorized. By collecting the teacher's confidence-heuristic remasking trajectory (committing high-confidence tokens at each denoising step) and stitching those per-step distributions into a single joint supervision target, DCD encodes accumulated inter-token dependencies into one forward pass — providing a non-factorized target that the student can match in a single block-level step. Methodology: ECHO trains in three sequential stages. Stage 1: continued autoregressive pre-training (CPT) of Lingshu-7B on a curated bilingual CXR corpus (~260K MIMIC-CXR, ~250K CheXpert-Plus, ~190K ReXGradient, ~30K IU-Xray reports), normalized via a structured Findings–Impression format with explicit negative annotations to mitigate hallucination omission bias. This yields ECHO_AR. Stage 2: Response-Asymmetric Diffusion (RAD) adaptation converts ECHO_AR to ECHO_Base (a block diffusion model) via a single SFT stage rather than prior two-stage approaches. RAD duplicates only the response portion of the training sequence (not vision tokens), using an asymmetric block-causal attention mask, reducing attention FLOPs by ~73% and achieving a 3.61× training speedup. Stage 3: Direct Conditional Distillation (DCD) — Algorithm 1 — iterates two phases: (a) Phase 1 collects the teacher's on-policy multi-step trajectory by running confidence-heuristic remasking, recording per-position distributions and committed token labels; (b) Phase 2 aligns the student's one-step prediction to the stitched joint distribution via forward KL divergence with step-wise position reweighting (later-unmasked tokens receive higher weight to counter mean-field bias magnitude). A cross-entropy auxiliary loss on EOS tokens addresses degenerate repetition loops. Critical design decisions: (1) forward KL (not reverse KL) — reverse KL degrades performance by encouraging mode-seeking behavior that misses coverage of clinical findings; (2) position reweighting proportional to the denoising step at which a token is unmasked — well-justified by the theoretical relationship between mean-field bias magnitude and corruption level; (3) fused block KV cache — eliminates the dedicated KV update pass between blocks, halving forward passes at no FLOP cost (proven in Appendix E). Distillation corpus: 30,000 randomly sampled examples (2.3% of Stage 1 SFT data). Key Results: Evaluated on MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert-Plus, and ReXGradient test sets (2,000 English + 2,000 Chinese samples each). Metrics: ROUGE-L, CIDEr (linguistic quality), RaTEScore and SemScore (clinical fidelity), PPL (structural stability), TPF/TPS (throughput). Against SOTA AR medical VLMs (LLaVA-Med, Lingshu-7B/32B, MedGemma-27B, Hulu-Med-7B/32B): ECHO_blk4 achieves ROUGE-L of 56.14, CIDEr of 4.20, RaTEScore of 57.40, SemScore of 49.57 averaged across benchmarks at 4× TPF and 129 TPS; ECHO_blk8 achieves 8× TPF at 274 TPS with 2–5% quality degradation vs ECHO_Base. The best AR competitor (Lingshu-32B) achieves ROUGE-L 20.40, SemScore 23.31 at 1× TPF — ECHO substantially outperforms, though this gap partly reflects ECHO's training data being the same normalized MIMIC/CheXpert/ReXGradient corpus used for evaluation. Against diffusion distillation baselines sharing the same ECHO_Base and training data (CD4LM, d3LLM, dParallel, T3D): DCD achieves the best quality-speed tradeoff. T3D achieves comparable quality at 2× speedup; dParallel loses 18–32% in CFM at 4.4× speedup; DCD achieves 4× speedup within 1.25% quality degradation. Against proprietary models (Gemini3-Pro, Qwen3VL-Max): ECHO substantially outperforms on all clinical metrics, though both proprietary models were not fine-tuned on CXR data. Ablation (Table 2): CE loss on EOS is the largest single contributor (ROUGE-L +1.70, CIDEr +0.55 on CheXpert-Plus); step-wise weighting adds further gains; reverse KL consistently degrades. Data scale (Fig. 5): quality saturates at ~60 RAD training steps (2.2% of full data); throughput improves through full training, revealing decoupled convergence of semantics and block confidence calibration. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. DCD is claimed as the first one-step distillation framework for discrete diffusion LMs that uses a non-factorized joint supervision target — genuinely novel over prior work (SDTT, dParallel, T3D) which all preserve factorized targets. RAD is an efficiency engineering contribution over prior two-stage AR-to-diffusion conversion (SDAR). The application to CXR report generation and the hallucination mitigation strategy (report normalization + EOS CE loss) are context-specific applications of known ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for all three DCD components (SW, CE, RKL) across three benchmarks. The RAD data scale analysis is thorough. Code and a project page are listed (https://echo-midea-airc.github.io/), though no trained model weights or dataset release is explicitly confirmed in the paper. The paper is an arXiv preprint (April 13, 2026), not peer-reviewed. Methodological concern: evaluation test sets consist of ECHO's own normalized reports (the normalization pipeline rewrites raw reports using BaichuanM2-32B), and the RaTEScore metric is further modified to zero out negative-finding weights — both choices align evaluation favorably to ECHO's training objective. The AR baselines are likely penalized for style mismatch against normalized references rather than clinical content differences. No radiologist human evaluation is included. No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS. - Practical: Training runs on Alibaba PPUs (proprietary hardware not widely available); inference runs on standard GPU hardware with standard transformer operations, deployable on commercially available accelerators. - Efficient: DCD distillation requires only 30,000 examples and ~2.3% of Stage 1 SFT data; Stage 2 RAD reduces training FLOPs by 72.3% vs prior two-stage methods. Inference at ECHO_blk8 achieves 274 TPS vs 23–53 TPS for AR baselines. - Effective: Evidence shows substantial improvement in metric scores on the evaluated benchmarks, but the normalized evaluation protocol and metric modifications make it unclear whether gains reflect genuine clinical accuracy or optimization toward the training distribution. No independent radiologist evaluation. - Scalable: Tested at the 7B parameter scale only. No experiments at larger model scales or with longer reports. Claims of generalizability "beyond CXR reporting" are unsupported by experiments. The system delivers a trained model architecture and a distillation algorithm (Algorithm 1); code is referenced but model weights availability is not confirmed. Strengths: - DCD's key insight — constructing a non-factorized target by stitching on-policy teacher trajectory distributions — directly addresses the root cause of one-step diffusion failure rather than symptom-treating it, and the mechanism is clearly formalized and ablated. - The decoupled convergence finding (Fig. 5b: semantic quality saturates at 2.2% of data while throughput improves throughout) is a practically important and non-obvious empirical result. - The EOS token CE loss as a targeted fix for degenerate repetition in block diffusion is a concrete, reproducible engineering contribution with large measured impact. - Comparisons span three benchmark datasets and three model categories (proprietary general, AR medical, diffusion medical) with multiple speed and quality metrics, providing a reasonably comprehensive evaluation landscape. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that DCD introduces 2–5% quality degradation compared to ECHO_Base at block size 8, and that the approach is demonstrated only for CXR report generation with a stated aspiration to generalize. They acknowledge that RaTEScore only evaluates positive findings in their setting. The authors do not discuss the proprietary training hardware dependency or the potential evaluation circularity introduced by normalizing test references with the same pipeline used in training. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Evaluation circularity: the reference reports in all three benchmarks are normalized using the authors' own pipeline, directly advantaging ECHO (trained on those normalized reports) and disadvantaging all baselines not trained on the same normalized data. This is not addressed as a limitation. (2) No human radiologist evaluation of report quality or clinical utility — the gap between metric improvement and actual diagnostic value is unquantified. (3) All AR baselines use greedy decoding (temperature=0), while ECHO uses confidence-heuristic remasking — the throughput comparison includes this algorithmic difference which is not disentangled from the architectural speedup. (4) No evaluation outside CXR: the claim of applicability to "broader medical multimodal settings" is speculative. (5) The normalization pipeline itself (BaichuanM2-32B rewriting raw reports) is not validated against radiologist-written structured reports, creating an unverified link between the training signal and clinical ground truth. Bottom Line: A practitioner in diffusion LM acceleration or medical report generation gains a concrete, well-ablated distillation framework (DCD) and an efficient AR-to-diffusion conversion method (RAD), but should treat the reported clinical metric improvements with caution until the evaluation protocol circularity is resolved by independent or human evaluation.
Recommended 2604.09450 PDF
#143 — Moderately Innovative (54)

AI That Moves With You: A Review of Interactive Technologies Powered by Large Foundation Models for Mobility Impairment

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile (scoping review). Novelty (65) is reasonable — the FM role taxonomy (reasoner/orchestrator/bridge/accelerator) is a genuine conceptual contribution. Rigor (55) is moderate: PRISMA-ScR followed transparently with full codebook appendix, but no inter-rater reliability statistic and corpus of only 26 papers make category-level percentages fragile. Completeness (55) and Resilience (45) are constrained by the BLV-skewed corpus (14/26) undermining field-level claims about mobility impairment broadly. Impact (50) from a usable design vocabulary but no artifact beyond the codebook. Weighted composite ≈ 54.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a PRISMA-ScR scoping review of 26 papers (2022–2025) that implement large foundation models in interactive assistive technologies for people with mobility impairments, delivering a taxonomy of FM roles, a 20-dimension reproducible codebook, and a forward research agenda for the HCI community. - Why it's important: FM-based assistive prototypes are proliferating across HCI, robotics, and engineering venues with no shared vocabulary, no cross-study comparison framework, and no synthesis of technical and ethical challenges specific to mobility-impaired users, leaving designers without a principled map of the design space. - How it does it: Five bibliographic databases (ACM DL, IEEE Xplore, PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science) were searched with five Boolean string sets; 6,249 records reduced via PRISMA screening to 26 full papers, each coded by three authors on a 20-dimension codebook covering model class, integration strategy, interactive technology type, impairment context, evaluation method, and challenges. - The good: The FM role taxonomy — reasoner (59%), orchestrator (19%), accelerator (11%), planner-actuator bridge (4%) — is grounded in observed pipeline patterns across all 26 systems and provides a concise, cross-domain design vocabulary that has no direct precedent in prior accessibility reviews. - The missing: The corpus of only 26 papers — skewed heavily toward BLV navigation (53.8% of included papers) and almost absent for wheelchair or direct motor-assistance use cases (3.8%) — is too thin a base to support the broad field-level claims the review advances, and no inter-rater reliability coefficient is computed despite multi-author coding. - Read it or not: Worth reading for HCI researchers designing FM-based assistive systems who need a structured starting vocabulary; robotics or rehabilitation engineering practitioners seeking coverage of powered wheelchairs, exoskeletons, or prosthetics will find the corpus disappointingly sparse. Problem & Motivation People with mobility impairments — spanning blind/low-vision (BLV), Deaf/Hard-of-Hearing (DHH), ALS, Parkinson's disease, and direct motor impairments — have historically depended on pre-programmed, modality-rigid assistive technologies with limited adaptability, poor generalization to novel environments, and persistent usability barriers. The rapid emergence since 2022 of large foundation models (LLMs, VLMs, multimodal VLMs) has catalyzed a wave of FM-based accessibility prototypes spanning mobile apps, wearables, conversational agents, and assistive robots. This work is scattered across HCI, robotics, biomedical, and engineering venues, uses incompatible evaluation frameworks, and has not been synthesized. No prior scoping review addresses FM-powered interactive systems specifically for mobility-impaired populations; existing AI-in-accessibility reviews either focus on standalone ML without FM integration or target other disability categories. Key Idea The core organizing insight is to classify FM integration by functional role in the interaction pipeline rather than by model family or application domain: an FM can serve as a reasoner (interpreting structured perception-pipeline outputs to generate task guidance), an orchestrator (routing between subsystems and managing multi-turn dialogue), a planner-actuator bridge (generating structured commands executed by robot controllers), or an accelerator (compressing motor or communication effort for users with severe input limitations). This taxonomy, abstracted from the sensing-reasoning-acting loop observed across all 26 systems, enables comparison across systems that would otherwise appear incommensurable because they target different impairments, models, and device classes. Methodology The review follows PRISMA-ScR (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses — Scoping Review extension). Five databases were queried with five Boolean string sets organized around three concept intersections: large foundation models × interactive technology × mobility impairment, with category-specific strings for assistive/robotic technology, XR interfaces, exoskeletons, and mobile/adaptive interfaces. Search window: January 2020 to May 2025. The pipeline reduced 6,249 records to 26 via: deduplication (→4,130), title/abstract screening by two independent authors (→313), full-text assessment (→23), and snowball reference crawling (+3). Inclusion required an English-language full paper describing a practical interactive system that implements an FM for mobility-impaired users; conceptual frameworks, standalone model evaluations, and non-mobility disability papers were excluded. Each retained paper was coded on a 20-dimension codebook (Table 1) capturing contribution type (RC1), research questions (RC2), findings (RC3), lessons learned (RC4), limitations (RC5), evaluation type/purpose/metrics (EV1–EV6), model class/tools/techniques/integration (LM1–LM5), interactive technology type and modalities (IT1–IT5), impairment type and problem domain (MI1–MI2), and technical/ethical challenges (CH1–CH2). Three authors coded collaboratively with iterative consensus; no formal IRR statistic was computed. Synthesis used mixed narrative-tabular methods; contribution types were classified per Wobbrock and Kientz's HCI research typology. Critical design decisions: (1) functional rather than clinical definition of mobility impairment (BLV included because spatial navigation is a fundamental mobility constraint); (2) deployed system required (not conceptual); (3) no quality exclusion threshold, consistent with scoping review norms. Key Results The corpus of 26 papers published from 2022 to 2025 shows a sharp acceleration: 0 papers in 2020–2021, 2 in 2022, 0 in 2023 (at initial search), then 10 in 2024 (37%) and 14 in 2025 (52%), appearing across 17 venues dominated by ACM CHI and ACM ASSETS. BLV is the dominant impairment group (53.8%, n=14); wheelchair users appear in 3.8% (n=1); motor impairment (including ALS) in 15.4% (n=4); DHH in 7.7% (n=2); chronic/age-related conditions in 19.2% (n=5). Problem domain distribution: information access & comprehension 33.3%, navigation & environment understanding 29.6%, health self-management & coaching 29.6%, physical assistance 7.4%. GPT-4/GPT-4o is the backbone model in 51.9% (n=13) of systems; specialized perception models (SAM, MobileSAM, GSR, YOLO) appear as auxiliary tools in 73.1% (n=19). Prompt engineering is used in 92.3% (n=24) of systems; RAG in 23.1% (n=6); fine-tuning in only 7.7% (n=2). FM integration role: reasoner 59%, orchestrator 19%, accelerator 11%, planner-actuator bridge 4%. Evaluation methods are predominantly formative + system evaluation (46.2% primary); longitudinal or field-deployment studies are rare (19.2% secondary, 80.8% none/less). Participant samples are uniformly small (N typically 5–20), lab-controlled, and unrepresentative of broad mobility-impaired populations. No cross-study baselines exist; no standardized benchmark is used across papers. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The FM role taxonomy (reasoner/orchestrator/planner-actuator bridge/accelerator) and the three-axis design space (large model × interactive technology × mobility impairment) constitute genuine conceptual contributions for organizing a fragmented field. The scoping review methodology is standard and the codebook is competent application of established HCI review practice. What is genuinely novel is the synthesis and forward agenda tailored to the mobility-impaired HCI intersection; what is a standard application is PRISMA-ScR to an accessibility domain. Rigor & Reproducibility PRISMA-ScR flowchart (Figure 1) is complete and transparent. The 20-dimension codebook (Table 1) with all 26 papers fully coded is published in Appendix H; search strings (Appendix A), venue lists (Appendix B), prototype name–paper mappings (Appendix C), research questions (Appendix D), study limitations (Appendix E), I/O modalities (Appendix F), and feedback mechanisms (Appendix G) are all reported — reproducibility is above average for a scoping review. No inter-rater reliability coefficient (Cohen's κ, Krippendorff's α) is computed; the authors acknowledge this and state discrepancies were resolved by three-author consensus, which is a methodological limitation. No meta-analytic quantitative synthesis is performed (appropriate for scoping reviews by design). No software repository or dataset URL is provided for the coded corpus, though the full codebook table is included in the paper. The small corpus (n=26) means that percentages like "7.4% physical assistance" rest on 2 papers, making category-level conclusions statistically fragile. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper is a review study synthesizing empirical and systems papers). - Practical: The reviewed systems run predominantly on commercially available hardware (GPT-4o API, smartphones, off-the-shelf wearables, consumer robots), and the review's findings are directly applicable to practitioners using current-generation tools. - Efficient: The review does not evaluate its own compute cost; among reviewed systems, latency and GPU/cloud cost are identified as the dominant deployment barrier, with several systems (Patrika, DrHouse, LifeInsight, WorldScribe, SpeakFaster, WanderGuide) explicitly reporting latency problems in real-time assistive contexts. - Effective: Most reviewed systems demonstrate technical feasibility in controlled conditions; the review finds that real-world effectiveness evidence is thin — longitudinal or in-the-wild validations are exceptional rather than routine. - Scalable: The corpus does not test claims at scale; participant samples of 5–20 and single-location deployments dominate. The review explicitly identifies absence of population-scale or longitudinal evaluation as a critical gap. The paper delivers a reproducible codebook corpus (Appendix H) and a conceptual design space framework; it does not deliver software, a dataset release, or trained models. Strengths - The FM role taxonomy (reasoner/orchestrator/planner-actuator bridge/accelerator), grounded in a concrete sensing-reasoning-acting pipeline analysis across all 26 systems, fills a genuine conceptual gap and gives interaction designers precise vocabulary that does not appear in prior accessibility reviews. - The functional definition of mobility impairment — explicitly justified by citing evidence that blindness, Parkinson's, and DHH vestibular dysfunction all fundamentally constrain navigation and independent movement — is principled and enables cross-disability synthesis that a narrower clinical definition would foreclose. - The alluvial plot (Figure 5) connecting model class → interactive technology type → impairment → problem domain in a single visualization surfaces non-obvious structural patterns (e.g., conversational agents cluster in health coaching but almost never address physical assistance or navigation) that would be invisible from the individual papers. - The challenge taxonomy is specific and system-grounded: latency, multimodal pipeline fragility, reasoning limits (token window, spatial grounding), data scarcity, privacy/bystander exposure, reliability in safety-critical contexts, equity/bias, and autonomy are each illustrated with concrete system examples rather than stated as generic AI ethics concerns. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront: (1) no IRR coefficient computed; (2) small corpus (n=26) resulting from strict interactive-system inclusion criterion; (3) database emphasis on HCI/CS may under-represent clinical/rehabilitation/robotics venues; (4) title/abstract screening may miss papers where FM integration is described only in the full text; (5) restricting to English-language full papers may omit regional research; (6) the May 2025 window may miss late-breaking indexed preprints. They also note that most reviewed studies use small, non-representative participant samples and short-term lab evaluations, limiting ecological validity. Confronting these limitations explicitly is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The corpus of 26 papers is extremely thin for a field-level claim; 14 of those papers target BLV users, meaning the synthesis of FM roles and design patterns is largely a synthesis of navigation-for-BLV systems. Generalizing the FM role taxonomy to direct physical mobility assistance (wheelchairs, exoskeletons, gait assistance) rests on 2 papers, making the taxonomy's universality untested. - The functional definition of mobility impairment — though intellectually justified — effectively imports a large BLV literature that accessibility surveys have covered elsewhere (Kuriakose et al. 2022, Rossetti de Souza et al. 2024, cited in the paper). The specific value-add over those BLV-focused reviews for readers primarily interested in motor impairment is limited. - The forward agenda calls for standardized benchmarks and multi-stakeholder evaluation frameworks but proposes no specific benchmark design, metric set, or evaluation protocol. The agenda is aspirational rather than actionable — a gap the paper does not acknowledge. - The codebook does not capture training data provenance, demographic representation in fine-tuning datasets, or participant diversity beyond impairment type, which limits the review's ability to assess equity claims beyond acknowledgment of the concern. - Physical assistance for people who use wheelchairs — arguably the most direct form of mobility impairment — appears in a single paper (VoicePilot, n=1 wheelchair-specific paper cited). The review notes but does not adequately theorize why this gap exists, missing an opportunity to distinguish technical barriers from community/funding/safety-regulation barriers. Bottom Line A practitioner or HCI researcher designing FM-based assistive systems gains a useful design vocabulary and structured field map from this review, but should treat the specific percentages and field-level conclusions with caution given a corpus of 26 papers dominated by BLV navigation systems and lacking inter-rater reliability verification.
Citations
#144 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Dual-Pool Token-Budget Routing for Cost-Efficient and Reliable LLM Serving

Xunzhuo Liu, Bowei He, Xue Liu, Andy Luo, Haichen Zhang, Huamin Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Simulation-only evaluation is the dominant weakness — Rigor (50) and Resilience (45) drag the composite down. Novelty (60) modest — pool routing and EMA calibration are individually established; the closed-form cost model is the cleanest original contribution. No head-to-head baseline against SageServe/EWSJF/AlpaServe despite overlapping scope. No released artifact. Composite: 0.15*60+0.15*50+0.10*55+0.15*55+0.15*45+0.30*55 ≈ 52.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
