Notably, W-Mamba alone (Mamba branch without multi-level contrastive learning) underperforms plain BGE fine-tune on MuSiQue (F1=0.4829 vs. 0.4864), indicating the Mamba branch only helps when combined with the training scheme. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The specific combination of a Transformer–Mamba dual-branch encoder with CAGF fusion and a three-level contrastive training scheme (including the slot-level penalty and token-level masked-recovery) for RAG retrieval is novel; individually, Mamba for long-context encoding, slot-based contrastive matching, and masked language model-style contrastive learning are known, but their joint formulation for dense retrieval fine-tuning has not been previously published. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation exists (Table 4) with seven configurations isolating Mamba, CAGF, and each contrastive level — this is a genuine strength. However, error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent throughout. The Chinese Zuo Zongtang dataset is in-house and not publicly released; results on it cannot be independently verified. No code or model weights are released (not mentioned anywhere in the paper). The slot extraction pipeline depends on GLM-4-32B, which is not fully specified for reproducibility. The paper evaluates on the same English benchmark datasets used to construct training data (HotpotQA, MuSiQue, SQuAD), raising a mild Goodhart's Law concern for the English results. The paper is peer-reviewed (accepted March 31, 2026, Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences — not a top-tier NLP venue), providing moderate confidence in the peer-review process. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on two Vgpu-48GB GPUs for training; inference uses the same hardware, and the retrieval encoder is BGE-base scale (~110M parameters), deployable on commodity hardware. - Efficient: Retrieval latency is 37% lower than Longformer on MuSiQue (49.3s vs. 78.3s cumulative), but adding the Mamba branch and CAGF module increases encoder complexity over a plain BGE base — the per-query overhead delta is not quantified. - Effective: Evidence on three English benchmarks and one in-house Chinese dataset shows consistent improvement over the fine-tuned BGE baseline, but gains are small (0.5–1.3 F1 points) and the in-house dataset is unverifiable. - Scalable: Tested only on standard benchmark scale (MuSiQue: 25K questions); no experiments at 10×–100× document corpus scale or with longer individual documents than those in the benchmarks. The paper claims Mamba enables better long-context handling but the length-bucket experiment (Table 7) shows nearly identical Hit@1 and Recall@1 between TM-RAG and plain BGE across short/medium/long buckets. The work delivers a trained model approach but no software artifact, library, or weights. Strengths: - The three-level contrastive learning scheme is technically detailed and internally consistent, with each level addressing a distinct failure mode of coarse embedding models. - The slot-consistency penalty in L_slot is a thoughtful mechanism: rather than treating all hard negatives equally, it down-weights negatives that legitimately share factual anchors with the query, reducing spurious gradient updates. - The inference latency experiment (Table 9) and the long-context encoder replacement experiment (Table 10) are genuine comparative tests that go beyond accuracy metrics to address practical deployment concerns. - The noisy-label and slot-extraction noise sensitivity experiments (Tables 6, 8) provide meaningful robustness characterization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge plans to strengthen the reranking stage and investigate learnable loss-weighting schemes (currently equal weights), suggesting awareness that fixed hyperparameters in the loss are a gap. They do not explicitly acknowledge the modest absolute gains over the fine-tuned BGE baseline, the absence of code release, or the in-house dataset reproducibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The length-bucket results (Table 7) do not support the paper's central claim that Mamba meaningfully helps long-evidence retrieval — Hit@1 differences between TM-RAG and plain BGE are within 0.002 across all length buckets. (2) Ablation shows W-Mamba alone is worse than BGE fine-tune on MuSiQue, yet the paper frames Mamba as a primary contribution; the actual driver of improvement appears to be the multi-level contrastive training, not the architecture. (3) E5-Mistral-7B is included as a baseline but is a 7B-parameter model, an apples-to-oranges comparison against a ~110M BGE-base encoder; no compute-normalized comparison is made. (4) The generation LLM (Qwen2.5-7B) is fixed across all conditions — it is unknown how results would change with a stronger generator, making retrieval-stage gains hard to disentangle from generator sensitivity. (5) No direct retrieval-only evaluation (e.g., MRR, Recall@k as primary metrics) is reported in the main tables; all headline metrics are end-to-end generation metrics, conflating retrieval and generation quality. Bottom Line: TM-RAG offers a technically coherent fine-tuning recipe for long-context dense retrieval, but the empirical case for the Mamba architectural contribution specifically is weak — the multi-level contrastive training scheme is the operative improvement, and practitioners who already fine-tune BGE-scale encoders with contrastive learning will find the incremental gains over that baseline modest and the Chinese dataset results unreproducible.
Citations
#182 — Incremental (38)

Hardware Utilization and Inference Performance of Edge Object Detection Under Fault Injection

Faezeh Pasandideh, Mehdi Azarafza, Achim Rettberg · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (35) and Completeness (35) drive the score down: the 'retention' metric is self-referential (never validated against ground-truth labels), each configuration is single-run, hallucination rates up to 63% are unexplained, and synthetic LDM degradations are not validated against real sensor failure statistics. Novelty (45) is modest — the VisionFault-350K dataset release is the most concrete contribution; the hardware-telemetry characterization is useful but mostly confirms what embedded engineers would expect. Impact (55) reflects the public dataset artifact. Weighted: 0.20*45 + 0.25*35 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*55 = 40.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 40 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper characterizes CPU load, GPU utilization, RAM consumption, power draw, throughput, and thermal behavior of TensorRT-optimized YOLOv10s, YOLOv11s, and YOLO2026v2 pipelines running on NVIDIA Jetson Nano under a large-scale synthetic fault injection campaign spanning 350,751 degraded images and 7,950 fault scenarios. - Why it's important: Autonomous edge systems must maintain hardware stability under sensor degradation, yet no prior work has systematically profiled hardware-level resource behavior — rather than just inference accuracy or timeout signals — during degraded visual input at this scale. - How it does it: A two-phase decoupled framework uses GPT-OSS-120B (via Ollama) to generate fault scenario descriptions and Stable Diffusion 2.1 Img2Img to synthesize degraded images offline, then runs TensorRT inference online on Jetson Nano while collecting per-folder hardware and latency telemetry. - The good: All three models sustain GPU utilization at 20–25%, keep temperatures below 42°C (well under the Jetson Nano throttling threshold), maintain detection retention above 89%, and show near-zero or negative tail-latency deltas across 130,000–212,000 fault-injected images per model. - The missing: The "retention" metric is self-referential — it measures how many base-run detections survive fault injection, never whether base-run detections were correct — so the paper reports hardware stability but provides no ground-truth accuracy evidence; hallucination rates reaching 63.37% in some fault categories are underanalyzed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for embedded systems or edge-AI reliability engineers who need grounded hardware telemetry data for YOLO/TensorRT on Jetson Nano; can be skipped by researchers primarily interested in model accuracy, fault-tolerance algorithms, or broader hardware platforms. Problem & Motivation Edge AI vision systems deployed in autonomous mobile robots must maintain stable inference under realistic sensor degradation — camera failures, motion blur, lighting changes, weather, and lens distortions. Existing fault injection literature focuses on timeout-based or software-level failure signals and treats resource monitoring as a performance optimization tool rather than a reliability indicator. No prior work systematically characterizes hardware-level behavior (power draw, GPU occupancy, RAM delta, thermal flux) of TensorRT inference pipelines when processing degraded visual inputs at scale. Autonomous system engineers therefore lack empirical grounding for whether their edge platforms remain within safe operational bounds under degraded conditions — the gap this paper targets. Key Idea The core insight is to fully decouple fault scenario generation (offline: LLM-driven scenario synthesis + LDM-based image degradation producing 350K images) from hardware characterization (online: TensorRT inference on Jetson Nano with per-folder metric sampling), enabling controlled, repeatable measurement of hardware behavior under synthetic but realistically motivated input degradation without risking physical hardware damage or introducing navigation instability. Methodology Two-phase pipeline. Offline: GPT-OSS-120B (via Ollama) generates 7,950 valid fault scenario descriptions (camera failures, motion blur, extreme weather, low light, lens distortion) from an initial 8,000+ raw LLM outputs after JSON cleaning. Stable Diffusion 2.1 Img2Img applies these as degradation prompts to 1,489 real JetBot RGB camera images (224×224), producing VisionFault-350K (350,751 images in ~1,600+ folders of 129 images each), publicly released on Zenodo. Online: Three TensorRT-compiled YOLO models (YOLOv10s, YOLOv11s, YOLO2026v2; trained 180 epochs on 256×256 images in Google Colab on NVIDIA T4, then exported PyTorch→ONNX→TensorRT) run inference on NVIDIA Jetson Nano (4 GB RAM). Per-folder metrics collected: Power_Delta (W), RAM_Delta (MB), Temp_Delta (°C), GPU_% (core utilization), Retention_% (fraction of base-run detected objects retained), FPS_Delta, p95/p99 tail-latency delta, Jitter_Delta (ms). Three critical design decisions: (1) decoupled perception/navigation architecture isolates fault effects on detection from downstream actuation — well-justified for repeatability but not representative of coupled real-world failure modes; (2) folder-level (129-image) metric aggregation trades temporal granularity for tractability across 200K+ images per run, limiting detection of frame-level transient spikes; (3) synthetic-only image degradation via LDM avoids physical hardware risk but does not formally validate that LDM degradations match real sensor failure statistics. Key Results Hardware metrics across 130K–212K fault-injected images per model per task: Power_Delta ranges +0.04W (YOLOv10s) to +1.41W (YOLO2026v2), with total average power below 5.2W — within Jetson Nano safe operating limits. RAM_Delta is consistently negative (−85 to −218 MB) after the warm-up phase, indicating TensorRT recycles memory buffers; peak RAM stays below 2,993 MB against a 4 GB ceiling. Temp_Delta ranges +2.93°C (YOLOv10s) to +5.94°C (YOLO2026v2); maximum recorded temperature 42°C across all fault categories, below thermal throttling threshold. GPU utilization centers 20–25% across all models and both tasks, confirming hardware-bound (not workload-bound) execution. Detection retention: YOLOv10s 89.28% mean (min 72.09%), YOLOv11s ~93%, YOLO2026v2 92.42%, all on the object-detection task. FPS_Delta is positive across all scenarios (e.g., YOLO2026v2: +1.11 FPS, absolute 39.80 FPS) because TensorRT reaches a steady optimized state after base-load warm-up. Tail latencies show near-zero or negative deltas (YOLO2026v2 p95_Delta −0.89 ms, p99_Delta +0.06 ms), confirming no latency regression under fault load. Jitter: mean absolute 0.55–0.72 ms, essentially unchanged from baseline. Notably, YOLOv10s hallucination rate averages 14.45% with a maximum of 63.37% (std 9.64%), indicating severe detection instability in some fault categories that the paper does not decompose. Baselines are the authors' own JetBot base-inference run on the same hardware; no external benchmarks or alternative platforms are compared. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. VisionFault-350K is a new public dataset for robotic vision fault injection. The empirical finding — that TensorRT YOLO pipelines on Jetson Nano remain hardware-stable (power, thermal, GPU, RAM) under sustained large-scale synthetic fault input — is new at this measurement scope and hardware-metric granularity. The methodology combines GPT prompt generation, Stable Diffusion Img2Img, and TensorRT telemetry collection, all individually well-established; the novelty is the combination applied to this hardware reliability question, not any component technique. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations isolating which fault categories or model architecture components drive variance in hardware metrics. Error bars are reported as standard deviations and min/max ranges, but no statistical significance tests (t-tests, confidence intervals) compare models or fault types. Each model-task combination is evaluated in a single run — no repeated trials or multiple seeds. VisionFault-350K dataset is publicly released on Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18695332); inference code and model weights are not explicitly released. The paper is a preprint (references cite a 2026 AITC poster for the LLM-LDM framework, suggesting this is concurrent or follow-up work). The retention metric is self-referential: it measures fraction of base-run detections preserved under fault injection, not accuracy against annotated ground truth — if the base run has false positives or false negatives, retention figures carry those errors forward silently. Evaluation is conducted entirely on the authors' own platform with their own collected dataset, with no external validation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — measurements on a real deployed robotic system. - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA Jetson Nano (~$99, 4 GB RAM), a commercially available embedded GPU platform widely used in robotics research. - Efficient: All three models consume under 25% GPU occupancy, under 3 GB RAM, and under 5.2W total average power — highly efficient for embedded edge deployment. - Effective: The paper establishes hardware stability (power, thermal, memory, GPU) under fault injection, but does not establish whether detection accuracy is maintained, since retention relative to a base run is a proxy metric rather than ground-truth mAP or IoU. - Scalable: Tested up to 212,205 images per run on a fixed-capacity device; no 10×–100× scaling tested or claimed, and Jetson Nano has no horizontal scaling path evaluated in this work. The paper delivers VisionFault-350K as a public dataset artifact on Zenodo; the inference characterization pipeline is described but not released as a reusable library or tool. Strengths - Scale and scope of empirical characterization: Systematically collecting hardware telemetry (power, RAM, thermal, GPU, latency, jitter) across 350K+ images and three YOLO architectures on the same hardware fills a genuine gap — prior work either measures accuracy under fault injection or hardware metrics under normal conditions, not both together at this scale. - VisionFault-350K: A publicly released, task-specific fault injection dataset derived from real autonomous robot camera recordings, covering diverse degradation categories, providing a concrete reusable artifact for the robotics safety community. - Hardware-first framing: Measuring power and thermal behavior alongside inference performance is the correct lens for safety-critical edge deployment certification, where hardware budget compliance is as important as model accuracy. - Methodological isolation: Decoupling object detection from navigation control during fault injection is a sound choice that enables repeatable, interpretable per-folder measurements without confounding from robot behavior changes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The robot followed a single direction line, limiting viewpoint diversity and preventing analysis of geometric pose variation in detections. Only software-based fault injection (image degradation) was used to avoid permanent hardware damage — real sensor or actuator hardware faults are not tested. Per-fault-category decomposition of hardware metrics is deferred to future work; current results aggregate across all fault types. The authors do not discuss the synthetic-to-real fidelity gap of LDM-generated degradations explicitly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Retention is not accuracy: The paper never evaluates detection correctness against annotated ground truth. Retention ≥89% could reflect retention of false positives as much as true detections; without mAP or IoU against labels, the robustness claim is incomplete. - Single-run evaluation: Each configuration is measured once, so reported variance reflects inter-folder variation within one run, not measurement repeatability — hardware metric distributions could differ across runs. - Hallucination rates are underanalyzed: YOLOv10s hallucinates at up to 63.37% in some fault scenarios with high standard deviation, a safety-critical failure mode that receives no per-category breakdown or root-cause analysis. - No real-fault validation: LDM Img2Img degradations are assumed to represent realistic sensor failures without any formal comparison to statistics of actual camera sensor failure outputs (noise power spectrum, spatial correlation, etc.). - Narrow baseline: Hardware metrics are compared only to the authors' own base-inference run on their own Jetbot platform; no comparison to PyTorch inference, other embedded platforms (Jetson Orin NX, Raspberry Pi 4B), or published edge-AI benchmarks is provided. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying YOLO on Jetson Nano for safety-critical robotics gains well-documented, credible hardware telemetry demonstrating platform stability under synthetic fault load, but must look elsewhere for accuracy-level robustness evidence since this paper characterizes hardware behavior, not detection correctness.
Search Results 2604.09631 PDF
#183 — Incremental (38)

Data Ontologies for Data-Driven Decision-Making

nap.nationalacademies.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (35) low: 9/18 biased pre-selected sample, no quantitative outcomes, no baselines, qualitative self-reports only. Novelty (45) — first systematic DOT-specific roadmap is a genuine gap-filling contribution but no algorithmic content. Completeness (40) and Practical Impact (45) — delivers a guide but no software, ROI, or validation. Authors honestly acknowledge unaddressed ROI gap.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 40 Resilience 35 Impact 45
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a practitioner-oriented conceptual framework (the "DATA Framework") and accompanying guide for state Departments of Transportation (DOTs) to develop and deploy data ontologies that enable cross-system, data-driven decision-making from legacy infrastructure. - Why it's important: State DOTs manage vast, siloed legacy systems with no shared semantics, causing unreliable, ad hoc decision-making and costly knowledge loss as expert staff turn over — ontologies offer a structured path to interoperability but no DOT-specific adoption roadmap existed before this work. - How it does it: The researchers synthesized findings from a targeted literature review, surveys of 9 state DOTs, in-depth follow-up interviews, and 5 case studies into a four-pillar cyclical methodology (Design, Assemble, Test, Adapt) with competency-question-driven guidance at each stage. - The good: The survey and interview data directly ground the framework in real DOT constraints — revealing that FDOT is simultaneously migrating 79 legacy systems, that WSDOT's WebProtégé-based Data Catalog is a replicable model, and that staff turnover is the dominant knowledge-loss driver — making the guidance actionable rather than generic. - The missing: No quantitative ROI evidence is presented for any ontology deployment (the report itself identifies this as a top future research gap), leaving practitioners with no data to justify the resource investment to leadership. - Read it or not: Transportation IT managers and data governance practitioners at state DOTs should read it; computer scientists or knowledge-engineering researchers will find no algorithmic novelty and should consult the referenced academic literature instead. Problem & Motivation: State DOTs accumulate data across dozens of siloed legacy systems — financial, asset management, LRS, construction project management — each designed for a narrow application with no shared semantics. Performance-based management and federal reporting mandates (e.g., ARNOLD) require cross-functional data integration, but DOTs lack both the conceptual vocabulary and the organizational methodology to achieve semantic interoperability. Staff turnover exacerbates the problem: tacit knowledge about legacy systems leaves with departing employees. Existing ontology literature addresses technical construction but not the organizational, cultural, and governance dimensions that determine whether an ontology program succeeds or is abandoned, as happened with WSDOT's PS AID proof-of-concept when dedicated funding was not secured. Key Idea: The core insight is that ontology adoption in public-sector transportation agencies fails not from technical complexity but from neglect of organizational and cultural prerequisites — planning, stakeholder buy-in, workforce capacity, and continuous governance — and that structuring these prerequisites into an explicit, cyclical lifecycle (DATA: Design, Assemble, Test, Adapt) can bridge the gap from proof-of-concept to sustained institutional practice. There is no single algorithmic trick; the contribution is the organizational framing applied to a domain where it was absent. Methodology: The research followed a two-phase approach. Phase I (Tasks 1–5) consisted of a targeted desktop literature scan (academic and professional sources, early 2000s to present), a structured survey questionnaire distributed to 18 pre-selected state DOTs (9 responded: Arkansas, Florida, Indiana, Iowa, Minnesota, Texas, Utah, Vermont, Washington), and follow-up phone/video interviews to clarify responses. Respondents were selected based on known notable practices and panel recommendations, not random sampling. Phase II (Tasks 6–11) synthesized findings into 5 case studies (including Indiana DOT's HCIontology for digital construction inspection, WSDOT's Data Catalog, and Disease Ontology from biomedicine as a transferable model) and developed the DATA Framework with actionable steps, competency questions, and implementation barriers for each pillar. Critical design decisions: (1) targeting 18 DOTs rather than all 50 — justified by the nascent state of ontology practice — but this limits representativeness; (2) drawing case studies from non-transportation domains (biomedical, transit) to fill the gap in transportation examples — a pragmatic choice that weakens direct applicability; (3) structuring guidance around competency questions (user-facing queries the ontology must answer) to keep development user-centered rather than technology-driven — well-justified by ontology engineering literature. Key Results: Of 9 responding DOTs, most reported little to no experience with formal ontologies; one agency (WSDOT) reported proficient experience using Stanford's WebProtégé and the GIST upper ontology. FDOT is actively migrating 79 legacy systems to a centralized cloud database under Florida's Cloud-First policy. ARDOT migrated its LRS to combine local road GIS data with NHS/Interstate GIS data to meet ARNOLD requirements. Indiana DOT's HCIontology (for construction inspection) was validated using HermiT reasoner and SPARQL queries within Protégé, and was integrated into an application that dynamically generates inspection forms — the most technically complete deployment documented. No quantitative performance metrics (time saved, error rates, query latency, ROI) are reported for any deployment. All findings are qualitative and self-reported by agencies. No baselines are compared. Novelty Classification: NEW FRAMEWORK / NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The DATA Framework is a new practitioner methodology tailored to the transportation-agency context. The empirical contribution is the first systematic documentation of the state of ontology practice across U.S. state DOTs. Neither the ontology concepts (classes, relations, triples, Protégé, SPARQL) nor the general lifecycle ideas (plan-build-test-maintain) are new; the novelty is their synthesis and contextualization for an audience — public-sector transportation managers — that had no prior domain-specific roadmap. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are applicable (not a computational study). No error bars, significance tests, or inter-rater reliability measures are reported for the qualitative survey analysis. Survey response rate was 9/18 (50%), but the 18 were pre-selected for known practices, so the sample is biased toward more advanced agencies. Case study selection used explicit criteria (responded to survey, demonstrated legacy migration, expressed interest in ontology, used ontology tools, geographic/size diversity) — a credible approach for purposive sampling, though it precludes generalization. No software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. The companion guide (NCHRP Research Report 1169) is the primary deliverable and is available via National Academies Press. This is a government-funded report (FHWA Agreement No. 693JJ32350025), not peer-reviewed in the journal sense; it underwent NCHRP panel review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (surveys, interviews, practitioner case studies). - Practical: The DATA Framework requires no specialized hardware; adoption demands organizational will, dedicated staff, and tools like WebProtégé or Protégé, which are freely available. - Efficient: No compute cost is involved; the cost is human labor — the report explicitly flags that ontology development is resource-intensive and that WSDOT's PS AID project was halted when dedicated funding was not secured, so resource requirements are real and non-trivial. - Effective: The evidence that the framework solves the stated problem is weak — the most complete deployment (Indiana HCIontology) is a single agency's proof-of-concept; no controlled outcome measures are reported across agencies. - Scalable: Not tested at scale; the report recommends future research to measure ROI and develop tools for quantifying benefits, implicitly acknowledging that scalability claims are unsubstantiated. The work delivers a practitioner guide (NCHRP Research Report 1169) and customizable PowerPoint communication materials; no software artifact is released. Strengths: - First systematic empirical survey of ontology practice across U.S. state DOTs, filling a genuine documentation gap. - DATA Framework integrates organizational and cultural prerequisites (leadership buy-in, workforce management, governance) alongside technical steps — a meaningful advance over purely technical ontology-building guides. - Case studies span proof-of-concept to live deployment across transportation and adjacent domains, providing concrete, named examples (HCIontology, WSDOT Data Catalog, Disease Ontology) that practitioners can examine directly. - Competency-question-driven methodology keeps the guide user-centered, reducing the risk of technically correct but practically useless ontologies. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state that data ontology is "still a developing practice" in transportation and that the guide draws on examples ranging from proof-of-concept to partial implementation. They acknowledge a significant gap in workforce knowledge and skills. They explicitly identify three future research needs: ROI measurement tools, stakeholder collaboration frameworks, and capacity-building programs — implicitly conceding that none of these are addressed in the current work. They note that many agencies responded that they have no formal or intentional ontologies, limiting the depth of case study evidence. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 50% survey response rate from a pre-selected set of 18 DOTs means the sample covers perhaps 10–15% of all state DOTs and is heavily skewed toward agencies already engaged with data modernization — the actual gap in practice across the full population is likely larger than reported. The framework presents no decision criteria for when an ontology is the right tool versus a simpler data dictionary, metadata catalog, or controlled vocabulary (MnDOT's drop-down menus approach is mentioned but not systematically compared). The cross-domain case studies (Disease Ontology from biomedicine) are instructive but introduce a significant translation burden not addressed by the guide. The report does not discuss interoperability between DOT ontologies across state lines — a critical gap given that ARNOLD and federal reporting require cross-state data harmonization. Bottom Line: A DOT data manager or CIO designing an ontology program will find this the most concrete U.S. transportation-specific roadmap available, but should treat it as a starting point rather than validated playbook, given the absence of outcome evidence and the known sampling bias toward already-progressive agencies.
Search Results
#184 — Incremental (38)

Hybrid Multi-Source RAG for Context-Aware Conversational Data Access

ceur-ws.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Severe Rigor problems (35): ~100 self-curated questions, single LLM judge from the same family used in the pipeline (circularity), no human calibration, no ablations on entity resolution or orchestration prompt, no comparison to published hybrid RAG baselines (MSPR, GNN-RAG). Novelty (35) low — late-fusion prompt orchestration is a straightforward architectural choice. Non-Obviousness (30) low. Completeness (40) and Resilience (35) weak. Impact (55) is the relative bright spot — open-source code released, runs on commercial hardware, sub-linear latency scaling. Systems weights: 0.15*35+0.15*35+0.10*30+0.15*40+0.15*35+0.30*55 = 40.5 ≈ 41. Pulled to 38 given Goodhart concerns from same-judge evaluation.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 40 Resilience 35 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a hybrid multi-source RAG architecture that combines graph-based and vector-based retrieval through a prompt-driven late-fusion orchestration layer, evaluated against single-modality baselines. - Why it's important: Real-world knowledge spans both unstructured text and structured relational data; systems relying on a single retrieval modality systematically fail on one category of queries, degrading answer quality in deployed conversational agents. - How it does it: A sequential pipeline runs both a vector RAG and a graph RAG (with automatic Cypher query generation and an entity-resolution subroutine), then passes both outputs to a final LLM that fuses, selects, or discards them based on prompt-encoded rules — no joint embedding or reranking is used. - The good: Hybrid RAG achieves 0.92 relevance and 0.90 faithfulness (0–1 normalized) versus Vector RAG's 0.71/0.75 and Graph RAG's 0.68/0.88, with the lowest variance across all three metrics, and end-to-end latency grows sub-linearly from max256 to max1024 graph scales. - The missing: The evaluation judge is a single open-weight LLM (Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct-FP8a), not human annotators, and the ~100-question manually curated dataset is small and self-constructed — the combination raises serious Goodhart's Law concerns about whether the scores reflect real answer quality. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building conversational agents over heterogeneous knowledge stores who want a modular, training-free hybrid RAG blueprint; not worth reading if you need rigorous evidence of generalization beyond the authors' own benchmark. Problem & Motivation: Conversational agents that access enterprise or domain knowledge must handle both unstructured text (where semantic similarity search excels) and structured relational data (where graph traversal and schema-compliant querying excel). Conventional RAG systems use only dense vector retrieval, producing fluent but occasionally hallucinated or relationally imprecise answers. Graph-only RAG handles structured facts faithfully but produces rigid, less fluent output and fails on underspecified queries. No prior system cleanly separates the two modalities while enabling transparent, training-free runtime fusion. Key Idea: Defer integration of heterogeneous retrieval modalities entirely to the LLM at response-generation time (late fusion), rather than merging at embedding or reranking stages. The LLM receives both retrieval outputs and explicit prompt instructions for when to merge, select, or discard each source — preserving modality-specific strengths without coupling the retrieval pipelines. Methodology: The pipeline executes three sequential stages. Stage 1 (Vector RAG): the user query is embedded via a transformer model, top-k chunks are retrieved from a Qdrant vector store (Qdrant 1.11.0), and an LLM generates a candidate answer. Stage 2 (Graph RAG): a large-context LLM (Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct-FP8a) translates the query into a Cypher query constrained by a schema whitelist (MATCH–WHERE–RETURN construct, allowed relationship types enumerated in the prompt), executes it against Neo4j 5.19.0, reformulates the result in natural language, and optionally invokes an Entity Resolution subroutine when opaque machine-readable identifiers (e.g., ns/m.07ssc) appear in graph output. Stage 3 (Hybrid Orchestration): both candidate answers are passed to a final LLM with a prompt specifying four rules — merge if both are relevant, select the more specific if only one is useful, prefer the factual source if they conflict, and decline if neither is relevant. Key design decisions: (1) late fusion instead of joint embedding — justified by modularity and interpretability, though the tradeoff against possible inter-source synergy is not ablated; (2) automatic Cypher generation with schema injection into a long-context LLM — well-justified given schema complexity; (3) entity resolution as a conditional subroutine — practical and justified by the WikiGraphs dataset's use of opaque Freebase IDs. No learned parameters are introduced; all coordination is prompt-driven. Orchestration runs via Flowise 3.0.0; LLM inference uses Groq LPU. Key Results: On a manually curated set of ~100 query–answer pairs drawn from WikiGraphs (WikiText-103 articles paired with Freebase subgraphs at max256/512/1024 nodes), Hybrid RAG scores 0.92 relevance / 0.90 faithfulness / 0.67 fluency versus Vector RAG (0.71 / 0.75 / 0.68) and Graph RAG (0.68 / 0.88 / 0.57). Hybrid RAG also achieves the lowest variance on all three metrics (var 0.039 / 0.035 / 0.024). Stratified by query category, Hybrid RAG leads on relevance in all four categories (general knowledge: 0.889; fine-grained factual: 0.894; relation-centric: 0.955; adversarial/underspecified: 0.887). Graph RAG achieves higher faithfulness on general knowledge (0.971 vs 0.908) and fine-grained factual (0.900 vs 0.841), indicating the hybrid does not universally dominate. Latency at max1024: Vector RAG ≈0.8 s, Graph RAG ≈1.9 s, Hybrid RAG ≈4.9 s; orchestration overhead (selection + generation) is stable across scales at ~1.1 s. End-to-end latency grows sub-linearly from max256 (≈4.7 s) to max1024 (≈4.9 s). Baselines are single-modality variants of the same system, not external published methods — no comparison to MSPR, PruningRAG, Clue-RAG, or GNN-RAG. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The late-fusion prompt-orchestration pattern for combining graph and vector RAG is a straightforward architectural choice applied to a new configuration; the automatic Cypher generation with schema injection reuses established prompt engineering. The genuinely novel contribution is the modular, training-free late-fusion system design and the empirical demonstration that it outperforms single-modality baselines on this benchmark — not a new algorithm or formal result. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported on the components of the hybrid (e.g., what happens if entity resolution is removed, or if the orchestration prompt rules are simplified). No error bars from multiple runs on the effectiveness evaluation (only the latency evaluation repeats each query three times). The judge is a single LLM (Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct-FP8a) scoring on a 1–10 scale normalized to [0,1] — no human validation of judge calibration is reported, and the judge model is also used in the pipeline itself (same family), which risks circularity. The benchmark of ~100 questions was manually constructed by the authors specifically for this evaluation, and the max1024 supplementary set was generated with ChatGPT and verified manually — both raise benchmark overfitting concerns. Source code is publicly released at github.com/Giorgia-DNicola/rag-multisource-agent. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on a single Intel Xeon Gold 5512U (2.1 GHz, 128 GB RAM) server with Neo4j, Qdrant, and Ollama in Docker containers and Groq LPU for inference — commercially accessible hardware. - Efficient: Hybrid RAG adds ≈3 s over Graph RAG alone (≈4.9 s total) due to the sequential pipeline and LLM orchestration step; this overhead is stable across scales but non-trivial for latency-sensitive applications. - Effective: Evidence shows it outperforms single-modality baselines on the authors' own benchmark scored by an LLM judge; whether this translates to real-world query distributions is untested. - Scalable: Tested at three graph scales (max256 to max1024, up to 742K relations and 973K vector chunks); latency is sub-linear with dataset growth, but no stress test beyond max1024 is reported and query-set size remains ~100–180 questions throughout. The work delivers a runnable software artifact (Flowise-based pipeline, open-source on GitHub). Strengths: - The late-fusion architecture genuinely preserves modality independence, enabling independent upgrade or replacement of either retrieval component without retraining anything — a concrete engineering advantage over joint-embedding approaches. - The Entity Resolution subroutine is a practical, targeted fix for a real failure mode (opaque Freebase IDs in graph output) that would otherwise degrade interpretability in production use. - Latency stability across three graph scales is a non-trivial empirical result: the orchestration overhead does not compound with datastore size, supporting the scalability claim. - The performance breakdown by query category (Table 2) honestly reveals that Graph RAG outperforms Hybrid on faithfulness for structured queries, rather than claiming uniform superiority. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that future work will explore adaptive weighting of retrieval sources (implicitly acknowledging that the current fixed prompt-rule fusion is coarse) and automated evaluation frameworks (implicitly acknowledging the LLM-as-a-Judge limitation). They do not explicitly call out the small benchmark size, the self-constructed evaluation set, or the absence of comparisons to published hybrid RAG baselines as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most serious gap is the evaluation design: ~100 manually curated questions scored by a single LLM judge with no human calibration, on a dataset the authors themselves constructed, with no comparison to any externally published hybrid RAG system. The judge (Llama-4-Scout) is also used in the graph pipeline, raising a conflict-of-interest risk in scoring. No ablation isolates the contribution of entity resolution, the specific orchestration prompt, or the Cypher generation strategy. The paper does not evaluate query latency in a multi-user or concurrent-request scenario. Fluency for Hybrid RAG (0.67) actually trails Vector RAG (0.68) and is not discussed. The relation-centric result where Graph RAG's faithfulness (0.764) trails Vector RAG (0.735) — and the adversarial Graph RAG faithfulness is entirely missing ("-" in Table 2) — is not explained. Bottom Line: A practitioner building a conversational agent over a mixed structured/unstructured knowledge base will find the architecture pattern and open-source implementation directly useful, but should treat the performance claims with caution given the small self-constructed benchmark and LLM-judge evaluation without human validation.
Citations
#185 — Incremental (38)

Enhancing Text-to-SPARQL Generation via In-Context Learning with Example Selection Strategies

mdpi.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (45) — structural taxonomy reused from same group, contribution is narrow ablation. Rigor (35) — no significance tests, single run, no code release, single model/dataset, no execution accuracy (the standard KGQA metric). Non-Obviousness (50) — finding that FT erases structural advantage is mildly interesting. Completeness (30) — no acknowledged limitations, no current-gen baselines. Resilience (30) — claims collapse under questions about execution accuracy and generalization. Impact (30) — no artifact, FLAN-T5-only. Pulled down by missing execution accuracy, no stats, and no released artifacts.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 30 Resilience 30 Impact 30
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes and evaluates a structure-aware example selection strategy for in-context learning (ICL) in text-to-SPARQL generation that combines RDF triple structural type matching with semantic similarity retrieval. - Why it's important: KGQA systems built on ICL alone fail to produce valid SPARQL when examples are structurally mismatched, yet prior ICL approaches select examples by semantic similarity only, ignoring query graph topology. - How it does it: SPARQL queries are abstracted into one of eight structural types (A–H) based on RDF triple entity/relation/class topology, and the top-k ICL examples are retrieved via cosine similarity from within the matching structural class. - The good: Without fine-tuning, the structure-aware semantic strategy (STSS) achieves 3-shot F1=0.5120 versus F1=0.2423 for fully random selection on LC-QuAD 1.0, a substantial gap that validates the structural congruence hypothesis. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to one dataset (LC-QuAD 1.0) and one model (FLAN-T5); execution accuracy against the live knowledge graph is never measured, making BLEU the sole success criterion. - Read it or not: Practitioners building ICL-based SPARQL pipelines on small or no-fine-tuning budgets will find the ablation directly actionable; those working with modern frontier models or requiring cross-dataset evidence can skip it. Problem & Motivation: Translating natural language questions into valid SPARQL for knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) requires not only correct entity–relation alignment but also adherence to rigid syntactic structure. In-context learning is attractive because it avoids the cost of fine-tuning, but the quality of the few-shot examples determines whether the model generates syntactically valid, structurally appropriate queries. Prior ICL work for text-to-SPARQL relies on semantic similarity (kNN with cosine similarity, or clustering with Sentence Transformers) or random selection, ignoring that SPARQL queries have graph topologies — number of triples, intermediate variables, entity vs. class anchors — that must match for the model to generalize the pattern. This structural blindness is the specific gap the paper addresses. Key Idea: Classify each SPARQL query into a discrete structural type by abstracting its RDF triples into an entity/relation/class subgraph pattern (ignoring lexical content), then constrain ICL example retrieval to the same structural class before applying semantic similarity ranking. The abstraction removes lexical noise while preserving the combinatorial query structure the model needs to replicate. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Structural classification: each SPARQL query is decomposed into RDF triples; subjects, predicates, and objects are typed as named entity (E), relation (R), or class (C); the resulting typed subgraph is matched to one of eight canonical topologies (A–H from Chen et al. [14,15]). (2) Encoding and retrieval: questions are encoded via the FLAN-T5 encoder's final hidden state flattened to a fixed-length vector; cosine similarity is computed pairwise against the training pool. (3) Prompt construction: selected examples are serialized with special tokens ([SEP], [ROOT], [TEMP], [END]) to mark triple boundaries and variables, then prepended to the target question. Four strategies are ablated: FR (fully random from full pool), SS (top-k cosine, no structural filter), STR (random within matched structural type), and STSS (top-k cosine within matched structural type). All experiments use 0-shot, 1-shot, and 3-shot settings, with and without fine-tuning. The structural taxonomy is borrowed wholesale from prior work by the same group; the cosine similarity metric and FLAN-T5 model are standard choices without ablation on alternatives. Key Results: Without fine-tuning: zero-shot baseline is very weak (BLEU=0.0529, F1=0.1566). STSS 3-shot achieves BLEU=0.3980, F1=0.5120, outperforming SS 3-shot (BLEU=0.3777, F1=0.4913) and FR 3-shot (BLEU=0.1496, F1=0.2423). With fine-tuning: all strategies converge to high performance; zero-shot fine-tuned achieves BLEU=0.8748, F1=0.9158. Best fine-tuned result is SS 3-shot (BLEU=0.8860, F1=0.9224); STSS 3-shot trails slightly (BLEU=0.8783, F1=0.9194); the maximum spread across all strategies is ~0.010 BLEU. The counter-intuitive finding is that structural matching becomes unnecessary after fine-tuning, while semantic diversity (FR) provides marginal benefit in 1-shot fine-tuned settings. Evaluation is on LC-QuAD 1.0 only; no current strong baselines (GPT-4, LLaMA-based SPARQL generators) are included. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The structural type taxonomy is adopted from the same group's prior publications; cosine-similarity retrieval and FLAN-T5 are standard. The genuine contribution is the empirical demonstration that (a) structural congruence of ICL examples produces a large performance gain in non-fine-tuned SPARQL generation, and (b) this advantage disappears after fine-tuning, collapsing strategy differences to under 0.01 BLEU. Rigor & Reproducibility: No statistical significance tests or error bars are reported; all results are single-run. No code or trained models are released. The LC-QuAD 1.0 dataset is publicly available. The structural type taxonomy is reused from the same research group's prior work, raising mild concern about circular design choices. Only one model and one dataset are evaluated. BLEU and token-level F1 are used as metrics; execution accuracy (whether the generated SPARQL retrieves the correct answer from the KG) is absent, which is the standard metric in KGQA evaluation and a significant methodological gap. Deployability: EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: FLAN-T5 runs on standard GPU hardware; the structural classification step is a lightweight rule-based abstraction with negligible cost. - Efficient: The overhead of structural classification and filtered retrieval is marginal relative to the baseline cosine-similarity retrieval pipeline. - Effective: The method demonstrably improves SPARQL token-level F1 in non-fine-tuned settings on LC-QuAD 1.0, but effectiveness on the actual task (correct answer retrieval) is unverified. - Scalable: Tested only on LC-QuAD 1.0 (~5,000 QA pairs on DBpedia); no evaluation on larger KGs (Wikidata, Freebase) or more complex query types (UNION, OPTIONAL, aggregation). No software artifact is released. Strengths: - The 2×4 ablation design (four strategies × with/without fine-tuning) is clean and isolates the specific effect of structural congruence, providing a genuinely informative result for ICL practitioners. - The finding that structural advantage collapses after fine-tuning is counter-intuitive and practically useful — it saves engineering effort when fine-tuning is an option. - Avoiding the cluster-count hyperparameter problem of prior clustering-based approaches is a real, if modest, practical advantage of the rule-based structural classification. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly acknowledge limitations; the conclusion section presents findings positively without identifying scope boundaries, which is a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Execution accuracy against the DBpedia endpoint is not measured — BLEU and F1 over token sequences do not distinguish queries that are near-misses from those that are entirely wrong at the answer level, the metric that matters in practice. The evaluation covers one dataset and one model; LC-QuAD 1.0 has a specific structural distribution (8 predefined types) that may not transfer to QALD, WebQSP, or Wikidata-based benchmarks. No comparison with any LLM larger than FLAN-T5 or with contemporary SPARQL generation systems. Differences in the fine-tuned condition (max ~0.007 BLEU, ~0.007 F1) are reported without confidence intervals, making it unclear whether the orderings are statistically meaningful. The structural type taxonomy is from the same group's prior work, so it is optimized for the LC-QuAD 1.0 distribution by construction. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating whether to invest in structure-aware ICL example selection for SPARQL generation will find the non-fine-tuned result useful, but the absence of execution accuracy, the single-dataset single-model scope, and the missing significance tests make this a suggestive pilot rather than a definitive finding.
Citations
#186 — Incremental (38)

Fully Homomorphic Encryption on Llama 3 model for privacy preserving LLM inference

Anes Abdennebi, Nadjia Kara, Laaziz Lahlou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical/Systems profile. Rigor (35) pulls the score down — no task-level benchmarks (top-k matching only), no code release, 2-bit quantization without ablation, calibration/evaluation use same 79 prompts (Goodhart), no error bars. Novelty (55) moderate as a first-at-scale engineering integration. Non-Obviousness (40) low — straightforward application of concrete-ml. Completeness (35) and Resilience (30) weak: SingleHead encrypts only 1/32 layers, undermining the flagship privacy claim; no comparison to TEEs/SMPC baselines. Impact (40) limited since no artifact released and scalability to larger models is unaddressed. Preprint reduces rigor further.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 30 Impact 40
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PQC-LLaMA-3, a modified LLaMA-3 inference pipeline that replaces attention mechanism operations with Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) primitives from the concrete-ml library, producing two variants: one encrypting a single attention layer and one encrypting all layers. - Why it's important: LLM deployments in healthcare, finance, and other sensitive domains expose user inputs, intermediate activations, and model weights to server-side adversaries, and quantum computers threaten existing encryption schemes protecting those pipelines. - How it does it: The attention mechanism's matrix multiplications (QKT and softmax) are replaced with FHE-compatible operations using 2-bit integer quantization and Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS) from the RLWE-based concrete-ml library, with a cleartext calibration pass establishing quantization parameters before FHE circuit compilation. - The good: The SingleHeadQLlamaModel achieves sub-second latency (0.236 s per inference on an i9-14900K) with 98.2% top-k=500 generation fidelity, and uses only 5,208 PBS operations versus 11,622 for a prior FHE GPT-2 implementation. - The missing: The accuracy metric is top-k token matching against the plaintext model's outputs rather than task-level evaluation (perplexity, BLEU, benchmark accuracy), making it impossible to assess whether encrypted inference produces useful text; additionally, the SingleHead variant encrypts only 1 of 32 transformer layers, providing a very narrow security perimeter. - Read it or not: Security researchers and MLSec engineers exploring FHE-LLM feasibility will find the infrastructure details and latency profiling useful, but practitioners needing evidence of task-level accuracy preservation under encryption should look for follow-up work with rigorous evaluation. Problem & Motivation: Organizations deploying LLMs for sensitive workloads (patient records, financial analysis, legal documents) face two overlapping risks: inference-time data exposure to honest-but-curious or compromised servers, and the emerging threat that quantum computers will break the RSA and ECC schemes currently used to protect data in transit. Existing mitigations — input/output sanitization, access control, differential privacy — operate at the application boundary and leave intermediate activations, attention weights, and gradients exposed within the model pipeline. Applying encryption directly to the forward pass would close this gap, but prior work on encrypted ML focused on smaller models (decision trees, small CNNs), and the computational overhead of FHE for large transformer models was considered prohibitive. Key Idea: The core insight is that concrete-ml's Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS) simultaneously resets ciphertext noise and evaluates a non-linear lookup table, making it possible to approximate softmax and activation functions inside the FHE circuit without an exponential noise blowup. By restricting FHE to the attention sub-layer (the most privacy-sensitive component) and using 2-bit integer quantization to reduce ciphertext polynomial dimensions, the authors bring per-token inference latency into the sub-second range on commodity CPUs. Methodology: The pipeline has five stages. (1) Calibration: the modified QLlamaLMHeadModel runs a cleartext forward pass to estimate per-layer scaling factors and zero-points for 2-bit fixed-point quantization, stored in concrete-ml's DualArray structure. (2) FHE circuit compilation: concrete-ml compiles the quantized attention forward pass into a Boolean/arithmetic circuit, statically placing PBS nodes wherever non-linear operations or noise-refresh steps are required. (3) Encryption: the client encrypts input token embeddings using the RLWE public key and sends them with the evaluation key to the server. (4) Encrypted evaluation: the server executes attention (QKT dot-products, softmax approximation, V-projection) homomorphically; remaining 31 layers run in plaintext. (5) Decryption and generation: decrypted integer logits are dequantized and fed to a plaintext softmax for token sampling. Two model variants are evaluated: SingleHeadQLlamaModel (1 of 32 decoder layers encrypted) and MultiHeadsQLlamaModel (all 32 layers encrypted). Three execution modes — disable (no quantization), simulate (quantized, no FHE overhead), execute (full FHE) — allow separating quantization error from homomorphic noise. Quantization is set to 2 bits, selected empirically as the best accuracy/performance trade-off. Ciphertext packing via CRT and SIMD batching amortizes noise and enables parallel matrix operations. Critical design decisions: (a) restricting FHE to the attention layer is justified by the privacy-sensitivity argument but is not formally analyzed; (b) 2-bit quantization is chosen empirically without ablation over other bit widths; (c) softmax replacement with a polynomial approximation is necessary but the approximation quality and its effect on downstream token distributions is not quantified. Key Results: Evaluated on 79 text-completion prompts on three CPU machines (AMD EPYC 7413 24-core M1, Intel i7-12700K M2, Intel i9-14900K M3), averaged over 5 runs per top-k setting. Short-context (1–10 new tokens), SingleHead: accuracy rises from 47.2% (top-k=1) to 81.7% (top-k=10); inference time on M3 ≈ 0.237–0.239 s; compile time ≈ 3.5–8.5 s. Short-context, MultiHead: accuracy 51.6% (top-k=1) to 87.4% (top-k=10); inference ≈ 0.835–1.26 s on M3; compile ≈ 12.3–26.3 s. Long-context (70–500 new tokens), execute mode: SingleHead 91.3%→98.2%, MultiHead 94.7%→97.7%, both on M3 at 0.233–0.276 s and 0.790–2.07 s respectively. Throughput (Appendix A, M3): SingleHead ≈ 81 tokens/s vs. plain LLaMA-3 ≈ 142 tokens/s (1.75× slowdown); MultiHead ≈ 30 tokens/s (4.7× slowdown). On M1, SingleHead counterintuitively outperforms plain LLaMA-3 (35–37 vs. 22–24 tokens/s), attributed to larger L1 cache fitting encrypted ciphertext blocks. FHE efficiency: 5,208 PBS operations for a 6-token sequence (vs. 11,622 for a prior FHE GPT-2 [ref 31]); memory 2.02 KB/token (single head), 1.12 KB/token (multi head). Baselines: comparison is exclusively against plain LLaMA-3 within this paper; no comparison against other privacy-preserving inference systems (secure MPC, TEEs, other FHE-LLM implementations). The accuracy metric is top-k generation matching: the fraction of inference steps where the plaintext model's top-1 next token appears within the encrypted model's top-k predictions. High accuracy at large k (e.g., 98% at top-k=500) is a weak signal — it does not measure whether generated text is coherent or correct on any task benchmark. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The integration of FHE into a production-scale LLM (LLaMA-3 8B) using concrete-ml is a genuine engineering advance beyond prior work limited to smaller models. The proposed FHE-specific metrics (PBS/token, memory/token) and the cache hierarchy analysis of encrypted inference are new empirical contributions. The core FHE theory (RLWE, PBS, BGV/TFHE variants) and the concrete-ml library are entirely existing work; the paper is a targeted application rather than a cryptographic or algorithmic contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation over quantization bit-width (2 bits selected without systematic comparison to 4 or 8 bits). No error bars or significance tests are reported for accuracy; 5-run averages are used for timing (standard deviation <5% noted for inference times). The evaluation set is 79 author-selected text prompts — no standard NLP benchmark (MMLU, HellaSwag, GSM8K, perplexity on C4/WikiText) is used. No code repository is mentioned or linked. The accuracy metric (top-k token matching against plaintext) is not a standard measure of language model quality and cannot be compared to the literature. Quantization parameters are calibrated on the same 79 prompts used for evaluation, raising Goodhart's Law concerns. The privacy/security analysis (Table 6) is qualitative, not a formal proof or adversarial evaluation. Deployability (EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs entirely on commodity CPUs (Intel i7/i9, AMD EPYC) with no GPU requirement; the i9-14900K machine achieving 0.236 s latency is commercially available. - Efficient: SingleHead incurs a 1.75× throughput penalty vs. plaintext on M3 (81 vs. 142 tokens/s); MultiHead incurs 4.7× (30 vs. 142 tokens/s); compile overhead of 3.5–26 s is a one-time cost per circuit. - Effective: Evidence of functional text generation is indirect — top-k matching confirms token-distribution preservation but no task-level evaluation (BLEU, ROUGE, benchmark accuracy) demonstrates that generated text is useful; the softmax approximation's impact on text quality is unmeasured. - Scalable: Tested only on LLaMA-3 8B; behavior for larger models (70B, 405B), longer sequences (>500 tokens), or batch inference is not evaluated and likely faces prohibitive FHE circuit sizes; no extrapolation is provided. The paper delivers a working software prototype (PQC-LLaMA-3) but does not release code. Strengths: - First reported integration of FHE at inference time for a contemporary production-scale LLM (LLaMA-3 8B), demonstrating sub-second per-token latency on CPU hardware. - Concrete FHE efficiency metrics (PBS/token, memory/token, compile time vs. inference time) provide a useful cost model for practitioners evaluating FHE-LLM trade-offs. - The cache hierarchy analysis (Appendix B) linking FHE ciphertext expansion to L2 pressure and LLC behavior is genuinely informative about where the performance cost originates. - The three-mode execution framework (disable/simulate/execute) cleanly separates quantization error from homomorphic noise, confirming that 2-bit quantization introduces negligible accuracy loss relative to FHE noise. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that FHE does not protect against side-channel attacks (timing, cache, EM), model extraction via black-box queries, or data poisoning by authorized users (Table 6). They note that compile time (3.5–26 s) is a practical bottleneck but argue it is amortized across multiple queries. They acknowledge that coupling with additional security mechanisms (differential privacy, TEEs, query rate limits) is necessary for comprehensive protection. These limitations are confronted honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The security guarantee is asymmetric: the SingleHead model encrypts 1 of 32 layers; an adversary observing plaintext intermediate activations from 31 layers can reconstruct substantial information about the input, undermining the privacy claim for the flagship result. - The accuracy metric (top-k token matching vs. plaintext) cannot distinguish a model generating coherent text from one generating plausible-but-wrong completions; the paper never shows an actual generated text sample or measures downstream task performance. - No comparison against alternative privacy-preserving inference approaches (Intel SGX/TDX TEEs, SMPC protocols, or other FHE-LLM systems) prevents any cost/benefit assessment. - The 79-prompt evaluation set is too small and not publicly described; results may not generalize across prompt distributions or domains. - 2-bit quantization is extreme and the effect on output quality for real-world tasks (summarization, Q&A, code generation) is entirely unknown. - Compilation times (up to 26 s for MultiHead) are presented as one-time costs, but the paper does not address what triggers recompilation (sequence length changes, prompt structure changes) in a real deployment. Bottom Line: This preprint demonstrates that FHE-encrypted LLaMA-3 inference is computationally feasible on commodity CPUs at acceptable latencies, but the absence of task-level evaluation, the weak single-layer security perimeter of the primary variant, and the non-standard accuracy metric leave the central claims — that PQC-LLaMA-3 both preserves output quality and provides meaningful privacy — unsubstantiated; a practitioner cannot adopt this without independent task-level and security analysis.
Search Results 2604.12168 PDF
#187 — Incremental (38)

Using Synthetic Data for Machine Learning-based Childhood Vaccination Prediction in Narok, Kenya

Jimmy Bach, Yang Li, Yaqi Liu, John Sankok, Rose Kimani, Carrie B. Dolan, Julius N. Odhiambo, Haipeng Chen · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (40) and Non-Obviousness (35) are low — applies existing TabSyn/XGBoost to a new dataset with no methodological contribution. Rigor (35) is pulled down by absent privacy evaluation despite privacy being RQ2, no synthetic-data baselines, single-run real condition, and no released data/code. Completeness (45) suffers because the central privacy claim is unsubstantiated. Practical Impact (35) is limited to a paper-form proof-of-concept with no artifact. Weighted: 0.20*40+0.25*35+0.15*35+0.15*45+0.15*45+0.10*35 ≈ 38.
empirical Novelty 40 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 35
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a pipeline combining Logistic Regression and XGBoost classifiers with TabSyn-generated synthetic tabular data to predict individual childhood vaccination outcomes and preserve patient privacy for a nomadic community clinic in Narok County, Kenya. - Why it's important: Approximately 20 million children globally remain unvaccinated or under-vaccinated, and community health providers in nomadic LMIC settings lack EHR infrastructure and cannot safely share paper-registry data for ML model development due to patient re-identification risk. - How it does it: TabSyn (a VAE plus score-based latent diffusion model) is trained on digitized MOH 510 paper vaccination registries to generate high-fidelity synthetic records, which are then used as a drop-in replacement for real data to train and evaluate classifiers. - The good: Classifiers trained on synthetic data alone match real-data performance within noise for early-stage vaccines (BCG XGBoost F1: real 0.8853, synthetic 0.8866), and column-wise density fidelity scores exceed 0.94 across all features. - The missing: The core privacy claim is never empirically tested — no membership inference attack, re-identification risk quantification, or differential privacy analysis is performed, leaving the paper's central privacy contribution unsubstantiated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for global health informatics practitioners evaluating synthetic data for privacy-preserving ML in data-scarce LMIC clinics; ML researchers will find no new methods and insufficient privacy evaluation. Problem & Motivation In rural nomadic communities like the Maasai in Narok County, Kenya, childhood vaccination records exist only as paper entries in the Ministry of Health's MOH 510 registry. The community health provider, Community Health Partners (CHP), operates four clinics without electronic health records, making real-time data-driven interventions infeasible. Vaccination coverage varies enormously across the 17-dose immunization schedule — from 95.6% for BCG to 13.7% for MR2 — suggesting substantial dropout during the vaccination cycle. Beyond data scarcity, the small, geographically concentrated nature of nomadic sub-communities makes patient re-identification a genuine risk if raw records are shared for ML development, raising legal and ethical barriers under Kenya's 2019 Data Protection Act. Existing ML literature on vaccination prediction predominantly relies on nationally representative surveys (e.g., DHS data), which do not generalize to subnational, clinic-level heterogeneity or to populations that do not engage with formal health systems consistently. Key Idea The core technical insight is that TabSyn — a latent diffusion model for mixed-type tabular data — can generate synthetic vaccination records that preserve both marginal distributions and inter-feature correlations well enough that classifiers trained exclusively on synthetic data achieve comparable predictive performance to those trained on the original digitized registry. This allows model development without exposing real patient records, enabling privacy-preserving deployment in settings where data pooling is otherwise prohibited. Methodology Data collection: 9,718 children recorded at four CHP clinics (September 2016–June 2024) were digitized from paper MOH 510 forms, then filtered to 6,913 children aged ≤8 residing in Narok County with complete records. Features include age (at end of observation window), gender, home village, clinic, and first visit day. Seventeen binary outcomes indicate receipt of each vaccine at any point during the study period. Predictive modeling (RQ1): Logistic Regression and XGBoost from scikit-learn, using default hyperparameters (intentionally untuned to enable direct comparison across training conditions). An 80/20 train-test split; SMOTE applied for class-imbalanced vaccines. Evaluation: class-weighted Precision, Recall, and F1 over 5 iterations for synthetic conditions. Synthetic data generation (RQ2): TabSyn consists of a VAE that encodes mixed numeric/categorical features into a continuous latent space (using quantile transformation for numerics and one-hot encoding for categoricals, then linear projection and Transformer encoder/decoder), followed by score-based diffusion trained in the latent space via a denoising score-matching objective. One TabSyn model is trained per vaccine on training data only to prevent leakage. Five synthetic datasets are sampled per vaccine. Three training conditions compared: real data only, synthetic data only, and real+synthetic combinations. Fidelity evaluation: Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test for numerical columns, Total Variation Distance for categorical columns, Pearson Correlation for numerical pairs, and Contingency Similarity for categorical pairs — all taken as complements so higher = more similar to real data. Critical design decisions: (1) Hyperparameters left at defaults — justified for comparison fairness but establishes a performance floor rather than ceiling. (2) One TabSyn model per vaccine outcome — avoids leakage but prevents joint modeling of correlated vaccine sequences. (3) SMOTE applied selectively for imbalanced outcomes — not systematically compared across all 17 vaccines. Key Results Early-stage vaccines with high coverage achieve strong predictive performance: BCG (95.6% positive) XGBoost F1 = 0.8853 (real) vs. 0.8866 (synthetic); DPT1 (84.4% positive) XGBoost F1 = 0.7723 (real) vs. 0.7692 (synthetic). Performance degrades for late-stage, lower-coverage vaccines: MR2 (13.7% positive) XGBoost F1 = 0.8172 (real) vs. 0.8097 (synthetic) — numerically similar but driven largely by the majority non-vaccinated class. MR1 (37.8% positive) XGBoost F1 = 0.7285 (real) vs. 0.7046 (synthetic), a 3.3% drop. Combining real and synthetic data does not consistently improve over real alone across any vaccine. Synthetic fidelity: Column-wise density scores exceed 0.94 for all features (clinic: 0.9888, age: 0.9628, gender: 0.9833, village: 0.9529, first visit day: 0.9846). Pairwise correlation scores exceed 0.85 for all feature pairs, with the age-related pairs slightly lower (0.85–0.95) due to the continuous, skewed age distribution. Feature importance rankings are consistent between real-trained and synthetic-trained XGBoost models — first visit day, age, and gender are top predictors in both conditions. Computational cost: TabSyn trains in ~90 minutes on CPU; sampling a dataset matching the original size takes ~20 seconds. No formal statistical significance tests between conditions are reported; variance is characterized by standard deviation across 5 synthetic iterations only. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING; ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (applied deployment). No new methods are introduced — TabSyn, Logistic Regression, and XGBoost are all existing tools. The genuine novelty is the empirical demonstration that latent diffusion-based tabular synthesis can replace real patient data without significant ML performance loss in a locally digitized, nomadic LMIC context, and the operationalization of this pipeline in a resource-constrained community clinic setting. The dataset itself (locally digitized, clinic-level, nomadic population in Kenya) is contextually novel but is not released. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations of TabSyn components are absent — no comparison to simpler synthetic methods (GAN, VAE-only, SMOTE-only augmentation), so it is unclear whether TabSyn's diffusion component adds value over the VAE alone or over a simpler generative baseline. Five synthetic sampling iterations provide variance estimates for the synthetic conditions, but the real-data condition reports a single run with no standard deviation (making direct statistical comparison of real vs. synthetic impossible). No formal privacy evaluation is included: membership inference attacks, attribute inference, or re-identification risk are not tested despite privacy being the paper's second research question. Hyperparameters are left at defaults, which is a legitimate comparison strategy but means all performance numbers understate achievable accuracy. Data are not publicly released (Section 7 states available on reasonable request to protect patient confidentiality). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.08902v1, April 2026) submitted to the Green Lab 2020/2021 ACM workshop — peer review status is limited. The paper was evaluated on a single community in a single county, with no external validation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: TabSyn trains on a consumer CPU (~90 minutes) with no GPU required, and the scikit-learn classifiers are similarly lightweight — deployable in low-resource settings without cloud infrastructure. - Efficient: The full synthetic data pipeline (train + sample) runs in under 2 hours locally; the 20-second sampling time makes iterative synthetic augmentation feasible at the clinic level. - Effective: Classifiers reliably identify high-coverage early-stage vaccine recipients (F1 > 0.85 for BCG) but struggle with clinically important late-stage dropout prediction (DPT3, MR1, MR2, F1 ~0.68–0.73), where the actual intervention need is greatest. - Scalable: Tested only at n=6,913 from four clinics in one county; the paper asserts scalability to other LMIC settings based solely on CPU training time, with no multi-site or larger-n experiment. The work delivers no software artifact or released dataset — it stops at paper form with data available on request. Strengths - The study uses a locally digitized, contextually representative dataset from a nomadic LMIC community clinic — not a nationally representative survey — filling a genuine gap in the literature where subnational, clinic-level data is rarely available. - Empirical demonstration that synthetic data performance matches real-data performance within noise across 17 vaccines and two classifier families provides a concrete, quantified proof-of-concept for synthetic data deployment in this context. - Feature importance consistency between real and synthetic trained models (same top-3 predictors: first visit day, age, gender) provides an indirect validation that synthetic data preserves causal structure, not just marginal distributions. - The ~90-minute CPU training time establishes practical feasibility for resource-constrained LMIC deployments without requiring cloud infrastructure or GPU access. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the dataset may underrepresent children who do not attend CHP clinics, limiting generalizability to the full catchment population; (2) data entry errors during manual digitization introduce potential noise and bias; (3) late-stage vaccine prediction is weaker due to class imbalance and smaller populations at advanced schedule stages. These limitations are stated directly and without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The privacy claim is the paper's second research question but is never empirically evaluated: no membership inference attack, re-identification risk score, or differential privacy analysis is conducted. In a small, geographically concentrated nomadic community where village and age jointly narrow the candidate pool, this omission is not minor. - No comparison to alternative synthetic data methods (ITS-GAN, medBGAN, VAE-only, SMOTE-only augmentation) means TabSyn is assumed superior by default rather than demonstrated as such. - The real-data condition reports a single evaluation run with no variance estimate, making it impossible to determine whether the real vs. synthetic F1 differences are statistically meaningful or within random fluctuation. - The paper evaluates only whether vaccination ever occurred in the 8-year window — not timeliness or adherence to schedule — which is a weaker proxy for the intervention target (identifying children at risk of missed doses). - Combining real and synthetic data never outperforms real alone, and this null finding is not analyzed or explained, leaving the practical utility of augmentation unresolved. Bottom Line A credible, honest proof-of-concept for privacy-preserving ML in a genuinely underserved data context, but the unsubstantiated privacy claim and absence of synthetic data baselines leave the work's core value proposition incomplete — global health informaticists exploring synthetic data for LMIC clinic deployment will find it directly relevant as a starting point, but should not treat the privacy findings as established.
Citations 2604.08902 PDF
#188 — Incremental (36)

Digital Technologies for Steelmaking

sustainsteel.ac.uk · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Theoretical profile (survey). Rigor (35) and Completeness (50) pulled the composite down — no systematic review protocol (PRISMA) is applied, selection criteria from 11,523→~11 profiled papers are unstated, and self-citation concentration (Beden et al. refs 8/9/10) is unacknowledged; it is a funded technical report, not peer-reviewed. Novelty (30) and Non-Obviousness (35) are modest — the RAMI 4.0 + SAAS + Hybrid AI synthesis for steelmaking is a useful framing but the constituent components are well-established elsewhere. Impact (25) is low: no artifact, no blueprint, only a research agenda. Weighted: 0.25*30+0.20*35+0.25*35+0.10*50+0.15*40+0.05*25 ≈ 36.
theoretical Novelty 30 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 25
Show full summary & analysis
